statement: During a physical examination, a clinician notices 'dimpling' of the skin over a breast mass. This clinical sign is caused by the tumor applying traction to which specific structures?
optionExplanations: Traction on lactiferous ducts causes nipple retraction, not skin dimpling.
Traction on the fibrous septa (suspensory ligaments of Cooper) causes dimpling of the overlying skin.
Areolar glands produce tubercles but do not cause dimpling when pulled.
Retromammary tissue is loose and does not transmit traction to the skin effectively.
Pectoral fascia is deep to the retromammary space.
citation: The lobes of the gland are separated by fibrous septa that serve as suspensory ligaments

statement: A patient with a nerve injury affecting the pectoral region shows significant difficulty when trying to fold his arms across his chest or scratch his opposite shoulder. Which primary actions of the pectoralis major are most likely impaired?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis major is an adductor, not an abductor.
The pectoralis major muscle adducts the arm and rotates it medially.
Pectoralis major is a flexor (clavicular head), not an extensor.
Protraction is primarily a function of the serratus anterior.
Depression of the shoulder is a function of the pectoralis minor and subclavius.
citation: Adducts arm and rotates it medially

statement: Where does the pectoralis minor muscle insert?
optionExplanations: The greater tubercle is an insertion for rotator cuff muscles.
The lesser tubercle is the insertion for the subscapularis.
The pectoralis minor inserts into the coracoid process of the scapula.
The acromion is an attachment for the deltoid and trapezius.
The bicipital groove is the insertion for the pectoralis major, latissimus dorsi, and teres major.
citation: Coracoid process of scapula

statement: A surgeon is performing an axillary lymph node clearance. He is warned to be extremely careful when working on the medial wall of the axilla because a specific motor nerve is unusually vulnerable due to its position. Where is this nerve located?
optionExplanations: Most motor nerves are deep to their muscles, but this one is an exception.
The long thoracic nerve is vulnerable because it descends over the superficial surface of the serratus anterior.
The medial pectoral nerve is deep to the pectoralis minor, but it is not the nerve on the medial axillary wall.
The lateral pectoral nerve is within the clavipectoral fascia.
The subscapular nerves are on the posterior wall.
citation: descends over the superficial surface of the serratus anterior muscle

statement: A 50-year-old woman notices that her left nipple has recently become inverted and cannot be pulled out. On examination, a firm mass is felt deep to the areola. What is the anatomical mechanism behind this nipple retraction?
optionExplanations: Traction on suspensory ligaments causes skin dimpling, not nipple retraction.
Carcinoma causes nipple retraction by pulling on and shortening the lactiferous ducts.
Infection usually causes swelling and pain, not isolated retraction.
Atrophy occurs post-menopause but does not cause localized retraction.
Pectoralis major contraction does not affect the nipple directly.
citation: A carcinoma within the breast substance can cause retraction of the nipple by pulling on the lactiferous ducts.

statement: A patient presents with a palpable mass in the axilla. The physician suspects it might be breast tissue rather than an enlarged lymph node. Which part of the mammary gland is known to pierce the deep fascia and enter the axilla?
optionExplanations: Suspensory ligaments are fibrous, not glandular tissue.
There is no structure called the retromammary process.
The axillary tail (Tail of Spence) is a normal extension of the breast that pierces the deep fascia to enter the axilla.
The ampulla is a dilation of the duct near the nipple.
The areola is the pigmented skin around the nipple.
citation: An extension of the gland called the axillary tail (see Fig. 3.14C) continues upward and laterally, pierces the deep fascia

statement: Which nerves provide the motor supply to the pectoralis major muscle?
optionExplanations: The long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior.
The thoracodorsal nerve supplies the latissimus dorsi.
The pectoralis major is supplied by both the medial and lateral pectoral nerves.
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid and teres minor.
The suprascapular nerve supplies the supraspinatus and infraspinatus.
citation: Medial and lateral pectoral nerves

statement: A medical student is identifying the boundaries of the clavipectoral fascia during a dissection. He traces the fascia upward from the pectoralis minor muscle. Where should he find its superior attachment?
optionExplanations: The first rib is a medial boundary for the axillary apex, not the superior fascia attachment.
The sternum is a medial attachment for the pectoral fascia, not the clavipectoral fascia.
The clavipectoral fascia attaches superiorly to the clavicle.
The coracoid process is a lateral attachment point for the fascia.
The acromion is not an attachment for this fascia.
citation: Superiorly, it attaches to the clavicle.

statement: A patient has a lesion of the medial pectoral nerve. While the pectoralis major still functions due to the lateral pectoral nerve, the pectoralis minor is paralyzed. Which specific movement of the shoulder girdle will be most affected?
optionExplanations: Elevation is performed by the trapezius and levator scapulae.
The pectoralis minor acts to depress the point of the shoulder.
Lateral rotation of the scapula is done by the serratus anterior and trapezius.
Abduction is a humeral movement, not a primary pectoralis minor action.
Extension is a humeral movement.
citation: Depresses point of shoulder

statement: Where does the serratus anterior muscle insert?
optionExplanations: The lateral border is the origin for the teres muscles.
The serratus anterior inserts along the entire medial border of the scapula.
The spine of the scapula is an attachment for the deltoid and trapezius.
The coracoid process is for the pectoralis minor and short head of biceps.
The glenoid cavity is an articular surface.
citation: Medial border of scapula

statement: A lactating mother develops a painful, red, and fluctuant mass in her right breast. The diagnosis of a breast abscess is made. To minimize damage to the lactiferous ducts and prevent the spread of infection between lobes, how should the surgical incision be oriented?
optionExplanations: Transverse incisions would cut across multiple radial ducts.
Vertical incisions do not align with the radial arrangement of the lobes.
A radial incision avoids cutting across the radially arranged ducts and limits infection spread between compartments.
Cruciate incisions cause excessive scarring and duct damage.
Circumferential incisions are used for areolar access but not for draining deep abscesses.
citation: Abscesses should be drained through a radial incision to avoid spreading of the infection into neighboring compartments

statement: During an operation, a surgeon accidentally severs a nerve as it emerges from the medial cord and enters the deep surface of the pectoralis minor. Which other muscle will also lose part of its innervation due to this injury?
optionExplanations: Subclavius is supplied by its own nerve from the upper trunk.
Serratus anterior is supplied by the long thoracic nerve.
The medial pectoral nerve supplies both the pectoralis minor and the pectoralis major.
Latissimus dorsi is supplied by the thoracodorsal nerve.
Coracobrachialis is supplied by the musculocutaneous nerve.
citation: The medial pectoral nerve arises from the medial cord of the brachial plexus, supplies and pierces the pectoralis minor muscle, and next supplies the pectoralis major muscle

statement: A patient presents with a minor injury to the root of the neck. The physician explains that a small muscle located between the first rib and the clavicle helps stabilize the sternoclavicular joint. What is the primary function of this subclavius muscle?
optionExplanations: The subclavius does not primarily elevate the rib.
The subclavius depresses the clavicle and steadies it during shoulder girdle movements.
Protraction is done by the serratus anterior.
The subclavius has no attachment to the humerus.
Retraction is done by the rhomboids and trapezius.
citation: Depresses the clavicle and steadies this bone during movements of the shoulder girdle

statement: A patient is brought to the ER with a stab wound to the 'pectoral region'. To which specific topographical area of the body is the triage nurse referring?
optionExplanations: The posterior aspect is the scapular region.
The pectoral region is defined as the anterior aspect of the shoulder.
The neck is a separate region.
The medial arm is the brachial region.
The back is the vertebral/scapular region.
citation: The pectoral region is the anterior aspect of the shoulder.

statement: A 20-year-old woman is concerned about a small, mole-like structure located in her skin just below the left inframammary fold. The doctor identifies it as a supernumerary nipple. This congenital anomaly occurs along which embryonic line?
optionExplanations: The nuchal line is on the skull.
Supernumerary nipples occur along the milk ridge, which extends from the axilla to the inguinal region.
The axillary fold is a surface landmark, not the embryonic line.
There is no embryonic 'cephalic groove' related to breast development.
The pectoral crest is not an embryonic term for breast development.
citation: a linear thickening of ectoderm appears called the milk ridge, which extends from the axilla obliquely to the inguinal region.

statement: A 52-year-old woman presents with a firm, painless mass in the upper lateral quadrant of her left breast. During a clinical examination, the physician palpates for enlarged lymph nodes. Which group of nodes is most likely to be involved first in the metastatic spread from this location?
optionExplanations: Internal thoracic nodes primarily drain the medial quadrants of the breast.
The lateral quadrants of the breast typically drain into the anterior axillary (pectoral) group of nodes.
Infraclavicular nodes receive superficial lymph from the lateral side of the limb, not primarily the lateral breast quadrant.
Supraclavicular nodes are usually involved in later stages or from different primary sites.
Posterior intercostal nodes drain a small portion of the breast following the posterior intercostal arteries.
citation: The lateral quadrants of the breast drain into the anterior axillary or pectoral group of nodes

statement: A 60-year-old female patient notices that her right nipple has recently become inverted and pulled inward. On examination, a hard mass is felt deep to the areola. What is the anatomical mechanism responsible for this specific physical finding?
optionExplanations: Contraction of suspensory ligaments typically causes skin dimpling, not nipple retraction.
Invasion of the retromammary space would cause the breast to become fixed to the pectoral muscles.
Carcinoma pulling on the lactiferous ducts is the classic cause of recent-onset nipple retraction.
Atrophy of glandular tissue occurs postmenopausally but does not cause localized retraction.
Obstruction of the ampulla might cause localized swelling or discharge but not retraction.
citation: A carcinoma within the breast substance can cause retraction of the nipple by pulling on the lactiferous ducts.

statement: A 24-year-old lactating mother presents with a painful, red, and swollen area in her left breast. The physician diagnoses an acute breast abscess. Why does this infection typically remain confined to a single wedge-shaped area of the breast initially?
optionExplanations: The retromammary space allows for breast mobility but does not compartmentalize infections.
Lactiferous ducts are the route of entry but do not provide the physical barrier for localization.
Fibrous septa (suspensory ligaments) divide the breast into compartments, localizing early infections.
The areolar skin is thin and does not prevent the spread of deep infections.
The pectoral fascia lies deep to the breast and does not divide the glandular tissue.
citation: Because of the presence of the fibrous septa, the infection remains localized to one compartment or lobe to begin with.

statement: A newborn is noted to have a significant depression on the right side of the chest. The physician suspects a congenital absence of a pectoral muscle. If the sternocostal portion of this muscle is missing, which movements of the shoulder joint will be most significantly weakened?
optionExplanations: Abduction is performed by the deltoid and supraspinatus, not the pectoralis major.
Lateral rotation is performed by the infraspinatus and teres minor.
The pectoralis major is a powerful adductor and medial rotator; its absence weakens these actions.
Extension is primarily a function of the latissimus dorsi and posterior deltoid.
Protraction is primarily the function of the serratus anterior.
citation: The sternocostal origin is the most commonly missing part, and this causes weakness in adduction and medial rotation of the shoulder joint.

statement: A 45-year-old woman is concerned about a lump she felt in her armpit during a self-examination. The physician explains that breast tissue can normally extend into this region. What is the name of this extension and what structure does it pierce to enter the axilla?
optionExplanations: Suspensory ligaments support the breast but do not extend into the axilla as a tail.
The axillary tail is a normal extension of the breast that pierces the deep fascia to enter the axilla.
Lactiferous ducts are internal to the breast lobes and do not pierce the pectoral fascia to enter the axilla.
Areolar glands are superficial and located on the skin of the areola.
The ampulla is a dilation of the duct near the nipple, not an extension into the axilla.
citation: An extension of the gland called the axillary tail... pierces the deep fascia at the lower border of the pectoralis major muscle, and enters the axilla.

statement: Which of the following structures pierces the clavipectoral fascia to reach the superficial tissues or the axilla?
optionExplanations: The medial pectoral nerve pierces the pectoralis minor, not the clavipectoral fascia directly.
The basilic vein pierces the deep fascia of the arm, not the clavipectoral fascia.
The cephalic vein is one of the key structures that pierces the clavipectoral fascia.
The long thoracic nerve descends on the superficial surface of the serratus anterior.
The axillary artery is deep to the fascia and does not pierce it.
citation: The lateral pectoral nerve, cephalic vein, branches of the thoracoacromial artery, and lymphatic channels from the infraclavicular nodes pierce the clavipectoral fascia

statement: During a surgical procedure in the axilla, a surgeon uses a thin, triangular muscle as a landmark to identify the different parts of the axillary artery. Which muscle is being used for this purpose?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis major is too superficial and large to serve as the specific landmark for the three parts.
The subclavius is located superiorly and does not cross the artery to divide it into three parts.
The serratus anterior forms the medial wall of the axilla but does not divide the artery.
The pectoralis minor crosses the axillary artery, dividing it into proximal, deep, and distal parts.
The coracobrachialis forms part of the lateral wall but is not the landmark for arterial division.
citation: The pectoralis minor muscle crosses in front of the axillary artery and divides it into three parts

statement: A physician is examining a patient with a breast tumor. He notes that the breast can be moved freely over the chest wall. This mobility is primarily due to the presence of which anatomical plane?
optionExplanations: The clavipectoral fascia is deep to the pectoral muscles and does not provide breast mobility.
The retromammary space contains loose tissue that allows the breast to move over the pectoral muscles.
Suspensory ligaments anchor the skin to the ducts, they do not provide mobility over the chest wall.
The breast lies within the superficial fascia, but the specific space deep to it is the retromammary space.
The axillary sheath encloses neurovascular structures, not the breast.
citation: A space filled by loose connective tissue called the retromammary space lies deep to the breast and superficial to the underlying pectoral muscles.

statement: A 58-year-old woman presents with 'dimpling' of the skin on her left breast, resembling the skin of an orange (peau d'orange). This clinical sign is caused by the involvement of which anatomical structures?
optionExplanations: Traction on lactiferous ducts causes nipple retraction, not skin dimpling.
Traction on the fibrous septa (suspensory ligaments) by a tumor pulls the skin inward, causing dimpling.
Areolar glands are not involved in the structural tethering of the breast skin.
The pectoral fascia is deep to the breast and does not attach directly to the skin.
The retromammary tissue is loose and does not cause skin dimpling when invaded.
citation: a scirrhous carcinoma or a disease such as a breast abscess may apply traction to the fibrous septa that causes dimpling of the skin.

statement: What are the primary actions of the pectoralis major muscle on the humerus?
optionExplanations: Abduction is the opposite of the pectoralis major's primary action.
The pectoralis major adducts and medially rotates the arm.
Extension is generally performed by the latissimus dorsi, and lateral rotation by the rotator cuff.
Protraction is a scapular movement, not a humeral movement.
Elevation and retraction are scapular movements performed by the trapezius and rhomboids.
citation: Adducts arm and medially rotates arm; clavicular fibers also flex arm

statement: What is the primary anatomical function of the subclavius muscle?
optionExplanations: The subclavius originates from the first rib but its action is on the clavicle.
Protraction of the scapula is the role of the serratus anterior.
The subclavius depresses and steadies the clavicle.
Medial rotation of the humerus is performed by the pectoralis major and subscapularis.
Abduction of the arm is performed by the deltoid and supraspinatus.
citation: Depresses the clavicle and steadies this bone during surface movements of the shoulder girdle

statement: During a mastectomy, a surgeon must ligate the arteries supplying the medial portion of the breast. These vessels are primarily branches of which major artery?
optionExplanations: The axillary artery supplies the lateral part of the breast via its branches.
The lateral thoracic artery is a branch of the axillary artery supplying the lateral breast.
The internal thoracic artery provides perforating branches that supply the medial quadrants.
The thoracoacromial artery supplies the superior part of the pectoral region.
The subscapular artery supplies the posterior axillary wall.
citation: The arterial supply to the breasts includes the perforating branches of the internal thoracic artery and the intercostal arteries.

statement: A patient who underwent a radical mastectomy now complains of difficulty combing her hair. On examination, she cannot raise her arm above her head, and her scapula protrudes posteriorly. Which muscle's function is impaired?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis major loss does not cause 'winging' of the scapula.
Pectoralis minor loss does not prevent overhead abduction.
Serratus anterior paralysis prevents scapular rotation needed for overhead abduction and causes winging.
Latissimus dorsi is an adductor and extensor, not an abductor.
Trapezius paralysis causes a 'dropped shoulder', not classic winging of the medial border.
citation: Paralysis of the serratus anterior results in the inability to rotate the scapula during abduction of the arm above a right angle.

statement: A 22-year-old woman in her second month of pregnancy notices that the skin around her nipples has become significantly darker. What is the anatomical reason for this change?
optionExplanations: Increased vascularity occurs but does not primarily cause the darkening of the areola.
Hypertrophy of areolar glands causes tubercles, not generalized darkening.
Increased melanin deposits in the epidermis cause the areola to darken during pregnancy.
Colostrum is fluid within the ducts and does not affect skin pigment.
Fibrous septa are internal structures and do not affect skin color.
citation: the areola becomes darker and more extensive as a result of increased deposits of melanin pigment in the epidermis.

statement: A mother is concerned because her 4-day-old male infant has slightly swollen breasts that secrete a tiny amount of milky fluid. What is the clinical term for this fluid?
optionExplanations: Colostrum is the first milk produced by a mother after childbirth.
Witch's milk is the term for the transient milky secretion in newborns due to maternal hormones.
Casein is a protein found in milk, not the name of the fluid itself.
Sebum is an oily secretion from sebaceous glands.
Lacteal exudate is not a standard anatomical or clinical term for this condition.
citation: In some cases, a milky fluid called witch’s milk may be expressed from the nipples.

statement: Where does the pectoralis major muscle insert?
optionExplanations: The medial lip is the insertion for the teres major.
The floor of the bicipital groove is the insertion for the latissimus dorsi.
The pectoralis major inserts specifically on the lateral lip of the bicipital groove.
The greater tuberosity is the insertion for three of the rotator cuff muscles.
The deltoid tuberosity is the insertion for the deltoid muscle.
citation: Lateral lip of bicipital groove of humerus

statement: What is the site of insertion for the pectoralis minor muscle?
optionExplanations: The acromion is the insertion for the trapezius and origin for the deltoid.
The spine of the scapula is an attachment for the trapezius and deltoid.
The pectoralis minor inserts on the medial border and upper surface of the coracoid process.
The greater tuberosity is on the humerus, not the scapula.
The lesser tuberosity is the insertion for the subscapularis.
citation: Coracoid process of scapula

statement: Lymphatic vessels from the medial quadrants of the breast primarily drain into which group of nodes?
optionExplanations: Lateral axillary nodes primarily drain the upper limb.
Pectoral axillary nodes primarily drain the lateral quadrants of the breast.
Internal thoracic nodes (parasternal nodes) receive drainage from the medial quadrants.
Subscapular nodes drain the posterior thoracic wall and back.
Central axillary nodes receive lymph from the pectoral, lateral, and subscapular groups.
citation: The medial quadrants drain by means of vessels that pierce the intercostal spaces and enter the internal thoracic group of nodes

statement: During a radical mastectomy, the surgeon removes the breast tissue and pectoral muscles. To maintain the function of the remaining muscles of the axillary walls, which nerve must be carefully identified and preserved?
optionExplanations: The lateral pectoral nerve is often removed with the pectoralis major.
The medial pectoral nerve is often removed with the pectoralis minor.
The nerve to the serratus anterior (long thoracic nerve) is preserved to prevent winging of the scapula.
The intercostobrachial nerve is often sacrificed, leading to sensory loss in the medial arm.
Supraclavicular nerves are sensory nerves of the neck and shoulder, not involved in axillary wall muscle function.
citation: The axillary blood vessels, the brachial plexus, and the nerves to the serratus anterior and the latissimus dorsi are preserved.

statement: A 14-year-old boy is brought to the clinic because he is distressed by the enlargement of his left breast. The physician reassures him that this is a common occurrence during puberty. What is the medical term for this condition?
optionExplanations: Micromastia refers to an excessively small breast.
Macromastia refers to diffuse hypertrophy of the breast in females.
Gynecomastia is the term for the enlargement of the male breast.
Polythelia refers to the presence of supernumerary nipples.
Amelia is the congenital absence of a limb.
citation: Unilateral or bilateral enlargement of the male breast occasionally occurs, usually at puberty.

statement: Which nerve provides the motor supply to the pectoralis minor muscle?
optionExplanations: The lateral pectoral nerve primarily supplies the pectoralis major.
The medial pectoral nerve supplies both the pectoralis major and minor.
The long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior.
The thoracodorsal nerve supplies the latissimus dorsi.
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid and teres minor.
citation: Medial pectoral nerve (C8, T1)

statement: What is the origin of the pectoralis minor muscle?
optionExplanations: The 1st rib is the origin for the subclavius.
The 2nd rib is not the starting point for the pectoralis minor origin.
The pectoralis minor arises from the 3rd, 4th, and 5th ribs near their cartilages.
The lower ribs provide origin for the serratus anterior.
The clavicle and sternum are origins for the pectoralis major.
citation: 3rd, 4th, and 5th ribs

statement: From which part of the brachial plexus does the nerve to the subclavius muscle originate?
optionExplanations: The roots give off the dorsal scapular and long thoracic nerves.
The nerve to the subclavius arises from the upper trunk.
The middle trunk does not give off any named branches.
The lateral cord gives off the lateral pectoral and musculocutaneous nerves.
The posterior cord gives off the subscapular and axillary nerves.
citation: Nerve to subclavius from upper trunk of brachial plexus (C5, 6)

statement: How many lobes typically comprise the glandular tissue of a single human breast?
optionExplanations: 2 to 5 is too few for the complex glandular structure of the breast.
5 to 10 is an underestimate of the number of lobes.
10 to 15 is slightly below the standard anatomical range.
The breast is composed of 15 to 20 lobes, each with its own duct.
25 to 30 exceeds the typical number of lobes found in the breast.
citation: Each breast consists of 15 to 20 lobes, which radiate out from the nipple

statement: Which nerves provide the motor innervation to the pectoralis major muscle?
optionExplanations: The medial pectoral nerve supplies the muscle, but not exclusively.
The lateral pectoral nerve supplies the muscle, but not exclusively.
The pectoralis major receives innervation from both the medial and lateral pectoral nerves.
The long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior.
The thoracodorsal nerve supplies the latissimus dorsi.
citation: Medial and lateral pectoral nerves

statement: A 52-year-old woman presents with a firm, painless lump in the upper lateral quadrant of her left breast. Biopsy confirms a scirrhous carcinoma. To which group of lymph nodes is this malignancy most likely to spread first?
optionExplanations: Internal thoracic nodes primarily drain the medial quadrants of the breast.
Posterior intercostal nodes drain a few vessels following the posterior intercostal arteries.
The lateral quadrants of the breast, where 60% of cancers occur, drain into the anterior axillary (pectoral) group.
Infraclavicular nodes receive superficial lymph from the lateral side of the limb.
Supraclavicular nodes are not the primary first-line drainage for the lateral breast quadrant.
citation: The lateral quadrants of the breast drain into the anterior axillary or pectoral group of nodes

statement: A newborn is examined and found to have a noticeable asymmetry of the chest wall. The physician suspects a partial absence of the pectoralis major muscle. If the most common part is missing, which movements will be significantly weakened?
optionExplanations: Abduction is performed by the deltoid and supraspinatus, not the pectoralis major.
The sternocostal part of the pectoralis major is the primary adductor and medial rotator of the arm.
Flexion is only partially affected as the clavicular head might still be present.
Extension is performed by the latissimus dorsi and teres major.
Protraction is primarily a function of the serratus anterior.
citation: The sternocostal origin is the most commonly missing part, and this causes weakness in adduction and medial rotation of the shoulder joint.

statement: What is the anatomical origin of the pectoralis minor muscle?
optionExplanations: The first and second ribs are superior to the origin of the pectoralis minor.
The second rib is not typically part of the origin.
The pectoralis minor arises from the 3rd, 4th, and 5th ribs near their costal cartilages.
The lower ribs are associated with the serratus anterior or abdominal muscles.
The sternum is the origin for the pectoralis major, not the minor.
citation: It arises from the third, fourth, and fifth ribs

statement: During a surgical dissection of the pectoral region, a resident identifies a nerve piercing the thick sheet of connective tissue deep to the pectoralis major. Which nerve is this?
optionExplanations: The medial pectoral nerve pierces the pectoralis minor, not the clavipectoral fascia directly.
The long thoracic nerve descends on the superficial surface of the serratus anterior.
The lateral pectoral nerve is one of the four structures that pierce the clavipectoral fascia.
The thoracodorsal nerve is located in the posterior wall of the axilla.
The axillary nerve passes through the quadrangular space.
citation: The lateral pectoral nerve, cephalic vein, branches of the thoracoacromial artery, and lymphatic channels from the infraclavicular nodes pierce the clavipectoral fascia

statement: An athlete suffers a severe avulsion injury where the primary muscle of the anterior chest wall is torn from its humeral attachment. At which specific site did the detachment occur?
optionExplanations: The medial lip is the insertion for the teres major.
The floor of the bicipital groove is the insertion for the latissimus dorsi.
The greater tuberosity receives the supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and teres minor.
The pectoralis major inserts into the lateral lip of the bicipital (intertubercular) groove.
The deltoid tuberosity is the insertion for the deltoid muscle.
citation: Lateral lip of bicipital groove of humerus

statement: Which nerve provides the motor supply to the subclavius muscle?
optionExplanations: The lateral pectoral nerve supplies the pectoralis major.
The medial pectoral nerve supplies the pectoralis minor and major.
The subclavius is supplied by the nerve to subclavius (C5, 6) from the upper trunk.
The long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior.
The suprascapular nerve supplies the supraspinatus and infraspinatus.
citation: Nerve to subclavius from upper trunk of brachial plexus

statement: A 40-year-old woman notices a small, tender mass in her right axilla that becomes more prominent during her menstrual cycle. The physician explains this is normal breast tissue. What structure did this tissue pierce to enter the axilla?
optionExplanations: The clavipectoral fascia is deep to the pectoralis major, not the site of piercing for the tail.
The axillary tail pierces the deep fascia at the lower border of the pectoralis major.
The tail does not pierce the pectoralis minor muscle.
Suspensory ligaments are internal fibrous septa, not a barrier the tail pierces.
The retromammary space is a plane of loose tissue deep to the breast.
citation: An extension of the gland called the axillary tail (see Fig. 3.14C) continues upward and laterally, pierces the deep fascia at the lower border of the pectoralis major muscle, and enters the axilla.

statement: A surgeon is performing a procedure to stabilize the shoulder and needs to identify the insertion of the pectoralis minor. Where should they look?
optionExplanations: The acromion is the insertion for the trapezius and origin for the deltoid.
The spine of the scapula is an attachment for the trapezius and deltoid.
The pectoralis minor inserts into the medial border and superior surface of the coracoid process.
The glenoid labrum is a fibrocartilage ring within the joint.
The clavicle is the origin for the clavicular head of the pectoralis major.
citation: runs upward and laterally to insert into the coracoid process of the scapula.

statement: What is the specific action of the clavicular part of the pectoralis major muscle?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis major does not extend the arm; it is a flexor.
The pectoralis major is an adductor, not an abductor.
The clavicular part of the pectoralis major is responsible for flexion of the arm.
The muscle medially rotates the arm, not laterally.
Depression of the scapula is a function of the pectoralis minor.
citation: Clavicular part flexes arm

statement: A medical student is asked to palpate the cephalic vein as it dives deep to join the axillary vein. In which surface landmark is this vein located before it pierces the fascia?
optionExplanations: The cubital fossa is at the elbow.
The quadrangular space is on the posterior aspect of the shoulder.
The cephalic vein travels in the deltopectoral groove/triangle between the deltoid and pectoralis major.
The retromammary space is deep to the breast tissue.
The anatomic snuffbox is at the wrist.
citation: This small, triangular depression is situated below the outer third of the clavicle and is bounded by the pectoralis major and deltoid muscles

statement: During an axillary lymph node dissection, the medial pectoral nerve is accidentally severed. Which of the following describes the functional deficit?
options: Loss of sensation in the axilla
optionExplanations: The lateral pectoral nerve also supplies the pectoralis major, so paralysis is not complete.
The subclavius is supplied by its own nerve from the upper trunk.
The medial pectoral nerve supplies both the pectoralis minor and the pectoralis major.
The medial pectoral nerve is a motor nerve, not primarily sensory for the axilla.
Protraction is primarily the serratus anterior's role.
citation: The medial pectoral nerve arises from the medial cord of the brachial plexus, supplies and pierces the pectoralis minor muscle, and next supplies the pectoralis major muscle

statement: A surgeon is performing a simple mastectomy. To ensure the breast is easily separated from the chest wall, they enter a plane of loose connective tissue. What is this plane called?
optionExplanations: The axillary tail is a part of the mammary gland itself.
The clavipectoral fascia is deep to the pectoralis major.
The retromammary space allows the breast to move freely over the pectoralis major.
Areolar glands are superficial, located in the skin of the areola.
Suspensory ligaments are within the breast, anchoring it to the skin.
citation: A space filled by loose connective tissue called the retromammary space lies deep to the breast and superficial to the underlying pectoral muscles.

statement: How many lobes typically comprise the glandular tissue of a mature female breast?
optionExplanations: 5 to 10 is too few for the human breast.
10 to 15 is slightly below the standard anatomical range.
The breast is composed of 15 to 20 lobes radiating from the nipple.
20 to 25 exceeds the standard anatomical description.
25 to 30 is significantly higher than the actual number of lobes.
citation: Each breast consists of 15 to 20 lobes, which radiate out from the nipple

statement: A patient with breast cancer presents with 'dimpling' of the skin over the tumor. This clinical sign is caused by the tumor infiltrating and exerting traction on which structures?
optionExplanations: Traction on lactiferous ducts causes nipple retraction, not skin dimpling.
Traction on the fibrous septa (suspensory ligaments of Cooper) causes skin dimpling.
Retromammary tissue is loose and does not cause skin dimpling when infiltrated.
The pectoral fascia is too deep to cause superficial skin dimpling directly.
The axillary tail is a region of the gland, not a supporting septum.
citation: The lobes of the gland are separated by fibrous septa that serve as suspensory ligaments

statement: A patient presents with significant weakness in initiating arm flexion. Imaging shows a lesion of the lateral pectoral nerve. Which part of the pectoralis major is most affected?
optionExplanations: The sternocostal head is primarily supplied by the medial pectoral nerve.
The abdominal part is a minor component of the sternocostal origin.
The lateral pectoral nerve primarily innervates the clavicular head of the pectoralis major.
The insertion tendon is where the muscle attaches, not a head supplied by a nerve.
The 6th rib origin is part of the sternocostal head.
citation: The lateral pectoral nerve supplies the pectoralis major muscle, primarily its clavicular head

statement: A patient is unable to effectively depress their shoulder against resistance. Which muscle of the pectoral region is likely dysfunctional?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis major adducts and rotates the arm.
Subclavius depresses the clavicle, but the 'point of shoulder' refers to the scapula.
The pectoralis minor depresses the point of the shoulder and pulls the scapula forward.
Serratus anterior protracts and rotates the scapula upward.
The deltoid abducts, flexes, or extends the arm.
citation: Depresses point of shoulder; pulls scapula forward

statement: During a dissection, a student follows the clavipectoral fascia upward. Where does this fascia find its superior attachment?
optionExplanations: The first rib is the origin of the subclavius, which the fascia encloses.
The coracoid process is the insertion for the pectoralis minor.
The clavipectoral fascia attaches superiorly to the clavicle.
The sternum is the origin for the pectoralis major.
The acromion is the lateral end of the scapular spine.
citation: Superiorly, it attaches to the clavicle.

statement: What is the nerve supply to the pectoralis minor muscle?
optionExplanations: The lateral pectoral nerve primarily supplies the pectoralis major.
The medial pectoral nerve (C8, T1) supplies the pectoralis minor.
The long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior.
The thoracodorsal nerve supplies the latissimus dorsi.
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid and teres minor.
citation: Medial pectoral nerve (C8, T1)

statement: A surgeon performing a mastectomy must be careful of heavy bleeding from vessels emerging through the intercostal spaces near the sternum. These are branches of which artery?
optionExplanations: The axillary artery supplies the breast via lateral thoracic and thoracoacromial branches.
The thoracoacromial artery supplies the pectoral muscles and acromial region.
The internal thoracic artery provides perforating branches to the medial part of the breast.
The lateral thoracic artery supplies the lateral part of the breast.
The subscapular artery supplies the posterior axillary wall.
citation: The arterial supply to the breasts includes the perforating branches of the internal thoracic artery and the intercostal arteries.

statement: A clinician is palpating the axilla for lymph nodes and feels a soft, lobulated mass. They suspect it is the axillary tail of Spence. This structure is an extension of which quadrant of the breast?
optionExplanations: The upper medial quadrant does not extend into the axilla.
The lower medial quadrant is near the sternum and lower ribs.
The axillary tail is an extension of the upper lateral quadrant of the breast.
The lower lateral quadrant is inferior to the axilla.
The central quadrant is located directly behind the nipple and areola.
citation: The axillary tail, which pierces the deep fascia and extends into the axilla.

statement: A patient who underwent a radical mastectomy has difficulty bringing their arm toward their body and turning it inward. This is due to the removal of which muscle?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis minor acts on the scapula, not primarily on arm rotation.
Serratus anterior acts on the scapula.
The pectoralis major is a powerful adductor and medial rotator of the arm.
Subclavius acts on the clavicle.
Coracobrachialis flexes and adducts the arm but is not the primary medial rotator.
citation: Adducts arm and rotates it medially

statement: During a physical exam, a patient shows instability of the clavicle when moving the shoulder. Which small muscle of the pectoral region is likely failing to steady the bone?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis major does not steady the clavicle; it originates from it.
Pectoralis minor attaches to the coracoid process, not the clavicle.
The subclavius depresses and steadies the clavicle during shoulder movements.
The trapezius elevates or rotates the scapula.
The sternocleidomastoid tilts or rotates the head.
citation: Depresses the clavicle and steadies this bone during surface movements of the shoulder girdle

statement: A patient has a malignant tumor in the lower medial quadrant of the breast. Which lymph nodes are most likely to be involved in the initial metastatic spread?
optionExplanations: Pectoral nodes primarily drain the lateral quadrants.
Lateral axillary nodes drain the upper limb.
The medial quadrants drain into the internal thoracic (parasternal) nodes inside the thorax.
Subscapular nodes drain the posterior thoracic wall and back.
Supratrochlear nodes drain the medial side of the hand and forearm.
citation: The medial quadrants drain by means of vessels that pierce the intercostal spaces and enter the internal thoracic group of nodes

statement: A weightlifter feels a 'pop' in his chest while performing a bench press. An MRI reveals a tear in the sternocostal head of the pectoralis major. From which structures does this head originate?
options: Sternum and upper six costal cartilages
correctOption: Sternum and upper six costal cartilages
optionExplanations: The clavicle is the origin for the clavicular head.
The sternocostal head originates from the sternum and the upper six costal cartilages.
Coracoid process and ribs 3-5 is the origin/insertion for pectoralis minor.
Spine and acromion are related to the deltoid and trapezius.
The lateral lip of the humerus is the insertion, not the origin.
citation: Sternum and upper six costal cartilages

statement: A surgeon is ligating the second part of the axillary artery. Which muscle must be retracted or divided to expose this specific segment of the vessel?
optionExplanations: Retracting the pectoralis major exposes the axilla but not the specific divisions of the artery.
The subclavius is superior to the second part of the artery.
The pectoralis minor muscle is the key landmark that divides the axillary artery into three parts.
The serratus anterior forms the medial wall of the axilla.
The teres major marks the end of the axillary artery.
citation: The pectoralis minor muscle crosses in front of the axillary artery and divides it into three parts

statement: A 52-year-old woman presents with a hard, painless lump in the upper lateral quadrant of her left breast. During a clinical examination, the physician palpates for enlarged lymph nodes. Which group of nodes is most likely to be the primary site of metastasis for this lesion?
optionExplanations: Internal thoracic nodes primarily drain the medial quadrants of the breast.
Posterior intercostal nodes receive a few vessels following the posterior intercostal arteries.
The lateral quadrants, where 60% of carcinomas occur, drain primarily into the anterior axillary (pectoral) group.
Supraclavicular nodes are not the primary drainage site for the lateral breast quadrants.
Infraclavicular nodes receive superficial lymph from the lateral side of the limb, not primarily the lateral breast quadrant.
citation: The lateral quadrants of the breast drain into the anterior axillary or pectoral group of nodes

statement: A 24-year-old weightlifter experiences sudden pain in his chest while performing a heavy bench press. Physical examination reveals a loss of the anterior axillary fold. Which set of movements will be most significantly weakened in this patient?
optionExplanations: Abduction is performed by the deltoid and supraspinatus, not the pectoralis major.
The pectoralis major is the primary adductor and medial rotator of the arm.
While clavicular fibers flex the arm, the pectoralis major medially rotates, not laterally rotates.
The pectoralis major is an adductor, not an abductor.
Protraction is primarily the action of the serratus anterior.
citation: Adducts and medially rotates arm; clavicular fibers flex arm

statement: What is the anatomical origin of the pectoralis minor muscle?
optionExplanations: The first rib is the origin for the subclavius muscle.
The second rib is not part of the standard origin for the pectoralis minor.
The pectoralis minor arises from the 3rd, 4th, and 5th ribs near their costal cartilages.
The sixth rib is too inferior for the origin of the pectoralis minor.
The clavicle and sternum are origins for the pectoralis major.
citation: It arises from the third, fourth, and fifth ribs

statement: A 60-year-old female patient is noted to have 'dimpling' of the skin over her right breast during a routine physical. This clinical sign is most likely caused by the tumor's effect on which of the following structures?
optionExplanations: Traction on lactiferous ducts causes nipple retraction, not skin dimpling.
The retromammary space allows for breast mobility; its invasion leads to fixation to the chest wall.
The fibrous septa (suspensory ligaments) extend from the skin to the deep fascia; traction on them causes skin dimpling.
The axillary tail is a part of the gland, not a supportive structure causing dimpling.
The pectoral fascia is deep to the breast and not directly responsible for skin dimpling.
citation: a scirrhous carcinoma or a disease such as a breast abscess may apply traction to the fibrous septa that causes dimpling of the skin.

statement: A 19-year-old athlete falls on his outstretched hand and sustains a fracture at the junction of the middle and outer thirds of the clavicle. The lateral fragment is displaced. Which muscle is primarily responsible for pulling this lateral fragment medially?
optionExplanations: The sternocleidomastoid tilts the medial fragment upward.
The trapezius is unable to support the weight of the arm, leading to depression of the lateral fragment.
The subclavius is a small muscle that steadies the clavicle but does not cause major displacement.
The pectoralis major is a strong adductor that pulls the lateral fragment medially and forward.
The deltoid's weight contributes to the depression of the lateral fragment but does not pull it medially.
citation: is pulled medially and forward by the strong adductor muscles of the shoulder joint, especially the pectoralis major.

statement: During a surgical dissection of the pectoral region, a resident must identify structures piercing the clavipectoral fascia. Which of the following structures is found in this location?
optionExplanations: The medial pectoral nerve pierces the pectoralis minor, not the clavipectoral fascia directly in the same manner.
The basilic vein joins the venae comitantes to form the axillary vein at the lower border of the teres major.
The cephalic vein pierces the clavipectoral fascia to drain into the axillary artery.
The long thoracic nerve descends on the superficial surface of the serratus anterior.
The axillary artery is deep to the clavipectoral fascia.
citation: The lateral pectoral nerve, cephalic vein, branches of the thoracoacromial artery, and lymphatic channels from the infraclavicular nodes pierce the clavipectoral fascia

statement: Where does the pectoralis minor muscle insert?
optionExplanations: The acromion is the insertion for the trapezius and origin for the deltoid.
The pectoralis minor inserts into the medial border and upper surface of the coracoid process.
The greater tubercle is the insertion for the supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and teres minor.
The lesser tubercle is the insertion for the subscapularis.
The lateral lip of the bicipital groove is the insertion for the pectoralis major.
citation: runs upward and laterally to insert into the coracoid process of the scapula.

statement: A physician examines a patient's breast and notes that the gland moves freely over the chest wall. This mobility is primarily due to the presence of which anatomical feature?
optionExplanations: Suspensory ligaments anchor the breast to the skin, not provide mobility over the muscle.
The lactiferous sinus is a dilated part of the duct near the nipple.
The retromammary space contains loose tissue that allows the breast to glide over the pectoralis major.
The clavipectoral fascia is deep to the pectoralis major.
The axillary tail is a lateral extension of the breast tissue.
citation: A space filled by loose connective tissue called the retromammary space lies deep to the breast and superficial to the underlying pectoral muscles.

statement: A patient is diagnosed with a malignant tumor in the lower medial quadrant of the breast. The surgeon is concerned about metastasis to nodes located inside the thoracic cavity. Which lymph node group is the most likely destination for lymph from this quadrant?
optionExplanations: Pectoral nodes primarily drain the lateral quadrants.
Lymph from the medial quadrants pierces the intercostal spaces to reach the internal thoracic nodes.
Subscapular nodes drain the posterior thoracic wall and back.
Lateral nodes drain the majority of the upper limb.
Apical nodes receive efferent lymph from all other axillary groups.
citation: The medial quadrants drain by means of vessels that pierce the intercostal spaces and enter the internal thoracic group of nodes

statement: Which nerves provide the motor innervation to the pectoralis major muscle?
optionExplanations: The long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior.
The thoracodorsal nerve supplies the latissimus dorsi.
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid and teres minor.
The pectoralis major is supplied by both the medial and lateral pectoral nerves.
The nerve to subclavius supplies the subclavius muscle.
citation: Medial and lateral pectoral nerves; clavicular head (C5, 6), sternocostal head (C6, 7, 8, T1)

statement: A 45-year-old woman notices a small, tender mass in her axilla that becomes more prominent during her menstrual cycle. The physician explains this is normal breast tissue. Through which structure does this tissue typically enter the axilla?
optionExplanations: The clavipectoral fascia is deep to the pectoralis major, not the site where the tail pierces.
Suspensory ligaments are internal to the breast.
The axillary tail pierces the deep fascia at the lower border of the pectoralis major to enter the axilla.
The retromammary space is a plane of cleavage deep to the breast.
The quadrangular space is in the posterior shoulder region.
citation: An extension of the gland called the axillary tail (see Fig. 3.14C) continues upward and laterally, pierces the deep fascia at the lower border of the pectoralis major muscle, and enters the axilla.

statement: A lactating mother presents with a localized, painful, erythematous area in her left breast diagnosed as an acute abscess. When performing a surgical drainage, why is a radial incision preferred?
optionExplanations: Skin tension lines (Langer's lines) are usually circular around the nipple, but radial incisions are used for a different functional reason here.
The axillary tail is not the primary concern in a localized lobe abscess.
Radial incisions parallel the lactiferous ducts, minimizing damage to them and preventing the spread of infection between compartments.
Nipple retraction is caused by cancer, not the type of incision used for an abscess.
The retromammary space is deep to the gland; the incision is for a glandular abscess.
citation: Abscesses should be drained through a radial incision to avoid spreading of the infection into neighboring compartments; a radial incision also minimizes the damage to the radially arranged ducts.

statement: What is the primary functional role of the subclavius muscle?
optionExplanations: The subclavius originates from the first rib but its action is on the clavicle.
The serratus anterior and pectoralis minor protract the scapula.
The subclavius depresses and steadies the clavicle during shoulder movements.
The pectoralis major and subscapularis medially rotate the humerus.
The pectoralis major (clavicular head) flexes the arm.
citation: Depresses the clavicle and steadies this bone during movements of the shoulder girdle

statement: A 55-year-old woman reports that her left nipple has recently begun to pull inward. The physician is concerned about a malignancy. Which structures are being pulled by the tumor to cause this effect?
optionExplanations: Traction on suspensory ligaments causes skin dimpling, not nipple retraction.
Carcinoma pulling on the lactiferous ducts causes the nipple to retract.
Fibrous septa are the same as suspensory ligaments.
Areolar glands (Montgomery glands) produce tubercles but do not cause retraction.
The pectoralis minor is deep to the breast and not involved in nipple position.
citation: A retracted nipple of recent occurrence is usually caused by an underlying carcinoma pulling on the lactiferous ducts.

statement: Where do the fibers of the pectoralis major muscle insert on the humerus?
optionExplanations: The medial lip is the insertion for the teres major.
The floor of the groove is the insertion for the latissimus dorsi.
The pectoralis major inserts into the lateral lip of the intertubercular (bicipital) groove.
The deltoid tuberosity is the insertion for the deltoid muscle.
The surgical neck is a site of fracture, not the primary insertion for the pectoralis major.
citation: Lateral lip of bicipital groove of humerus

statement: A neonate is brought to the pediatrician because of bilateral swelling of the mammary glands and a small amount of milky discharge from the nipples. What is the most likely explanation for this finding?
optionExplanations: Infection (mastitis) would typically be unilateral and associated with systemic signs.
Ductal cysts do not typically produce milky discharge in neonates.
Maternal and placental hormones cross the barrier, causing duct proliferation and 'witch's milk' secretion.
Precocious puberty occurs much later in childhood.
Lymphatic obstruction causes swelling (edema) but not milk secretion.
citation: In some cases, a milky fluid called witch’s milk may be expressed from the nipples.

statement: A 12-year-old boy is noted to have a flattened chest wall on the right side and a missing anterior axillary fold. He has difficulty with certain arm movements. Which specific functional deficit would you expect to find?
optionExplanations: Abduction is performed by the deltoid and supraspinatus.
Lateral rotation is performed by the infraspinatus and teres minor.
Absence of the sternocostal part of the pectoralis major causes weakness in adduction and medial rotation.
Shrugging is the action of the trapezius and levator scapulae.
Protraction is primarily the action of the serratus anterior.
citation: The sternocostal origin is the most commonly missing part, and this causes weakness in adduction and medial rotation of the shoulder joint.

statement: During a dissection of the anterior shoulder, a student removes the pectoralis major. Deep to it, they find a strong sheet of connective tissue. Which muscles are enclosed by the splitting of this fascia?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis major is superficial to the clavipectoral fascia.
The clavipectoral fascia splits to enclose the subclavius and the pectoralis minor.
The subscapularis is part of the posterior axillary wall.
The serratus anterior forms the medial axillary wall.
The deltoid is covered by its own deep fascia.
citation: Superiorly, it attaches to the clavicle. Inferiorly, it splits to enclose the subclavius and pectoralis minor muscles

statement: How many lobes typically comprise the glandular tissue of the human breast?
optionExplanations: 5 to 10 is too few lobes.
10 to 15 is slightly below the standard anatomical range.
The breast consists of 15 to 20 lobes radiating from the nipple.
20 to 25 exceeds the standard anatomical description.
25 to 30 is incorrect.
citation: Each breast consists of 15 to 20 lobes, which radiate out from the nipple

statement: A patient presents with a postural abnormality where the shoulder on one side appears elevated and the scapula is retracted. If this is due to muscle weakness in the pectoral region, which muscle is likely affected?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis major acts on the humerus, not primarily on the 'point of the shoulder' position.
Subclavius acts on the clavicle.
The pectoralis minor depresses the point of the shoulder and draws the scapula forward (protraction).
Serratus anterior protracts the scapula but does not primarily depress the shoulder point.
Trapezius elevation would cause the opposite effect (shrugging).
citation: Depresses point of shoulder; draws scapula forward

statement: A surgeon performing a radical mastectomy must ligate the arterial branches supplying the breast that originate from the second part of the axillary artery. Which artery is a major source here?
optionExplanations: The internal thoracic artery is a branch of the subclavian artery.
The highest thoracic artery is a branch of the first part of the axillary artery.
The thoracoacromial artery is a branch of the second part of the axillary artery and supplies the breast.
The subscapular artery is a branch of the third part of the axillary artery.
The thoracodorsal artery is a branch of the subscapular artery.
citation: The axillary artery also supplies the gland via its lateral thoracic and thoracoacromial branches.

statement: A 14-year-old boy is concerned about a slight, non-tender enlargement of his breast tissue. The physician reassures him that this is a common occurrence at his age. What is the clinical term for this condition?
optionExplanations: Micromastia refers to an excessively small breast.
Macromastia refers to diffuse hypertrophy in females.
Polythelia refers to supernumerary nipples.
Gynecomastia is the term for male breast enlargement, often seen at puberty.
Amelia is the congenital absence of a limb.
citation: Unilateral or bilateral enlargement of the male breast occasionally occurs, usually at puberty.

statement: In a complete fracture of the midshaft of the clavicle, the medial fragment is often observed to be displaced superiorly. Which muscle is responsible for this specific displacement?
optionExplanations: The trapezius attaches to the lateral third of the clavicle.
The pectoralis major pulls the lateral fragment medially.
The sternocleidomastoid muscle attaches to the medial third and tilts it upward after a fracture.
The subclavius is located inferior to the clavicle.
The scalenus anterior attaches to the first rib, not the clavicle.
citation: The medial end is tilted upward by the sternocleidomastoid muscle.

statement: A patient presents with difficulty initiating the forward swinging motion of the arm (flexion) while walking, but can still adduct the arm. Which specific portion of a pectoral muscle is likely weakened?
optionExplanations: The sternocostal head is primarily for adduction and medial rotation.
The clavicular fibers of the pectoralis major are specifically responsible for arm flexion.
The pectoralis minor acts on the scapula, not the humerus.
The subclavius steadies the clavicle.
The serratus anterior rotates the scapula for high abduction.
citation: clavicular fibers flex arm

statement: A 22-year-old bodybuilder presents to the clinic with a suspected muscle tear after performing heavy bench presses. Physical examination reveals significant weakness when he attempts to bring his arm toward the midline and rotate it inward. Which muscle is most likely injured?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis major is the primary adductor and medial rotator of the arm.
Pectoralis minor primarily stabilizes the scapula and does not medially rotate the arm.
Subclavius acts on the clavicle, not the humerus.
Serratus anterior is involved in scapular protraction.
Trapezius acts on the scapula and neck, not arm rotation.
citation: The pectoralis major muscle adducts and medially rotates the arm

statement: Where does the pectoralis minor muscle typically insert?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis minor inserts into the coracoid process of the scapula.
The acromion is the insertion for the trapezius and deltoid.
The greater tubercle is the insertion for several rotator cuff muscles.
The lesser tubercle is the insertion for the subscapularis.
The lateral lip of the bicipital groove is the insertion for the pectoralis major.
citation: insert into the coracoid process of the scapula

statement: What is the anatomical origin of the subclavius muscle?
optionExplanations: The subclavius originates from the first rib at the junction with its cartilage.
The second rib is not the origin for the subclavius.
The manubrium is an origin for the pectoralis major.
The coracoid process is an insertion point for the pectoralis minor.
The clavicle is the insertion point for the subclavius.
citation: The subclavius muscle arises from the first rib

statement: During a radical mastectomy, a surgeon accidentally damages a nerve arising from the lateral cord of the brachial plexus. Post-operatively, the patient shows significant atrophy of the pectoralis major. Which nerve was most likely injured?
optionExplanations: The lateral pectoral nerve arises from the lateral cord and supplies the pectoralis major.
The medial pectoral nerve arises from the medial cord.
The long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior.
The thoracodorsal nerve supplies the latissimus dorsi.
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid and teres minor.
citation: The lateral pectoral nerve supplies the pectoralis major muscle

statement: A patient is unable to pull the point of their shoulder downward against resistance. This suggests a functional deficit in which of the following muscles?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis minor depresses the point of the shoulder by pulling the coracoid process down.
Pectoralis major primarily moves the humerus.
Subclavius depresses the clavicle but not the 'point' of the shoulder specifically.
Deltoid abducts, flexes, or extends the arm.
Serratus anterior protracts the scapula.
citation: depresses the point of the shoulder

statement: A surgeon is performing a deep dissection in the pectoral region. To reach the axillary space from an anterior approach, they must identify structures piercing a specific fascial sheet. Which of the following structures is found piercing this fascia?
optionExplanations: The cephalic vein is one of the key structures that pierces the clavipectoral fascia.
The basilic vein pierces the deep fascia of the arm, not the clavipectoral fascia.
The axillary artery is deep to the fascia.
The lateral pectoral nerve pierces it, but the medial pectoral nerve usually pierces the pectoralis minor.
The long thoracic nerve descends on the superficial surface of the serratus anterior.
citation: cephalic vein, branches of the thoracoacromial artery, and lymphatic channels from the infraclavicular nodes pierce the clavipectoral fascia

statement: What is the specific insertion site of the pectoralis major muscle on the humerus?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis major inserts into the lateral lip of the bicipital (intertubercular) groove.
The medial lip is the insertion for the teres major.
The floor is the insertion for the latissimus dorsi.
The deltoid tuberosity is for the deltoid muscle.
The greater tubercle is for the supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and teres minor.
citation: lateral lip of bicipital groove of humerus

statement: A newborn is noted to have a chest wall deformity where the anterior axillary fold is absent on the right side. The physician suspects a congenital absence of a pectoral muscle. Which part of this muscle is most likely to be missing?
optionExplanations: The sternocostal head of the pectoralis major is the most frequently absent part in congenital cases.
The clavicular head is less commonly missing than the sternocostal head.
The insertion is usually present if the muscle fibers exist.
The abdominal part is a minor component compared to the sternocostal head.
The pectoralis major does not attach to the coracoid process.
citation: The sternocostal origin is the most commonly missing part

statement: A 55-year-old woman is diagnosed with a malignant tumor in the upper lateral quadrant of her left breast. To which group of lymph nodes is the cancer most likely to spread first?
optionExplanations: The lateral quadrants drain primarily into the pectoral (anterior) group of axillary nodes.
Internal thoracic nodes receive drainage from the medial quadrants.
Infraclavicular nodes receive drainage from the lateral side of the limb and cephalic vein area.
Subscapular nodes receive drainage from the back.
Central nodes receive lymph from the pectoral, subscapular, and lateral groups.
citation: lateral quadrants of the breast drain into the anterior axillary or pectoral group of nodes

statement: During a breast augmentation surgery, the surgeon creates a pocket for the implant. To ensure the implant is placed behind the glandular tissue but in front of the muscle, which anatomical space must be dissected?
optionExplanations: The retromammary space is the layer of loose connective tissue between the breast and the pectoral fascia.
The axillary space is lateral to the pectoral region.
The infraclavicular fossa is superior to the breast.
The deltopectoral triangle is between the deltoid and pectoralis major.
The suprasternal notch is at the top of the sternum.
citation: retromammary space lies deep to the breast and superficial to the underlying pectoral muscles

statement: What is the origin of the pectoralis minor muscle?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis minor arises from the 3rd, 4th, and 5th ribs near their cartilages.
The 1st rib is the origin for the subclavius.
The lower ribs are not the origin for this muscle.
The clavicle and sternum are origins for the pectoralis major.
The coracoid process is the insertion, not the origin.
citation: arises from the third, fourth, and fifth ribs

statement: A 48-year-old female patient notices a 'dimpling' of the skin on the upper part of her right breast. This clinical sign is caused by the involvement of which structures by an underlying malignancy?
optionExplanations: Traction on the suspensory ligaments (fibrous septa of Cooper) causes skin dimpling.
Involvement of lactiferous ducts causes nipple retraction, not skin dimpling.
The lactiferous sinus is a dilation of the duct and does not cause dimpling.
Pectoralis major fascia involvement might fix the breast to the wall but doesn't cause dimpling directly.
Retromammary tissue is loose and does not cause skin dimpling.
citation: carcinoma or a disease such as a breast abscess may apply traction to the fibrous septa that causes dimpling of the skin

statement: A patient presents with a palpable lump in the axilla. The physician explains that breast tissue can sometimes extend into the armpit. Which part of the breast is this referring to?
optionExplanations: The axillary tail (of Spence) is an extension of the breast that pierces the deep fascia to enter the axilla.
The areola is the pigmented skin around the nipple.
The ampulla is a dilated part of the lactiferous duct.
Suspensory ligaments are fibrous bands within the breast.
The retromammary space is a potential space behind the breast.
citation: axillary tail (see Fig. 3.14C) continues upward and laterally, pierces the deep fascia

statement: A patient has a nerve injury that specifically affects the fibers of the pectoralis major originating from the collar bone. Which specific movement of the arm will be most weakened?
optionExplanations: The clavicular fibers of the pectoralis major are responsible for flexing the arm.
The pectoralis major does not extend the arm (it is a flexor).
The pectoralis major is an adductor, not an abductor.
The pectoralis major is a medial rotator, not a lateral rotator.
Circumduction is a combination of movements, but flexion is the specific action of these fibers.
citation: clavicular fibers flex the arm

statement: What are the spinal nerve roots for the nerve that supplies the subclavius muscle?
optionExplanations: The nerve to the subclavius is derived from the C5 and C6 roots.
C6, C7 are roots for other nerves like the lateral pectoral nerve.
C7, C8 are not the primary roots for this nerve.
C8, T1 are roots for the medial cord nerves.
C5, C6, C7 are the roots for the long thoracic nerve.
citation: Nerve to subclavius (C5, 6)

statement: During a surgical procedure in the pectoral region, a nerve is seen emerging through the substance of the pectoralis minor muscle to reach the pectoralis major. Which nerve is this?
optionExplanations: The medial pectoral nerve pierces the pectoralis minor to supply it and then continues to the pectoralis major.
The lateral pectoral nerve usually passes superior to the pectoralis minor.
The long thoracic nerve is on the serratus anterior.
The intercostobrachial nerve is sensory to the axilla skin.
The subscapular nerve is in the posterior wall of the axilla.
citation: medial pectoral nerve arises from the medial cord of the brachial plexus, supplies and pierces the pectoralis minor

statement: A 19-year-old athlete falls on his shoulder and sustains a fracture at the junction of the middle and outer thirds of the clavicle. The lateral fragment is displaced medially. Which muscle is primarily responsible for this medial displacement?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis major is a strong adductor that pulls the lateral fragment of a fractured clavicle medially.
The sternocleidomastoid tilts the medial fragment upward.
The trapezius cannot hold the weight of the arm up after the strut is broken.
The subclavius is too small to cause significant displacement.
The deltoid's weight helps depress the lateral fragment but doesn't pull it medially.
citation: pulled medially and forward by the strong adductor muscles of the shoulder joint, especially the pectoralis major

statement: A patient presents with a recently inverted nipple. The physician explains that a tumor may be pulling on internal structures. Which structures are being pulled to cause this retraction?
optionExplanations: Retraction of the nipple is caused by a carcinoma pulling on the lactiferous ducts.
Pulling on suspensory ligaments causes skin dimpling, not nipple retraction.
Areolar glands produce tubercles but don't cause retraction.
Pectoral fascia involvement fixes the breast to the chest wall.
Fat does not provide the structural tension needed for retraction.
citation: carcinoma within the breast substance can cause retraction of the nipple by pulling on the lactiferous ducts

statement: How many lobes typically make up the glandular tissue of the human breast?
optionExplanations: The breast is composed of 15 to 20 lobes radiating from the nipple.
5 to 10 is too few.
25 to 30 is too many.
2 to 4 is incorrect.
50 to 60 is incorrect.
citation: Each breast consists of 15 to 20 lobes

statement: A radiologist is describing the location of an aneurysm in the axillary artery. They state it is located 'behind the pectoralis minor'. Which part of the axillary artery are they referring to?
optionExplanations: The second part of the axillary artery lies directly posterior to the pectoralis minor.
The first part is proximal to the muscle.
The third part is distal to the muscle.
The brachial artery begins after the teres major.
The subclavian artery is proximal to the first rib.
citation: pectoralis minor muscle crosses in front of the axillary artery and divides it into three parts

statement: A patient has a minor injury to the nerve to the subclavius. While there is no major loss of movement, the patient feels the collar bone is 'unstable' during overhead reaching. What is the primary function of this muscle?
optionExplanations: The subclavius steadies the clavicle in the sternoclavicular joint during movements.
The deltoid and supraspinatus abduct the arm.
The serratus anterior and trapezius rotate the scapula.
The subclavius does not act on the neck.
The subclavius arises from the first rib but its action is to move the clavicle down.
citation: Depresses the clavicle and steadies it during movements of the shoulder girdle

statement: An isolated lesion of the lateral pectoral nerve would most significantly impact which portion of the pectoral musculature?
optionExplanations: The lateral pectoral nerve primarily supplies the clavicular head of the pectoralis major.
The sternocostal head is primarily supplied by the medial pectoral nerve.
The pectoralis minor is supplied by the medial pectoral nerve.
The subclavius is supplied by its own nerve from the upper trunk.
The serratus anterior is supplied by the long thoracic nerve.
citation: lateral pectoral nerve supplies the pectoralis major muscle, primarily its clavicular head

statement: How is the pectoral region topographically defined?
optionExplanations: The pectoral region is the anterior aspect of the shoulder area.
The scapular region is the posterior aspect.
The arm is the brachium.
The axilla is the armpit space.
The root of the neck is superior to the pectoral region.
citation: pectoral region is the anterior aspect of the shoulder

statement: A lactating mother develops a painful, fluctuant mass in her left breast. The physician diagnoses a breast abscess. To minimize damage to the lactiferous ducts during drainage, which type of incision should be made?
optionExplanations: A radial incision follows the direction of the ducts, minimizing damage to them.
A transverse incision would cut across multiple ducts.
A vertical incision is not standard for breast abscess drainage.
Circumareolar incisions are used for cosmetic surgery but not typically for abscess drainage.
Cruciate incisions increase scarring and duct damage.
citation: Abscesses should be drained through a radial incision to avoid spreading of the infection

statement: A patient presents with weakness in adduction of the arm. Atrophy is noted in the lower, larger portion of the pectoralis major. Which nerve is most likely involved?
optionExplanations: The medial pectoral nerve primarily supplies the sternocostal head of the pectoralis major.
The lateral pectoral nerve primarily supplies the clavicular head.
The nerve to subclavius supplies the subclavius muscle.
The long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior.
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid.
citation: medial pectoral nerve... next supplies the pectoralis major muscle, primarily its sternocostal head

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A 45-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sharp pain over the point of her right shoulder. Physical examination of the shoulder joint is normal, but she has tenderness in the right hypochondrium. Which nerve is most likely responsible for transmitting this referred pain?
optionExplanations: The axillary nerve supplies the lower half of the deltoid, not the point of the shoulder.
The supraclavicular nerves (C3-C4) supply the skin over the shoulder point and can carry referred pain from diaphragmatic irritation.
The suprascapular nerve is primarily motor to the supraspinatus and infraspinatus.
The long thoracic nerve is a motor nerve to the serratus anterior.
The upper lateral cutaneous nerve is a branch of the axillary nerve supplying the lower deltoid region.
citation: The supraclavicular nerves (C3, 4 from the cervical plexus) provide the sensory nerve supply to the skin over the point of the shoulder to halfway down the deltoid muscle.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm
statement: A carpenter sustains a deep laceration on the lateral side of his arm just above the elbow. During the repair, the surgeon notes damage to the terminal sensory continuation of the musculocutaneous nerve. Which area will most likely exhibit sensory loss?
optionExplanations: The medial aspect is supplied by the medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm.
The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm (from musculocutaneous) supplies the lateral aspect of the forearm.
The posterior arm is supplied by the posterior cutaneous nerve of the arm.
The palmar surface is supplied by the median and ulnar nerves.
The little finger is supplied by the ulnar nerve.
citation: The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm supplies the skin of the front and lateral aspects of the forearm down as far as the root of the thumb.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A medical student is performing venipuncture on the median cubital vein. The student is reminded that a specific fibrous structure protects the underlying brachial artery from accidental needle entry. What is this structure?
optionExplanations: The clavipectoral fascia is located in the pectoral region.
The bicipital aponeurosis separates the superficial median cubital vein from the deep brachial artery.
The flexor retinaculum is at the wrist.
The interosseous membrane connects the radius and ulna.
The axillary sheath is in the axilla.
citation: In the cubital fossa, the median cubital vein crosses over the brachial artery and the median nerve, but it is separated from them by the bicipital aponeurosis.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: During a surgical dissection of the axilla, the surgeon identifies a strong sheet of tissue deep to the pectoralis major. Which of the following structures is expected to be found piercing this tissue to reach the superficial structures?
optionExplanations: The medial pectoral nerve pierces the pectoralis minor, not the clavipectoral fascia directly to reach the major.
The thoracodorsal nerve is deep in the axilla.
The cephalic vein pierces the clavipectoral fascia to enter the axillary vein.
The basilic vein pierces the deep fascia of the arm, not the clavipectoral fascia.
The long thoracic nerve descends on the serratus anterior.
citation: The lateral pectoral nerve, cephalic vein, branches of the thoracoacromial artery, and lymphatic channels from the infraclavicular nodes pierce the clavipectoral fascia

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: What is the superior attachment of the clavipectoral fascia?
optionExplanations: The first rib is a medial boundary of the axillary apex.
The coracoid process is an insertion for pectoralis minor.
The clavipectoral fascia attaches superiorly to the clavicle.
The sternum is medial to the fascia.
The acromion is lateral to the fascia.
citation: Superiorly, it attaches to the clavicle.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A 20-year-old athlete suffers an inferior dislocation of the glenohumeral joint. Following reduction, he complains of numbness on the lateral aspect of his upper arm. Sensory testing is most likely to show a deficit in which specific area?
optionExplanations: The point of the shoulder is supplied by supraclavicular nerves.
The axillary nerve (via the upper lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm) supplies the lower half of the deltoid.
The medial side of the arm is supplied by the medial cutaneous nerve of the arm.
