The posterior forearm is supplied by the posterior cutaneous nerve of the forearm.
The root of the thumb is supplied by the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm.
citation: consequently, there is a loss of skin sensation over the lower half of the deltoid muscle.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A patient requires a central venous catheter. The clinician decides to use the cephalic vein in the deltopectoral triangle. Into which vessel does this vein directly drain?
optionExplanations: The basilic vein joins the brachial veins.
The brachial veins are deep veins.
The cephalic vein pierces the clavipectoral fascia to terminate in the axillary vein.
The axillary vein becomes the subclavian vein at the first rib.
The cephalic vein may communicate with the external jugular, but it terminates in the axillary.
citation: terminates by draining into the axillary vein.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Medial cutaneous nerve of the arm
statement: A patient with a high axillary tumor presents with sensory loss in the armpit and along the medial aspect of the proximal arm. Which nerves are most likely compressed?
optionExplanations: The medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm supplies the forearm, not the armpit.
The ulnar nerve supplies the hand and medial forearm.
The intercostobrachial nerve (T2) and medial cutaneous nerve of the arm (T1) supply the armpit and medial arm.
The radial nerve supplies the posterior aspect.
The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the lateral aspect.
citation: The medial cutaneous nerve of the arm (T1) and the intercostobrachial nerve (a branch of the T2 intercostal nerve) supply the skin of the armpit and the medial side of the arm.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: From which structure does the cephalic vein originate?
options: Medial side of dorsal venous arch
Lateral side of dorsal venous arch
correctOption: Lateral side of dorsal venous arch
optionExplanations: The basilic vein arises from the medial side.
The cephalic vein arises from the lateral side of the dorsal venous arch.
The palmar veins drain into the dorsal arch.
The median cubital vein is a communication in the elbow.
The axillary vein is the termination site.
citation: The cephalic vein arises from the lateral side of the dorsal venous arch

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Lateral pectoral nerve and clavipectoral fascia
statement: During a radical mastectomy, the surgeon must be careful when removing the clavipectoral fascia. Which nerve piercing this fascia must be preserved to maintain the function of the clavicular head of the pectoralis major?
optionExplanations: The medial pectoral nerve pierces the pectoralis minor.
The lateral pectoral nerve pierces the clavipectoral fascia to supply the pectoralis major.
The long thoracic nerve is on the medial wall of the axilla.
The thoracodorsal nerve supplies the latissimus dorsi.
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid.
citation: The lateral pectoral nerve, cephalic vein, branches of the thoracoacromial artery, and lymphatic channels from the infraclavicular nodes pierce the clavipectoral fascia in order to make their superficial–deep connections.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Lower lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm
statement: A patient presents with a midshaft humerus fracture. While testing for sensory deficits, the physician finds numbness on the lateral and anterior aspects of the lower arm. Which branch of the radial nerve is likely involved?

Upper lateral cutaneous nerve of arm
correctOption: Lower lateral cutaneous nerve of arm
optionExplanations: The posterior cutaneous nerve of the arm supplies the back of the arm.
The posterior cutaneous nerve of the forearm supplies the back of the forearm.
The lower lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm (from radial) supplies the lateral/anterior lower arm.
The upper lateral cutaneous nerve is from the axillary nerve.
The superficial radial nerve supplies the dorsum of the hand.
citation: The lower lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm supplies the skin over the lateral and anterior aspects of the lower part of the arm

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A radiologist is performing a venogram. He traces the basilic vein as it ascends the medial side of the arm. At the lower border of the teres major, he observes it uniting with other vessels. What is the result of this union?
optionExplanations: The brachial veins are the venae comitantes that join the basilic.
The subclavian vein begins at the first rib.
The basilic vein joins the brachial venae comitantes to form the axillary vein at the teres major.
The cephalic vein is on the lateral side.
The superior vena cava is in the thorax.
citation: at the lower border of the teres major joins the venae comitantes of the brachial artery to form the axillary vein

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: Which two muscles are enclosed by the clavipectoral fascia?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis major is superficial to the fascia.
Pectoralis major is superficial to the fascia.
The clavipectoral fascia splits to enclose the subclavius and pectoralis minor.
Serratus anterior is on the medial wall.
Deltoid is lateral.
citation: it splits to enclose the subclavius and pectoralis minor muscles

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Palmar cutaneous branch of the median nerve
statement: A 50-year-old office worker is diagnosed with carpal tunnel syndrome. She reports tingling in her index and middle fingers but notes that the skin over her thenar eminence feels normal. Why is sensation in this specific area preserved?
options: Branch passes superficial to the retinaculum
Branch passes deep to the retinaculum
correctOption: Branch passes superficial to the retinaculum
optionExplanations: The ulnar nerve supplies the hypothenar eminence.
The radial nerve supplies the dorsum.
The palmar cutaneous branch of the median nerve passes superficial to the flexor retinaculum and is not compressed.
If it passed deep, it would be compressed.
The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the lateral forearm.
citation: no paresthesia occurs over the thenar eminence because the palmar cutaneous branch of the median nerve, which passes superficial to the flexor retinaculum, supplies this area of skin.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A nurse is looking for a vein to draw blood in the cubital fossa. She identifies a vein that connects the cephalic vein to the basilic vein. What is the typical direction of blood flow in this communicating vessel?
optionExplanations: The vein runs upward and medially.
The median cubital vein typically runs upward and medially from the cephalic to the basilic vein.
The direction is upward.
The direction is medially.
It is an oblique connection.
citation: The median cubital vein, a branch of the cephalic vein in the cubital fossa, runs upward and medially and joins the basilic vein.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A surgeon is performing an axillary clearance. After identifying the pectoralis minor, he follows the fascia enclosing it inferiorly. This fascia continues as which structure that helps maintain the hollow of the armpit?

The clavipectoral fascia continues inferiorly as the suspensory ligament of the axilla.
The axillary sheath surrounds the neurovascular bundle.
Intermuscular septa are in the arm/forearm.
The palmar aponeurosis is in the hand.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm source
statement: The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm is the terminal continuation of which nerve?
optionExplanations: The median nerve has a palmar cutaneous branch.
The radial nerve has a posterior cutaneous nerve of the forearm.
The musculocutaneous nerve becomes the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm.
The axillary nerve becomes the upper lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm.
The ulnar nerve has palmar and dorsal cutaneous branches.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb

statement: A patient presents with a deep cut at the wrist, just lateral to the pisiform bone. He has lost sensation over the fleshy mound at the base of his little finger. Which nerve is most likely damaged?

The palmar cutaneous branch of the ulnar nerve supplies the skin over the hypothenar eminence.
The dorsal branch supplies the back of the hand.
The radial nerve supplies the lateral dorsum.
The lateral cutaneous nerve supplies the lateral forearm.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Cephalic vein in the deltopectoral triangle
statement: During an orthopedic procedure on the shoulder, the surgeon uses the deltopectoral groove as an anatomical landmark. Which superficial vein is found within this groove before it dives deep?
optionExplanations: The basilic vein is on the medial side.
The median cubital vein is in the elbow.
The cephalic vein travels in the deltopectoral triangle/groove.
The axillary vein is deep.
The brachial vein is deep.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb

The subscapular group is in the posterior wall.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: What is the primary anatomical role of the median cubital vein?
optionExplanations: Digital veins drain the fingers.
The median cubital vein links the cephalic and basilic veins in the cubital fossa.
The basilic and brachial veins form the axillary.
Veins do not supply arteries.
The cephalic and basilic veins drain the arch.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A woman undergoing a modified radical mastectomy complains of post-operative numbness in her axilla. Which nerve was most likely sacrificed during the removal of the axillary lymph nodes?

Injury to the thoracodorsal nerve causes latissimus dorsi weakness.
The intercostobrachial nerve (T2) supplies the skin of the armpit and is often injured in axillary surgery.
The lateral pectoral nerve is motor to pectoralis major.
The axillary nerve is in the quadrangular space.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: Where is the clavipectoral fascia located in relation to the pectoralis major muscle?

The clavipectoral fascia lies immediately deep to the pectoralis major.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb

The long thoracic nerve descends on the superficial surface of the serratus anterior.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb

The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm is from the musculocutaneous nerve.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb

The long thoracic nerve is on the medial wall of the axilla.
The thoracodorsal nerve is on the posterior wall of the axilla.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb

The ulnar nerve supplies the medial hand, not the forearm skin.
The intercostobrachial nerve supplies the axilla and medial arm.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb

The median cubital vein is at the elbow.
The radial vein is a deep vein.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb

The long thoracic nerve descends on the superficial surface of the serratus anterior.
The basilic vein pierces the deep fascia of the arm, not the clavipectoral fascia.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Median cubital vein and bicipital aponeurosis
statement: A nurse is performing venipuncture in the cubital fossa. She chooses the median cubital vein because it is protected from the underlying brachial artery by which structure?
optionExplanations: The bicipital aponeurosis provides a protective barrier between the superficial vein and the deep artery.
The brachialis muscle forms the floor of the cubital fossa, deep to the artery.
The deep fascia is present but the aponeurosis is the specific protective structure mentioned.
The pronator teres forms the medial boundary of the fossa.
The flexor retinaculum is located at the wrist.
citation: In the cubital fossa, the median cubital vein crosses over the brachial artery and the median nerve, but it is separated from them by the bicipital aponeurosis.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm
statement: A patient presents with a deep laceration at the lateral margin of the biceps tendon. Physical examination reveals sensory loss on the lateral aspect of the forearm. Which nerve is likely damaged?
optionExplanations: The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm is the continuation of the musculocutaneous nerve.
The medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm supplies the medial aspect.
The posterior cutaneous nerve of the forearm supplies the back of the forearm.
The superficial radial nerve supplies the dorsum of the hand.
The palmar cutaneous branch supplies the palm.
citation: The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm supplies the skin of the front and lateral aspects of the forearm down as far as the root of the thumb.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A clinician decides to use the basilic vein for central venous catheterization rather than the cephalic vein. What anatomical advantage does the basilic vein offer in this procedure?
optionExplanations: The basilic vein is in a direct line with the axillary vein, making catheter advancement easier.
The basilic vein does contain valves.
The basilic vein pierces the deep fascia halfway up the arm.
The basilic vein increases in diameter as it ascends.
The cephalic vein often joins the axillary vein at a right angle, which is a disadvantage.
citation: the basilic vein increases in diameter and is in direct line with the axillary vein

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A patient with enlarged axillary lymph nodes complains of tingling and numbness in the armpit and along the inner side of the upper arm. Which nerve is being compressed?
optionExplanations: The intercostobrachial nerve (T2) supplies the axilla and medial arm skin.
The medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm supplies the forearm, not the armpit.
The long thoracic nerve is a motor nerve to the serratus anterior.
The thoracodorsal nerve is a motor nerve to the latissimus dorsi.
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid region.
citation: The intercostobrachial nerve (a branch of the T2 intercostal nerve) supply the skin of the armpit and the medial side of the arm.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Upper lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm
statement: A 19-year-old athlete sustains a fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus. On examination, there is a loss of sensation over the lower half of the deltoid muscle. Which nerve is involved?
optionExplanations: The upper lateral cutaneous nerve is a branch of the axillary nerve, which is related to the surgical neck.
The lower lateral cutaneous nerve is a branch of the radial nerve.
The posterior cutaneous nerve of the arm is a branch of the radial nerve.
The supraclavicular nerves supply the upper half of the deltoid.
The medial cutaneous nerve supplies the inner arm.
citation: the posterior terminal branch... emerges from the posterior border of the deltoid as the upper lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Palmar cutaneous branch of the median nerve
statement: A patient with severe carpal tunnel syndrome has numbness in the index and middle fingers but retains normal sensation over the thenar eminence. Why is the thenar skin sensation spared?
optionExplanations: The palmar cutaneous branch of the median nerve does not enter the carpal tunnel.
The thenar eminence skin is supplied by the median nerve, not ulnar.
The radial nerve supplies the dorsum of the hand.
Guyon's canal contains the ulnar nerve.
The thenar eminence is typically C6.
citation: no paresthesia occurs over the thenar eminence because the palmar cutaneous branch of the median nerve, which passes superficial to the flexor retinaculum, supplies this area of skin.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Superficial branch of the radial nerve
statement: A patient presents with a laceration in the anatomical snuffbox. They report loss of sensation on the lateral part of the dorsum of the hand. Which nerve is most likely severed?
optionExplanations: The superficial radial nerve supplies the lateral dorsum of the hand and passes through the snuffbox area.
The deep branch of the radial nerve is purely motor in the forearm.
The dorsal branch of the ulnar nerve supplies the medial dorsum.
The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm ends at the root of the thumb.
The median nerve supplies the palmar aspect.
citation: The superficial branch of the radial nerve is a cutaneous nerve to the wrist and hand.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A 12-year-old boy falls from a tree and sustains a midshaft clavicle fracture. A large, rapidly expanding hematoma is noted in the deltopectoral region. This is likely due to damage to a communication between the cephalic vein and which other vessel?
optionExplanations: The cephalic vein frequently communicates with the external jugular vein across the clavicle.
The internal jugular vein is deep in the neck.
The subclavian vein is deep to the subclavius muscle.
The cephalic vein drains into the axillary vein, but the communication mentioned is with the jugular.
The basilic vein is on the medial side of the arm.
citation: Fracture of the clavicle can result in rupture of this communicating vein, with the formation of a large hematoma.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm origin
statement: A patient with a tumor in the lower neck has a lesion specifically affecting the medial cord of the brachial plexus. Which cutaneous area will show sensory deficit?
optionExplanations: The medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm (from the medial cord) supplies the medial forearm skin.
The lateral aspect is supplied by the musculocutaneous nerve (lateral cord).
The dorsum of the arm is supplied by the radial nerve (posterior cord).
The lateral arm is supplied by axillary or radial nerves.
The thumb and index finger are supplied by the median nerve.
citation: The medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm arises from the medial cord of the brachial plexus

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: During an axillary clearance for breast cancer, the surgeon identifies a fascial sheet that maintains the concavity of the axilla. This structure is the inferior continuation of which fascia?
optionExplanations: The clavipectoral fascia continues inferiorly as the suspensory ligament of the axilla.
The pectoral fascia is superficial to the pectoralis major.
The axillary fascia forms the floor, but the suspensory ligament comes from the clavipectoral.
The deltoid fascia covers the deltoid muscle.
The prevertebral fascia is continuous with the axillary sheath, not the suspensory ligament.
citation: continues downward as the suspensory ligament of the axilla and joins the fascial floor of the armpit.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Posterior cutaneous nerve of the forearm
statement: A patient with a radial nerve injury in the spiral groove of the humerus presents with sensory loss. Which cutaneous nerve branch of the radial nerve is responsible for sensation on the posterior forearm?
optionExplanations: The posterior cutaneous nerve of the forearm is a branch of the radial nerve supplying the back of the forearm.
The lower lateral cutaneous nerve supplies the lower lateral arm.
The posterior cutaneous nerve of the arm supplies the back of the arm.
The superficial radial nerve supplies the dorsum of the hand.
The dorsal ulnar nerve is not a branch of the radial nerve.
citation: The posterior cutaneous nerve of the forearm runs down the middle of the back of the forearm as far as the wrist

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A patient has a severe infection on the back of the hand. The clinician notes that the superficial venous drainage from this area primarily begins at which structure?
optionExplanations: The dorsal venous arch is the starting point for the cephalic and basilic veins.
The palmar plexus is on the anterior side.
The median cubital vein is at the elbow.
The axillary vein is in the armpit.
Venae comitantes are deep veins.
citation: The dorsal venous arch drains into the cephalic and basilic veins at its lateral and medial ends, respectively.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Lower lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm
statement: A patient sustains a midshaft humeral fracture. While testing sensation, the doctor finds a deficit on the lateral aspect of the arm just above the elbow. Which nerve is likely involved?
optionExplanations: The lower lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm is a branch of the radial nerve.
The upper lateral cutaneous nerve is a branch of the axillary nerve.
The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm is in the forearm.
The medial cutaneous nerve is on the opposite side.
The intercostobrachial nerve is in the axilla/upper arm.
citation: The lower lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm supplies the skin over the lateral and anterior aspects of the lower part of the arm

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A medical student is asked to identify the muscles enclosed by the clavipectoral fascia. Which pair of muscles should they name?
optionExplanations: The clavipectoral fascia specifically encloses the subclavius and pectoralis minor.
The pectoralis major is superficial to this fascia.
The pectoralis major is not enclosed by it.
The serratus anterior is on the medial wall of the axilla.
The deltoid is covered by its own fascia.
citation: Superiorly, it attaches to the clavicle. Inferiorly, it splits to enclose the subclavius and pectoralis minor muscles

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: Where does the cephalic vein typically terminate in the upper limb?
optionExplanations: The cephalic vein pierces the clavipectoral fascia to join the axillary vein.
The basilic vein joins the brachial veins to form the axillary vein.
The axillary vein becomes the subclavian vein at the first rib.
The brachial veins are deep.
It may communicate with the external jugular, but does not terminate there.
citation: terminates by draining into the axillary vein.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: What is the spinal nerve origin of the supraclavicular nerves?
optionExplanations: The supraclavicular nerves arise from the C3 and C4 roots of the cervical plexus.
C5 and C6 are the upper roots of the brachial plexus.
C1 and C2 do not form cutaneous nerves for the shoulder.
C8 and T1 form the lower trunk of the brachial plexus.
T1 and T2 supply the medial arm and axilla.
citation: The supraclavicular nerves (C3, 4 from the cervical plexus)

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: Which superficial vein connects the cephalic vein to the basilic vein in the cubital fossa?
optionExplanations: The median cubital vein is the standard shunt between the two main superficial veins at the elbow.
The median antebrachial vein drains the front of the forearm.
The axillary vein is a deep vein.
The profunda brachii is a deep vein.
Communicating vein is too generic.
citation: The median cubital vein, a branch of the cephalic vein in the cubital fossa, runs upward and medially and joins the basilic vein.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: What is the superior attachment of the clavipectoral fascia?
optionExplanations: The clavipectoral fascia is anchored to the clavicle superiorly.
The first rib is the medial boundary of the axillary inlet.
The coracoid process is a lateral attachment point for the pectoralis minor.
The sternum is medial.
The acromion is the lateral end of the scapular spine.
citation: Superiorly, it attaches to the clavicle.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: At what level does the basilic vein typically pierce the deep fascia of the arm?
optionExplanations: The basilic vein becomes deep halfway up the arm.
It is superficial at the elbow.
It is already deep by the time it reaches the axilla.
It is superficial at the wrist.
The axillary tail of the breast pierces the fascia at the lower border of pectoralis major.
citation: Halfway up the arm, it pierces the deep fascia

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A paramedic needs to establish peripheral access in a patient with low blood pressure. He looks for a vein that is consistently found just posterior to the radial styloid process. Which vein is this?
optionExplanations: The cephalic vein is consistently located posterior to the radial styloid at the wrist.
The basilic vein is on the medial (ulnar) side.
The median cubital vein is at the elbow.
Radial veins are deep (venae comitantes).
Ulnar veins are deep.
citation: The cephalic vein lies fairly constantly in the superficial fascia, immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Medial cutaneous nerve of the arm
statement: A patient presents with sensory loss strictly limited to the inner (medial) aspect of the upper arm. Which nerve is most likely involved?
optionExplanations: The medial cutaneous nerve of the arm specifically supplies the medial upper arm skin.
The medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm supplies the forearm.
The ulnar nerve supplies the hand.
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid area.
The radial nerve supplies the posterior aspect.
citation: The medial cutaneous nerve of the arm (T1) arises from the medial cord of the brachial plexus... It supplies the skin on the medial side of the arm

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: During a subpectoral breast implant procedure, the surgeon must be careful of vessels piercing the fascia deep to the pectoralis major. Which artery's branches are found here?
optionExplanations: The thoracoacromial artery pierces the clavipectoral fascia to supply the pectoral region.
The lateral thoracic artery runs along the lower border of pectoralis minor.
The internal thoracic artery is inside the thoracic cavity.
The subscapular artery is on the posterior axillary wall.
The highest thoracic artery is very small and variable, usually above the fascia.
citation: branches of the thoracoacromial artery... pierce the clavipectoral fascia

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: Which three nerves provide the entire cutaneous innervation to the hand?
optionExplanations: The median, radial, and ulnar nerves are the three primary sensory nerves for the hand.
The musculocutaneous nerve ends at the wrist.
The axillary nerve does not reach the hand.
The axillary nerve does not reach the hand.
The musculocutaneous nerve ends at the wrist.
citation: The median, radial, and ulnar nerves provide the cutaneous innervation of the hand.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: Where does the clavipectoral fascia attach at its superior extent?
optionExplanations: The clavipectoral fascia is a strong sheet of connective tissue that attaches superiorly to the clavicle.
The first rib is related to the medial wall of the axilla, not the superior attachment of this fascia.
The coracoid process is an insertion point for muscles enclosed by the fascia, but not its superior attachment.
The manubrium is part of the sternoclavicular joint, not the attachment for this fascia.
The acromion is the lateral extension of the scapular spine.
citation: Superiorly, it attaches to the clavicle.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: During a surgical procedure in the infraclavicular region, a surgeon identifies a nerve piercing the clavipectoral fascia to reach the deep surface of the pectoralis major. Which nerve is this?
optionExplanations: The lateral pectoral nerve is one of the four structures that pierce the clavipectoral fascia.
The medial pectoral nerve pierces the pectoralis minor, not the clavipectoral fascia itself.
The long thoracic nerve descends on the superficial surface of the serratus anterior.
The thoracodorsal nerve supplies the latissimus dorsi in the posterior axillary wall.
The axillary nerve passes through the quadrangular space.
citation: The lateral pectoral nerve, cephalic vein, branches of the thoracoacromial artery, and lymphatic channels from the infraclavicular nodes pierce the clavipectoral fascia

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A nurse is attempting to establish peripheral venous access in a patient with low blood pressure. She palpates for a vein that is consistently located just posterior to the radial styloid process. Which vein is she identifying?
optionExplanations: The cephalic vein has a constant position posterior to the radial styloid process at the wrist.
The basilic vein winds around the medial border of the forearm.
The median cubital vein is located in the cubital fossa.
The median antebrachial vein drains the front of the forearm.
The ulnar vein is a deep vein (vena comitans).
citation: The cephalic vein lies fairly constantly in the superficial fascia, immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: Which of the following muscles is enclosed by the clavipectoral fascia as it splits inferiorly?
optionExplanations: The clavipectoral fascia splits to enclose both the subclavius and the pectoralis minor.
The pectoralis major lies superficial to the clavipectoral fascia.
The serratus anterior forms the medial wall of the axilla.
The subscapularis forms part of the posterior wall of the axilla.
The teres major forms part of the posterior axillary fold.
citation: Inferiorly, it splits to enclose the subclavius and pectoralis minor muscles

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A physician decides to perform central venous catheterization via a superficial vein in the arm. He chooses the vein that is in a direct line with the axillary vein to ensure easier passage of the catheter. Which vein is he using?
optionExplanations: The basilic vein is the preferred choice for catheterization because it is in direct line with the axillary vein.
The cephalic vein often joins the axillary vein at a right angle, making maneuvering difficult.
The median cubital vein is a connecting branch, not the main trunk used for this purpose.
The brachial vein is a deep vein, not a superficial one.
The external jugular vein is in the neck.
citation: the basilic vein increases in diameter and is in direct line with the axillary vein

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A 45-year-old patient with suspected pleurisy complains of referred pain at the point of the shoulder. Which nerves are responsible for the sensory supply to this specific skin region?
optionExplanations: The supraclavicular nerves (C3,4) supply the skin over the shoulder point and are involved in referred pain from the diaphragm.
The axillary nerve supplies the skin over the lower half of the deltoid.
The radial nerve supplies the skin on the posterior aspect of the limb.
The intercostobrachial nerve supplies the skin of the armpit.
The medial cutaneous nerve of the arm supplies the medial side of the arm.
citation: The supraclavicular nerves (C3, 4 from the cervical plexus) provide the sensory nerve supply to the skin over the point of the shoulder to halfway down the deltoid muscle.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: During venipuncture in the cubital fossa, a student is cautioned about the risk of accidentally injecting into the brachial artery. Which anatomical structure provides a protective barrier between the median cubital vein and the artery?
optionExplanations: The bicipital aponeurosis separates the superficial median cubital vein from the deeper brachial artery.
The clavipectoral fascia is located in the pectoral region, not the cubital fossa.
The deep fascia is present but the specific protective structure is the aponeurosis.
The brachialis muscle forms the floor of the cubital fossa, deep to the artery.
The pronator teres forms the medial boundary of the cubital fossa.
citation: In the cubital fossa, the median cubital vein is separated from the underlying brachial artery by the bicipital aponeurosis.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A patient presents with loss of sensation on the lateral aspect of the forearm following a deep laceration near the elbow. Which nerve is most likely damaged?
optionExplanations: The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm (from the musculocutaneous nerve) supplies this region.
The medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm supplies the medial aspect.
The posterior cutaneous nerve of the forearm supplies the middle of the back of the forearm.
The superficial radial nerve supplies the dorsum of the hand.
The median nerve supplies the lateral part of the palm.
citation: The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm supplies the skin of the front and lateral aspects of the forearm down as far as the root of the thumb.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: What does the clavipectoral fascia become after it splits to enclose the pectoralis minor muscle?
optionExplanations: The clavipectoral fascia continues inferiorly as the suspensory ligament of the axilla.
The axillary sheath is a continuation of the prevertebral fascia.
Sibson's fascia is the suprapleural membrane.
Prevertebral fascia is in the neck.
Pectoral fascia covers the pectoralis major.
citation: continues downward as the suspensory ligament of the axilla and joins the fascial floor of the armpit.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A patient with a clavicular fracture develops a massive hematoma in the neck. The physician suspects the rupture of a communicating vein that links a superficial limb vein to a neck vein. Which two veins are connected by this vessel?
optionExplanations: A small vein frequently connects the cephalic vein to the external jugular vein across the clavicle.
The basilic vein does not typically communicate with the internal jugular vein.
The cephalic vein drains into the axillary vein, not directly into the subclavian via a clavicular branch.
The basilic vein joins the brachial veins to form the axillary vein.
The median cubital vein connects the cephalic and basilic veins in the elbow.
citation: the cephalic vein frequently communicates with the external jugular vein by a small vein that crosses in front of the clavicle.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: Which nerves provide sensory innervation to the skin of the armpit?
optionExplanations: The medial cutaneous nerve of the arm and the intercostobrachial nerve supply the armpit skin.
The lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm is a branch of the axillary nerve.
Supraclavicular nerves supply the shoulder point.
The musculocutaneous nerve becomes the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm.
The ulnar nerve supplies the medial hand.
citation: The medial cutaneous nerve of the arm (T1) and the intercostobrachial nerve (a branch of the T2 intercostal nerve) supply the skin of the armpit and the medial side of the arm.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A surgeon is performing a radical mastectomy. While removing the clavipectoral fascia, he must be careful of the vessels piercing it. Which artery's branches are found here?
optionExplanations: Branches of the thoracoacromial artery pierce the clavipectoral fascia.
The lateral thoracic artery runs along the lower border of the pectoralis minor.
The subscapular artery is a branch of the third part of the axillary artery.
The internal thoracic artery is a branch of the subclavian artery.
The highest thoracic artery is a branch of the first part of the axillary artery.
citation: The lateral pectoral nerve, cephalic vein, branches of the thoracoacromial artery, and lymphatic channels from the infraclavicular nodes pierce the clavipectoral fascia

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A patient reports numbness in a narrow strip down the middle of the posterior aspect of his forearm. Which nerve is likely affected?
optionExplanations: The posterior cutaneous nerve of the forearm (from the radial nerve) supplies this specific strip.
The lateral cutaneous nerve supplies the lateral aspect of the forearm.
The medial cutaneous nerve supplies the medial aspect of the forearm.
The superficial radial nerve supplies the lateral dorsum of the hand.
The dorsal branch of the ulnar nerve supplies the medial dorsum of the hand.
citation: The posterior cutaneous nerve of the forearm runs down the middle of the back of the forearm as far as the wrist

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: Where does the cephalic vein typically terminate?
optionExplanations: The cephalic vein pierces the clavipectoral fascia to drain into the axillary vein.
The axillary vein becomes the subclavian vein at the first rib.
The basilic vein joins the brachial veins to form the axillary vein.
The brachial veins are deep veins.
The internal jugular vein is in the neck.
citation: terminates by draining into the axillary vein.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Palmar Cutaneous Branch of Median Nerve
statement: A patient with Carpal Tunnel Syndrome complains of numbness in his fingers but notes that the skin over his thenar eminence still has normal sensation. Why is the sensation in this area preserved?
optionExplanations: The palmar cutaneous branch of the median nerve passes superficial to the flexor retinaculum and is not compressed in the carpal tunnel.
The thenar eminence skin is supplied by the median nerve, not the ulnar nerve.
The radial nerve supplies the dorsum of the hand.
Guyon's canal contains the ulnar nerve.
The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the lateral forearm.
citation: Palmar cutaneous branch arises in the lower part of the forearm, crosses the superficial side of the flexor retinaculum, and distributes to the skin over the lateral part of the palm

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A surgeon is using the deltopectoral approach to reach the shoulder joint. He identifies a large superficial vein in the groove between the deltoid and pectoralis major. Which vein is this?
optionExplanations: The cephalic vein travels in the deltopectoral groove/triangle before piercing the clavipectoral fascia.
The basilic vein pierces the deep fascia halfway up the arm.
The axillary vein is a deep vein.
The subclavian vein is deep to the clavicle.
The median cubital vein is in the cubital fossa.
citation: It dives into the deltopectoral triangle, pierces the clavipectoral fascia, and terminates by draining into the axillary vein.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: Which nerve provides sensory innervation to the skin over the lateral part of the dorsum of the hand?
optionExplanations: The superficial radial nerve is the primary sensory supply to the lateral dorsum of the hand.
The deep radial nerve is purely motor (posterior interosseous nerve).
The median nerve supplies the palmar aspect of the lateral fingers.
The ulnar nerve supplies the medial part of the dorsum of the hand.
The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the lateral forearm.
citation: The superficial radial nerve is sensory and supplies the skin over the lateral part of the dorsum of the hand

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Palmar Cutaneous Branch of Ulnar Nerve
statement: A patient presents with numbness specifically over the hypothenar eminence after a wrist injury. Which nerve branch is most likely involved?
optionExplanations: The palmar cutaneous branch of the ulnar nerve supplies the skin over the hypothenar eminence.
The dorsal branch supplies the medial dorsum of the hand.
The superficial branch supplies the skin of the medial one and a half fingers.
The deep branch is primarily motor.
The palmar cutaneous branch of the median nerve supplies the lateral palm.
citation: The palmar cutaneous branch supplies the skin over the hypothenar eminence

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A phlebotomist is looking for the most common vein used for blood withdrawal in the elbow region. This vein typically connects the cephalic and basilic veins. What is its name?
optionExplanations: The median cubital vein is the standard connection between the cephalic and basilic veins in the cubital fossa.
The median antebrachial vein is on the front of the forearm.
The brachial vein is deep.
The axillary vein is in the armpit.
The ulnar vein is deep.
citation: The median cubital vein, a branch of the cephalic vein in the cubital fossa, runs upward and medially and joins the basilic vein.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Upper Lateral Cutaneous Nerve of Arm
statement: A 20-year-old athlete suffers an inferior shoulder dislocation. On examination, he has lost sensation over the lower part of his deltoid muscle. Which nerve's cutaneous branch is affected?
optionExplanations: The axillary nerve gives off the upper lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm, which supplies the skin over the lower deltoid.
The radial nerve supplies the posterior arm and forearm.
The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the lateral forearm.
Supraclavicular nerves supply the upper half of the deltoid.
The medial cutaneous nerve supplies the medial arm.
citation: consequently, there is a loss of skin sensation over the lower half of the deltoid muscle.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A patient with a medial cord lesion of the brachial plexus would most likely lose sensation in which of the following areas?
optionExplanations: The medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm is a direct branch of the medial cord.
The lateral side of the forearm is supplied by the musculocutaneous nerve (lateral cord).
The posterior side of the forearm is supplied by the radial nerve (posterior cord).
The lateral side of the arm is supplied by the axillary and radial nerves.
The point of the shoulder is supplied by supraclavicular nerves.
citation: The medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm... supplies the skin on the medial side of the forearm

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A carpenter accidentally cuts the palm of his hand. He now has no feeling on the palmar surface of his thumb, index, and middle fingers. Which nerve is likely severed?
optionExplanations: The median nerve provides sensory supply to the palmar aspect of the lateral three and a half fingers.
The ulnar nerve supplies the medial one and a half fingers.
The radial nerve supplies the lateral dorsum of the hand.
The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the lateral forearm.
The axillary nerve supplies the shoulder skin.
citation: gives sensory innervation to the skin of the palmar aspect of the lateral three and a half fingers

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: From which anatomical structure does the basilic vein originate?
optionExplanations: The basilic vein begins at the medial end of the dorsal venous arch.
The cephalic vein arises from the lateral side of the dorsal venous arch.
The palmar venous plexus communicates with the arch but is not the direct origin.
The axillary vein is the termination point, not the origin.
The median cubital vein is a tributary that joins it later.
citation: The basilic vein arises from the medial side of the dorsal venous arch on the back of the hand

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A patient with a midshaft humeral fracture and radial nerve damage is likely to have sensory loss in which area?
optionExplanations: The radial nerve gives off the posterior cutaneous nerve of the arm, supplying the back of the arm.
The medial side of the arm is supplied by the medial cutaneous nerve of the arm.
The front of the arm is supplied by the musculocutaneous nerve.
The armpit is supplied by the intercostobrachial nerve.
The point of the shoulder is supplied by supraclavicular nerves.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A clinician needs to perform central venous catheterization on a patient in the ER. Why is the basilic vein preferred over the cephalic vein for this procedure?

correctOption: It is in direct line with axillary
optionExplanations: The basilic vein does have valves.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb

The basilic vein increases in diameter as it ascends.
