optionExplanations: The lateral cutaneous nerve supplies the lateral side.
The medial cutaneous nerve supplies the medial side.
The posterior cutaneous nerve of the forearm (from the radial nerve) supplies the middle of the back of the forearm.
The dorsal ulnar nerve supplies the medial dorsum of the hand.
The superficial radial nerve supplies the lateral dorsum of the hand.
citation: The posterior cutaneous nerve of the forearm runs down the middle of the back of the forearm

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Lateral pectoral nerve and clavipectoral fascia
statement: During a subpectoral breast implant procedure, the surgeon must be careful not to damage the nerve that pierces the clavipectoral fascia to supply the pectoralis major. Which nerve is this?
optionExplanations: The medial pectoral nerve pierces the pectoralis minor.
The lateral pectoral nerve pierces the clavipectoral fascia.
The long thoracic nerve is on the serratus anterior.
Intercostal nerves supply the thoracic wall.
Subscapular nerves supply the subscapularis.
citation: The lateral pectoral nerve... pierce the clavipectoral fascia

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A patient has a blockage in the proximal part of the cephalic vein. Into which vessel does this vein normally drain its blood?
optionExplanations: The subclavian vein is the continuation of the axillary vein.
The basilic vein joins the brachial veins to form the axillary vein.
The cephalic vein specifically terminates in the axillary vein.
The brachial veins are deep veins of the arm.
The external jugular is in the neck.
citation: terminates by draining into the axillary vein.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A patient has a skin lesion on the upper part of the shoulder. The clinician notes the area is innervated by the supraclavicular nerves. How far down the arm does this nerve's distribution typically extend?
optionExplanations: The elbow is supplied by nerves of the arm/forearm.
The insertion of the deltoid is too distal.
The supraclavicular nerves supply skin to halfway down the deltoid muscle.
The wrist is far beyond its reach.
It extends well beyond the clavicle onto the shoulder.
citation: The supraclavicular nerves... to halfway down the deltoid muscle.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Lateral cutaneous nerve of forearm appearance
statement: A surgeon is performing a repair of a ruptured biceps tendon. He identifies a sensory nerve appearing at the lateral margin of the tendon just above the elbow. Which nerve is this?
optionExplanations: The radial nerve is deeper in the cubital fossa.
The median nerve is medial to the biceps tendon.
The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm appears at the lateral margin of the biceps tendon.
The musculocutaneous nerve is the parent trunk, but it changes name to 'lateral cutaneous' at this point.
The superficial radial nerve appears lower in the forearm.
citation: The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm... appears at the lateral margin of the biceps tendon

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: During a routine blood draw, the phlebotomist selects the median cubital vein. This vein typically shunts blood from the cephalic vein into which other vessel?
optionExplanations: The axillary vein is a deep vessel.
The median cubital vein runs upward and medially to join the basilic vein.
The brachial veins are deep.
The subclavian vein is in the root of the neck.
The radial vein is a deep vein of the forearm.
citation: The median cubital vein... joins the basilic vein.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: Where does the clavipectoral fascia attach at its superior extent?
optionExplanations: The clavipectoral fascia is anchored to the clavicle superiorly.
The first rib is a medial boundary of the axillary apex, not the superior attachment.
The coracoid process is an insertion point for pectoralis minor, which the fascia encloses.
The manubrium is the medial attachment for the sternoclavicular joint.
The acromion is the lateral attachment for the acromioclavicular joint.
citation: Superiorly, it attaches to the clavicle.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: Into which vessel does the cephalic vein typically drain at its termination?
optionExplanations: The cephalic vein pierces the clavipectoral fascia to join the axillary vein.
The basilic vein joins the brachial veins to form the axillary vein.
The brachial veins are deep veins that join the basilic vein.
The axillary vein becomes the subclavian vein at the first rib.
The cephalic vein may communicate with the external jugular, but it does not terminate there.
citation: terminates by draining into the axillary vein.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Lateral Cutaneous Nerve of Forearm Origin
statement: Which nerve continues into the forearm as the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm?
optionExplanations: The musculocutaneous nerve becomes the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm after piercing the deep fascia.
The radial nerve gives rise to the posterior cutaneous nerve of the forearm.
The median nerve provides palmar cutaneous branches, not lateral forearm branches.
The axillary nerve gives rise to the upper lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm.
The ulnar nerve provides the posterior cutaneous branch for the medial hand.
citation: The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm supplies the skin of the front and lateral aspects of the forearm

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: Which of the following structures is a content that pierces the clavipectoral fascia?
optionExplanations: Branches of the thoracoacromial artery are among the four structures piercing this fascia.
The medial pectoral nerve pierces the pectoralis minor, not the clavipectoral fascia directly.
The long thoracic nerve descends on the superficial surface of the serratus anterior.
The basilic vein pierces the deep fascia of the arm, not the clavipectoral fascia.
The musculocutaneous nerve pierces the coracobrachialis muscle.
citation: The lateral pectoral nerve, cephalic vein, branches of the thoracoacromial artery, and lymphatic channels from the infraclavicular nodes pierce the clavipectoral fascia

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: Which nerves provide sensory innervation to the skin over the point of the shoulder?
optionExplanations: The supraclavicular nerves supply the skin from the shoulder point down to the deltoid midpoint.
The axillary nerve supplies the skin over the lower half of the deltoid.
The intercostobrachial nerve supplies the skin of the armpit and medial arm.
The dorsal scapular nerve is a motor nerve to the rhomboids.
The long thoracic nerve is a motor nerve to the serratus anterior.
citation: The supraclavicular nerves (C3, 4 from the cervical plexus) provide the sensory nerve supply to the skin over the point of the shoulder

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A 22-year-old medical student is practicing venipuncture on a classmate. They select a prominent vein in the cubital fossa that connects the lateral and medial superficial venous systems. Which vein are they most likely using?
optionExplanations: The median cubital vein is the standard connection between the cephalic and basilic veins in the cubital fossa.
The cephalic vein is the lateral source of the median cubital vein.
The basilic vein is the medial recipient of the median cubital vein.
The brachial vein is a deep vein, not used for routine superficial venipuncture.
The axillary vein is a deep vein located in the axilla.
citation: The median cubital vein, a branch of the cephalic vein in the cubital fossa, runs upward and medially and joins the basilic vein.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A 45-year-old man presents to the ER with a fractured clavicle. A massive hematoma is noted in the neck region. The surgeon suspects a rupture of a communicating vessel between the superficial venous system of the arm and the neck. Which vessel is likely involved?
optionExplanations: The cephalic vein often communicates with the external jugular vein across the clavicle, making it vulnerable in fractures.
There is no standard basilic-internal jugular communication across the clavicle.
The median cubital vein is located in the cubital fossa, far from the clavicle.
The axillary-subclavian junction is deep to the first rib, not superficial to the clavicle.
The thoracoacromial vein drains into the axillary vein and does not cross the clavicle superficially.
citation: In the deltopectoral triangle, the cephalic vein frequently communicates with the external jugular vein by a small vein that crosses in front of the clavicle.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A clinician needs to perform central venous catheterization. They choose the medial vein of the arm because it provides a straighter path to the axillary vein compared to the lateral alternative. Which vein is being utilized?
optionExplanations: The basilic vein is preferred for catheterization because it aligns directly with the axillary vein.
The cephalic vein often joins the axillary vein at a difficult right angle.
The median cubital vein is a connecting branch, not the main trunk used for the entire path.
The brachial veins are deep and usually paired, making them less ideal than the basilic.
Venae comitantes are deep paired veins accompanying arteries.
citation: The basilic vein... is in direct line with the axillary vein

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Lateral Pectoral Nerve and Clavipectoral Fascia
statement: During a surgical dissection of the axilla, the surgeon identifies a nerve piercing the strong sheet of tissue deep to the pectoralis major to reach its target muscle. Which nerve is this?
optionExplanations: The lateral pectoral nerve pierces the clavipectoral fascia to supply the pectoralis major.
The medial pectoral nerve pierces the pectoralis minor muscle.
The long thoracic nerve stays on the superficial surface of the serratus anterior.
The thoracodorsal nerve descends to the latissimus dorsi.
The axillary nerve passes through the quadrangular space.
citation: The lateral pectoral nerve... pierce the clavipectoral fascia

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Lateral Cutaneous Nerve of Forearm Distribution
statement: A patient sustains a deep laceration to the lateral aspect of the elbow. During examination, they report a loss of sensation on the lateral side of the forearm extending to the base of the thumb. Which nerve is likely damaged?
options: Palmar cutaneous branch of median nerve
optionExplanations: This nerve supplies the lateral forearm skin down to the root of the thumb.
The medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm supplies the medial aspect.
The posterior cutaneous nerve of the forearm supplies the middle of the back of the forearm.
The superficial radial nerve supplies the lateral dorsum of the hand.
The palmar cutaneous branch of the median nerve supplies the lateral palm.
citation: The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm... down as far as the root of the thumb.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Upper Lateral Cutaneous Nerve of Arm
statement: A 30-year-old athlete suffers an inferior dislocation of the shoulder. On examination, there is a patch of anesthesia over the lower part of the deltoid muscle. Which nerve is responsible for this sensory loss?
options: Upper lateral cutaneous nerve of arm
Lower lateral cutaneous nerve of arm
correctOption: Upper lateral cutaneous nerve of arm
optionExplanations: This nerve is a branch of the axillary nerve and supplies the skin over the lower deltoid.
The lower lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm is a branch of the radial nerve.
The posterior cutaneous nerve of the arm supplies the back of the arm.
Supraclavicular nerves supply the skin over the point of the shoulder.
The intercostobrachial nerve supplies the medial arm and axilla.
citation: upper lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm... supplies... the skin that covers its lower part.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A patient undergoing an axillary lymph node clearance reports numbness in the axilla and the inner aspect of the upper arm post-operatively. Which nerves were likely affected during the procedure?
options: Medial cutaneous nerve of arm and intercostobrachial nerve
correctOption: Medial cutaneous nerve of arm and intercostobrachial nerve
optionExplanations: These two nerves provide sensory supply to the armpit and medial arm.
The medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm supplies the forearm, not the armpit.
The lateral cutaneous nerves supply the lateral aspect of the arm.
The ulnar nerve supplies the medial hand, not the armpit skin.
The long thoracic nerve is purely motor to the serratus anterior.
citation: The medial cutaneous nerve of the arm (T1) and the intercostobrachial nerve... supply the skin of the armpit and the medial side of the arm.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A carpenter accidentally strikes the posterior aspect of his mid-forearm with a heavy tool. He now feels numbness running down the center of the back of his forearm to the wrist. Which nerve is involved?
optionExplanations: This nerve, from the radial nerve, supplies the skin of the middle posterior forearm.
The lateral cutaneous nerve supplies the lateral aspect of the forearm.
The medial cutaneous nerve supplies the medial aspect of the forearm.
The superficial radial nerve supplies the lateral dorsum of the hand.
The dorsal branch of the ulnar nerve supplies the medial dorsum of the hand.
citation: The posterior cutaneous nerve of the forearm runs down the middle of the back of the forearm as far as the wrist

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Posterior Cutaneous Branch of Ulnar Nerve
statement: A patient presents with a sensory deficit on the medial side of the back of the hand and the dorsal surface of the little finger. The sensation on the palm is normal. Where is the most likely site of ulnar nerve injury?
optionExplanations: The posterior cutaneous branch arises in the distal forearm; an injury at the wrist would spare it.
Injury at Guyon's canal spares the posterior cutaneous branch, which arises higher up.
Injury at the medial epicondyle would affect both palmar and dorsal sensation.
Axillary injury would affect the entire ulnar distribution.
Cubital fossa injury would affect both palmar and dorsal sensation.
citation: The posterior cutaneous branch... supplies the medial third of the dorsum of the hand and the medial one and a half fingers

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A woman with carpal tunnel syndrome complains of tingling in her fingers. Which specific area is most likely to exhibit these symptoms?
options: Dorsum of the lateral 2/3 of the hand
optionExplanations: The median nerve's digital branches supply the lateral 3.5 fingers on the palmar side.
The medial 1.5 fingers are supplied by the ulnar nerve.
The lateral dorsum of the hand is supplied by the radial nerve.
The thenar eminence skin is supplied by the palmar cutaneous branch, which spares the carpal tunnel.
The hypothenar eminence is supplied by the ulnar nerve.
citation: The palmar digital branches supply the palmar aspect of the lateral three and a half fingers

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A patient has a sensory loss restricted to the lateral part of the dorsum of the hand and the proximal parts of the lateral 3.5 fingers. Which nerve is affected?
optionExplanations: The superficial radial nerve supplies the lateral 2/3 of the dorsal hand.
The dorsal ulnar nerve supplies the medial 1/3 of the dorsal hand.
The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm ends at the root of the thumb.
The median nerve supplies the distal dorsal parts of the lateral fingers, not the proximal dorsum.
The posterior cutaneous nerve of the forearm ends at the wrist.
citation: The superficial branch of the radial nerve... supplies the lateral two thirds of the dorsum of the hand

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: During surgery, a resident is asked to identify the structure that maintains the hollow of the armpit. The lead surgeon explains it is a continuation of the clavipectoral fascia. What is this structure called?
optionExplanations: The clavipectoral fascia continues inferiorly as the suspensory ligament to the axillary floor.
The axillary sheath encloses the neurovascular bundle, not the floor.
Prevertebral fascia is continuous with the axillary sheath, not the suspensory ligament.
The bicipital aponeurosis is in the cubital fossa.
The palmar aponeurosis is in the hand.
citation: continues downward as the suspensory ligament of the axilla and joins the fascial floor of the armpit.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A surgeon is performing a deltopectoral approach to the shoulder. They identify a large superficial vein lying in the groove between the deltoid and pectoralis major. To reach the axillary vein, what must this vein pierce?
optionExplanations: The cephalic vein pierces the clavipectoral fascia in the deltopectoral triangle.
The cephalic vein passes superior to the pectoralis minor.
The bicipital aponeurosis is in the elbow region.
The axillary sheath is deep to the clavipectoral fascia.
Intercostal muscles are in the chest wall, not the deltopectoral triangle.
citation: dives into the deltopectoral triangle, pierces the clavipectoral fascia

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: During an intravenous injection in the cubital fossa, a nurse is careful not to go too deep to avoid the brachial artery. Which anatomical structure provides a natural protective barrier between the superficial vein and the deep artery?
optionExplanations: The bicipital aponeurosis separates the median cubital vein from the brachial artery.
The clavipectoral fascia is in the pectoral region.
The deep fascia is present, but the aponeurosis is the specific reinforcing structure here.
The pronator teres forms the medial boundary of the fossa.
The brachialis forms the floor of the fossa.
citation: separated from them by the bicipital aponeurosis.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: An anatomist is tracing the basilic vein proximally. At which landmark does the basilic vein join the deep veins to become the axillary vein?
optionExplanations: The basilic vein joins the brachial venae comitantes at the teres major's lower border.
The pectoralis major forms the anterior axillary fold, not the venous junction point.
The pectoralis minor divides the axillary artery, not the start of the vein.
The axillary vein becomes the subclavian vein at the first rib.
The brachial artery bifurcates at the neck of the radius.
citation: at the lower border of the teres major joins the venae comitantes of the brachial artery to form the axillary vein

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A patient reports loss of sensation on the medial aspect of the forearm following a mid-arm injury. Which nerve, arising from the medial cord of the brachial plexus, is likely involved?
optionExplanations: This nerve supplies the medial forearm skin down to the wrist.
The ulnar nerve supplies the medial hand, not the medial forearm skin.
The medial cutaneous nerve of the arm supplies the medial arm skin.
The median nerve supplies the lateral palm and fingers.
The radial nerve supplies the posterior and lateral aspects.
citation: The medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm... supplies the skin on the medial side of the forearm

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A patient presents with numbness on the posterior aspect of the upper arm reaching the elbow. Which branch of the radial nerve is most likely affected?
options: Lower lateral cutaneous nerve of arm
Upper lateral cutaneous nerve of arm
optionExplanations: This radial nerve branch supplies the skin on the back of the arm.
The lower lateral cutaneous nerve supplies the lateral arm.
The posterior cutaneous nerve of the forearm supplies the back of the forearm.
The upper lateral cutaneous nerve is from the axillary nerve.
The intercostobrachial nerve supplies the medial arm.
citation: The posterior cutaneous nerve of the arm supplies the skin of the back of the arm down to the elbow

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A clinician is examining the 'anatomic snuffbox' on a patient's wrist. They observe a superficial vein crossing this area. Which vein is this?
optionExplanations: The cephalic vein crosses the snuffbox as it winds from the dorsal arch to the forearm.
The basilic vein is on the medial side of the wrist.
The median cubital vein is in the elbow region.
The radial vein is a deep vein accompanying the radial artery.
The ulnar vein is a deep vein on the medial side.
citation: The cephalic vein crosses the anatomic snuffbox

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: In a patient with breast cancer, malignant cells are found to have spread from the infraclavicular nodes to the deeper axillary nodes. Through which structure did these lymphatic channels pass?
optionExplanations: Lymphatic channels from the infraclavicular nodes pierce the clavipectoral fascia to reach deep structures.
The channels pierce the fascia deep to the pectoralis major, not the muscle fibers themselves.
The suspensory ligament is the inferior continuation of the fascia.
The bicipital aponeurosis is in the elbow.
The costocoracoid ligament is a part of the clavipectoral fascia, but the fascia as a whole is the piercing site.
citation: lymphatic channels from the infraclavicular nodes pierce the clavipectoral fascia

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A 50-year-old woman with gallbladder disease complains of sharp pain over the point of her right shoulder. Which nerve is responsible for the cutaneous sensation in the area where the pain is being referred?
options: Lower lateral cutaneous nerve of arm
optionExplanations: The supraclavicular nerves (C3,4) supply the shoulder point skin; pain is referred here via shared segments with the phrenic nerve.
The axillary nerve supplies the lower deltoid, not the shoulder point.
The phrenic nerve carries the original stimulus from the diaphragm, but the cutaneous sensation is supraclavicular.
The vagus nerve does not supply the shoulder skin.
The lower lateral cutaneous nerve supplies the lower arm.
citation: Pain may be referred to this region as a result of inflammatory lesions involving the diaphragmatic pleura or peritoneum.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A surgeon is performing an infraclavicular approach to the axilla. After reflecting the pectoralis major muscle, a strong fascial sheet is encountered. Which structure is this?
optionExplanations: The clavipectoral fascia is the specific layer located immediately deep to the pectoralis major.
The axillary sheath is a deeper structure enclosing neurovascular bundles.
The prevertebral fascia is located in the neck and is continuous with the axillary sheath, not deep to the pectoralis major.
The pectoral fascia is superficial to the pectoralis major muscle.
The suspensory ligament is the inferior continuation of the clavipectoral fascia.
citation: The clavipectoral fascia is a strong sheet of connective tissue lying immediately deep to the pectoralis major muscle

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A 25-year-old male requires a central venous line. The clinician identifies a superficial vein in the deltopectoral triangle. To reach its termination, which fascia must this vein pierce?
optionExplanations: The pectoral fascia is superficial to the vein in this region.
The cephalic vein must pierce the clavipectoral fascia to enter the axillary vein.
The axillary sheath is the final destination, but the fascia is the barrier pierced.
The deep fascia of the arm is distal to the deltopectoral triangle.
Intermuscular septa divide the arm into compartments, not the deltopectoral triangle.
citation: It dives into the deltopectoral triangle, pierces the clavipectoral fascia, and terminates by draining into the axillary vein.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: During a surgical dissection of the pectoral region, a nerve is identified piercing the clavipectoral fascia to supply the pectoralis major. Which nerve is this?
optionExplanations: The medial pectoral nerve pierces the pectoralis minor, not the clavipectoral fascia directly.
The long thoracic nerve descends on the superficial surface of the serratus anterior.
The lateral pectoral nerve is one of the four structures that pierce the clavipectoral fascia.
The thoracodorsal nerve supplies the latissimus dorsi in the posterior axillary wall.
The axillary nerve passes through the quadrangular space.
citation: The lateral pectoral nerve, cephalic vein, branches of the thoracoacromial artery, and lymphatic channels from the infraclavicular nodes pierce the clavipectoral fascia

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A nurse is performing venipuncture on the median cubital vein. She is cautious because of the underlying brachial artery. Which structure provides a natural protective barrier between the vein and the artery?
optionExplanations: The brachialis muscle forms the floor of the cubital fossa, deep to the artery.
The bicipital aponeurosis separates the superficial median cubital vein from the deep brachial artery.
The deep fascia is a general layer, but the aponeurosis is the specific protective structure here.
The pronator teres forms the medial boundary of the fossa.
The flexor retinaculum is located at the wrist.
citation: In the cubital fossa, the median cubital vein crosses over the brachial artery and the median nerve, but it is separated from them by the bicipital aponeurosis.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A clinician chooses the basilic vein over the cephalic vein for central venous catheterization. What anatomical feature makes the basilic vein the preferred choice for this procedure?
options: It is in direct line with the axillary vein
It originates from the lateral venous arch
correctOption: It is in direct line with the axillary vein
optionExplanations: Both are superficial veins.
The basilic vein does have valves which can sometimes be troublesome.
The direct linear relationship with the axillary vein facilitates easier catheter passage.
The cephalic vein pierces the clavipectoral fascia, which can make maneuvering difficult.
The basilic vein originates from the medial side of the dorsal venous arch.
citation: The median cubital or basilic veins are the veins of choice for central venous catheterization, because from the cubital fossa until the basilic vein reaches the axillary vein, the basilic vein increases in diameter and is in direct line with the axillary vein.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A patient presents with numbness on the lateral aspect of the forearm following a deep laceration just above the elbow, lateral to the biceps tendon. Which nerve is likely severed?
optionExplanations: The medial cutaneous nerve supplies the medial side of the forearm.
The superficial radial nerve supplies the dorsum of the hand.
The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm (from musculocutaneous) supplies this specific area.
The posterior cutaneous nerve supplies the back of the forearm.
The musculocutaneous nerve trunk is deeper and proximal to this cutaneous continuation.
citation: It then runs down the lateral aspect of the forearm as the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: Following an axillary lymph node clearance, a patient complains of sensory loss in the armpit and the medial aspect of the upper arm. Which nerve was most likely damaged during the surgery?
optionExplanations: The long thoracic nerve is motor to the serratus anterior.
The thoracodorsal nerve is motor to the latissimus dorsi.
The intercostobrachial nerve provides cutaneous supply to the armpit and medial arm.
The ulnar nerve supplies the medial hand, not the armpit.
The lateral pectoral nerve is motor to the pectoralis major.
citation: The medial cutaneous nerve of the arm (T1) and the intercostobrachial nerve (a branch of the T2 intercostal nerve) supply the skin of the armpit and the medial side of the arm.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A patient with suspected gallbladder inflammation reports sharp pain at the 'point of the shoulder.' Which cutaneous nerves are responsible for transmitting this referred sensation?
optionExplanations: Axillary nerves supply the lower half of the deltoid.
Supraclavicular nerves (C3,4) supply the shoulder point and share segments with the phrenic nerve.
Upper lateral cutaneous nerves are branches of the axillary nerve.
Intercostobrachial nerves supply the armpit.
Dorsal scapular nerves are motor to the rhomboids.
citation: The supraclavicular nerves (C3, 4 from the cervical plexus) provide the sensory nerve supply to the skin over the point of the shoulder to halfway down the deltoid muscle.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A patient with a displaced mid-shaft clavicle fracture presents with a massive hematoma in the deltopectoral triangle. This is likely due to the rupture of a communicating vein between the cephalic vein and which other vessel?
optionExplanations: The internal jugular is deep in the neck.
The subclavian vein is deep to the subclavius muscle.
The cephalic vein often communicates with the external jugular vein across the clavicle.
The cephalic vein drains into the axillary vein, but the communication is with the external jugular.
The basilic vein is on the medial side of the limb.
citation: In the deltopectoral triangle, the cephalic vein frequently communicates with the external jugular vein by a small vein that crosses in front of the clavicle.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A surgeon is ligating small arterial branches supplying the pectoral muscles. These vessels are found piercing the clavipectoral fascia. From which parent artery do these branches originate?
optionExplanations: The lateral thoracic artery runs along the lower border of the pectoralis minor.
The internal thoracic artery is inside the thoracic cavity.
Branches of the thoracoacromial artery are among the structures that pierce the clavipectoral fascia.
The highest thoracic artery is a small branch from the first part of the axillary artery.
The subscapular artery is a branch of the third part of the axillary artery.
citation: The lateral pectoral nerve, cephalic vein, branches of the thoracoacromial artery, and lymphatic channels from the infraclavicular nodes pierce the clavipectoral fascia

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A patient with a lesion of the medial cord of the brachial plexus will exhibit sensory loss on which part of the forearm?
optionExplanations: The lateral side is supplied by the musculocutaneous nerve.
The posterior side is supplied by the radial nerve.
The medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm, from the medial cord, supplies the medial side.
The dorsal aspect of the thumb is supplied by the radial nerve.
Finger tips are supplied by the median and ulnar nerves.
citation: The medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm arises from the medial cord of the brachial plexus and descends in front of the axillary artery.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A patient wearing a very tight watch strap complains of numbness on the lateral part of the dorsum of the hand. Which nerve is likely being compressed?
optionExplanations: The palmar cutaneous branch supplies the palm.
The superficial radial nerve supplies the lateral dorsum of the hand.
The dorsal ulnar branch supplies the medial dorsum of the hand.
The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm ends at the root of the thumb.
The deep branch of the radial nerve is purely motor in the forearm.
citation: The superficial branch of the radial nerve is a cutaneous nerve to the wrist and hand.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Thenar Sensation in Carpal Tunnel Syndrome
statement: A 45-year-old woman with carpal tunnel syndrome reports numbness in her index and middle fingers but notes that sensation over the fleshy base of her thumb (thenar eminence) is normal. What explains this sparing?
options: Palmar cutaneous branch is superficial to the tunnel
Thenar eminence is supplied by C8
correctOption: Palmar cutaneous branch is superficial to the tunnel
optionExplanations: Ulnar overlap does not cover the thenar eminence.
Radial nerve supplies the dorsum, not the palmar thenar eminence.
The palmar cutaneous branch of the median nerve bypasses the carpal tunnel.
The recurrent branch is motor, not sensory.
The thenar eminence skin is primarily C6/C7 via the median nerve.
citation: As you would expect, no paresthesia occurs over the thenar eminence because the palmar cutaneous branch of the median nerve, which passes superficial to the flexor retinaculum, supplies this area of skin.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A patient has a localized infection on the lateral side of the dorsum of the hand. The clinician observes red streaks (lymphangitis) following the superficial vein that drains this area. Which vein is this?
optionExplanations: The basilic vein drains the medial end of the arch.
The median cubital vein is in the elbow region.
The cephalic vein drains the lateral end of the dorsal venous arch.
The axillary vein is a deep vein in the armpit.
The ulnar vein is a deep vein of the forearm.
citation: The dorsal venous arch drains into the cephalic and basilic veins at its lateral and medial ends, respectively.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Upper Lateral Cutaneous Nerve of Arm
statement: A patient with a fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus shows sensory loss over the lower half of the deltoid muscle. Which specific cutaneous nerve is non-functional?
options: Upper lateral cutaneous nerve of arm
Lower lateral cutaneous nerve of arm
correctOption: Upper lateral cutaneous nerve of arm
optionExplanations: Supraclavicular nerves supply the upper half of the deltoid.
The upper lateral cutaneous nerve (from axillary) supplies the lower half of the deltoid.
The lower lateral cutaneous nerve is a branch of the radial nerve.
The medial cutaneous nerve supplies the medial arm.
The posterior cutaneous nerve supplies the back of the arm.
citation: A posterior terminal branch gives off a branch to the teres minor muscle and a few branches to the deltoid and then emerges from the posterior border of the deltoid as the upper lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A patient suffers a mid-shaft humeral fracture involving the radial groove. In addition to motor loss, they have sensory deficits along the posterior midline of the forearm. Which nerve is involved?
optionExplanations: The lateral cutaneous nerve is from the musculocutaneous nerve.
The medial cutaneous nerve is from the medial cord.
The posterior cutaneous nerve of the forearm is a branch of the radial nerve arising in the radial groove.
The superficial radial nerve supplies the hand, not the middle of the forearm.
The dorsal ulnar nerve supplies the medial hand.
citation: The posterior cutaneous nerve of the forearm runs down the middle of the back of the forearm as far as the wrist

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: During a physical examination of the cubital fossa, a medical student notes that the superficial venous pattern resembles an 'M' rather than the typical 'H' shape. This variation involves the connection between which two main veins?
optionExplanations: These are deep veins.
The median cubital vein (or its variants) connects the superficial cephalic and basilic veins.
These are deep veins of the forearm.
The brachial is a deep vein.
These are veins of the neck.
citation: The median cubital vein is normally present, but the form in which it connects the cephalic and basilic veins is subject to variation

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A surgeon is clearing the axillary fat and notes a fascial band that seems to pull the floor of the axilla upward. This band is the inferior continuation of which structure?
optionExplanations: The pectoral fascia does not form the suspensory ligament.
The clavipectoral fascia continues inferiorly as the suspensory ligament of the axilla.
The axillary sheath surrounds the neurovascular bundle.
The pretracheal fascia is in the neck.
The deltoid fascia is lateral to the axilla.
citation: Inferiorly, it splits to enclose the subclavius and pectoralis minor muscles and then continues downward as the suspensory ligament of the axilla and joins the fascial floor of the armpit.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A patient has a traction injury to the medial cord of the brachial plexus. Which cutaneous nerve, providing sensation to the medial side of the upper arm, will be affected?
optionExplanations: The lateral cutaneous nerve is from the axillary or radial nerves.
The posterior cutaneous nerve is from the radial nerve.
The medial cutaneous nerve of the arm arises directly from the medial cord.
The intercostobrachial nerve arises from the T2 intercostal nerve, not the medial cord.
Supraclavicular nerves are from the cervical plexus.
citation: The medial cutaneous nerve of the arm (T1) arises from the medial cord of the brachial plexus

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A paramedic is attempting to establish peripheral IV access in a patient with low blood pressure. Which vein can be reliably found just posterior to the radial styloid process?
optionExplanations: The basilic vein is on the medial side of the wrist.
The median cubital vein is at the elbow.
The cephalic vein is consistently found posterior to the radial styloid process at the wrist.
The radial vein is a deep vein.
The ulnar vein is a deep vein.
citation: The cephalic vein lies fairly constantly in the superficial fascia, immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: What is the superior attachment of the clavipectoral fascia?
optionExplanations: The first rib is a medial relation, not the superior attachment.
The coracoid process is a lateral attachment point for the pectoralis minor enclosed by the fascia.
The clavipectoral fascia attaches superiorly to the clavicle.
The sternum is medial to the fascia.
The acromion is lateral to the fascia.
citation: Superiorly, it attaches to the clavicle.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: Which vein does the median cubital vein typically join after running upward and medially?
optionExplanations: It does not join the axillary vein directly.
It arises from the cephalic vein.
The median cubital vein joins the basilic vein in the cubital fossa.
The brachial vein is a deep vein.
The subclavian vein is in the root of the neck.
citation: The median cubital vein, a branch of the cephalic vein in the cubital fossa, runs upward and medially and joins the basilic vein.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Lateral Cutaneous Nerve of Forearm Supply
statement: What is the distal extent of the skin area supplied by the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm?
optionExplanations: It passes the wrist joint.
The nerve supplies the lateral forearm down to the root of the thumb.
The index finger is supplied by the median nerve.
It extends beyond the mid-forearm.
The little finger is supplied by the ulnar nerve.
citation: The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm supplies the skin of the front and lateral aspects of the forearm down as far as the root of the thumb.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: Which of the following structures pierces the clavipectoral fascia?
optionExplanations: The medial pectoral nerve pierces the pectoralis minor muscle.
The basilic vein pierces the deep fascia of the arm.
The cephalic vein pierces the clavipectoral fascia in the deltopectoral triangle.
The brachial artery is deep in the arm.
The ulnar nerve is a deep structure in the arm and forearm.
citation: The lateral pectoral nerve, cephalic vein, branches of the thoracoacromial artery, and lymphatic channels from the infraclavicular nodes pierce the clavipectoral fascia

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: Where does the superficial venous network of the upper limb begin?
optionExplanations: The palmar plexus communicates with the arch but is not the primary origin described.
The network begins with the dorsal venous arch on the back of the hand.
The axillary vein is the termination of the network.
The median cubital vein is a connection at the elbow.
The cephalic vein is a continuation of the arch.
citation: The superficial venous network begins with the dorsal venous arch in the dorsum of the hand.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A 45-year-old woman presents with sharp pain over the point of her right shoulder. Physical examination of the shoulder joint is normal, but she has tenderness in the right upper quadrant of her abdomen. Which nerve is responsible for the referred pain to this specific cutaneous area?
optionExplanations: The supraclavicular nerves (C3-4) supply the skin over the shoulder and can carry referred pain from diaphragmatic irritation.
The axillary nerve supplies the skin over the lower half of the deltoid, not the point of the shoulder.
The radial nerve supplies the posterior aspect of the limb.
The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the lateral forearm skin.
The intercostobrachial nerve supplies the skin of the armpit and medial arm.
citation: The supraclavicular nerves (C3, 4 from the cervical plexus) provide the sensory nerve supply to the skin over the point of the shoulder to halfway down the deltoid muscle.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: Which of the following nerves pierces the clavipectoral fascia to reach its target muscle?
optionExplanations: The lateral pectoral nerve pierces the clavipectoral fascia to supply the pectoralis major.
The medial pectoral nerve pierces the pectoralis minor, not the clavipectoral fascia.
The long thoracic nerve descends on the superficial surface of the serratus anterior.
The thoracodorsal nerve runs in the axilla to the latissimus dorsi.
The suprascapular nerve passes through the suprascapular notch.
citation: The lateral pectoral nerve, cephalic vein, branches of the thoracoacromial artery, and lymphatic channels from the infraclavicular nodes pierce the clavipectoral fascia

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A 50-year-old carpenter complains of burning pain and 'pins and needles' in his thumb, index, and middle fingers. On examination, there is sensory loss in these fingers, but sensation over the fleshy thenar eminence is completely preserved. Which nerve branch explains this preservation?
optionExplanations: The palmar cutaneous branch of the median nerve passes superficial to the carpal tunnel and is spared in carpal tunnel syndrome.
The recurrent branch is motor to the thenar muscles.
Proper palmar digital branches pass through the carpal tunnel and would be compressed.
The anterior interosseous nerve is a deep motor branch in the forearm.
The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm supplies the lateral forearm, not the thenar eminence.
citation: no paresthesia occurs over the thenar eminence because the palmar cutaneous branch of the median nerve, which passes superficial to the flexor retinaculum, supplies this area of skin.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A 19-year-old athlete sustains a fracture of the middle third of the clavicle. Six weeks later, while the bone is healing, he develops persistent aching pain over the side of his neck and the point of his shoulder. This is most likely due to the involvement of which nerves in the healing process?
optionExplanations: The supraclavicular nerves are closely related to the clavicle and can be trapped in healing callus.
The phrenic nerve is deep in the neck and not involved in clavicular callus.
The vagus nerve is within the carotid sheath.
The accessory nerve is in the posterior triangle but not directly on the clavicle.
The long thoracic nerve is deeper in the axilla.
citation: The close relationship of the supraclavicular nerves to the clavicle may result in their involvement in callus formation after fracture of the bone.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: What is the superior attachment of the clavipectoral fascia?
optionExplanations: The clavipectoral fascia attaches superiorly to the clavicle.
The first rib is a medial boundary of the axillary apex.
The coracoid process is a lateral attachment for the pectoralis minor, which the fascia encloses.
The manubrium is medial to the fascia's primary extent.
The scapular spine is on the posterior aspect of the shoulder.
citation: Superiorly, it attaches to the clavicle.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Protection of the brachial artery in the cubital fossa
statement: During a difficult venipuncture in the cubital fossa, a medical student accidentally pierces the deep wall of the median cubital vein. Which structure provides a natural surgical barrier that protects the underlying brachial artery from the needle?
optionExplanations: The bicipital aponeurosis separates the superficial median cubital vein from the deep brachial artery.
The flexor retinaculum is at the wrist.
The palmar aponeurosis is in the hand.
The interosseous membrane is between the radius and ulna.
The clavipectoral fascia is in the pectoral region.
citation: In the cubital fossa, the median cubital vein crosses over the brachial artery and the median nerve, but it is separated from them by the bicipital aponeurosis.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm
statement: A 22-year-old man sustains a deep laceration on the lateral side of his forearm. He reports loss of sensation on the front and lateral aspects of the forearm reaching the base of the thumb. Which nerve is most likely severed?
optionExplanations: The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm (from musculocutaneous) supplies this specific territory.
The medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm supplies the medial aspect.
The posterior cutaneous nerve of the forearm supplies the middle of the back of the forearm.
The superficial radial nerve supplies the dorsum of the hand.
The ulnar nerve supplies the medial side of the hand.
citation: The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm supplies the skin of the front and lateral aspects of the forearm down as far as the root of the thumb.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Posterior cutaneous nerve of the arm
statement: A patient using poorly adjusted crutches presents with 'crutch palsy.' In addition to motor weakness, he has sensory loss specifically on the skin of the back of the arm extending to the elbow. Which branch of the radial nerve is affected?
options: Lower lateral cutaneous nerve of arm
optionExplanations: The posterior cutaneous nerve of the arm is a branch of the radial nerve in the axilla supplying the back of the arm.
The lower lateral cutaneous nerve supplies the lateral/anterior lower arm.
The posterior cutaneous nerve of the forearm supplies the back of the forearm.
The superficial radial nerve is sensory to the hand.
The axillary nerve supplies the lower deltoid skin.
citation: The posterior cutaneous nerve of the arm supplies the skin of the back of the arm down to the elbow

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A 30-year-old woman presents with an infected wound on her hand. The physician notes several thin, red, tender lines extending up her forearm toward the axilla. What is the most likely diagnosis for these visible streaks?
optionExplanations: Red streaks following lymphatic vessels indicate lymphangitis.
Lymphadenitis refers to the enlargement and inflammation of the lymph nodes themselves.
Thrombophlebitis involves inflammation of a vein with clot formation.
Cellulitis is a diffuse infection of the skin and subcutaneous tissue.
Arteritis is inflammation of the arteries.
citation: Red streaks along the course of the lymph vessels are characteristic of the condition.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A clinician needs to perform central venous catheterization. Which superficial vein is preferred because it increases in diameter as it ascends and is in a direct line with the axillary vein?
optionExplanations: The basilic vein is preferred for its direct path and increasing diameter.
The cephalic vein often joins the axillary vein at a difficult right angle.
The median cubital vein is used for access but the basilic is the continuation into the axilla.
The external jugular is in the neck.
Venae comitantes are deep veins.
citation: The median cubital or basilic veins are the veins of choice for central venous catheterization

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A young man falls on his outstretched hand and complains of pain in the 'anatomic snuffbox.' While checking for a scaphoid fracture, the physician tests sensation on the lateral part of the dorsum of the hand. Which nerve is being evaluated?
optionExplanations: The superficial radial nerve provides sensory supply to the lateral dorsum of the hand.
The dorsal cutaneous branch of the ulnar nerve supplies the medial dorsum.
The palmar cutaneous branch of the median nerve supplies the lateral palm.
The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm ends at the root of the thumb.
The medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm supplies the medial forearm.
citation: The superficial radial nerve is sensory and supplies the skin over the lateral part of the dorsum of the hand

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Origin of the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm
statement: The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm is the direct continuation of which nerve?
optionExplanations: The musculocutaneous nerve becomes the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm after supplying the arm muscles.
The radial nerve continues as the superficial radial nerve.
The median nerve does not have a 'lateral cutaneous' continuation in the forearm.
The ulnar nerve continues into the hand.
The axillary nerve ends in the shoulder region.
citation: The musculocutaneous nerve... then runs down the lateral aspect of the forearm as the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A patient presents with a fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus. In addition to deltoid paralysis, where would you expect to find a localized area of sensory loss?
optionExplanations: The axillary nerve (upper lateral cutaneous branch) supplies the skin over the lower deltoid.
The point of the shoulder is supplied by supraclavicular nerves.
The medial arm is supplied by the medial cutaneous nerve of the arm.
The back of the arm is supplied by the posterior cutaneous nerve of the arm.
The lateral forearm is supplied by the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm.
citation: consequently, there is a loss of skin sensation over the lower half of the deltoid muscle.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm
statement: A 35-year-old woman reports tingling and numbness on the medial aspect of her forearm following a compression injury in the axilla. Which nerve is most likely involved?
optionExplanations: The medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm (from the medial cord) supplies the medial forearm skin.
The lateral cutaneous nerve supplies the lateral forearm.
The posterior cutaneous nerve supplies the posterior forearm.
The intercostobrachial nerve supplies the armpit and medial arm.
The supraclavicular nerve supplies the shoulder.
citation: The medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm... supplies the skin on the medial side of the forearm

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: Which superficial vein pierces the clavipectoral fascia to terminate in the axillary vein?
optionExplanations: The cephalic vein pierces the clavipectoral fascia in the deltopectoral triangle.
The basilic vein pierces the deep fascia of the arm, not the clavipectoral fascia.
The median cubital vein is a communication in the cubital fossa.
The axillary vein is a deep vein.
The thoracoacromial vein is a tributary, not a superficial vein piercing the fascia.
citation: The cephalic vein... pierces the clavipectoral fascia

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A patient undergoing axillary lymph node dissection for breast cancer reports sensory loss in the armpit postoperatively. Which nerve was most likely damaged during the procedure?
optionExplanations: The intercostobrachial nerve (T2) supplies the skin of the armpit and is often at risk during axillary surgery.
The long thoracic nerve is motor to the serratus anterior.
The thoracodorsal nerve is motor to the latissimus dorsi.
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid and shoulder skin.
The radial nerve supplies the posterior limb.
citation: The medial cutaneous nerve of the arm (T1) and the intercostobrachial nerve... supply the skin of the armpit

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Palmar cutaneous branch of the ulnar nerve
statement: A 25-year-old man sustains a superficial laceration at the wrist, just lateral to the pisiform bone. He has lost sensation over the medial part of his palm, but sensation on the back of his hand is normal. Which branch is affected?
optionExplanations: The palmar cutaneous branch of the ulnar nerve supplies the medial palm skin.
The dorsal cutaneous branch supplies the back of the hand.
The superficial branch supplies the medial 1.5 fingers.
The deep branch is primarily motor.
The palmar cutaneous branch of the median nerve supplies the lateral palm.
citation: The palmar cutaneous branch... supplies the skin over the medial part of the palm

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Lower lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm
statement: A patient presents with sensory loss over the lateral and anterior aspects of the lower part of the arm. Which nerve branch is responsible for this area?
options: Lower lateral cutaneous nerve of arm
Upper lateral cutaneous nerve of arm
correctOption: Lower lateral cutaneous nerve of arm
optionExplanations: The lower lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm (from radial) supplies this territory.
The upper lateral cutaneous nerve (from axillary) supplies the deltoid area.
The posterior cutaneous nerve supplies the back of the arm.
The medial cutaneous nerve supplies the medial arm.
The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm supplies the forearm, not the arm.
citation: The lower lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm supplies the skin over the lateral and anterior aspects of the lower part of the arm

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: Which two muscles are enclosed by the clavipectoral fascia?
optionExplanations: The clavipectoral fascia splits to enclose the subclavius and pectoralis minor.
The pectoralis major is superficial to this fascia.
The pectoralis major is not enclosed by the clavipectoral fascia.
The serratus anterior is on the medial wall of the axilla.
The deltoid is lateral to this region.
citation: Inferiorly, it splits to enclose the subclavius and pectoralis minor muscles

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A child has an infected scratch on his little finger. The pediatrician finds a small, tender, palpable mass just above the medial epicondyle of the humerus. Which lymph node is this?
optionExplanations: The supratrochlear node drains the medial fingers and is located above the medial epicondyle.
Pectoral nodes are in the axilla.
Subscapular nodes are on the posterior axillary wall.
Lateral axillary nodes are along the axillary vein.
Apical nodes are at the axillary apex.
citation: The supratrochlear lymph node lies in the superficial fascia over the upper part of the cubital fossa, above the trochlea.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: During a breast examination, a clinician palpates a mass in the axilla. She recalls that a specific part of the mammary gland pierces the deep fascia at the lower border of the pectoralis major. What is this structure?
optionExplanations: The axillary tail of Spence pierces the deep fascia to enter the axilla.
Suspensory ligaments (Cooper's) separate the lobes.
The areola is the pigmented skin around the nipple.
The ampulla is a dilation of the lactiferous duct.
Lactiferous ducts open on the nipple.
citation: An extension of the gland called the axillary tail (see Fig. 3.14C) continues upward and laterally, pierces the deep fascia

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A patient is in hypovolemic shock, and his superficial veins are not visible. Where should a clinician look for the cephalic vein to perform an emergency cutdown at the wrist?
options: Posterior to the styloid process of radius
Anterior to the styloid process of ulna
Lateral to the hook of hamate
correctOption: Posterior to the styloid process of radius
optionExplanations: The cephalic vein is consistently found posterior to the radial styloid process at the wrist.
The basilic vein is related to the medial side/ulna.
The ulnar artery/nerve are lateral to the pisiform.
The hook of hamate is deep in the palm.
The olecranon is at the elbow.
citation: The cephalic vein lies fairly constantly in the superficial fascia, immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
coreConcept: Medial cutaneous nerve of the arm
statement: Which nerve provides sensory innervation to the skin on the medial side of the arm?
optionExplanations: The medial cutaneous nerve of the arm (T1) supplies the medial arm skin.
The lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm (from radial/axillary) supplies the lateral side.
The posterior cutaneous nerve supplies the back of the arm.
The intercostobrachial nerve also contributes to the medial arm and armpit.
The supraclavicular nerve supplies the shoulder.
citation: The medial cutaneous nerve of the arm (T1)... supplies the skin on the medial side of the arm

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: The clavipectoral fascia continues inferiorly to join the fascial floor of the armpit as which structure?
optionExplanations: The clavipectoral fascia becomes the suspensory ligament of the axilla inferiorly.
Suspensory ligaments of the breast are Cooper's ligaments.
The bicipital aponeurosis is in the cubital fossa.
The axillary sheath is derived from prevertebral fascia.
Intermuscular septa divide the arm/forearm into compartments.
citation: continues downward as the suspensory ligament of the axilla and joins the fascial floor of the armpit.

category: Dermatomes and cutaneous innervation of Upper Limb
statement: A patient has a burn on the dorsum of the distal phalanx of his index finger. Which nerve carries the sensory information from this specific distal dorsal area?
optionExplanations: The palmar digital nerves (median/ulnar) wrap around to supply the distal dorsal skin and nail beds.
The radial nerve only supplies the proximal dorsal skin of the lateral 3.5 fingers.
The ulnar nerve only supplies the proximal dorsal skin of the medial 1.5 fingers.
The lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm ends at the wrist.
The posterior cutaneous nerve of the forearm ends at the wrist.
citation: The dorsal digital branches of the radial and ulnar nerves do not extend far beyond the proximal phalanx.

statement: Which of the following is a unique developmental feature of the clavicle?
options: Only bone with two primary centers
Only bone lacking a marrow cavity
optionExplanations: The text explicitly states the clavicle is the first bone to begin the process of ossification.
The clavicle is the first, not the last, to begin ossification.
While it has unique centers, the text emphasizes its timing as the first.
The text does not state it lacks a marrow cavity.
The text specifies it is the first to begin, not necessarily the first to finish.
citation: It is the first bone to begin ossification.

statement: A 19-year-old athlete falls on his shoulder, sustaining a fracture at the junction of the middle and outer thirds of the clavicle. On examination, the medial fragment of the bone is noticeably elevated. Which muscle is primarily responsible for this displacement?
optionExplanations: The trapezius cannot counteract the downward pull on the lateral fragment, but it does not tilt the medial end up.
The pectoralis major pulls the lateral fragment medially and forward.
The sternocleidomastoid muscle is responsible for tilting the medial end of the fractured clavicle upward.
The subclavius is too small to cause this significant upward tilt.
The deltoid is attached to the lateral fragment, not the medial one.
citation: The medial end is tilted upward by the sternocleidomastoid muscle.

statement: A 45-year-old man presents to the ER after a bicycle accident. Radiographs confirm a midshaft clavicular fracture. The lateral fragment of the bone is displaced inferiorly. What is the primary cause of this specific displacement?
optionExplanations: The deltoid may pull the fragment, but the text specifically cites the weight of the arm as the cause of depression.
The text states the lateral fragment is depressed specifically by the weight of the arm after a fracture.
Pectoralis minor acts on the scapula, not directly on the lateral clavicle fragment.
The subclavius is involved in stabilizing the clavicle but does not cause this depression.
Atmospheric pressure is irrelevant to bony displacement in this context.
citation: The lateral fragment is depressed by the weight of the arm

statement: A patient undergoes a lymph node biopsy in the posterior triangle of the neck. Postoperatively, she complains of a 'dropped shoulder' and inability to shrug. Which nerve was likely injured during the procedure?
optionExplanations: The dorsal scapular nerve supplies the rhomboids, not the trapezius.
The long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior; injury causes winged scapula.
The spinal accessory nerve (CN XI) provides motor innervation to the trapezius; injury leads to dropped shoulder.
The thoracodorsal nerve supplies the latissimus dorsi.
The suprascapular nerve supplies the supraspinatus and infraspinatus.
citation: The spinal accessory nerve... provides motor fibers to the trapezius

statement: Which nerve provides the primary motor supply to the latissimus dorsi muscle?
optionExplanations: The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid and teres minor.
The thoracodorsal nerve is the specific motor supply for the latissimus dorsi.
The lower subscapular nerve supplies the subscapularis and teres major.
The dorsal scapular nerve supplies the rhomboids.
The medial pectoral nerve supplies the pectoralis major and minor.
citation: The thoracodorsal nerve... supplies the latissimus dorsi muscle

statement: During a physical examination, a clinician asks a patient to place his hands on his hips and push his elbows backward against resistance. The clinician notes weakness in retracting the scapula on the right side. Which nerve is most likely dysfunctional?
optionExplanations: Long thoracic nerve injury affects protraction, not retraction.
The dorsal scapular nerve supplies the rhomboids, which are the primary retractors of the scapula tested in this maneuver.
Suprascapular nerve injury affects the rotator cuff.
Upper subscapular nerve supplies the subscapularis.
Thoracodorsal nerve supplies the latissimus dorsi, which adducts and rotates the humerus.
citation: The dorsal scapular nerve... supplies the levator scapulae and rhomboid muscles.

statement: What is the origin of the pectoralis minor muscle?
optionExplanations: The 1st and 2nd ribs are not part of the origin.
The 2nd rib is not part of the origin.
The text states the pectoralis minor arises from the 3rd, 4th, and 5th ribs.
The 6th rib is not part of the origin.
The 6th and 7th ribs are not part of the origin.
citation: The pectoralis minor... arises from the third, fourth, and fifth ribs

statement: A patient presents with significant weakness in adduction and medial rotation of the humerus following a deep laceration to the upper pectoral region. The clinician suspects damage to the nerve supplying the clavicular head of the pectoralis major. Which nerve is this?
optionExplanations: The medial pectoral nerve primarily supplies the sternocostal head and pectoralis minor.
The lateral pectoral nerve supplies the pectoralis major, specifically its clavicular head.
The long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior.
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid.
The thoracodorsal nerve supplies the latissimus dorsi.
citation: The lateral pectoral nerve supplies the pectoralis major muscle

statement: Which nerve is known to pierce the pectoralis minor muscle to reach its target?
optionExplanations: The lateral pectoral nerve does not pierce the pectoralis minor.
The medial pectoral nerve supplies and pierces the pectoralis minor.
The intercostobrachial nerve is sensory to the skin of the axilla.
The long thoracic nerve descends on the surface of the serratus anterior.
The thoracodorsal nerve supplies the latissimus dorsi.
citation: The medial pectoral nerve... supplies and pierces the pectoralis minor muscle

statement: Which of the following statements regarding the surface anatomy of the clavicle is correct?
options: Only the medial half is palpable
Only the lateral half is palpable

Thoracodorsal nerve supplies latissimus dorsi.
citation: accessory nerve provides motor fibers to the trapezius

statement: A patient is unable to perform a powerful downward stroke of the arm while swimming. Testing reveals weakness in extension, adduction, and medial rotation of the humerus. Which muscle is most likely affected?
optionExplanations: Trapezius acts on the scapula, not primarily on humerus rotation.
Pectoralis minor stabilizes the scapula and does not rotate the arm.
The latissimus dorsi extends, adducts, and medially rotates the arm.
Rhomboid major retracts the scapula.
Levator scapulae elevates the scapula.
citation: Extends, adducts, and medially rotates arm

statement: During a physical exam, a clinician asks a patient to retract their shoulders. He notices the medial border of the scapula does not move toward the midline effectively below the level of the scapular spine. Which muscle's insertion is located in this specific area?
optionExplanations: Rhomboid minor inserts at the level of the spine, not below it.
Rhomboid major inserts on the medial border of the scapula from the spine to the inferior angle.
Trapezius inserts on the spine, acromion, and clavicle, not the medial border.
Serratus anterior inserts on the costal surface of the medial border.
Levator scapulae inserts on the medial border above the spine.
citation: Medial border of scapula from spine to inferior angle

statement: A 50-year-old woman complains of numbness in her right hand and a weakened radial pulse when she carries a heavy bag. Anatomically, this is often due to compression of neurovascular structures in which specific space?
options: Interval between clavicle and first rib
correctOption: Interval between clavicle and first rib
optionExplanations: The quadrangular space transmits the axillary nerve, not the main brachial plexus/artery.
Narrowing of the interval between the clavicle and the first rib can compress the brachial plexus and subclavian vessels.
The deltopectoral triangle contains the cephalic vein, not the main neurovascular bundle.
The bicipital groove houses the biceps tendon.
The suprascapular notch only contains the suprascapular nerve.
citation: interval between the clavicle and the first rib in some patients may become narrowed

statement: What type of joint is formed by the articulation of the medial end of the clavicle with the manubrium sterni?
optionExplanations: It is a mobile synovial joint, not fibrous.
It is not a synchondrosis.
The sternoclavicular joint is a compound synovial joint containing an articular disc.
It is not a symphysis.
Sutures are only found in the skull.
citation: articulates with the clavicular notch of the sternum through a compound synovial joint

statement: A victim of a high-speed automobile accident presents with a fractured scapula. Given the high force required to break this bone, which other injury should the trauma team immediately screen for?
optionExplanations: Colles' fracture is a distal radius injury from low-energy falls.
Scapular fractures result from severe trauma and are frequently associated with rib fractures.
Lunate dislocation is a wrist injury.
Bennett's fracture involves the thumb.
Tennis elbow is an inflammatory condition, not an acute traumatic fracture.
citation: Injuries are usually associated with fractured ribs

statement: A student is practicing surface anatomy. To locate the coracoid process of the scapula, where should they apply deep pressure?
optionExplanations: The acromion is posterior/lateral to the coracoid.
The sternum is far medial to the coracoid.
The coracoid process is palpated via deep pressure through the anterior deltoid, inferior to the lateral clavicle.
The inferior angle is at the level of the 7th rib posteriorly.
The suprasternal notch is in the midline of the neck.
citation: palpated via deep pressure through the anterior part of the deltoid muscle

statement: A patient has a puncture wound just inferior to the lateral third of the clavicle. The physician is concerned about the muscle that inserts into the 'crow's beak' bony projection. Which muscle is this?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis major inserts on the humerus.
Subclavius inserts on the clavicle.
The pectoralis minor inserts into the coracoid process (Greek for 'crow's beak').
Serratus anterior inserts on the medial border of the scapula.
Trapezius inserts on the spine, acromion, and clavicle.
citation: runs upward and laterally to insert into the coracoid process of the scapula

statement: During an axillary dissection, the lateral pectoral nerve is accidentally severed. Which specific functional deficit would be most prominent in the pectoralis major?
optionExplanations: The sternocostal head is primarily supplied by the medial pectoral nerve.
The lateral pectoral nerve primarily supplies the clavicular head of the pectoralis major.
The medial pectoral nerve would still supply the sternocostal part.
Pectoral nerves are motor nerves.
Pectoralis minor is primarily supplied by the medial pectoral nerve.
citation: Lateral pectoral nerve supplies the pectoralis major muscle, primarily its clavicular head

statement: A surgeon is working in the infraclavicular region and identifies a short arterial trunk arising from the second part of the axillary artery. This vessel then splits into four branches. What is this artery?
optionExplanations: Lateral thoracic artery runs along the lower border of pectoralis minor.
Highest thoracic artery is a small branch from the first part.
The thoracoacromial artery is a short trunk from the second part that gives four branches to the pectoral region.
Subscapular artery is from the third part.
Internal thoracic artery arises from the subclavian artery.
citation: immediately divides into four terminal branches that supply the pectoral muscles

statement: Which of the following describes the extensive origin of the latissimus dorsi muscle?
optionExplanations: It does not originate from cervical spines.
The latissimus dorsi originates from the iliac crest, lumbar fascia, and lower thoracic spines.
These are sites for pectoralis minor and serratus anterior.
This is an insertion site for rhomboids.
This is the origin for pectoralis major.
citation: Iliac crest, lumbar fascia, spines of lower six thoracic vertebrae

statement: A patient presents with weakness in retracting the scapula. An EMG study shows denervation of the rhomboid minor and major muscles. Which nerve is most likely damaged?
optionExplanations: Long thoracic nerve supplies serratus anterior.
Thoracodorsal nerve supplies latissimus dorsi.
The dorsal scapular nerve (C5) supplies both rhomboid major and minor.
Axillary nerve supplies deltoid and teres minor.
Suprascapular nerve supplies supraspinatus and infraspinatus.
citation: Dorsal scapular nerve (C5)

statement: A medical student is asked to explain why the human arm can move so freely away from the trunk. He explains that a specific bone acts as a mechanical support. Which bone and description is correct?
optionExplanations: The scapula is mobile but the clavicle is the primary connection to the thorax.
The clavicle forms a light strut connecting the upper limb to the thorax, allowing free movement.
The humerus is a bone of the arm, not the connecting strut.
The sternum is part of the axial skeleton.
The first rib is part of the thoracic cage.
citation: forms a light strut that connects the upper limb to the thorax

statement: During a surgical procedure in the pectoral region, a nerve is seen emerging through the fibers of the pectoralis minor to reach the pectoralis major. Which nerve is this?
optionExplanations: The lateral pectoral nerve does not typically pierce the pectoralis minor.
The medial pectoral nerve supplies and pierces the pectoralis minor before reaching the pectoralis major.
The long thoracic nerve stays on the surface of the serratus anterior.
The intercostobrachial nerve is sensory to the axilla skin.
The thoracodorsal nerve is deep in the axilla.
citation: supplies and pierces the pectoralis minor muscle

statement: A clinician is using the scapula as a landmark to identify vertebral levels. He palpates the medial border of the scapula and follows it to where the scapular spine begins. Which vertebral level does this correspond to?
optionExplanations: The superior angle is at the level of T2.
The spine of the scapula joins the medial border at the level of the third thoracic spine (T3).
T4 is below the level of the scapular spine.
The inferior angle is at the level of T7.
T1 is above the superior border of the scapula.
citation: joins at the level of the third thoracic spine

statement: A patient is asked to shrug his shoulders against resistance to test cranial nerve XI. Which specific fibers of the muscle being tested are primarily responsible for this movement?
optionExplanations: Lower fibers depress the scapula.
Middle fibers retract the scapula.
The upper fibers of the trapezius elevate the scapula (shrugging).
Latissimus dorsi does not elevate the scapula.
Pectoralis major acts on the humerus.
citation: Upper fibers elevate scapula

statement: A 19-year-old athlete falls onto his outstretched hand during a rugby match. He presents with severe pain and a visible deformity in the mid-clavicular region. Based on the typical transmission of force, where is the most likely site of the fracture?
options: Junction of middle and outer thirds
correctOption: Junction of middle and outer thirds
optionExplanations: The sternal end is thickened and rarely the primary site of trauma-induced fracture.
The acromial end is flattened and articulates with the scapula but is not the weakest point.
The junction of the middle and outer thirds is the weakest point of the clavicle where most fractures occur.
The conoid tubercle is a landmark on the inferior surface, not a common fracture site.
The subclavian groove is on the inferior surface and is not the weakest point.
citation: The fracture usually occurs as a result of a fall on the shoulder or outstretched hand. The force is transmitted along the clavicle, which breaks at its weakest point, the junction of the middle and outer thirds.

statement: A patient presents with a displaced fracture of the right clavicle. Physical examination reveals that the medial fragment of the bone is significantly elevated. Which muscle is primarily responsible for this upward tilting?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis major pulls the lateral fragment medially and forward.
Trapezius attaches to the lateral third and does not elevate the medial fragment.
The sternocleidomastoid muscle attaches to the medial third and tilts it upward after a fracture.
Subclavius is a small muscle that depresses the clavicle.
Deltoid attaches to the lateral third and would pull the lateral fragment.
citation: The medial end is tilted upward by the sternocleidomastoid muscle.

statement: Following a mid-shaft clavicular fracture, the lateral fragment is observed to be depressed and shifted toward the midline. Which muscle is the main driver of this medial and forward displacement?
optionExplanations: Latissimus dorsi is an adductor but does not primarily displace the lateral clavicular fragment.
The pectoralis major is a strong adductor that pulls the lateral fragment medially and forward.
Serratus anterior acts on the scapula, not directly on the clavicular fragment displacement.
Teres major is an adductor but is less significant than the pectoralis major in this displacement.
Coracobrachialis is a weak adductor and flexor.
citation: is pulled medially and forward by the strong adductor muscles of the shoulder joint, especially the pectoralis major.

statement: Which of the following is a unique developmental characteristic of the clavicle compared to other bones in the human body?
options: It is the last bone to ossify
It is the first bone to begin ossification
It has no secondary ossification centers
correctOption: It is the first bone to begin ossification
optionExplanations: The clavicle undergoes both intramembranous and endochondral ossification.
It is not the last; it is among the earliest.
The clavicle is the first bone in the body to start the ossification process.
While it has unique features, its primary distinction is the timing of ossification.
It does have a secondary center at the sternal end.
