citation: It is the first bone to begin ossification.

statement: A 30-year-old woman undergoes a radical mastectomy. Post-operatively, she notices that the medial border of her scapula protrudes prominently when she pushes against a wall. This 'winged' appearance indicates damage to the nerve supplying which muscle?
optionExplanations: Trapezius paralysis causes a dropped shoulder, not classic winging.
Latissimus dorsi paralysis results in weak extension and adduction.
Paralysis of the serratus anterior (long thoracic nerve) causes the scapula to wing.
Rhomboid paralysis causes mild winging but is less common in this surgical context.
Levator scapulae elevates the scapula.
citation: winged scapula (Fig. 3.4), caused by paralysis of the serratus anterior.

statement: A patient presents to the clinic after a neck biopsy with a noticeably lower shoulder on the affected side and difficulty shrugging. Which clinical sign is most likely present?
optionExplanations: Winged scapula is associated with the serratus anterior.
Ape hand is a median nerve injury sign.
A dropped shoulder is a classic sign of trapezius paralysis, often due to spinal accessory nerve injury.
Wrist drop is a radial nerve injury sign.
Claw hand is an ulnar nerve injury sign.
citation: dropped shoulder, which occurs with paralysis of the trapezius

statement: In the anatomical position, what is the normal vertebral level/rib span of the scapula on the posterior thoracic wall?
optionExplanations: The scapula starts lower than the first rib.
The scapula normally spans the area between the second and seventh ribs.
The eighth rib is usually below the inferior angle.
The seventh rib is the standard inferior landmark.
The fourth rib is too low for the superior angle.
citation: lies on the posterior chest wall between the second and seventh ribs.

statement: A patient has a calcified superior transverse scapular ligament. Which structure passing immediately beneath this ligament is most likely to be compressed, leading to wasting of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles?
optionExplanations: The suprascapular artery passes superior to the ligament ('Army over the bridge').
The suprascapular nerve passes inferior to the ligament ('Navy under the bridge').
The dorsal scapular nerve supplies the rhomboids and is medial.
The axillary nerve passes through the quadrangular space.
The subscapular artery is a branch of the axillary artery in the axilla.
citation: the suprascapular nerve passes inferior to it

statement: A young man is born with a congenital absence of the lower part of his chest muscle. He notices he has significant difficulty when trying to pull his arm toward his body or rotate it inward. Which specific part of the muscle is likely missing?
optionExplanations: The clavicular head is less commonly absent than the sternocostal part.
The sternocostal origin is the most commonly missing part of the pectoralis major.
Pectoralis minor is a separate muscle and its absence is not the primary cause of these symptoms.
Subclavius is a small muscle with minimal effect on adduction.
Serratus anterior absence causes winging, not primary adduction weakness.
citation: The sternocostal origin is the most commonly missing part, and this causes weakness in adduction and medial rotation of the shoulder joint.

statement: Which nerve provides the primary motor supply to the trapezius muscle and can be found on its deep surface?
optionExplanations: Dorsal scapular nerve supplies the rhomboids.
Thoracodorsal nerve supplies the latissimus dorsi.
The spinal accessory nerve (CN XI) supplies the trapezius.
Long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior.
Suprascapular nerve supplies supraspinatus and infraspinatus.
citation: The accessory nerve runs along the deep aspect of the belly of the trapezius muscle

statement: A rock climber is using his upper limbs to pull his trunk upward. Which muscle of the back is primarily responsible for the powerful extension and adduction of the arm required for this movement?
optionExplanations: Trapezius acts on the scapula, not primarily as an arm adductor.
Latissimus dorsi is a powerful extender, adductor, and medial rotator of the arm.
Rhomboid major retracts the scapula.
Levator scapulae elevates the scapula.
Pectoralis minor stabilizes the scapula.
citation: Extends, adducts, and medially rotates arm

statement: What is the nerve supply to the Rhomboid major and minor muscles?
optionExplanations: Long thoracic nerve is for serratus anterior.
Thoracodorsal nerve is for latissimus dorsi.
The dorsal scapular nerve (C4, 5) supplies both rhomboid muscles.
Accessory nerve is for trapezius.
Axillary nerve is for deltoid and teres minor.
citation: Dorsal scapular nerve (C4, 5)

statement: During a surgical dissection of the axilla, the surgeon identifies a thin triangular muscle arising from the 3rd, 4th, and 5th ribs. To which bony landmark does this muscle insert?
optionExplanations: The acromion is the insertion for the trapezius and origin for the deltoid.
The spine of the scapula is an attachment for trapezius and deltoid.
The pectoralis minor inserts into the coracoid process of the scapula.
The greater tubercle is for rotator cuff muscles.
The lesser tubercle is for the subscapularis.
citation: runs upward and laterally to insert into the coracoid process of the scapula.

statement: A patient has an infection in the infraclavicular region that has spread deep to the pectoralis major. Which of the following structures is most likely to be involved as it pierces the clavipectoral fascia?
optionExplanations: The medial pectoral nerve pierces the pectoralis minor, not the fascia alone.
The basilic vein pierces the deep fascia of the arm, not the clavipectoral fascia.
The lateral pectoral nerve is one of the structures that pierces the clavipectoral fascia.
The long thoracic nerve descends on the serratus anterior.
The axillary artery is deep to the fascia.
citation: The lateral pectoral nerve, cephalic vein, branches of the thoracoacromial artery, and lymphatic channels from the infraclavicular nodes pierce the clavipectoral fascia

statement: The inferior angle of the scapula is a vital surface landmark. At which vertebral level is it typically located?
optionExplanations: T2 corresponds to the superior angle.
T4 is roughly the level of the spine of the scapula.
The inferior angle marks the level of the T7 spine.
T9 is too low.
T12 is the level of the last rib.
citation: marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra.

statement: A patient is asked to shrug their shoulders against resistance to test the integrity of the 11th cranial nerve. Which part of the trapezius muscle is primarily being tested?
optionExplanations: Lower fibers depress the scapula.
Middle fibers retract the scapula.
The upper fibers of the trapezius are responsible for elevating the scapula (shrugging).
Horizontal fibers is another name for middle fibers.
The trapezius is a superficial muscle.
citation: Upper fibers elevate scapula

statement: A patient presents with weakness in swimming, specifically during the downward stroke. Examination shows atrophy of the latissimus dorsi. Which nerve is likely injured?
optionExplanations: Axillary nerve supplies the deltoid.
The thoracodorsal nerve (C6, 7, 8) supplies the latissimus dorsi.
Long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior.
Dorsal scapular nerve supplies the rhomboids.
Lower subscapular nerve supplies teres major and subscapularis.
citation: Thoracodorsal nerve (C6, 7, 8)

statement: During an axillary lymph node clearance, the surgeon must be careful not to damage the nerves supplying the pectoralis major to avoid post-operative weakness in arm adduction. Which nerves must be preserved?
optionExplanations: These supply serratus anterior and latissimus dorsi.
The pectoralis major is supplied by both the medial and lateral pectoral nerves.
These supply the rotator cuff and deltoid.
These supply the subscapularis and teres major.
These supply the rhomboids and trapezius.
citation: Medial and lateral pectoral nerves

statement: A 45-year-old man is involved in a high-speed car crash and sustains a comminuted fracture of the scapular body. Why do these fractures often require only conservative management rather than surgical fixation?
options: The bone has high regenerative capacity
The scapula is protected by the ribs
optionExplanations: Regeneration is not the reason for conservative management.
While true, it doesn't explain the lack of displacement.
The muscles on the anterior (subscapularis) and posterior (supraspinatus/infraspinatus) surfaces splint the fragments.
Ribs are often fractured alongside the scapula.
They can displace, but muscles usually hold them in place.
citation: Most fractures of the scapula require little direct treatment because the muscles on the anterior and posterior surfaces adequately splint the fragments.

statement: A clinician is trying to locate the coracoid process to perform a nerve block. Where should they apply deep pressure to palpate this structure?
options: Inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle
Posterior to the spine of the scapula
In the center of the axilla
correctOption: Inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle
optionExplanations: The acromion is the most lateral point.
The sternoclavicular joint is medial.
The coracoid process is palpated inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle through the deltoid.
The spine is posterior.
The axilla contains the head of the humerus, not the coracoid process.
citation: It can be palpated via deep pressure through the anterior part of the deltoid muscle, inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle.

statement: What is a characteristic feature of the sternoclavicular joint?
options: It is a simple fibrous joint
optionExplanations: It is a synovial joint.
All synovial joints have capsules.
The sternoclavicular joint contains a fibrocartilaginous articular disc.
It is a double-plane synovial joint.
It is supported by strong ligaments.
citation: It articulates with the clavicular notch of the sternum through a compound synovial joint containing an articular disc.

statement: A patient is asked to pull their shoulders backward as if trying to make their shoulder blades touch. Which muscle is the primary agonist for this retraction of the scapula?
optionExplanations: Serratus anterior protracts the scapula.
Pectoralis minor pulls the scapula forward and downward.
The rhomboids (major and minor) are the primary retractors of the scapula.
Levator scapulae elevates the scapula.
Subclavius depresses the clavicle.
citation: Retracts scapula

statement: A patient presents with difficulty performing a 'bear hug' movement or folding their arms across their chest. Which muscle's primary actions are likely impaired?
optionExplanations: Trapezius moves the scapula.
Pectoralis major adducts the arm and rotates it medially.
Latissimus dorsi adducts but also extends the arm.
Supraspinatus abducts the arm.
Teres minor rotates the arm laterally.
citation: Adducts arm and rotates it medially

statement: A medical student is identifying landmarks on a 3D model of the scapula. Which set of structures represents the three large bony processes of this bone?
optionExplanations: These are borders, not processes.
These are angles, not processes.
The spine, acromion, and coracoid are the three large bony processes of the scapula.
These are surfaces.
These are fossae (depressions).
citation: three large bony processes (spine, acromion, coracoid)

statement: Six weeks after a clavicular fracture, a patient complains of persistent, sharp pain over the side of the neck and the point of the shoulder. What is the most likely anatomical explanation for this late-onset pain?
optionExplanations: Non-union causes instability and localized pain, not radiating neck pain.
Brachial plexus compression usually causes symptoms further down the arm.
Callus formation can involve the supraclavicular nerves, causing persistent pain in the neck and shoulder.
This would present with vascular deficits.
Muscle spasm is common but doesn't explain the nerve-like distribution of pain.
citation: The close relationship of the supraclavicular nerves to the clavicle may result in their involvement in callus formation after fracture of the bone.

statement: A 22-year-old athlete presents with localized pain in the shoulder after a collision. The physician attempts to palpate a beak-like bony projection by applying deep pressure through the anterior deltoid, just below the lateral clavicle. Which structure is being assessed?
optionExplanations: The acromion is the lateral extension of the spine, not assessed through the deltoid.
The coracoid process is located inferior to the lateral clavicle and requires deep pressure through the deltoid to palpate.
The greater tubercle is more lateral and covered by the deltoid but is not beak-like.
The lesser tubercle is on the anterior humerus, not the scapula.
The conoid tubercle is on the inferior surface of the clavicle itself.
citation: It can be palpated via deep pressure through the anterior part of the deltoid muscle, inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle.

statement: Which nerve is primarily responsible for the motor supply to the clavicular head of the pectoralis major muscle?
optionExplanations: The medial pectoral nerve primarily supplies the sternocostal head and pectoralis minor.
The lateral pectoral nerve is the primary supply for the clavicular head of the pectoralis major.
The long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior.
The thoracodorsal nerve supplies the latissimus dorsi.
The dorsal scapular nerve supplies the rhomboids.
citation: lateral pectoral nerve supplies the pectoralis major muscle, primarily its clavicular head

statement: During a surgical exploration of the suprascapular notch, a surgeon identifies a vascular structure passing over the superior transverse scapular ligament. Based on the standard anatomical mnemonic, which structure is this?
optionExplanations: The nerve passes inferior to the ligament ('Navy goes under the bridge').
The suprascapular artery passes superior to the ligament ('Army goes over the bridge').
The circumflex scapular artery passes around the axillary border of the scapula.
The dorsal scapular nerve is medial to the notch.
The thoracodorsal artery is in the axilla.
citation: suprascapular artery passes superior to this ligament

statement: A 30-year-old rock climber complains of difficulty pulling his body upward. Physical examination reveals weakness in extension, adduction, and medial rotation of the humerus. Which muscle is most likely affected?
optionExplanations: The trapezius acts on the scapula, not the humerus.
The latissimus dorsi is a powerful extensor, adductor, and medial rotator of the arm, essential for climbing.
The rhomboid major retracts the scapula.
The pectoralis minor depresses the shoulder.
The levator scapulae elevates the scapula.
citation: Extends, adducts, and medially rotates arm

statement: A patient is unable to raise their arm above the horizontal level despite an intact deltoid muscle. The physician notes a failure of the scapula to rotate upward. Which muscle's upper and lower fibers must work together to achieve this rotation?
optionExplanations: The latissimus dorsi adducts and medially rotates the arm.
The rhomboid minor retracts the scapula.
The trapezius uses its upper and lower fibers in tandem to rotate the scapula, allowing abduction above 90 degrees.
The pectoralis major is an adductor.
The subclavius depresses the clavicle.
citation: Upper and lower fibers together rotate the scapula

statement: The blunt, thickened proximal end of the clavicle forms a joint with which specific part of the axial skeleton?
optionExplanations: The acromion articulates with the lateral end.
The coracoid process does not articulate directly with the clavicle.
The sternal extremity articulates with the clavicular notch of the sternum.
The first costal cartilage is involved but the primary articulation is the notch.
The xiphoid process is the inferior part of the sternum.
citation: articulates with the clavicular notch of the sternum through a compound synovial joint

statement: What is the primary anatomical purpose of the fibrocartilage ring known as the glenoid labrum?
options: To provide attachment for the deltoid
To broaden and deepen the joint cavity
To act as a light strut
To connect the clavicle to the scapula
correctOption: To broaden and deepen the joint cavity
optionExplanations: The deltoid attaches to the acromion and humerus.
The glenoid labrum rims the glenoid cavity to increase its depth and breadth for the humeral head.
Synovial fluid is secreted by the synovial membrane.
The clavicle acts as a light strut.
The coracoclavicular ligament connects the clavicle to the scapula.
citation: serves to broaden and deepen the joint cavity

statement: A 15-year-old boy is noted to have a flattened chest wall on the right side. Functional testing shows significant weakness when attempting to medially rotate and adduct the arm. This clinical presentation is most consistent with the congenital absence of which muscle?
optionExplanations: Trapezius absence would affect scapular elevation/retraction.
Latissimus dorsi absence would affect extension and adduction but is not associated with chest wall flattening.
Congenital absence of the pectoralis major (often the sternocostal part) leads to weakness in adduction and medial rotation.
Serratus anterior absence causes winging of the scapula.
Rhomboid major absence affects scapular retraction.
citation: causes weakness in adduction and medial rotation of the shoulder joint

statement: A patient presents with a 'slumped' shoulder posture. The physician asks the patient to pull their shoulder blades together toward the midline. Which muscle is primarily being tested during this retraction maneuver?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis major protracts the shoulder girdle.
Serratus anterior is the primary protractor.
The rhomboid major (along with the minor and trapezius) is responsible for retracting the scapula.
Latissimus dorsi acts on the humerus.
Subclavius depresses the clavicle.
citation: Retracts scapula

statement: Where is the supraglenoid tubercle specifically located on the scapula?
options: At the apex of the glenoid cavity
correctOption: At the apex of the glenoid cavity
optionExplanations: The inferior angle is at the junction of the medial and lateral borders.
The supraglenoid tubercle is a small elevation at the apex of the glenoid cavity.
The costal surface contains the subscapular fossa.
The acromion is a process, not a tubercle of the glenoid.
The medial border is the vertebral border.
citation: located at the apex of the glenoid cavity

statement: A patient develops a deep neck infection. The physician is concerned that the infection may spread into the axilla by following the path of the axillary sheath. This spread is possible because the sheath is a continuation of which fascia?
optionExplanations: Investing fascia surrounds the neck but does not form the axillary sheath.
Pretracheal fascia is anterior to the trachea.
The axillary sheath is a direct continuation of the prevertebral fascia from the neck into the axilla.
Pectoral fascia covers the pectoralis major.
Clavipectoral fascia is deep to the pectoralis major but does not originate as prevertebral fascia.
citation: sheath is continuous with the prevertebral fascia in the root of the neck

statement: Which muscle serves as the key anatomical landmark for dividing the axillary artery into three distinct descriptive parts?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis major is superficial to the artery but not the landmark for its divisions.
The pectoralis minor crosses the artery, defining the first (proximal), second (deep), and third (distal) parts.
Subclavius is superior to the artery.
Serratus anterior forms the medial wall of the axilla.
Teres major marks the end of the axillary artery.
citation: pectoralis minor muscle crosses in front of the axillary artery and divides it into three parts

statement: A patient has a cyst compressing the structures within the spinoglenoid (greater scapular) notch. Which clinical finding would most likely result from this compression?
options: Loss of sensation over the deltoid
Wasting of both supraspinatus and infraspinatus
optionExplanations: The nerve to the supraspinatus branches off before the notch.
The suprascapular nerve passes through the spinoglenoid notch *after* supplying the supraspinatus, so compression here only affects the infraspinatus.
Sensation over the deltoid is axillary nerve (C5-6).
Scapular retraction is rhomboids/trapezius.
Both would only be affected if the nerve was compressed at the suprascapular notch.
citation: allows the suprascapular nerve and vessels to pass between these fossae

statement: A patient undergoes surgery in the axilla, and the thoracodorsal nerve is accidentally injured. Which spinal cord segments contribute to this nerve?
optionExplanations: C3-5 is the phrenic nerve.
C5-6 contributes to the axillary and suprascapular nerves.
The thoracodorsal nerve is derived from the C6, C7, and C8 roots.
C8-T1 contributes to the ulnar nerve.
T1-3 are intercostal nerves.
citation: Thoracodorsal nerve (C6, 7, 8)

statement: During a physical exam, a physician asks a patient to 'square their shoulders' by pulling them backward. Which specific part of the trapezius is primarily responsible for this action?
optionExplanations: Upper fibers elevate the scapula.
Middle fibers are the primary retractors of the scapula.
Lower fibers depress the scapula.
The fibers have distinct actions based on their orientation.
The trapezius does not have a 'clavicular head' in the same sense as the pectoralis major.
citation: Middle fibers retract scapula

statement: What is the name of the large, shallow concavity found on the costal (anterior) surface of the scapula?
optionExplanations: Supraspinous fossa is on the dorsal surface.
Infraspinous fossa is on the dorsal surface.
Glenoid fossa is on the lateral angle.
The subscapular fossa occupies most of the costal surface.
There is no 'coracoid fossa' on the scapula.
citation: large part of this surface forms a shallow concavity, the subscapular fossa

statement: A 40-year-old man falls onto his outstretched hand. He presents with severe pain in the mid-clavicular region. Anatomically, why is the clavicle so frequently fractured in this mechanism of injury?
options: It is the only bone in the shoulder girdle.
It transmits all forces from the upper limb to the trunk.
It is protected by thick muscle layers.
It is the last bone to ossify.
correctOption: It transmits all forces from the upper limb to the trunk.
optionExplanations: The scapula is also part of the shoulder girdle.
The clavicle is the sole bony link between the limb and the axial skeleton, making it the primary transmitter of impact forces.
It is subcutaneous and poorly protected by muscle.
The text does not mention a lack of medullary cavity as a reason for fracture.
It is actually the first bone to begin ossification.
citation: transmits all forces from the upper limb to the trunk

statement: A patient with a nerve injury is unable to medially rotate their arm against resistance. Which muscle of the pectoral region is a major contributor to this specific movement?
optionExplanations: Trapezius rotates the scapula, not the arm.
Serratus anterior protracts the scapula.
The pectoralis major is a powerful adductor and medial rotator of the humerus.
Rhomboid major retracts the scapula.
Levator scapulae elevates the scapula.
citation: Adducts and medially rotates arm

statement: A patient presents with a scapula that sits further from the midline than normal (lateral displacement). The physician suspects a nerve injury. Which nerve, originating from the C5 root, is likely damaged?
optionExplanations: Long thoracic nerve (C5-7) supplies serratus anterior.
Suprascapular nerve (C5-6) supplies supra/infraspinatus.
The dorsal scapular nerve (C5) supplies the rhomboids, which retract the scapula.
Thoracodorsal nerve (C6-8) supplies latissimus dorsi.
Axillary nerve (C5-6) supplies deltoid.
citation: Dorsal scapular nerve (C5)

statement: A nurse is preparing to give an intramuscular injection into the deltoid muscle. She uses a bony landmark that forms the 'tip of the shoulder' to determine the injection site. Which structure is she palpating?
optionExplanations: The coracoid is anterior and deep.
The sternal end is at the midline.
The acromion is the lateral-most bony point and forms the tip of the shoulder.
The spine is posterior.
The greater tubercle is lateral but covered by the deltoid muscle.
citation: forms the easily palpable tip of the shoulder

statement: A medical student is practicing surface anatomy. He finds a bone that can be felt just under the skin from the sternum all the way to the shoulder. Which bone is this?
optionExplanations: The first rib is mostly deep to the clavicle.
The scapula is mostly covered by thick muscles.
The clavicle is subcutaneous and palpable along its entire length.
The humerus is surrounded by muscle.
The sternum is in the midline only.
citation: clavicle is subcutaneous and easily palpable along its entire length

statement: A patient has a complete lesion of the spinal accessory nerve. While they have lost motor function of the trapezius, they still feel pain when the muscle is pinched. Which nerves provide this remaining sensory innervation?
optionExplanations: The vagus nerve does not supply the trapezius.
The accessory nerve is purely motor to the trapezius; sensory fibers come from the C3 and C4 cervical nerves.
Long thoracic is motor to serratus anterior.
Phrenic is motor to the diaphragm.
Dorsal scapular is motor to the rhomboids.
citation: cervical nerves supply sensory innervation

statement: A surgeon is operating on the anterior aspect of the proximal humerus. He identifies a broad, flat tendon inserting into the floor of the intertubercular (bicipital) groove. This tendon belongs to which muscle?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis major inserts into the lateral lip of the groove.
Teres major inserts into the medial lip of the groove.
The latissimus dorsi inserts into the floor of the bicipital groove ('A lady between two majors').
Subscapularis inserts into the lesser tubercle.
Deltoid inserts into the deltoid tuberosity.
citation: Floor of bicipital groove of humerus

statement: Which part of the scapula is described as its thickest and most complex region?
optionExplanations: The superior angle is thin.
The inferior angle is thin.
The lateral angle contains the glenoid cavity and head, making it the thickest and most complex part.
The medial border is long and thin.
The superior border is the shortest and thinnest.
citation: thickest and most complex part of the scapula

statement: During a dissection of the pectoral region, a nerve is seen emerging through the substance of the pectoralis minor muscle to reach the deep surface of the pectoralis major. Which nerve is this?
optionExplanations: The lateral pectoral nerve usually passes superior to the pectoralis minor.
The medial pectoral nerve typically pierces the pectoralis minor to reach the pectoralis major.
The intercostobrachial nerve is sensory to the axilla.
The long thoracic nerve is on the surface of the serratus anterior.
The subclavius nerve is superior to the pectoral muscles.
citation: medial pectoral nerve... supplies and pierces the pectoralis minor

statement: A 25-year-old male presents with persistent pain over the side of his neck six weeks after sustaining a mid-shaft clavicular fracture. Radiographs confirm significant callus formation at the fracture site. Which of the following structures is most likely involved in the healing process?
optionExplanations: The supraclavicular nerves lie in close proximity to the clavicle and can be entrapped in the callus during fracture healing.
The phrenic nerve is located deeper in the neck and is not typically involved in clavicular callus.
The accessory nerve is located in the posterior triangle but is not directly related to the mid-shaft of the clavicle.
The long thoracic nerve descends behind the axillary vessels and is not involved in clavicular callus.
The dorsal scapular nerve supplies the rhomboids and is not related to the clavicle.
citation: close relationship of the supraclavicular nerves to the clavicle may result in their involvement in callus formation

statement: A 40-year-old victim of a high-speed motor vehicle accident is diagnosed with a comminuted scapular fracture. Despite the severity of the bone fragmentation, the orthopedic surgeon decides on conservative management. What anatomical factor justifies this decision?
optionExplanations: The extensive musculature on both the anterior and posterior surfaces of the scapula acts as a natural splint for bone fragments.
Callus formation is a result of healing, not the primary reason for conservative management in this context.
While the scapula is not weight-bearing, the primary reason for fragment stability is muscular splinting.
Proximity to the lungs would be a reason for concern, not a justification for conservative bone management.
Nerve entrapment is a potential complication, not a reason for conservative treatment.
citation: muscles on the anterior and posterior surfaces adequately splint the fragments

statement: During a physical examination, the inferior angle of the scapula is used as a surface landmark. Which rib level does this structure correspond to?
optionExplanations: The second rib corresponds to the superior angle of the scapula.
The third rib corresponds to the root of the scapular spine.
The fifth rib is between the superior and inferior angles.
The inferior angle of the scapula is a reliable landmark for the level of the seventh rib.
The ninth rib is inferior to the scapula.
citation: marks the level of the seventh rib

statement: A 19-year-old male is noted to have a flattened chest wall on the right side. Physical examination reveals a congenital absence of the sternocostal head of the pectoralis major. Which functional deficit is most likely to be observed?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis major is a powerful adductor and medial rotator; its absence leads to weakness in these movements.
Abduction is performed by the deltoid and supraspinatus, not the pectoralis major.
Lateral rotation is performed by the infraspinatus and teres minor.
Elbow flexion is primarily the function of the biceps and brachialis.
Winging of the scapula is caused by serratus anterior paralysis.
citation: causes weakness in adduction and medial rotation of the shoulder joint

statement: A patient undergoes a lymph node biopsy in the posterior triangle of the neck. Post-operatively, they present with a 'dropped shoulder' and an inability to shrug. At which anatomical location is the motor nerve to the affected muscle most vulnerable?
optionExplanations: The spinal accessory nerve (CN XI) runs along the deep aspect of the trapezius at the junction of its middle and lower thirds.
The superior border of the scapula is related to the suprascapular nerve.
The quadrangular space transmits the axillary nerve.
The deltopectoral triangle contains the cephalic vein and thoracoacromial artery.
The bicipital groove houses the tendon of the long head of the biceps.
citation: runs along the deep aspect of the belly of the trapezius muscle at the junction of its middle and lower thirds

statement: A clinician is palpating the posterior boundary of the axilla to assess for lymphadenopathy. Which muscle's tendon primarily contributes to the formation of this fold?
optionExplanations: The latissimus dorsi and teres major tendons form the posterior axillary fold.
The pectoralis major forms the anterior axillary fold.
The serratus anterior forms the medial wall of the axilla.
The subclavius is part of the anterior wall but does not form the fold.
The pectoralis minor is part of the anterior wall.
citation: posterior axillary fold (formed by the tendons of the latissimus dorsi and teres major muscles)

statement: What is the specific insertion site of the rhomboid minor muscle?
optionExplanations: The rhomboid minor inserts into the medial border of the scapula specifically at the level of the spine's root.
The rhomboid major inserts from the spine to the inferior angle.
The pectoralis minor and short head of biceps attach to the coracoid process.
The trapezius and deltoid attach to the acromion.
The supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and teres minor insert into the greater tuberosity.
citation: Medial border of scapula at root of spine

statement: During an axillary dissection for breast cancer staging, a surgeon uses a thin triangular muscle as a key landmark to identify the second part of the axillary artery. Which muscle is being used?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis minor is the standard anatomical landmark used to divide the axillary artery into three parts.
The pectoralis major is superficial to the pectoralis minor and does not define the artery's parts.
The subclavius is superior to the pectoralis minor.
The coracobrachialis forms part of the lateral wall of the axilla.
The teres major marks the termination of the axillary artery.
citation: pectoralis minor muscle crosses in front of the axillary artery and divides it into three parts

statement: A radiologist is reviewing an MRI of the sternoclavicular joint in a patient with suspected trauma. They note a fibrocartilaginous structure that divides the joint space into two distinct compartments. What is this structure?
optionExplanations: The sternoclavicular joint contains an articular disc that divides the joint into medial and lateral compartments.
The conoid ligament is part of the coracoclavicular ligament at the lateral end of the clavicle.
The costoclavicular ligament is an accessory ligament that anchors the medial clavicle to the first rib.
The interclavicular ligament connects the two clavicles across the suprasternal notch.
Radiate ligaments are associated with the ribs and sternum, not the SC joint.
citation: articulates with the clavicular notch of the sternum through a compound synovial joint containing an articular disc

statement: A patient presents with isolated atrophy of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles. A surgeon suspects compression at the superior transverse scapular ligament. What are the normal neurovascular relations at this site?
optionExplanations: The suprascapular nerve passes through the notch (inferior to the ligament), while the artery passes superior to it.
The artery normally passes superior to the ligament, not inferior.
The nerve passes inferior to the ligament, through the notch.
The nerve passes through the notch, not through the ligament fibers themselves.
The artery passes over the ligament, not through the notch.
citation: suprascapular artery passes superior to this ligament, whereas the suprascapular nerve passes inferior to it

statement: What is the primary action of the levator scapulae muscle?
optionExplanations: As its name implies, the levator scapulae elevates the medial border of the scapula.
Depression is performed by the lower fibers of the trapezius and pectoralis minor.
Protraction is primarily the function of the serratus anterior.
Medial rotation of the arm is performed by muscles like the subscapularis and pectoralis major.
Abduction of the arm is performed by the deltoid and supraspinatus.
citation: Elevates scapula

statement: A patient sustains a deep laceration in the axilla that severs the medial pectoral nerve. Which portion of the pectoralis major muscle will show the most significant functional impairment and subsequent atrophy?
optionExplanations: The medial pectoral nerve primarily supplies the sternocostal head of the pectoralis major.
The clavicular head is primarily supplied by the lateral pectoral nerve.
The abdominal part is a minor component of the sternocostal head.
The insertion is a tendon, not a muscle head.
The origin from the clavicle corresponds to the clavicular head.
citation: supplies the pectoralis major muscle, primarily its sternocostal head

statement: A 12-year-old girl falls on her outstretched hand and sustains a fracture at the junction of the middle and outer thirds of the clavicle. On examination, the shoulder on the affected side is visibly lower than the other. What is the primary cause of this displacement?
optionExplanations: The lateral fragment of a fractured clavicle is depressed primarily by the gravity/weight of the upper limb.
The pectoralis major pulls the lateral fragment medially and forward, but not primarily downward.
The sternocleidomastoid tilts the medial fragment upward.
The trapezius is unable to support the weight of the arm once the clavicular strut is broken.
The subclavius is too small to cause significant displacement.
citation: lateral fragment is depressed by the weight of the arm

statement: A physical therapist is identifying bony landmarks to measure the range of motion of the glenohumeral joint. Which part of the scapula serves as the easily palpable tip of the shoulder?
optionExplanations: The acromion is the lateral extension of the scapular spine and forms the tip of the shoulder.
The coracoid process is palpable deep to the deltoid but is not the tip of the shoulder.
The scapular spine is a ridge on the posterior surface.
The glenoid cavity is deep within the joint and not palpable.
The infraglenoid tubercle is deep and serves as an attachment for the triceps.
citation: forms the easily palpable tip of the shoulder

statement: Which of the following sets of structures represents the insertion of the trapezius muscle?
optionExplanations: The trapezius inserts into the lateral third of the clavicle, the acromion, and the scapular spine.
The medial third of the clavicle is an origin for the pectoralis major and sternocleidomastoid.
The pectoralis minor inserts into the coracoid process.
The latissimus dorsi and pectoralis major insert into the bicipital groove.
The subscapularis inserts into the lesser tuberosity.
citation: Lateral third of clavicle, acromion, spine of scapula

statement: A patient is unable to perform a 'pull-up' exercise due to significant weakness in extending, adducting, and medially rotating the humerus. Which nerve, arising from the posterior cord of the brachial plexus, is likely affected?
optionExplanations: The thoracodorsal nerve (C6, 7, 8) supplies the latissimus dorsi, which is essential for the 'climbing' or 'pull-up' motion.
The long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior.
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid and teres minor.
The suprascapular nerve supplies the supraspinatus and infraspinatus.
The dorsal scapular nerve supplies the levator scapulae and rhomboids.
citation: supplies the latissimus dorsi muscle

statement: A surgeon is performing a radical mastectomy and needs to identify the origin of the pectoralis minor to ensure complete removal of the associated axillary lymph nodes. Where does this muscle originate?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis minor arises from the 3rd, 4th, and 5th ribs near their costal cartilages.
The first and second ribs are superior to the pectoralis minor origin.
The second rib is not typically part of the pectoralis minor origin.
The sixth to eighth ribs are inferior to the pectoralis minor origin.
The sternum and clavicle are origins for the pectoralis major.
citation: arises from the third, fourth, and fifth ribs

statement: A clinician is preparing to perform a thoracocentesis. They use the inferior angle of the scapula as a guide to identify the correct intercostal space. Which vertebral level does this landmark correspond to?
optionExplanations: The inferior angle of the scapula is at the level of the T7 spinous process.
The T3 spine corresponds to the root of the scapular spine.
The T1 spine is superior to the scapula.
The T12 spine is at the level of the last rib.
The L2 spine is in the lower back, far below the scapula.
citation: marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 12-year-old boy is brought to the ER after falling from his bicycle and landing directly on his right shoulder. He is holding his right arm with his left hand, and a deformity is noted in the middle of his collarbone. What is the most likely mechanism of this injury?
optionExplanations: The sternum is medial to the clavicle and not the typical site of force transmission for this fracture.
According to the text, clavicle fractures usually occur due to a fall on the shoulder or an outstretched hand.
Neck hyperextension affects the cervical spine, not the clavicle strut.
Forced abduction is more likely to cause shoulder dislocation than a clavicle fracture.
Overhead lifting causes chronic stress but not acute mid-shaft fractures.
citation: The fracture usually occurs as a result of a fall on the shoulder or outstretched hand.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 25-year-old rugby player sustains a fracture at the junction of the middle and outer thirds of the clavicle. Physical examination reveals that the medial fragment of the bone is significantly elevated. Which muscle is primarily responsible for this upward displacement?
optionExplanations: The trapezius attaches to the lateral third and would not pull the medial fragment up.
The pectoralis major pulls the lateral fragment medially and forward, not the medial fragment up.
The text states the medial end is tilted upward by the sternocleidomastoid muscle after a fracture.
The subclavius is a weak depressor and would not cause upward tilting.
The deltoid attaches laterally and contributes to the depression of the lateral fragment.
citation: The medial end is tilted upward by the sternocleidomastoid muscle.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Following a mid-shaft clavicular fracture, a patient presents with a shoulder that appears lower than the unaffected side. The lateral fragment is displaced medially and anteriorly. Which muscle is the main driver of this medial and forward pull?
optionExplanations: While an adductor, it is not cited as the primary muscle pulling the fragment forward.
The text specifies that the lateral fragment is pulled medially and forward by the adductors, especially the pectoralis major.
The serratus anterior acts on the scapula, not the lateral fragment of the clavicle.
Teres major is an adductor but is not the primary force described in this displacement.
Coracobrachialis is a weak adductor compared to the pectoralis major.
citation: the lateral fragment is depressed by the weight of the arm and is pulled medially and forward by the strong adductor muscles of the shoulder joint, especially the pectoralis major.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Which of the following statements is a unique developmental feature of the clavicle?
options: It is the last bone to ossify
It is the first bone to begin ossification
correctOption: It is the first bone to begin ossification
optionExplanations: The clavicle is the first, not the last, to begin the process.
The text does not discuss the type of ossification, only the timing.
The text explicitly states the clavicle is the first bone to begin ossification.
The text does not mention the absence of a medullary cavity.
Ossification begins in utero, as it is the first bone to start.
citation: It is the first bone to begin ossification.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: What is the primary functional role of the clavicle regarding force transmission?
options: Sole link for force transmission to trunk
Acts as a secondary origin for biceps
Prevents medial rotation of the humerus
correctOption: Sole link for force transmission to trunk
optionExplanations: Protection is a secondary benefit, not its primary force-transmission role.
The text identifies the clavicle as the sole link that transmits all forces from the upper limb to the trunk.
The biceps does not originate from the clavicle.
The glenoid labrum deepens the glenoid cavity, not the clavicle.
The clavicle acts as a strut for mobility, not a block for rotation.
citation: It is the sole link between the upper limb and the axial skeleton, and so transmits all forces from the upper limb to the trunk.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient returns to the clinic six weeks after a clavicle fracture. While the bone is healing well with visible callus formation on X-ray, the patient complains of persistent, sharp pain over the side of the neck. What is the most likely anatomical cause?
optionExplanations: Phrenic nerve pain is usually referred to the shoulder, not caused by clavicle callus.
The accessory nerve is deeper and more posterior in the neck.
The text notes that supraclavicular nerves can be involved in callus formation, causing persistent neck pain.
Muscle tears present acutely, not typically as persistent pain during callus formation.
Artery compression would cause vascular symptoms like pallor or pulselessness.
citation: The close relationship of the supraclavicular nerves to the clavicle may result in their involvement in callus formation after fracture of the bone.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 40-year-old tailor complains of numbness and tingling in his right hand that worsens when he carries a heavy bag over his shoulder. An anatomical narrowing is suspected. Between which two structures is the neurovascular bundle most likely being compressed?
optionExplanations: The coracoid process is lateral to the main site of thoracic outlet compression.
The text states that narrowing of the interval between the clavicle and the first rib can compress nerves and vessels.
This describes the subacromial space, related to rotator cuff impingement.
The neurovascular bundle passes over the first rib, not between the first and second.
The scapulothoracic interface is a muscular plane, not a site for major bundle compression.
citation: The interval between the clavicle and the first rib in some patients may become narrowed and thus is responsible for compression of nerves and blood vessels.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Between which rib levels is the scapula normally positioned on the posterior chest wall?
optionExplanations: The scapula starts at the second rib, not the first.
The text explicitly states the scapula lies between the second and seventh ribs.
The eighth rib is too low for the normal resting position of the scapula.
The scapula extends down to the seventh rib, not the sixth.
The first rib is superior to the superior border of the scapula.
citation: the scapula (scapul- is Latin for “shoulder blade”) is a large, flat, triangular bone that lies on the posterior chest wall between the second and seventh ribs.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: During a surgical dissection of the superior border of the scapula, a surgeon identifies the superior transverse scapular ligament. To avoid injuring the suprascapular nerve, where should the surgeon look for it in relation to this ligament?
optionExplanations: The artery passes superior to the ligament (Army over the bridge).
The text states the suprascapular nerve passes inferior to the ligament (Navy under the bridge).
The nerve passes through the notch, which is at the base of the coracoid, not lateral to it.
The notch is lateral to the superior angle.
The nerve reaches the posterior aspect only after passing through the notch.
citation: normally, the suprascapular artery passes superior to this ligament, whereas the suprascapular nerve passes inferior to it

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A clinician is performing a physical exam and uses the inferior angle of the scapula as a landmark. This landmark corresponds to which vertebral level?
optionExplanations: T5 is too high; it corresponds more closely to the root of the scapular spine.
The text states the inferior angle marks the level of the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra (T7).
T9 is too low for the resting inferior angle.
C7 is the vertebra prominens at the base of the neck.
L1 is in the lumbar region, far below the scapula.
citation: The inferior angle of the scapula can be palpated easily in the living subject and marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: What is the primary anatomical purpose of the glenoid labrum?
options: Acts as a hinge for the humerus
Connects the scapula to the clavicle
optionExplanations: Synovial fluid is secreted by the synovial membrane, not the fibrocartilage labrum.
The text states the glenoid labrum serves to broaden and deepen the joint cavity.
The deltoid attaches to the acromion and spine, not the labrum.
The shoulder is a ball-and-socket joint, not a hinge.
The acromioclavicular ligaments connect the scapula to the clavicle.
citation: A fibrocartilage ring (glenoid labrum) rims the margin of the glenoid cavity and serves to broaden and deepen the joint cavity.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient presents with isolated wasting of the infraspinatus muscle, but the supraspinatus muscle is normal. A cyst is suspected to be compressing the suprascapular nerve at a specific bony landmark. Where is this compression most likely occurring?
optionExplanations: Compression at the scapular notch would affect both supraspinatus and infraspinatus.
The spinoglenoid notch connects the two fossae; compression here affects the nerve after it has supplied the supraspinatus.
The infraglenoid tubercle is an attachment for the triceps, not a nerve passageway.
The subscapular fossa is on the anterior surface and contains the subscapularis.
The AC joint is superior and not a passageway for the suprascapular nerve.
citation: The lateral border of the spine blends into the neck of the scapula and forms a notchlike passageway (spinoglenoid, or greater scapular, notch) that connects the supraspinous fossa with the infraspinous fossa.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: To palpate the coracoid process of the scapula, where should a student apply deep pressure?
options: Anterior deltoid, inferior to lateral clavicle
correctOption: Anterior deltoid, inferior to lateral clavicle
optionExplanations: The acromion is the most lateral point; the coracoid is anterior.
The sternum is far medial to the scapular processes.
The text states the coracoid process is palpated through the anterior deltoid, inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle.
The spine is on the posterior surface; the coracoid is anterior.
The axillary floor allows palpation of the humeral head, not the coracoid.
citation: It can be palpated via deep pressure through the anterior part of the deltoid muscle, inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 30-year-old man is involved in a high-speed motor vehicle accident and sustains a comminuted fracture of the scapular body. Why is surgery often unnecessary for this specific bone fracture?
options: The bone has no blood supply
Surrounding muscles act as natural splints
The scapula heals faster than other bones
The bone is made of elastic cartilage
correctOption: Surrounding muscles act as natural splints
optionExplanations: The scapula has a rich blood supply.
While not weight-bearing in the traditional sense, this is not the reason it doesn't require surgery.
The text explains that muscles on both surfaces adequately splint the fragments, requiring little direct treatment.
Healing time is standard; the splinting effect is the key factor.
The scapula is a bone, not cartilage.
citation: Most fractures of the scapula require little direct treatment because the muscles on the anterior and posterior surfaces adequately splint the fragments.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: During a radical mastectomy, the long thoracic nerve is accidentally severed. Post-operatively, the patient is unable to keep the medial border of the scapula against the chest wall when pushing against a wall. What is this clinical sign called?
optionExplanations: Dropped shoulder is caused by trapezius paralysis.
Ape hand involves the median nerve and the thumb.
The text explicitly links winged scapula to paralysis of the serratus anterior.
Claw hand involves the ulnar nerve.
Wrist drop involves the radial nerve.
citation: winged scapula (Fig. 3.4), caused by paralysis of the serratus anterior.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient presents with a visible sagging of the right shoulder and an inability to shrug against resistance. Which muscle is likely paralyzed?
optionExplanations: Serratus anterior paralysis causes winging, not a dropped shoulder.
Levator scapulae elevates the scapula but its isolated loss doesn't typically cause the 'dropped shoulder' deformity described.
The text states that a dropped shoulder occurs with paralysis of the trapezius.
Rhomboids retract the scapula.
Latissimus dorsi depresses the shoulder but its loss doesn't cause sagging at rest.
citation: dropped shoulder, which occurs with paralysis of the trapezius

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A young man is born with a congenital absence of the sternocostal head of the pectoralis major. Which pair of movements at the shoulder joint will be most significantly weakened?
optionExplanations: These are actions of the deltoid and rotator cuff.
While pectoralis major flexes, the specific weakness mentioned in the text for the sternocostal part is different.
The text states that absence of the sternocostal origin causes weakness in adduction and medial rotation.
Protraction is primarily serratus anterior.
Elevation and depression involve the trapezius and levator scapulae.
citation: The sternocostal origin is the most commonly missing part, and this causes weakness in adduction and medial rotation of the shoulder joint.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Where does the pectoralis minor muscle insert?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis major inserts on the humerus, not the minor.
The deltoid and trapezius attach to the acromion.
The text states the pectoralis minor inserts into the coracoid process.
The serratus anterior and rhomboids insert on the medial border.
The subclavius inserts on the clavicle.
citation: It arises from the third, fourth, and fifth ribs and runs upward and laterally to insert into the coracoid process of the scapula.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient can abduct his arm to 90 degrees using the deltoid, but cannot raise it any further toward his head. Which muscle is failing to rotate the scapula to allow this continued movement?
optionExplanations: Supraspinatus initiates the first 15 degrees of abduction.
The text states that paralysis of the serratus anterior results in the inability to rotate the scapula for abduction above a right angle.
Pectoralis minor depresses the scapula.
Subclavius depresses the clavicle.
Teres major adducts and medially rotates the humerus.
citation: Paralysis of the serratus anterior results in the inability to rotate the scapula during abduction of the arm above a right angle.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A baseball pitcher undergoes an MRI that shows a tear in the rotator cuff. What is the primary functional consequence of this injury regarding the glenohumeral joint?
options: Loss of all sensation in the arm
Decreased stability of the humeral head
correctOption: Decreased stability of the humeral head
optionExplanations: Sensation is mediated by nerves, not the rotator cuff tendons.
Elbow flexion is performed by the biceps and brachialis.
The text states the rotator cuff tone assists in holding the humeral head in the glenoid cavity during movements.
While related, a rotator cuff tear does not inherently cause a biceps rupture.
The scapula remains mobile; the glenohumeral joint becomes unstable.
citation: The tone of these muscles assists in holding the head of the humerus in the glenoid cavity of the scapula during movements at the shoulder joint.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 50-year-old painter complains of shoulder pain only when he raises his arm between 60 and 120 degrees of abduction. Outside of this range, he has no pain. What is the most likely diagnosis?
optionExplanations: Adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder) causes pain throughout the entire range of motion.
The text describes a 'painful arc' in the middle range of abduction as characteristic of supraspinatus tendinitis due to impingement on the acromion.
Teres major pain would occur during adduction or medial rotation.
Serratus anterior paralysis causes winging and inability to abduct above 90 degrees, but not specifically a middle-range painful arc.
A fracture would cause constant pain and deformity.
citation: It is characterized by the presence of a spasm of pain in the middle range of abduction, when the diseased area impinges on the acromion.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient is unable to start raising his arm out to the side from a resting position. However, once the examiner lifts the arm to about 20 degrees, the patient can continue the movement himself. Which muscle's tendon is likely ruptured?
optionExplanations: The deltoid takes over after the first 15 degrees; if it were ruptured, the patient couldn't complete the movement.
Infraspinatus is a lateral rotator.
The text states that a patient with a ruptured supraspinatus cannot initiate abduction but can continue if passively assisted past 15 degrees.
Subscapularis is a medial rotator.
Teres minor is a lateral rotator.
citation: The patient with a ruptured supraspinatus tendon is unable to initiate abduction of the arm. However, if the arm is passively assisted for the first 15° of abduction, the deltoid can then take over

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient is asked to pull his shoulders back (scapular retraction) to stand at attention. Which part of the trapezius muscle is primarily responsible for this medial pull of the scapulae?
optionExplanations: Upper fibers elevate the scapula.
The text (Table 3.4) specifies that the middle fibers of the trapezius pull the scapula medially.
Lower fibers pull the medial border downward.
The fibers have distinct functional roles based on their orientation.
The middle fibers are the primary retractors.
citation: Upper fibers elevate the scapula; middle fibers pull scapula medially; lower fibers pull medial border of scapula downward

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A rock climber uses a specific muscle to pull his trunk upward toward his fixed arms. This muscle also extends, adducts, and medially rotates the humerus. Which muscle is it?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis minor acts on the scapula, not the humerus.
The text (Table 3.4) lists extension, adduction, and medial rotation of the arm as the actions of the latissimus dorsi.
Levator scapulae elevates the scapula.
Rhomboids retract the scapula.
Subclavius depresses the clavicle.
citation: Extends, adducts, and medially rotates the arm

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Which nerve provides the motor supply to the rhomboid major and rhomboid minor muscles?
optionExplanations: Long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior.
Thoracodorsal nerve supplies the latissimus dorsi.
The text (Table 3.4) identifies the dorsal scapular nerve as the supply for the rhomboids.
Suprascapular nerve supplies supraspinatus and infraspinatus.
Axillary nerve supplies deltoid and teres minor.
citation: Dorsal scapular nerve C4, 5

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 22-year-old rugby player is brought to the ER after a heavy fall on his shoulder. Radiographs reveal a fracture at the junction of the middle and outer thirds of the clavicle. On physical examination, the medial fragment of the bone is noticeably elevated. Which muscle is primarily responsible for this upward tilt?
optionExplanations: The trapezius attaches to the lateral third and does not tilt the medial fragment upward.
The pectoralis major pulls the lateral fragment medially and forward.
The sternocleidomastoid muscle attaches to the medial third of the clavicle and pulls it superiorly when the bone is fractured.
The subclavius is a weak depressor and would not cause upward tilting.
The deltoid attaches to the lateral third and acts on the lateral fragment.
citation: The medial end is tilted upward by the sternocleidomastoid muscle.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Which of the following statements is a unique developmental feature of the clavicle?
options: It is the last bone to ossify
It is the first bone to begin ossification
correctOption: It is the first bone to begin ossification
optionExplanations: The clavicle is not the last bone to ossify.
The clavicle is embryologically unique as the first bone in the human body to start the ossification process.
The clavicle undergoes intramembranous ossification primarily.
The clavicle does contain a marrow cavity.
The clavicle has a secondary ossification center at the sternal end.
citation: It is the first bone to begin ossification.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 30-year-old patient presents with persistent, nagging pain over the side of the neck six weeks after a midshaft clavicular fracture. The fracture is healing well with significant callus formation visible on X-ray. Which of the following is the most likely anatomical cause of this specific pain?
optionExplanations: The phrenic nerve is deeper in the neck and not typically involved in clavicular callus.
The supraclavicular nerves cross the clavicle subcutaneously and can become trapped in the healing callus, causing chronic neck pain.
The long thoracic nerve is located in the axilla and lateral chest wall.
The accessory nerve is in the posterior triangle but not directly related to midshaft clavicular callus.
The vagus nerve is located within the carotid sheath, far from the clavicle.
citation: The close relationship of the supraclavicular nerves to the clavicle may result in their involvement in callus formation after fracture of the bone.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Where is the conoid tubercle located on the clavicle?
optionExplanations: The superior surface is smooth and lacks the conoid tubercle.
The medial end features the impression for the costoclavicular ligament, not the conoid tubercle.
The superior surface is generally devoid of major tubercles.
The conoid tubercle is found on the inferior surface of the lateral (acromial) end of the clavicle.
The midshaft does not contain the conoid tubercle.
citation: The conoid tubercle (cono- is Greek for “pine cone”) is a small, roughened elevation on the inferior surface, near the acromial end.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 45-year-old factory worker complains of numbness and tingling in his right hand, especially when carrying heavy loads on his shoulder. The physician suspects a narrowing of the space between the collar bone and the first rib. Which structures are most likely being compressed in this interval?
options: Cephalic vein and lateral pectoral nerve
Phrenic nerve and internal thoracic artery
optionExplanations: The axillary nerve is located in the axilla, distal to this interval.
The costoclavicular space contains the brachial plexus and the subclavian artery and vein; narrowing here causes neurovascular symptoms.
The cephalic vein pierces the clavipectoral fascia lower down.
The long thoracic nerve passes behind the brachial plexus and is not the primary structure in this specific interval.
The phrenic nerve is medial to this specific compression point.
citation: The interval between the clavicle and the first rib in some patients may become narrowed and thus is responsible for compression of nerves and blood vessels.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: The inferior angle of the scapula serves as a surface landmark for which vertebral level?
optionExplanations: T2 corresponds roughly to the superior angle.
T3 corresponds to the root of the scapular spine.
T5 is between the spine and the inferior angle.
The inferior angle is a standard landmark for the 7th thoracic vertebra (T7) and the 7th rib.
T12 is much lower, near the end of the rib cage.
citation: The inferior angle of the scapula can be palpated easily in the living subject and marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: During a surgical dissection of the superior border of the scapula, a surgeon identifies the superior transverse scapular ligament bridging the scapular notch. To avoid nerve damage, which structure must the surgeon identify passing directly through the notch, beneath the ligament?
optionExplanations: The suprascapular artery passes over (superior to) the ligament.
The suprascapular nerve passes under (inferior to) the ligament through the notch.
The dorsal scapular nerve runs along the medial border of the scapula.
The subscapular artery arises from the third part of the axillary artery.
The circumflex scapular artery is a branch of the subscapular artery.
citation: Normally, the suprascapular artery passes superior to this ligament, whereas the suprascapular nerve passes inferior to it

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Which process of the scapula is described as resembling a crow's beak?
optionExplanations: The acromion means 'point of the shoulder'.
The spine is a triangular ridge.
The coracoid process is named for its resemblance to a crow's beak (coraco-).
There is no 'glenoid process'; it is the glenoid cavity.
The styloid process is found on the radius and ulna, not the scapula.
citation: The coracoid process (coraco- is Greek for “like a crow’s beak”) is a thick, beaklike structure that projects anterolaterally from the junction of the neck and lateral end of the superior border of the scapula.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 25-year-old woman undergoes a radical mastectomy. Post-operatively, she notices that the medial border of her right scapula protrudes significantly when she pushes against a wall. Which muscle's paralysis is responsible for this 'winged' appearance?
optionExplanations: Trapezius paralysis causes a 'dropped shoulder', not classic winging.
Latissimus dorsi paralysis affects arm extension and adduction.
Rhomboid paralysis causes mild winging but is less common and less severe than serratus anterior loss.
The serratus anterior holds the medial border of the scapula against the chest wall; its paralysis causes the scapula to wing.
Pectoralis minor acts on the coracoid process and does not prevent winging.
citation: winged scapula (Fig. 3.4), caused by paralysis of the serratus anterior.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A victim of a high-speed motor vehicle accident is diagnosed with a comminuted fracture of the scapular body. Despite the severity of the trauma, the orthopedic surgeon decides on conservative management rather than surgery. What anatomical factor justifies this decision?
options: The bone has no blood supply
The bone is made of fibrocartilage
optionExplanations: The scapula has a rich blood supply.
While not weight-bearing in the traditional sense, it is vital for limb function.
The extensive muscle attachments (subscapularis, supraspinatus, infraspinatus) act as natural splints for the bone fragments.
The scapula is a flat bone, not fibrocartilage.
Fragments do not always align spontaneously, but they are held in place by muscles.
citation: Most fractures of the scapula require little direct treatment because the muscles on the anterior and posterior surfaces adequately splint the fragments.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Which anatomical structure allows the suprascapular nerve to pass from the supraspinous fossa to the infraspinous fossa?
optionExplanations: The scapular notch is on the superior border, not between the fossae.
The spinoglenoid (greater scapular) notch connects the two posterior fossae.
The supraglenoid tubercle is for the long head of biceps.
The infraglenoid tubercle is for the long head of triceps.
There is no 'subscapular notch' in this context.
citation: The lateral border of the spine blends into the neck of the scapula and forms a notchlike passageway (spinoglenoid, or greater scapular, notch) that connects the supraspinous fossa with the infraspinous fossa.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 19-year-old male athlete is noted to have a visible asymmetry of his chest wall. Physical exam reveals the absence of the lower, larger portion of the pectoralis major muscle. Which of the following movements will be most significantly weakened in this patient?
optionExplanations: Abduction is performed by the deltoid and supraspinatus.
The pectoralis major is a powerful adductor and medial rotator; loss of its sternocostal head impairs these actions.
The pectoralis major flexes the arm, but it medially rotates, not laterally rotates.
The pectoralis major does not abduct.
Protraction is primarily serratus anterior; elevation is trapezius/levator scapulae.
citation: The sternocostal origin is the most commonly missing part, and this causes weakness in adduction and medial rotation of the shoulder joint.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: What is the primary function of the serratus anterior muscle regarding the scapula?
options: Holding medial border against chest wall
correctOption: Holding medial border against chest wall
optionExplanations: Retraction is done by the rhomboids and trapezius.
Elevation is done by levator scapulae and trapezius.
The serratus anterior is essential for keeping the scapula applied to the thoracic wall.
It performs lateral (upward) rotation, not medial rotation.
Depression is primarily pectoralis minor and lower trapezius.
citation: Serratus anterior: Rotates scapula for abduction of arm above right angle; holds medial border of scapula against chest wall

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 50-year-old professional tennis player presents with chronic shoulder pain that worsens during the 'serve' motion. An MRI confirms a tear in one of the rotator cuff tendons. Statistically, which muscle is most likely involved?
optionExplanations: Subscapularis tears are less common than supraspinatus tears.
Teres major is not part of the rotator cuff.
Infraspinatus is the second most common, but not the first.
The supraspinatus tendon is the most frequently injured component of the rotator cuff due to its position under the acromion.
Teres minor is rarely injured in isolation.
citation: The supraspinatus is the most commonly injured muscle in the rotator cuff.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 65-year-old man cannot lift his arm out to the side from a resting position. However, if the examiner lifts the arm to about 20 degrees, the patient can then continue the movement to 90 degrees using his own strength. Which muscle is ruptured?
optionExplanations: If the deltoid were ruptured, the patient could not complete the movement to 90 degrees.
Pectoralis major is an adductor.
The supraspinatus initiates the first 15-20 degrees of abduction; if it is torn, the patient cannot start the movement but can finish it once the deltoid has a mechanical advantage.
Serratus anterior rotates the scapula for abduction above 90 degrees.
Subscapularis is a medial rotator.
citation: The patient with a ruptured supraspinatus tendon is unable to initiate abduction of the arm. However, if the arm is passively assisted for the first 15° of abduction, the deltoid can then take over

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Which nerve supplies the teres major muscle?
optionExplanations: The axillary nerve supplies teres minor and deltoid.
The upper subscapular nerve supplies only the subscapularis.
The lower subscapular nerve supplies both the subscapularis and the teres major.
The thoracodorsal nerve supplies the latissimus dorsi.
The suprascapular nerve supplies supraspinatus and infraspinatus.
citation: Teres major: Lower subscapular nerve (C5, 6)

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: What is the insertion point of the pectoralis minor muscle?
optionExplanations: The greater tuberosity is for rotator cuff muscles.
The lesser tuberosity is for subscapularis.
The acromion is for deltoid and trapezius.
The pectoralis minor inserts into the coracoid process of the scapula.
The medial border is for rhomboids and serratus anterior.
citation: Pectoralis minor: Coracoid process of scapula

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient is asked to abduct his arm fully to 180 degrees. According to the principles of scapulohumeral rhythm, how many degrees of this total movement are contributed by the rotation of the scapula on the thoracic wall?
optionExplanations: 30 degrees would be a 5:1 ratio, which is incorrect.
The ratio is 2:1 (GH joint to Scapula). In 180 degrees, 120 degrees occur at the GH joint and 60 degrees occur via scapular rotation.
90 degrees would be a 1:1 ratio.
120 degrees is the contribution of the glenohumeral joint.
180 degrees is the total movement.
citation: For every 3° of abduction of the arm, a 2° abduction occurs in the shoulder joint and a 1° abduction occurs by rotation of the scapula.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: What is the primary action of the subclavius muscle?
optionExplanations: It originates from the first rib but its action is on the clavicle.
Serratus anterior protracts the scapula.
The subclavius pulls the clavicle inferiorly (depression) and steadies it.
It does not retract the clavicle.
It has no attachment to the humerus.
citation: Subclavius: Depresses clavicle and steadies this bone during movements of shoulder girdle

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 35-year-old man sustains a deep laceration to the posterior aspect of his shoulder, just below the glenohumeral joint. The surgeon is concerned about structures in the quadrangular space. Which nerve is at risk in this specific intermuscular space?
optionExplanations: The radial nerve passes through the triangular interval, lower down.
The suprascapular nerve passes through the scapular and spinoglenoid notches.
The axillary nerve, along with the posterior circumflex humeral artery, passes through the quadrangular space.
The thoracodorsal nerve is in the axilla.
The lower subscapular nerve is on the anterior surface of the subscapularis.
citation: the axillary nerve and the posterior circumflex humeral vessels emerge through this space to reach their terminal destinations in the shoulder.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient presents with a 'dropped shoulder' on the left side following a neck biopsy in the posterior triangle. He is unable to shrug his shoulder against resistance. Which muscle is most likely paralyzed?
optionExplanations: Levator scapulae elevates the scapula but its loss doesn't cause a classic 'dropped shoulder'.
Rhomboids retract the scapula.
Serratus anterior loss causes winging.
The trapezius is the main muscle maintaining the height of the shoulder; its paralysis (often via the accessory nerve) causes the shoulder to drop.
The deltoid abducts the arm.
citation: dropped shoulder, which occurs with paralysis of the trapezius

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Using the 'Army/Navy' mnemonic for the suprascapular notch, which structure represents the 'Navy' passing under the bridge (ligament)?
optionExplanations: The Artery (Army) goes over the bridge.
The Nerve (Navy) goes under the bridge (through the notch).
The vein usually follows the artery.
The subscapular artery is not related to this notch.
The subscapular nerve is in the axilla.
citation: the suprascapular nerve passes inferior to it (remember: the Army goes over the bridge; the Navy goes under the bridge).

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: When determining the side of a clavicle, how is the medial end identified?
optionExplanations: The flattened end is the lateral (acromial) end.
The medial (sternal) end is characterized by a blunt, thickened, quadrangular shape.
The conoid tubercle is on the lateral third.
The medial two-thirds are convex anteriorly.
The medial end articulates with the sternum.
citation: The sternal extremity (Fig. 3.1) is the blunt, thickened, proximal (medial) end of the clavicle.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: What is the primary action of the infraspinatus muscle?
optionExplanations: Medial rotation is done by subscapularis and teres major.
Abduction is done by supraspinatus and deltoid.
The infraspinatus is a powerful lateral rotator of the humerus.
Adduction is done by teres major and pectoralis major.
Flexion is done by anterior deltoid and pectoralis major.
citation: Infraspinatus: Lateral rotation of shoulder joint; helps stabilize shoulder joint

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: In a typical clavicular fracture, why is the lateral fragment pulled medially and forward?
optionExplanations: The deltoid helps depress the fragment but doesn't pull it medially.
The trapezius cannot support the fragment once the bone is broken.
The pectoralis major is a strong adductor that pulls the lateral fragment toward the midline.
The subclavius is too weak to cause this displacement.
Gravity causes the depression, but the medial/forward pull is muscular.
citation: the lateral fragment is depressed by the weight of the arm and is pulled medially and forward by the strong adductor muscles of the shoulder joint, especially the pectoralis major.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 19-year-old rugby player is brought to the ER after a heavy fall on his shoulder. Physical examination reveals a fracture at the junction of the middle and outer thirds of the clavicle. The medial fragment of the bone is noticeably displaced superiorly. Which of the following explains this specific displacement?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis major pulls the lateral fragment medially and forward, not the medial fragment upward.
The weight of the arm causes depression of the lateral fragment, not elevation of the medial one.
The sternocleidomastoid muscle is attached to the medial third of the clavicle and tilts the medial fragment upward after a fracture.
The subclavius muscle is too small to cause significant upward tilting of the medial fragment.
The trapezius attaches to the lateral third and does not elevate the medial fragment in this scenario.
citation: The medial end is tilted upward by the sternocleidomastoid muscle.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Which of the following is a unique developmental feature of the clavicle compared to other bones in the human body?
