options: It is the first bone to begin ossification
It is the last bone to begin ossification
correctOption: It is the first bone to begin ossification
optionExplanations: The clavicle actually begins ossification in membrane, not entirely in cartilage.
The clavicle is embryologically unique as the first bone in the body to start the ossification process.
While it has a thin medullary cavity, this is not its primary unique developmental feature.
Ossification begins early in fetal life, well before birth.
It is the first, not the last, to begin ossification.
citation: It is the first bone to begin ossification.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 32-year-old woman undergoes a radical mastectomy for breast cancer. Postoperatively, she notices that her right scapula protrudes abnormally from the posterior chest wall when she pushes against a wall. Which muscle is most likely paralyzed?
optionExplanations: Trapezius paralysis causes a dropped shoulder, not winging.
Latissimus dorsi paralysis affects adduction and extension but does not cause winging.
The serratus anterior holds the medial border of the scapula against the chest wall; its paralysis leads to a winged scapula.
Rhomboids retract the scapula but are not the primary cause of winging when paralyzed.
Pectoralis minor is an anterior muscle and does not maintain the scapula's posterior position.
citation: winged scapula (Fig. 3.4), caused by paralysis of the serratus anterior.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 65-year-old painter complains of severe shoulder pain and difficulty lifting his arm. On examination, he cannot lift his arm sideways from his body. However, if the examiner passively moves the arm to 20 degrees of abduction, the patient can then continue the movement using his deltoid. What is the most likely diagnosis?
optionExplanations: Axillary nerve injury would paralyze the deltoid, making the patient unable to continue abduction after 20 degrees.
Teres major is an adductor and medial rotator, not an abductor.
The supraspinatus initiates the first 15-20 degrees of abduction; if ruptured, the patient cannot start the movement but can finish it if assisted.
Serratus anterior paralysis affects rotation of the scapula for abduction above 90 degrees.
Subscapularis is a medial rotator and does not initiate abduction.
citation: The patient with a ruptured supraspinatus tendon is unable to initiate abduction of the arm.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient returns to the clinic six weeks after a midshaft clavicular fracture. While the bone is healing well, he complains of persistent, sharp pain over the side of his neck. Which of the following structures is most likely being compressed by the developing callus?
optionExplanations: The phrenic nerve is deeper in the neck and not typically involved in clavicular callus.
The supraclavicular nerves cross the clavicle subcutaneously and can be trapped in healing callus, causing neck pain.
The long thoracic nerve is located deeper in the axilla and medial chest wall.
The axillary nerve is related to the surgical neck of the humerus, not the clavicle.
Arterial compression would cause vascular symptoms (ischemia), not isolated sharp neck pain.
citation: The close relationship of the supraclavicular nerves to the clavicle may result in their involvement in callus formation after fracture of the bone.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: During a physical examination, a clinician uses the inferior angle of the scapula as a surface landmark. This structure typically corresponds to which anatomical level?
optionExplanations: The superior angle, not the inferior angle, is at the level of T2.
The third rib is too high for the inferior angle.
The fifth thoracic vertebra is superior to the inferior angle.
The inferior angle of the scapula is a standard landmark for the T7 spinous process and the 7th rib.
The ninth rib is inferior to the scapula's normal resting position.
citation: marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 25-year-old weightlifter presents with numbness in his right hand and a weakened radial pulse when his shoulder is abducted and externally rotated. Anatomically, where is the most likely site of neurovascular compression?
optionExplanations: The quadrangular space contains the axillary nerve, which does not affect the radial pulse.
Fractures here damage the axillary nerve, not the main neurovascular bundle to the hand.
The space between the clavicle and the first rib is a site for thoracic outlet syndrome, compressing the brachial plexus and subclavian vessels.
The bicipital groove houses the biceps tendon, not the main neurovascular bundle.
The spinoglenoid notch contains the suprascapular nerve, which is purely motor to infraspinatus and sensory to the joint.
citation: The interval between the clavicle and the first rib in some patients may become narrowed and thus is responsible for compression of nerves and blood vessels.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A young boy is noted to have a flattened chest wall on the left side. He has difficulty performing forceful adduction and medial rotation of his left arm. Which anatomical variation is most likely present?
options: Missing sternocostal head of pectoralis major
correctOption: Missing sternocostal head of pectoralis major
optionExplanations: Absent serratus anterior causes winging, not primary weakness in adduction.
Congenital absence of the sternocostal part of the pectoralis major is a known anomaly that weakens adduction and medial rotation.
Rhomboid paralysis affects scapular retraction, not primary arm adduction.
The subclavius is too small to affect the contour of the chest or arm rotation significantly.
The deltoid is an abductor; its absence would not primarily affect adduction.
citation: The sternocostal origin is the most commonly missing part, and this causes weakness in adduction and medial rotation of the shoulder joint.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Regarding the structures associated with the scapular notch and the superior transverse scapular ligament, which of the following is correct?
options: The nerve goes over the ligament
The artery goes under the ligament
The nerve passes inferior to the ligament
Both pass inferior to the ligament
Both pass superior to the ligament
correctOption: The nerve passes inferior to the ligament
optionExplanations: The nerve passes under (inferior to) the ligament, not over.
The artery passes over (superior to) the ligament.
The suprascapular nerve passes through the notch, which is inferior to the bridging ligament.
Only the nerve passes inferiorly; the artery passes superiorly.
Only the artery passes superiorly; the nerve passes inferiorly.
citation: the suprascapular artery passes superior to this ligament, whereas the suprascapular nerve passes inferior to it

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: In a typical midshaft clavicle fracture, the lateral fragment is displaced. Which muscle is primarily responsible for pulling this lateral fragment medially and forward?
optionExplanations: The SCM pulls the medial fragment upward, not the lateral fragment medially.
The trapezius is unable to resist the downward pull of gravity on the lateral fragment.
The pectoralis major is a strong adductor that pulls the lateral fragment of a fractured clavicle medially and forward.
The subclavius may pull the fragment slightly but is not the primary force.
The serratus anterior acts on the scapula, not directly on the clavicular fragments.
citation: is pulled medially and forward by the strong adductor muscles of the shoulder joint, especially the pectoralis major.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: What is the primary anatomical purpose of the glenoid labrum in the scapula?
options: To provide attachment for the deltoid
To broaden and deepen the joint cavity
correctOption: To broaden and deepen the joint cavity
optionExplanations: The deltoid attaches to the spine and acromion, not the labrum.
The glenoid labrum is a fibrocartilage ring that increases the surface area and depth of the shallow glenoid cavity.
Synovial fluid is secreted by the synovial membrane, not the fibrocartilaginous labrum.
The axillary nerve is related to the surgical neck of the humerus, not the labrum.
The labrum improves stability but does not specifically function to limit medial rotation.
citation: serves to broaden and deepen the joint cavity.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient presents with a 'dropped shoulder' on the right side following a deep laceration in the posterior triangle of the neck. Physical examination shows an inability to shrug the shoulder. Which muscle is affected?
optionExplanations: Serratus anterior paralysis causes winging, not a dropped shoulder.
Levator scapulae elevates the scapula but its isolated loss does not typically present as a 'dropped shoulder'.
The trapezius maintains the shoulder's height; its paralysis (often via the accessory nerve) results in a dropped shoulder.
Rhomboids retract the scapula; their loss does not cause the shoulder to drop significantly.
Latissimus dorsi is a depressor of the arm, not an elevator of the shoulder girdle.
citation: dropped shoulder, which occurs with paralysis of the trapezius

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 45-year-old man is involved in a high-speed motor vehicle accident and sustains a comminuted fracture of the body of the scapula. Why do these fractures usually require little direct surgical treatment?
options: The bone has no significant attachments
The fragments are splinted by surrounding muscles
The scapula has a very poor blood supply
The bone is not essential for arm movement
The fracture line always stays extra-articular
correctOption: The fragments are splinted by surrounding muscles
optionExplanations: The scapula has numerous important muscle attachments.
The extensive musculature on the costal (subscapularis) and dorsal (supraspinatus/infraspinatus) surfaces acts as a natural splint for body fractures.
The scapula has an excellent collateral blood supply.
The scapula is vital for the scapulohumeral rhythm and arm movement.
Scapular fractures can involve the glenoid (articular surface), though body fractures are more common.
citation: the muscles on the anterior and posterior surfaces adequately splint the fragments.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 50-year-old tennis player complains of shoulder pain. During examination, he experiences no pain when his arm is at his side or when it is fully elevated. However, he feels sharp pain when abducting the arm between 60 and 120 degrees. This 'painful arc' is most characteristic of which condition?
optionExplanations: Adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder) causes pain and restriction in all ranges of motion.
In supraspinatus tendinitis, pain occurs in the middle range of abduction as the inflamed tendon impinges on the acromion.
Teres major does not participate in abduction and would not cause a painful arc.
AC dislocation causes localized pain at the joint, usually at the end of abduction or during shrugging.
Subclavius hypertrophy is rare and does not cause an abduction painful arc.
citation: characterized by the presence of a spasm of pain in the middle range of abduction

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Which of the following bony processes of the scapula can be felt by applying deep pressure through the anterior fibers of the deltoid muscle, inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle?
optionExplanations: The acromion is subcutaneous and does not require pressure through the deltoid to be felt.
The spine is posterior and subcutaneous.
The coracoid process is located anteriorly and can be palpated through the deltoid in the deltopectoral region.
The infraglenoid tubercle is deep in the axilla and not easily palpable.
The superior angle is covered by the trapezius and is located more medially.
citation: It can be palpated via deep pressure through the anterior part of the deltoid muscle

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: What is the nerve supply to the serratus anterior muscle?
optionExplanations: The thoracodorsal nerve supplies the latissimus dorsi.
The dorsal scapular nerve supplies the rhomboids and levator scapulae.
The long thoracic nerve (C5-C7) specifically innervates the serratus anterior.
The suprascapular nerve supplies the supraspinatus and infraspinatus.
The lower subscapular nerve supplies the subscapularis and teres major.
citation: Long thoracic nerve (C5, 6, 7)

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A clinician is evaluating a patient's shoulder mobility. If the patient abducts his arm to a total of 90 degrees, how much of this movement is theoretically contributed by the rotation of the scapula on the thoracic wall?
optionExplanations: 15 degrees would be the contribution for 45 degrees of total abduction.
Based on the 2:1 ratio, for 90 degrees of total abduction, 60 degrees occur at the glenohumeral joint and 30 degrees occur via scapular rotation.
45 degrees would imply a 1:1 ratio, which is incorrect.
60 degrees is the glenohumeral contribution, not the scapular one.
90 degrees would mean the scapula does all the work, which is incorrect.
citation: For every 3° of abduction of the arm, a 2° abduction occurs in the shoulder joint and a 1° abduction occurs by rotation of the scapula.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 22-year-old man sustains a fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus. He subsequently develops weakness in abducting his arm and loss of sensation over the lower half of the deltoid. Which anatomical space did the injured nerve pass through before reaching the muscle?
optionExplanations: The triangular space contains the circumflex scapular artery, not the axillary nerve.
The suprascapular notch contains the suprascapular nerve.
The axillary nerve and posterior circumflex humeral artery pass through the quadrangular space to reach the deltoid.
The clavipectoral fascia is anterior and related to the pectoral muscles.
The spinoglenoid notch is related to the suprascapular nerve.
citation: the axillary nerve and the posterior circumflex humeral vessels emerge through this space

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Where does the pectoralis minor muscle insert?
optionExplanations: The greater tuberosity is the insertion for supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and teres minor.
The lesser tuberosity is the insertion for subscapularis.
The pectoralis minor inserts into the coracoid process of the scapula.
The acromion is an origin for the deltoid and insertion for the trapezius.
The medial border is the insertion for the rhomboids and serratus anterior.
citation: runs upward and laterally to insert into the coracoid process of the scapula.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 10-year-old girl falls from a tree onto her outstretched hand. She is clutching her collarbone in pain. Radiographs confirm a midshaft fracture. Which function of the clavicle was overwhelmed by the force of the fall?
options: Transmission of forces from the limb to the axial skeleton
correctOption: Transmission of forces from the limb to the axial skeleton
optionExplanations: The clavicle does not primarily protect the lungs.
The clavicle acts as a strut that transmits forces from the upper limb to the axial skeleton (sternum).
The diaphragm does not attach to the clavicle.
The clavicle does not medially rotate the humerus.
The clavicle articulates with the sternum and scapula, not primarily to stabilize the first rib.
citation: transmits all forces from the upper limb to the trunk.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient is unable to medially rotate and adduct his arm against resistance. While the latissimus dorsi is intact, another muscle in the scapular region that shares these exact actions is suspected to be injured. Which muscle is it?
optionExplanations: Supraspinatus is an abductor.
Infraspinatus is a lateral rotator.
The teres major adducts and medially rotates the arm, similar to the latissimus dorsi.
Teres minor is a lateral rotator.
The deltoid is primarily an abductor (middle fibers).
citation: Adducts and medially rotates arm

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient presents with pain at the base of the neck. Examination reveals swelling at the sternoclavicular joint. Anatomically, what feature within this joint helps absorb shock and allows for the 'double-plane' movement of the clavicle?
optionExplanations: Hyaline cartilage covers the surfaces but does not provide the double-plane mechanism alone.
The sternoclavicular joint contains a fibrocartilaginous articular disc that divides the joint into two compartments.
The conoid ligament is at the lateral end of the clavicle.
The coracoid process is part of the scapula, far from the sternum.
The interclavicular ligament strengthens the joint but is not an internal shock absorber.
citation: It articulates with the clavicular notch of the sternum through a compound synovial joint containing an articular disc.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A professional volleyball player has isolated atrophy of the infraspinatus muscle, but the supraspinatus muscle is normal. A cyst is suspected to be compressing a nerve as it passes through a specific scapular landmark. Where is the most likely site of compression?
optionExplanations: Compression at the scapular notch would affect both supraspinatus and infraspinatus.
The suprascapular nerve passes through the spinoglenoid notch after supplying the supraspinatus; compression here only affects the infraspinatus.
The quadrangular space contains the axillary nerve.
The triangular space contains the circumflex scapular artery.
The bicipital groove is on the humerus.
citation: This allows the suprascapular nerve and vessels to pass between these fossae.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: During a physical therapy session, a patient is performing exercises to stabilize the shoulder girdle. Which small anterior axio-appendicular muscle helps steady the clavicle by pulling it inferiorly during these movements?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis minor depresses the point of the shoulder (scapula), not primarily the clavicle.
The subclavius muscle depresses and steadies the clavicle.
Serratus anterior protracts the scapula.
Subscapularis is a rotator cuff muscle acting on the humerus.
The omohyoid is a neck muscle that does not primarily steady the clavicle.
citation: Depresses the clavicle and steadies this bone during movements of the shoulder girdle

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: When determining the side of an isolated clavicle, which feature is correctly used to identify the inferior and lateral aspect of the bone?
options: The conoid tubercle on the inferior surface
The groove for the subclavius on the superior surface
The costal tuberosity on the acromial end
correctOption: The conoid tubercle on the inferior surface
optionExplanations: The superior surface is smooth, but this identifies superior/inferior, not specifically lateral.
The blunt end is medial (sternal), not lateral.
The conoid tubercle is located on the inferior surface near the lateral (acromial) end.
The groove for the subclavius is on the inferior surface, not the superior surface.
The costal tuberosity is near the sternal (medial) end, not the acromial end.
citation: is a small, roughened elevation on the inferior surface, near the acromial end.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 22-year-old athlete falls directly onto his shoulder during a football match. Radiographs confirm a fracture at the junction of the middle and outer thirds of the clavicle. On physical examination, the medial fragment of the bone is noticeably prominent and displaced superiorly. Which muscle is primarily responsible for this upward displacement?
optionExplanations: The trapezius attaches to the lateral third and would not pull the medial fragment up.
The pectoralis major pulls the lateral fragment medially and forward.
The sternocleidomastoid muscle attaches to the medial part of the clavicle and tilts it upward after a fracture.
The subclavius is a weak depressor and does not cause upward tilting.
The deltoid attaches to the lateral fragment and contributes to its depression.
citation: The medial end is tilted upward by the sternocleidomastoid muscle.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Which of the following statements regarding the developmental osteology of the clavicle is correct?
options: It is the last bone to ossify
It is the first bone to ossify
It ossifies only in the 12th year
It lacks a primary ossification center
correctOption: It is the first bone to ossify
optionExplanations: The clavicle is not the last; it is the first.
The clavicle is unique as the first bone in the human body to begin the ossification process.
Carpal bones finish ossification by the 12th year, not the clavicle.
The clavicle has distinct ossification centers.
The clavicle undergoes intramembranous ossification primarily.
citation: It is the first bone to begin ossification.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 30-year-old woman undergoes a radical mastectomy for breast cancer. Postoperatively, she complains of difficulty raising her arm above her head to brush her hair. Physical examination reveals that the medial border of her scapula protrudes significantly when she pushes against a wall. What is the most likely cause of this deformity?
optionExplanations: Trapezius paralysis causes a dropped shoulder, not winging of the medial border.
Paralysis of the serratus anterior, often due to long thoracic nerve injury during surgery, causes a winged scapula.
Axillary nerve injury affects the deltoid and abduction up to 90 degrees, but not scapular winging.
Supraspinatus rupture interferes with the initiation of abduction.
Coracoid fractures are rare and do not cause this specific protrusion.
citation: winged scapula (Fig. 3.4), caused by paralysis of the serratus anterior.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: During a physical examination, the inferior angle of the scapula is used as a surface landmark. At which anatomical level is this structure located?
optionExplanations: The superior angle is at the level of T2.
The root of the scapular spine is at the level of T3.
T5 is between the spine and the inferior angle.
The inferior angle of the scapula marks the level of the 7th rib and the T7 spinous process.
T9 is too low for the scapular inferior angle.
citation: marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 15-year-old boy falls off his bicycle and lands on his outstretched hand. He presents to the emergency department with severe pain in his collarbone region. Based on the common clinical patterns of transmission of force, where is the most likely site of the fracture?
options: Junction of middle and outer thirds
correctOption: Junction of middle and outer thirds
optionExplanations: The sternal end is thick and blunt, rarely the primary site of fracture.
The acromial end is flattened but not the weakest point.
The clavicle typically breaks at its weakest point, which is the junction of the middle and outer thirds.
The conoid tubercle is a landmark on the inferior surface, not a common fracture site.
The subclavian groove is on the inferior surface of the middle third.
citation: breaks at its weakest point, the junction of the middle and outer thirds.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Which of the following is the site of insertion for the pectoralis minor muscle?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis major inserts into the humerus, not the minor.
The subscapularis inserts into the lesser tuberosity.
The pectoralis minor inserts into the coracoid process of the scapula.
The trapezius and deltoid relate to the acromion.
The serratus anterior and rhomboids insert into the medial border.
citation: insert into the coracoid process of the scapula.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Which group of muscles collectively forms the 'rotator cuff' to stabilize the glenohumeral joint?
optionExplanations: Teres major is not part of the rotator cuff.
The rotator cuff consists of the supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, and subscapularis.
Pectoralis major and deltoid are not cuff muscles.
Teres major is a distractor; it is not part of the cuff.
Deltoid is a superficial muscle, not part of the cuff.
citation: subscapularis, supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and teres minor muscles

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 55-year-old carpenter presents with chronic shoulder pain. On examination, he is completely unable to begin lifting his arm away from his side. However, if the examiner passively lifts the arm to 20 degrees, the patient can then continue the abduction movement using his deltoid muscle. Which muscle is most likely ruptured?
optionExplanations: Infraspinatus is primarily a lateral rotator.
Subscapularis is a medial rotator.
The supraspinatus initiates the first 15-20 degrees of abduction; without it, the patient cannot initiate the movement.
Teres minor is a lateral rotator.
Serratus anterior is required for rotation of the scapula for abduction above 90 degrees.
citation: unable to initiate abduction of the arm.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: According to the scapular-humeral mechanism, what is the ratio of movement between the glenohumeral joint and the scapula during full abduction of the arm?
optionExplanations: The ratio is not equal.
For every 3 degrees of total abduction, 2 degrees occur at the shoulder joint and 1 degree occurs via scapular rotation (2:1).
This reverses the correct anatomical ratio.
The ratio is 2:1, not 3:1.
The ratio is 2:1, not 4:1.
citation: For every 3° of abduction of the arm, a 2° abduction occurs in the shoulder joint and a 1° abduction occurs by rotation of the scapula.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A newborn is noted to have a chest wall deformity where the anterior axillary fold is missing on the right side. The clinician suspects a congenital absence of the sternocostal head of the pectoralis major. Which pair of movements will be significantly weakened in this infant as they grow?
optionExplanations: Abduction is performed by the deltoid and supraspinatus.
Lateral rotation is performed by the infraspinatus and teres minor.
The pectoralis major is a powerful adductor and medial rotator of the humerus.
Extension is primarily latissimus dorsi and teres major.
Protraction is primarily serratus anterior.
citation: causes weakness in adduction and medial rotation of the shoulder joint.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 40-year-old man is brought to the ER after being struck by a car. He has a confirmed fracture of the body of the scapula. Given the high-energy nature of this injury, which other traumatic finding is most commonly associated with scapular fractures?
optionExplanations: Colles' fracture involves the distal radius and is unrelated to scapular trauma.
Scapular fractures result from severe trauma and are frequently associated with rib fractures.
Lunate dislocation is a wrist injury.
Scaphoid fracture is a wrist injury.
Bennett's fracture involves the base of the thumb.
citation: Injuries are usually associated with fractured ribs.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Which nerve provides the motor innervation to the serratus anterior muscle?
optionExplanations: Axillary nerve supplies the deltoid.
Suprascapular nerve supplies supraspinatus and infraspinatus.
Thoracodorsal nerve supplies latissimus dorsi.
The long thoracic nerve (C5, C6, C7) supplies the serratus anterior.
Dorsal scapular nerve supplies the rhomboids and levator scapulae.
citation: Long thoracic nerve (C5 to 7)

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 35-year-old heavy-lifter complains of numbness and tingling in his right hand, along with occasional coldness and pallor of the fingers. The symptoms worsen when he carries a heavy backpack. The clinician suspects compression of the neurovascular bundle. In which anatomical interval is this compression most likely occurring?
options: Between the clavicle and first rib
correctOption: Between the clavicle and first rib
optionExplanations: Narrowing of the space between the clavicle and the first rib can compress the brachial plexus and subclavian vessels.
The quadrangular space contains the axillary nerve, not the main neurovascular bundle to the hand.
The bicipital groove houses the biceps tendon.
The ulnar nerve is behind the medial epicondyle, but this wouldn't explain vascular symptoms in the whole hand.
Carpal tunnel syndrome involves the median nerve only, not vascular structures.
citation: interval between the clavicle and the first rib in some patients may become narrowed

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Regarding the suprascapular notch and the superior transverse scapular ligament, which of the following is the correct anatomical relationship?
options: Nerve and artery both pass superiorly
Nerve and artery both pass inferiorly
Artery passes superiorly, nerve passes inferiorly
Nerve passes superiorly, artery passes inferiorly
Artery passes through the coracoid process
correctOption: Artery passes superiorly, nerve passes inferiorly
optionExplanations: Only the artery passes superiorly.
Only the nerve passes inferiorly.
The 'Army' (Artery) goes over the bridge (ligament), and the 'Navy' (Nerve) goes under the bridge.
This is the reverse of the actual relationship.
The artery does not pass through the coracoid process.
citation: suprascapular artery passes superior to this ligament, whereas the suprascapular nerve passes inferior to it

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient is unable to maintain his arm in a horizontal position (90 degrees of abduction) against gravity after the first 15 degrees of movement have been completed. Which muscle is the primary 'workhorse' for this specific range of motion?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis major is an adductor.
Latissimus dorsi is an adductor.
The middle fibers of the deltoid are the primary abductors of the arm from 15 to 90 degrees.
Subclavius stabilizes the clavicle.
Teres major is an adductor.
citation: middle fibers of the deltoid, assisted by the supraspinatus, are involved.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 25-year-old man presents with a 'dropped shoulder' on the left side following a deep laceration in the posterior triangle of his neck. He is unable to shrug his shoulder against resistance. Which muscle is paralyzed?
optionExplanations: Serratus anterior paralysis causes winging, not a dropped shoulder.
Rhomboids retract the scapula.
Levator scapulae elevates the scapula but its loss alone doesn't typically present as a 'dropped shoulder' in this context.
Paralysis of the trapezius (Spinal Accessory Nerve injury) results in a dropped shoulder.
Deltoid paralysis affects abduction, not the resting height of the shoulder girdle.
citation: dropped shoulder, which occurs with paralysis of the trapezius

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: What is the primary functional role of the subclavius muscle?
optionExplanations: It attaches to the first rib but its action is on the clavicle.
Serratus anterior protracts the scapula.
The subclavius depresses the clavicle and steadies it during shoulder girdle movements.
Subscapularis medially rotates the humerus.
Deltoid abducts the arm.
citation: depresses the clavicle and steadies this bone during movements of the shoulder girdle.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient presents with a 'shoulder separation' after a fall. Imaging shows a complete tear of the ligaments connecting the coracoid process to the clavicle. Why is this specific injury so disabling for the support of the upper limb?
optionExplanations: Rotation is not the primary issue here.
This involves the acromioclavicular region, not the sternoclavicular joint.
The coracoclavicular ligament is the primary structure suspending the weight of the scapula and arm from the clavicle.
Elbow flexion is unrelated to these ligaments.
Ligamentous tears do not cause nerve paralysis.
citation: largely responsible for suspending the weight of the scapula and the upper limb from the clavicle

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: When palpating the posterior chest wall, the root of the spine of the scapula is found at the level of which vertebral spinous process?
optionExplanations: T1 is too high.
T2 corresponds to the superior angle.
The spine of the scapula joins the medial border at the level of the T3 spinous process.
T4 is below the root of the spine.
T5 is significantly below the root of the spine.
citation: medial border of the scapula, which it joins at the level of the third thoracic spine

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 45-year-old woman has difficulty reaching behind her back to fasten her bra. This movement requires significant medial rotation of the humerus. Which of the following muscles is a primary medial rotator of the shoulder joint?
optionExplanations: Infraspinatus is a lateral rotator.
Teres minor is a lateral rotator.
The subscapularis is a major medial rotator of the shoulder joint.
Supraspinatus is an abductor.
Trapezius acts on the scapula, not directly as a rotator of the humerus.
citation: subscapularis, latissimus dorsi, teres major, and the anterior fibers of the deltoid muscle perform this.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: In side determination of the clavicle, how is the medial (sternal) end distinguished from the lateral (acromial) end?
optionExplanations: The lateral (acromial) end is flattened.
The medial (sternal) end is blunt and thickened.
The conoid tubercle is near the lateral end.
The medial two-thirds are convex anteriorly.
Both ends have articular surfaces.
citation: sternal extremity (Fig. 3.1) is the blunt, thickened, proximal (medial) end

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient is able to abduct his arm to 90 degrees, but cannot lift it any further toward the vertical position. There is no evidence of a rotator cuff tear. Weakness in which muscle would specifically limit abduction above the horizontal plane?
optionExplanations: Supraspinatus initiates abduction.
Deltoid abducts to 90 degrees.
The serratus anterior is required to rotate the scapula to allow abduction above 90 degrees.
Subclavius does not assist in abduction.
Pectoralis minor depresses the shoulder.
citation: inability to rotate the scapula during abduction of the arm above a right angle.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 50-year-old man had a clavicle fracture that healed with significant callus formation. He now complains of persistent, nagging pain over the side of his neck and the point of his shoulder. Which nerves were likely entrapped in the healing bone tissue?
optionExplanations: Axillary nerves are in the axilla/shoulder joint region.
Suprascapular nerves are in the scapular notch.
Supraclavicular nerves (C3-C4) lie close to the clavicle and can be involved in callus formation, causing neck/shoulder pain.
Long thoracic nerves are on the lateral chest wall.
Phrenic nerves are deeper in the neck.
citation: close relationship of the supraclavicular nerves to the clavicle may result in their involvement in callus formation

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Which nerve provides the innervation to the teres major muscle?
optionExplanations: Upper subscapular nerve supplies only the subscapularis.
The lower subscapular nerve supplies both the subscapularis and the teres major.
Thoracodorsal nerve supplies latissimus dorsi.
Axillary nerve supplies deltoid and teres minor.
Suprascapular nerve supplies supraspinatus and infraspinatus.
citation: lower subscapular nerve supplies the teres major muscle.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: During a surgical dissection of the axilla, a surgeon identifies a nerve that pierces the pectoralis minor muscle before entering the deep surface of the pectoralis major. If this nerve is accidentally severed, which muscle(s) will lose innervation?
optionExplanations: The medial pectoral nerve supplies both muscles.
The medial pectoral nerve supplies both muscles.
The medial pectoral nerve supplies the pectoralis minor and then continues to supply the pectoralis major.
Serratus anterior is supplied by the long thoracic nerve.
Subclavius is supplied by the nerve to subclavius.
citation: medial pectoral nerve arises from the medial cord of the brachial plexus, supplies and pierces the pectoralis minor muscle, and next supplies the pectoralis major

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Which of the following is a unique developmental feature of the clavicle compared to other bones in the human body?
options: It is the last bone to ossify
It is the first bone to begin ossification
It only begins ossification after birth
correctOption: It is the first bone to begin ossification
optionExplanations: The clavicle is not the last; it is the first.
The clavicle is the first bone in the body to start the ossification process.
The text does not state it ossifies entirely from cartilage; it focuses on the timing.
The text does not mention the absence of a medullary cavity.
Ossification begins in the embryo, not after birth.
citation: It is the first bone to begin ossification.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 19-year-old rugby player falls directly onto his shoulder and is brought to the ER. Radiographs confirm a fracture at the junction of the middle and outer thirds of the clavicle. Which of the following best describes the typical displacement of the lateral fragment in this injury?
options: Depressed by arm weight and pulled medially
correctOption: Depressed by arm weight and pulled medially
optionExplanations: The trapezius cannot counteract the weight of the arm in this fracture.
The medial fragment, not the lateral one, is tilted upward by the sternocleidomastoid.
The lateral fragment is depressed by the arm's weight and pulled medially/forward by adductors like the pectoralis major.
Posterior displacement is not the standard presentation described for this fracture site.
The deltoid is part of the weight depressing the fragment, not rotating it laterally.
citation: the lateral fragment is depressed by the weight of the arm and is pulled medially and forward by the strong adductor muscles

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient undergoes a radical mastectomy for breast cancer. Post-operatively, the physician notices that the medial border of the patient's scapula protrudes significantly when she pushes against a wall. This clinical sign is most likely caused by paralysis of which muscle?
optionExplanations: Trapezius paralysis causes a dropped shoulder, not winging.
Latissimus dorsi paralysis affects arm extension and adduction.
Pectoralis minor paralysis does not cause scapular winging.
Paralysis of the serratus anterior, often due to long thoracic nerve injury, results in a winged scapula.
Rhomboid paralysis affects scapular retraction but does not cause classic winging.
citation: winged scapula (Fig. 3.4), caused by paralysis of the serratus anterior.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: During a physical examination, the inferior angle of the scapula is used as a surface landmark. At which anatomical level does this structure typically lie?
optionExplanations: The superior angle lies at the level of T2.
The spine of the scapula meets the medial border at T3.
The inferior angle is lower than the fifth rib.
The inferior angle of the scapula marks the level of the seventh rib and the T7 spinous process.
The inferior angle is higher than the ninth rib.
citation: marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 55-year-old painter presents with chronic shoulder pain. On examination, he can maintain his arm in an abducted position if the examiner places it there, but he cannot begin the movement of moving his arm away from his side. Which structure is most likely damaged?
optionExplanations: The deltoid completes abduction but cannot initiate the first 15 degrees effectively.
The supraspinatus is responsible for initiating abduction; its rupture prevents the start of the movement.
The infraspinatus is primarily a lateral rotator.
The teres major adducts and medially rotates the arm.
The subscapularis is a medial rotator.
citation: The patient with a ruptured supraspinatus tendon is unable to initiate abduction of the arm.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A newborn is noted to have a chest wall deformity where the anterior axillary fold is absent on the right side. If this is due to the congenital absence of the pectoralis major, which pair of movements will be most significantly weakened?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis major is an adductor and medial rotator, not an abductor.
Protraction is primarily serratus anterior; elevation is trapezius/levator scapulae.
The pectoralis major is a powerful adductor and medial rotator of the shoulder joint.
Retraction is rhomboids/trapezius; depression is pectoralis minor/lower trapezius.
Supination is the biceps brachii/supinator.
citation: The sternocostal origin is the most commonly missing part, and this causes weakness in adduction and medial rotation

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Regarding the structures associated with the scapular notch and the superior transverse scapular ligament, which of the following is correct?
options: The nerve passes over the ligament
The artery passes under the ligament
The nerve passes through the coracoid process
The artery passes superior to the ligament
Both nerve and artery pass under the ligament
correctOption: The artery passes superior to the ligament
optionExplanations: The nerve passes inferior to (under) the ligament.
The artery normally passes superior to (over) the ligament.
The nerve passes through the notch, not the coracoid process.
The suprascapular artery passes superior to the superior transverse scapular ligament.
They are separated by the ligament (Army over, Navy under).
citation: normally, the suprascapular artery passes superior to this ligament, whereas the suprascapular nerve passes inferior to it

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 12-year-old boy falls from a tree and sustains a mid-shaft clavicular fracture. On physical exam, the medial fragment of the bone is palpably displaced superiorly. Which muscle is responsible for this upward pull?
optionExplanations: The trapezius attaches to the lateral third of the clavicle.
The pectoralis major pulls the lateral fragment medially and forward.
The subclavius pulls the clavicle downward.
The sternocleidomastoid muscle attaches to the medial fragment and tilts it upward after a fracture.
The deltoid attaches to the lateral third and contributes to depressing the lateral fragment.
citation: The medial end is tilted upward by the sternocleidomastoid muscle.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient presents with recurrent shoulder subluxations. The physician explains that the stability of the glenohumeral joint is largely dependent on a group of muscles known as the rotator cuff. What is the primary functional role of these muscles during joint movement?
options: Holding the humeral head in the glenoid cavity
Initiating downward rotation of the scapula
Acting as the primary flexors of the arm
Preventing upward displacement of the clavicle
correctOption: Holding the humeral head in the glenoid cavity
optionExplanations: Heavy lifting involves larger muscles like the pectoralis major and latissimus dorsi.
The rotator cuff muscles provide dynamic stability by keeping the humeral head centered in the shallow glenoid cavity.
Scapular rotation is performed by the serratus anterior and trapezius.
Primary flexors include the deltoid and pectoralis major.
Clavicular displacement is managed by ligaments and the SCM/subclavius.
citation: The tone of these muscles assists in holding the head of the humerus in the glenoid cavity

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient is unable to reach for a book on a high shelf (overhead reach) following an injury to the long thoracic nerve. Which of the following best explains this deficit?
optionExplanations: Deltoid initiation is not the issue; the deltoid is supplied by the axillary nerve.
The serratus anterior is required to rotate the scapula to allow abduction beyond 90 degrees.
Supraspinatus is supplied by the suprascapular nerve.
The long thoracic nerve does not supply extensors.
Elbow flexion is primarily musculocutaneous nerve.
citation: paralysis of the serratus anterior results in the inability to rotate the scapula during abduction of the arm above a right angle.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Where is the conoid tubercle located on the clavicle?
optionExplanations: The superior surface is relatively smooth.
The conoid tubercle is found on the inferior surface near the lateral (acromial) end.
It is not on the anterior border.
It is on the inferior, not superior, surface.
The sternal end is the medial end; the tubercle is lateral.
citation: The conoid tubercle (cono- is Greek for “pine cone”) is a small, roughened elevation on the inferior surface, near the acromial end.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 30-year-old man is involved in a high-speed motor vehicle accident and sustains a comminuted fracture of the scapular body. Despite the severity of the bone fragmentation, the surgeon decides on conservative management. What anatomical factor justifies this decision?
options: The bone has a high regenerative capacity
The overlying skin is very thick
Surrounding muscles act as natural splints
The scapula does not bear weight
The fragments are held by the glenoid labrum
correctOption: Surrounding muscles act as natural splints
optionExplanations: Regenerative capacity is not the primary reason for conservative treatment here.
Skin thickness does not provide orthopedic stability.
The extensive muscle attachments (subscapularis, infraspinatus, etc.) provide excellent natural splinting for the bone.
While true, it doesn't explain why fragments don't need surgical alignment.
The labrum only affects the glenoid cavity, not the body of the scapula.
citation: Most fractures of the scapula require little direct treatment because the muscles on the anterior and posterior surfaces adequately splint the fragments.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Six weeks after a clavicular fracture, a patient complains of persistent, sharp pain over the side of his neck and the point of his shoulder. This is most likely due to the entrapment of which nerves within the healing bone callus?
optionExplanations: The phrenic nerve is deeper in the neck.
The suprascapular nerve passes through the scapular notch, not over the clavicle.
The supraclavicular nerves (C3-C4) cross the clavicle and can be caught in the healing callus.
The long thoracic nerve is in the axilla/lateral chest wall.
The axillary nerve is related to the surgical neck of the humerus.
citation: The close relationship of the supraclavicular nerves to the clavicle may result in their involvement in callus formation

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient presents with a 'dropped shoulder' on the left side following a deep laceration in the posterior triangle of the neck. Which muscle's loss of function is responsible for this deformity?
optionExplanations: Levator scapulae elevates the scapula but its loss alone doesn't cause the classic 'dropped shoulder'.
Rhomboids retract the scapula.
Serratus anterior paralysis causes winging.
The trapezius is the primary muscle maintaining the height of the shoulder; its paralysis leads to a dropped appearance.
The deltoid acts on the humerus, not the height of the shoulder girdle.
citation: dropped shoulder, which occurs with paralysis of the trapezius

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: During a physical therapy session, a patient is asked to perform movements that depress the clavicle. Which small muscle, located between the first rib and the clavicle, is being primarily tested?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis minor depresses the shoulder/scapula, not specifically the clavicle.
The subclavius muscle originates from the 1st rib and inserts into the clavicle, acting to depress it.
Serratus anterior protracts the scapula.
Sternohyoid is an infrahyoid muscle.
Omohyoid acts on the hyoid bone.
citation: the subclavius (depresses the clavicle) because of its nerve

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A surgeon is performing a dissection in the pectoral region. To identify the pectoralis minor, she looks for its insertion point. Where should she look?
optionExplanations: The acromion is the insertion for the trapezius and deltoid.
The greater tuberosity is for rotator cuff muscles.
The pectoralis minor inserts into the coracoid process of the scapula.
The subclavius is related to the clavicle.
The serratus anterior and rhomboids relate to the medial border.
citation: runs upward and laterally to insert into the coracoid process of the scapula.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient is unable to abduct his arm to a horizontal position (90 degrees). If the supraspinatus is intact, which part of the deltoid muscle is likely weak or paralyzed?
optionExplanations: Anterior fibers primarily flex and medially rotate.
Posterior fibers primarily extend and laterally rotate.
The middle (acromial) fibers of the deltoid are the primary abductors of the arm to 90 degrees.
Clavicular head is another name for anterior fibers.
Sternocostal head refers to the pectoralis major.
citation: The middle fibers of the deltoid, assisted by the supraspinatus, are involved.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A swimmer complains of weakness when performing the downward stroke (pulling the arm down and back). This movement involves extension and adduction of the humerus. Which muscle is the primary driver of this action?
optionExplanations: Trapezius acts on the scapula, not the humerus.
The latissimus dorsi extends, adducts, and medially rotates the arm (the 'swimmer's muscle').
Levator scapulae elevates the scapula.
Supraspinatus abducts the arm.
Serratus anterior protracts the scapula.
citation: Extends, adducts, and medially rotates the arm

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient has difficulty retracting their scapulae (pulling the shoulder blades together). If this is due to a nerve lesion, which nerve is most likely affected?
optionExplanations: Long thoracic nerve supplies serratus anterior.
Thoracodorsal nerve supplies latissimus dorsi.
The dorsal scapular nerve (C4, C5) supplies the rhomboids and levator scapulae.
Suprascapular nerve supplies supraspinatus and infraspinatus.
Axillary nerve supplies deltoid and teres minor.
citation: Dorsal scapular nerve C4, 5

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: What is the primary anatomical function of the glenoid labrum in the shoulder joint?
options: Connects the humerus to the acromion
optionExplanations: Synovial fluid is secreted by the synovial membrane.
The fibrocartilage ring (labrum) increases the surface area and depth of the shallow glenoid fossa.
Ligaments and tendons connect the bones.
It is a passive structure, not a muscle.
It has no protective role for the axillary nerve.
citation: serves to broaden and deepen the joint cavity.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient is being tested for muscle strength. The physician asks the patient to medially rotate and adduct their arm against resistance. Which muscle of the scapular region is being specifically targeted?
optionExplanations: Teres minor laterally rotates the arm.
Infraspinatus laterally rotates the arm.
Supraspinatus abducts the arm.
The teres major adducts and medially rotates the arm.
Posterior deltoid fibers laterally rotate and extend the arm.
citation: Adducts and medially rotates the arm

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient presents with weakness in lateral rotation of the shoulder. MRI shows atrophy of the muscle occupying the large fossa inferior to the scapular spine. Which muscle is this?
optionExplanations: Supraspinatus is superior to the spine.
Subscapularis is on the anterior (costal) surface.
The infraspinatus occupies the infraspinous fossa and is a lateral rotator.
Teres major is near the lateral border, not filling the fossa.
Serratus anterior is on the medial border/anterior surface.
citation: Laterally rotates the arm

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient sustains a posterior dislocation of the shoulder joint, which is occasionally accompanied by a fracture of the lesser tuberosity. Which rotator cuff muscle would lose its insertion in this scenario?
optionExplanations: Supraspinatus inserts on the greater tuberosity.
Infraspinatus inserts on the greater tuberosity.
Teres minor inserts on the greater tuberosity.
The subscapularis is the only rotator cuff muscle that inserts on the lesser tuberosity.
The deltoid inserts on the deltoid tuberosity of the shaft.
citation: Lesser tuberosity of the humerus

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient is unable to shrug their shoulders. This indicates a failure of the upper fibers of which muscle?
optionExplanations: Latissimus dorsi depresses the arm.
Pectoralis major adducts the arm.
The upper fibers of the trapezius are responsible for elevating the scapula (shrugging).
Serratus anterior protracts the scapula.
Subclavius depresses the clavicle.
citation: Upper fibers elevate the scapula; middle fibers pull the scapula medially; lower fibers pull the medial border of the scapula downward

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A child falls on an outstretched hand. The physician explains to the parents that the force of the impact traveled up the arm to the collar bone. Why is the clavicle so frequently fractured in this mechanism?
options: It is the strongest bone in the body
It transmits all forces to the trunk
It is protected by thick muscle
It is not part of the axial skeleton
correctOption: It transmits all forces to the trunk
optionExplanations: It is actually quite slender and easily broken.
As the sole link between the limb and the axial skeleton, it must transmit all forces, making it vulnerable.
It is subcutaneous and lacks thick muscular protection.
It has many strong ligaments (e.g., coracoclavicular).
While true, it doesn't explain the fracture mechanism.
citation: transmits all forces from the upper limb to the trunk.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Which of the following is a unique developmental feature of the clavicle compared to other bones in the human body?
optionExplanations: The clavicle is not the last bone to ossify.
The clavicle is the very first bone in the human body to start the ossification process.
The clavicle has multiple ossification centers.
The clavicle undergoes intramembranous ossification primarily.
Ossification begins much earlier than puberty.
citation: It is the first bone to begin ossification.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A clinician palpates the inferior angle of the scapula to identify a specific vertebral level. Which level does this landmark represent?
optionExplanations: The superior angle is near T2.
The root of the scapular spine is at T3.
T5 is not marked by the inferior angle.
The inferior angle of the scapula corresponds to the level of the T7 spine and the 7th rib.
T9 is too low for the scapular inferior angle.
citation: The inferior angle of the scapula can be palpated easily in the living subject and marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Which structure normally passes inferior to the superior transverse scapular ligament within the scapular notch?
optionExplanations: The artery passes superior to the ligament ('Army over the bridge').
The suprascapular nerve passes inferior to the ligament ('Navy under the bridge').
The dorsal scapular nerve does not pass through this notch.
The circumflex scapular artery is related to the lateral border.
The subscapular nerves arise from the posterior cord in the axilla.
citation: normally, the suprascapular artery passes superior to this ligament, whereas the suprascapular nerve passes inferior to it

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: What is the anatomical origin of the pectoralis minor muscle?
optionExplanations: The origin is lower than the first two ribs.
The origin starts at the third rib.
The pectoralis minor arises from the 3rd, 4th, and 5th ribs near their cartilages.
This describes the pectoralis major.
The coracoid process is the site of insertion, not origin.
citation: It arises from the third, fourth, and fifth ribs

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Which of the following muscles is a component of the rotator cuff?
optionExplanations: Teres major is not part of the rotator cuff.
The deltoid is a superficial shoulder muscle, not part of the cuff.
The subscapularis is one of the four SITS muscles forming the rotator cuff.
Serratus anterior is an axio-appendicular muscle.
Pectoralis minor is an anterior axio-appendicular muscle.
citation: The tendons of the subscapularis, supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and teres minor muscles are fused to the underlying capsule of the shoulder joint.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 19-year-old rugby player falls hard on his shoulder during a match. He presents with severe pain and a visible deformity in the mid-clavicular region. At which specific site is this bone most likely to have fractured?
options: Junction of middle and outer thirds
correctOption: Junction of middle and outer thirds
optionExplanations: The sternal end is thick and rarely the primary site of fracture.
The acromial end is less common than the mid-shaft junction.
The junction of the middle and outer thirds is the weakest point of the clavicle and the most common fracture site.
The conoid tubercle is a landmark on the inferior surface, not a common fracture site.
The subclavian groove is on the inferior surface of the shaft.
citation: The fracture usually occurs as a result of a fall on the shoulder or outstretched hand. The force is transmitted along the clavicle, which breaks at its weakest point, the junction of the middle and outer thirds.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Following a complete fracture of the mid-shaft of the clavicle, the patient's medial bony fragment is observed to be significantly displaced superiorly. Which muscle is primarily responsible for this upward pull?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis major pulls the lateral fragment medially and forward.
Trapezius cannot counteract the weight of the arm on the lateral fragment.
Subclavius is too weak and actually pulls the bone inferiorly.
The sternocleidomastoid muscle attaches to the medial third and tilts the medial fragment upward after a fracture.
Sternohyoid is deep and does not cause this displacement.
citation: The medial end is tilted upward by the sternocleidomastoid muscle.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient with a fractured clavicle shows a characteristic deformity where the shoulder drops and the lateral fragment is pulled toward the chest. Which muscle is the main driver of this medial and forward displacement?
optionExplanations: The deltoid is attached to the lateral fragment but does not pull it medially.
The pectoralis major is a strong adductor that pulls the lateral fragment medially and forward.
Serratus anterior acts on the scapula, not directly on the clavicular fragment.
Latissimus dorsi is an adductor but pectoralis major is the primary driver here.
Teres major is an adductor but less significant in this specific displacement.
citation: the lateral fragment is depressed by the weight of the arm and is pulled medially and forward by the strong adductor muscles of the shoulder joint, especially the pectoralis major.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient returns to the clinic six weeks after a clavicle fracture. Although the bone is healing, he complains of persistent, sharp pain radiating up the side of his neck. Involvement of which nerves in the healing callus is most likely?
optionExplanations: The phrenic nerve is deeper and not typically involved in clavicular callus.
The supraclavicular nerves (C3, C4) are subcutaneous and cross the clavicle, making them prone to entrapment in a healing callus.
The long thoracic nerve is posterior to the axillary vessels.
The axillary nerve is related to the surgical neck of the humerus.
Ansa cervicalis is located deeper in the neck.
citation: The close relationship of the supraclavicular nerves to the clavicle may result in their involvement in callus formation after fracture of the bone. This may be the cause of persistent pain over the side of the neck.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: During a physical examination, a patient is asked to push against a wall with both hands. The medial border of her right scapula protrudes significantly backward. Paralysis of which muscle is indicated?
optionExplanations: Trapezius paralysis causes a 'dropped shoulder'.
Latissimus dorsi paralysis affects adduction and extension.
Paralysis of the serratus anterior results in 'winged scapula' because the muscle can no longer hold the medial border against the chest wall.
Rhomboids retract the scapula; their loss doesn't cause classic winging.
Levator scapulae elevates the scapula.
citation: winged scapula (Fig. 3.4), caused by paralysis of the serratus anterior.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient presents with a visible sagging of the right shoulder and an inability to shrug against resistance. Which muscle is likely paralyzed?
optionExplanations: Deltoid loss affects abduction, not shrugging.
Serratus anterior loss causes winging.
The trapezius maintains the shoulder level and elevates the scapula; its paralysis leads to a 'dropped shoulder'.
Pectoralis major is an adductor.
Rhomboids assist in elevation but trapezius is the primary muscle for shrugging.
citation: dropped shoulder, which occurs with paralysis of the trapezius

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A professional tennis player complains of shoulder pain that occurs only when he abducts his arm between 60 and 120 degrees. What is the most likely anatomical explanation for this 'painful arc'?
options: Teres major rubbing on the humerus
optionExplanations: Teres major is not involved in this impingement mechanism.
In the middle range of abduction, a diseased supraspinatus tendon or subacromial bursa impinges on the acromion, causing pain.
Deltoid tears cause pain throughout the range of motion.
Biceps rupture usually presents with a 'Popeye' deformity.
Subscapularis is an internal rotator and less likely to impinge on the acromion during abduction.
citation: It is characterized by the presence of a spasm of pain in the middle range of abduction, when the diseased area impinges on the acromion.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 65-year-old man is unable to lift his arm sideways from his body. However, if the examiner lifts the arm to about 20 degrees, the patient can then continue the abduction movement himself. Which structure is likely ruptured?
optionExplanations: If the deltoid were ruptured, the patient could not complete the abduction.
The supraspinatus initiates the first 15 degrees of abduction; if ruptured, the patient cannot start the movement but can finish it if assisted past the start.
Infraspinatus is a lateral rotator.
Ligament injury would not cause this specific motor deficit.
Teres minor is a lateral rotator.
citation: The patient with a ruptured supraspinatus tendon is unable to initiate abduction of the arm. However, if the arm is passively assisted for the first 15° of abduction, the deltoid can then take over

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A victim of a high-speed car accident is diagnosed with a comminuted fracture of the scapular body. Why do these fractures often require minimal surgical intervention for stabilization?
options: The bone has no blood supply
Surrounding muscles act as natural splints
The bone heals faster than the ribs
correctOption: Surrounding muscles act as natural splints
optionExplanations: The scapula has an extensive blood supply.
While not weight-bearing, stabilization is usually needed for movement; however, the scapula is unique.
The extensive muscle attachments (subscapularis, infraspinatus, etc.) on both surfaces provide natural splinting of the fragments.
Healing time is not the reason for lack of treatment.
Fragments can displace, but the muscles limit this significantly.
citation: Most fractures of the scapula require little direct treatment because the muscles on the anterior and posterior surfaces adequately splint the fragments.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A newborn is noted to have a flattened chest wall on the left side. As the child grows, he exhibits significant weakness when trying to rotate his arm inward and pull it toward his body. Which muscle is likely partially absent?
optionExplanations: Serratus anterior absence causes winging.
Pectoralis minor absence is less functionally obvious than major.
Congenital absence of the pectoralis major (often the sternocostal head) leads to weakness in adduction and medial rotation.
Subclavius absence is clinically insignificant.
Latissimus dorsi is a posterior muscle.
citation: The sternocostal origin is the most commonly missing part, and this causes weakness in adduction and medial rotation of the shoulder joint.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient suffers a severe 'shoulder separation' where the coracoclavicular ligament is completely torn. What is the primary functional consequence regarding weight transmission?
options: Weight is shifted to the sternum
Weight cannot be transmitted to the clavicle
correctOption: Weight cannot be transmitted to the clavicle
optionExplanations: The sternum is the end of the chain, but the link to the clavicle is broken.
The coracoclavicular ligament is the primary structure that transmits the weight of the upper limb to the clavicle.
Inferior dislocation is a glenohumeral issue.
The joint becomes unstable, but 'hypermobile' is not the primary clinical description of the weight-bearing failure.
Rib fractures are not a direct result of this ligament tear.
citation: The greater part of the weight of the upper limb is transmitted to the clavicle through this ligament, and rotary movements of the scapula occur at this important ligament.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient can abduct his arm to 120 degrees but cannot raise it any further toward his head. Which movement is likely restricted?
optionExplanations: Gliding is part of the first 120 degrees.
After 120 degrees, the humerus hits the acromion; further elevation requires the scapula to rotate on the chest wall.
The clavicle must elevate, not depress, for high abduction.
The elbow is not involved in shoulder abduction.
Lateral rotation, not medial, helps clear the acromion, but the question asks about the primary mechanism for elevation above 120 degrees.
citation: At about 120° of abduction of the arm, the greater tuberosity of the humerus comes into contact with the lateral edge of the acromion. Further elevation of the arm above the head is accomplished by rotating the scapula.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: To locate the coracoid process during a physical exam, where should the clinician apply deep pressure?
options: Medial to the sternal end of clavicle
Inferior to the lateral end of clavicle
In the center of the axilla
correctOption: Inferior to the lateral end of clavicle
optionExplanations: This is the suprasternal notch area.
This is the trapezius area.
The coracoid process is felt by pressing through the anterior deltoid, just below the lateral third of the clavicle.
The humeral head is felt in the axilla.
This is the infraspinous fossa.
citation: It can be palpated via deep pressure through the anterior part of the deltoid muscle, inferior to the lateral end of the clavicle.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient presents with numbness in the hand and a weakened radial pulse. Imaging shows a narrow space between the collar bone and the first rib. Which structures are being compressed?
options: Phrenic nerve and internal thoracic artery
optionExplanations: The axillary structures are further distal in the axilla.
The space between the clavicle and 1st rib transmits the brachial plexus and the subclavian artery/vein.
The long thoracic nerve is not typically compressed in this specific interval.
These are in the carotid sheath, not the thoracic outlet.
The phrenic nerve is anterior to the scalenus anterior.
citation: The interval between the clavicle and the first rib in some patients may become narrowed and thus is responsible for compression of nerves and blood vessels.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient has a tear in the muscle that occupies the costal surface of the scapula. Which shoulder movement will be most significantly weakened?
optionExplanations: Abduction is supraspinatus/deltoid.
Lateral rotation is infraspinatus/teres minor.
The subscapularis occupies the costal (anterior) surface and is a powerful medial rotator.
Extension is latissimus dorsi/teres major/posterior deltoid.
Elevation is trapezius/levator scapulae.
citation: Medial rotation: ... The subscapularis, latissimus dorsi, teres major, and the anterior fibers of the deltoid muscle perform this.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient is unable to pull his arm toward his trunk against resistance. A muscle arising from the lower third of the lateral border of the scapula is found to be injured. Which muscle is this?
optionExplanations: Teres minor arises higher on the lateral border and is a lateral rotator.
Teres major arises from the lower part of the lateral border and is an adductor and medial rotator.
Infraspinatus arises from the infraspinous fossa.
Supraspinatus arises from the supraspinous fossa.
Subscapularis arises from the subscapular fossa.
citation: Adduction: ... The pectoralis major, latissimus dorsi, teres major, and teres minor muscles perform this.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: During a radical mastectomy, a surgeon accidentally severs a nerve running vertically on the superficial surface of the medial wall of the axilla. What clinical sign will the patient likely show post-operatively?
optionExplanations: Wrist drop is radial nerve injury.
Ape hand is median nerve injury.
The long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior; its injury causes winged scapula.
Claw hand is ulnar nerve injury.
Dropped shoulder is accessory nerve injury.
citation: The long thoracic nerve (C5 to 7) ... descends over the superficial surface of the serratus anterior muscle, which it supplies

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient presents to the ER with difficulty breathing and swallowing after a direct blow to the front of his chest. Examination reveals a deformity at the sternoclavicular joint. Why is this injury considered a medical emergency?
options: Compression of the trachea and esophagus
correctOption: Compression of the trachea and esophagus
optionExplanations: Pneumothorax is a lung injury, not a direct result of this dislocation.
A posterior sternoclavicular dislocation can compress vital structures like the trachea and esophagus located immediately behind the joint.
The deltoid is not related to this joint.
The scapular spine is posterior and unrelated.
Diaphragm paralysis involves the phrenic nerve, which is deeper and more lateral.
citation: Posterior dislocation ... is the more serious one because the displaced clavicle may press on the trachea, the esophagus, and major blood vessels in the root of the neck.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A football player is tackled and lands directly on the 'point' of his shoulder. He has a prominent 'step-off' deformity at the lateral end of his clavicle. What is the clinical name for this injury?
optionExplanations: Shoulder dislocation refers to the glenohumeral joint.
Shoulder separation is the clinical term for acromioclavicular joint dislocation.
Frozen shoulder is adhesive capsulitis.
Rotator cuff tear involves tendons, not the AC joint bony alignment.
A fracture involves a break in the bone, not a joint displacement.
citation: a severe blow on the point of the shoulder ... can result in the acromion being thrust beneath the lateral end of the clavicle, tearing the coracoclavicular ligament. This condition is known as shoulder separation.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A therapist is using surface landmarks to locate the T3 spinous process. Which scapular feature should they align with to find this level?
options: Medial end of the scapular spine
correctOption: Medial end of the scapular spine
optionExplanations: Superior angle is at T2.
Inferior angle is at T7.
Coracoid process is anterior.
The medial end of the scapular spine aligns with the T3 spinous process.
The acromion is the lateral end of the spine.
citation: the medial border of the scapula, which it joins at the level of the third thoracic spine

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Which of the following is a unique developmental feature of the clavicle compared to other bones of the appendicular skeleton?
options: It is the first bone to begin ossification.
It ossifies entirely from three primary centers.
It is the last bone to finish ossification.
It lacks a medullary cavity throughout life.
It develops solely via endochondral ossification.
correctOption: It is the first bone to begin ossification.
optionExplanations: The clavicle is the first bone in the human body to begin the process of ossification.
The text does not state it has three primary centers; it focuses on the timing of initiation.
The clavicle is the first to start, not necessarily the last to finish.
While it is a long bone without a typical medullary cavity, the text emphasizes its ossification timing.
The clavicle actually undergoes intramembranous ossification, though the text focuses on its timing.
citation: It is the first bone to begin ossification.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 19-year-old athlete falls on his shoulder during a rugby match. Radiographs confirm a fracture at the junction of the middle and outer thirds of the clavicle. Which muscle is primarily responsible for the superior displacement of the medial fragment?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis major pulls the lateral fragment medially and forward.
Trapezius is unable to hold the lateral fragment against the weight of the arm.
The sternocleidomastoid muscle attaches to the medial third and tilts it upward after a fracture.
Subclavius is too small to cause significant displacement of the medial fragment.
The deltoid pulls the lateral fragment downward and medially.
citation: The medial end is tilted upward by the sternocleidomastoid muscle.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Following a radical mastectomy, a patient notices that her medial scapular border protrudes significantly when she pushes against a wall. This clinical sign is most likely associated with which of the following?
options: Injury to the dorsal scapular nerve
optionExplanations: Trapezius paralysis causes a 'dropped shoulder,' not winging.
Thoracodorsal nerve injury affects the latissimus dorsi, causing weak extension.
Winging of the scapula is the classic sign of serratus anterior paralysis, often due to long thoracic nerve injury.
Dorsal scapular nerve injury affects the rhomboids, which retract the scapula.
Rhomboid rupture would affect retraction but does not typically cause classic winging.
citation: winged scapula (Fig. 3.4), caused by paralysis of the serratus anterior.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: During a physical examination, the inferior angle of the scapula is used as a surface landmark. At which anatomical level does this structure typically lie?
options: Spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra.
correctOption: Spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra.
optionExplanations: The superior angle lies at the level of T2.
The root of the scapular spine lies at the level of T3.
T5 is between the spine and the inferior angle.
The inferior angle of the scapula corresponds to the level of the 7th rib and the T7 spinous process.
T9 is well below the scapular framework.
citation: marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient presents with wasting of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles. The clinician suspects entrapment at the scapular notch by the superior transverse scapular ligament. Which structure is specifically compressed within the notch?
options: Suprascapular nerve passes inferior to it
correctOption: Suprascapular nerve passes inferior to it
optionExplanations: The suprascapular artery typically passes superior to the ligament ('Army over the bridge').
The suprascapular nerve passes through the notch, inferior to the ligament ('Navy under the bridge').
The circumflex scapular artery arises from the subscapular artery and does not pass through this notch.
The dorsal scapular nerve stays medial to the scapula.
The subscapular nerves arise from the posterior cord in the axilla.
citation: the suprascapular nerve passes inferior to it

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
