statement: A 50-year-old carpenter complains of chronic shoulder pain. Examination reveals that the tendons of the subscapularis, supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and teres minor are intact but inflamed. What is the primary functional role of this collective group of muscles?
options: Suspending the limb from the clavicle
Providing the main force for extension
optionExplanations: Adduction is primarily performed by Pec major and Latissimus dorsi.
While subscapularis medially rotates, the group's collective role is stability.
The rotator cuff muscles fuse with the joint capsule to stabilize the head of the humerus in the glenoid cavity.
The coracoclavicular ligament is primarily responsible for suspending the limb's weight.
Extension is primarily the role of the posterior deltoid and latissimus dorsi.
citation: The cuff plays a very important role in stabilizing the glenohumeral (shoulder) joint.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Where is the most common site for a fracture of the clavicle to occur?
options: Junction of the middle and outer thirds.
correctOption: Junction of the middle and outer thirds.
optionExplanations: The sternal end is thickened and blunt, rarely the site of fracture.
The acromial end is flattened but not the weakest point.
The junction of the middle and outer thirds is the weakest point and the most common fracture site.
The conoid tubercle is a landmark on the inferior surface, not a common fracture site.
The medial half is generally stronger than the junction with the lateral third.
citation: breaks at its weakest point, the junction of the middle and outer thirds.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A newborn is found to have a congenital absence of the sternocostal head of the pectoralis major. As the child grows, which functional deficit will be most apparent?
options: Weakness in adduction and medial rotation of the shoulder joint.
Loss of the ability to shrug
correctOption: Weakness in adduction and medial rotation of the shoulder joint.
optionExplanations: Scapular protraction is primarily the function of the serratus anterior.
The pectoralis major is a powerful adductor and medial rotator; its absence significantly weakens these actions.
Shrugging is the function of the trapezius and levator scapulae.
Abduction is initiated by the supraspinatus.
Elbow extension is the function of the triceps brachii.
citation: this causes weakness in adduction and medial rotation of the shoulder joint.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient is unable to begin lifting his arm away from his side but can complete the movement if the arm is initially raised to 20 degrees. Which muscle is likely damaged?
options: Supraspinatus muscle initiates the movement of abduction
correctOption: Supraspinatus muscle initiates the movement of abduction
optionExplanations: The deltoid takes over abduction after the first 15-20 degrees.
Pectoralis minor is involved in scapular stabilization and depression.
The supraspinatus is responsible for the first 15 degrees of abduction.
Teres major is an adductor and medial rotator.
Serratus anterior rotates the scapula for abduction above 90 degrees.
citation: The supraspinatus muscle initiates the movement of abduction

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: During an axillary lymph node dissection, the surgeon must be careful to identify a nerve lying on the medial wall of the axilla. Why is this nerve particularly vulnerable to trauma in this region?
options: It passes through the quadrangular space
It is embedded within the subscapularis
It descends over the superficial surface of the serratus anterior muscle, which it supplies
It winds around the surgical neck
It is protected by the clavicle
correctOption: It descends over the superficial surface of the serratus anterior muscle, which it supplies
optionExplanations: The axillary nerve passes through the quadrangular space.
The subscapular nerves supply the subscapularis.
The long thoracic nerve is unusual because it lies on the superficial surface of its target muscle, making it prone to injury.
The axillary nerve winds around the surgical neck of the humerus.
The long thoracic nerve is in the axilla, well below the clavicle's protection.
citation: descends over the superficial surface of the serratus anterior muscle, which it supplies

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: The scapula is a flat, triangular bone. How many borders are described in its standard anatomical orientation?
optionExplanations: The scapula has surfaces (costal/dorsal), not just two borders.
The scapula is defined by three borders: superior, medial (vertebral), and lateral (axillary).
There is no distinct 'inferior border'; the medial and lateral borders meet at the inferior angle.
The spine is a process, not a border.
The scapula is triangular, not circular.
citation: three borders (superior, medial, lateral)

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient presents to the clinic with a visible deformity where one shoulder sits significantly lower than the other. Physical examination suggests a cranial nerve injury. What is the most likely anatomical correlation?
options: Winging due to serratus anterior loss
Dropped shoulder, which occurs with paralysis of the trapezius
Ape hand due to median nerve loss
Wrist drop due to radial nerve loss
Claw hand due to ulnar nerve loss
correctOption: Dropped shoulder, which occurs with paralysis of the trapezius
optionExplanations: Winging is a protrusion, not a vertical drop of the shoulder girdle.
Paralysis of the trapezius (supplied by the spinal accessory nerve) leads to a 'dropped shoulder' because the muscle can no longer elevate the scapula.
Ape hand is a hand deformity, not a shoulder deformity.
Wrist drop affects the forearm and hand.
Claw hand is a distal nerve injury manifestation.
citation: dropped shoulder, which occurs with paralysis of the trapezius

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Six weeks after a midshaft clavicular fracture, a patient complains of persistent, sharp pain over the side of the neck and the point of the shoulder. What is the most likely anatomical cause?
options: Injury to the long thoracic nerve
Involvement of the supraclavicular nerves in callus formation
correctOption: Involvement of the supraclavicular nerves in callus formation
optionExplanations: The axillary nerve is related to the humerus, not the clavicle.
The long thoracic nerve is deep in the axilla.
The supraclavicular nerves cross the clavicle and can become trapped in the healing bone (callus), causing referred pain.
SCM injury would cause neck tilt, not necessarily localized shoulder point pain.
While possible, nerve entrapment in a callus is a specific complication mentioned for clavicle fractures.
citation: close relationship of the supraclavicular nerves to the clavicle may result in their involvement in callus formation

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A physical therapist is measuring the range of motion in a patient's shoulder. During 90 degrees of total arm abduction, how much of that movement is expected to have occurred specifically at the glenohumeral joint?
optionExplanations: 30 degrees would be the scapular contribution (1/3 of 90).
45 degrees is a 1:1 ratio, which is incorrect.
Based on the 2:1 ratio (2 degrees glenohumeral for every 1 degree scapular), 60 degrees occurs at the joint.
90 degrees would mean no scapular rotation occurred.
120 degrees exceeds the total movement described.
citation: For every 3° of abduction of the arm, a 2° abduction occurs in the shoulder joint and a 1° abduction occurs by rotation of the scapula.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient is diagnosed with a 'Grade III acromioclavicular separation.' Radiographs show the scapula has dropped significantly away from the lateral clavicle. The rupture of which structure is primarily responsible for this vertical displacement?
optionExplanations: The AC ligament prevents horizontal displacement but is weak.
The coracoacromial ligament forms a roof over the humerus, it doesn't connect to the clavicle.
The coracoclavicular ligament is the primary weight-bearing ligament suspending the scapula from the clavicle.
The sternoclavicular ligament is at the medial end of the clavicle.
The costoclavicular ligament anchors the medial clavicle to the first rib.
citation: largely responsible for suspending the weight of the scapula and the upper limb from the clavicle

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Which nerves provide the motor supply to the subscapularis muscle?
optionExplanations: The suprascapular nerve supplies supraspinatus and infraspinatus.
The axillary nerve supplies deltoid and teres minor.
The thoracodorsal nerve supplies latissimus dorsi.
The subscapularis is unique in being supplied by both the upper and lower subscapular nerves.
The dorsal scapular nerve supplies the rhomboids and levator scapulae.
citation: Upper and lower subscapular nerves

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A surgeon is using the pectoralis minor as a landmark to identify the three parts of the axillary artery. To which bony landmark of the scapula does this muscle attach?
optionExplanations: The acromion is the insertion for the trapezius and origin for the deltoid.
The spine is an attachment for trapezius and deltoid.
The glenoid labrum is a fibrocartilage ring, not a muscle insertion site.
The pectoralis minor inserts into the coracoid process, which it pulls forward and downward.
The infraglenoid tubercle is the origin for the long head of the triceps.
citation: insert into the coracoid process of the scapula.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient has a midshaft humeral fracture. The physician notes that the proximal fragment is abducted. This is due to the pull of the muscle that attaches to the 'rough triangular elevation' on the lateral humerus. What is this landmark?
optionExplanations: The greater tubercle is on the proximal end, not the midshaft.
The lesser tubercle is on the anterior proximal end.
The deltoid tuberosity is the midshaft landmark where the deltoid inserts.
The radial groove houses the radial nerve but is not a muscle attachment elevation.
The medial epicondyle is at the distal end of the humerus.
citation: serves as the attachment area for the deltoid muscle.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient is unable to scratch the small of his back, a movement requiring internal rotation and adduction of the humerus. Which muscle of the scapular region is a primary contributor to these specific actions?
optionExplanations: Supraspinatus is an abductor.
Infraspinatus is a lateral rotator.
Teres minor is a lateral rotator.
Teres major adducts and medially rotates the arm, assisting in reaching behind the back.
Levator scapulae acts on the scapula, not the humerus.
citation: Adducts and medially rotates arm

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient presents with difficulty shrugging their shoulders. While the trapezius is the main muscle involved, which other muscle of the back group assists in this specific movement?
optionExplanations: Latissimus dorsi depresses the shoulder girdle.
The levator scapulae, as its name suggests, elevates the scapula.
Serratus anterior protracts and rotates the scapula.
Pectoralis minor depresses the scapula.
Subclavius steadies the clavicle.
citation: Elevates scapula

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient undergoes surgery in the lower axilla. Post-operatively, they exhibit significant weakness when trying to climb a rope or perform a pull-up. Which nerve was likely injured?
optionExplanations: Long thoracic nerve injury causes winging, not weakness in powerful extension.
Axillary nerve injury affects abduction.
The thoracodorsal nerve supplies the latissimus dorsi, the 'climbing muscle' used for powerful extension and adduction.
The lower subscapular nerve supplies teres major and subscapularis.
The radial nerve supplies the triceps and extensors.
citation: Thoracodorsal nerve

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: During a physical exam, a clinician notes that the patient's clavicle seems unstable during rapid arm movements. Which small muscle is primarily responsible for steadying this bone against the sternum and first rib?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis minor acts on the scapula.
The subclavius muscle depresses and steadies the clavicle during shoulder girdle movements.
Serratus anterior acts on the scapula.
SCM primarily rotates the head or elevates the medial clavicle.
Trapezius acts on the scapula and lateral clavicle.
citation: Depresses the clavicle and steadies this bone during movements of the shoulder girdle

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A radiologist is reviewing a contrast study of the shoulder vasculature. They identify a vessel passing just above the superior transverse scapular ligament. Which vessel is this?
optionExplanations: The nerve passes inferior to the ligament.
The suprascapular artery passes superior to the superior transverse scapular ligament.
Circumflex humeral arteries are related to the surgical neck of the humerus.
The subscapular artery is in the axilla.
The thoracoacromial artery arises from the second part of the axillary artery.
citation: the suprascapular artery passes superior to this ligament

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: An MRI of a baseball pitcher's shoulder shows a tear in the fibrocartilage rim of the glenoid fossa. What is the primary anatomical purpose of this rim?
options: To serve as the origin for the deltoid
To broaden and deepen the joint cavity.
To attach the clavicle to the scapula
correctOption: To broaden and deepen the joint cavity.
optionExplanations: Synovial fluid is secreted by the synovial membrane.
The deltoid originates from the spine, acromion, and clavicle.
The glenoid labrum is a fibrocartilage ring that increases the surface area and depth of the shallow glenoid cavity.
Rotation is limited by muscles and ligaments, not primarily the labrum.
The coracoclavicular ligament attaches the clavicle to the scapula.
citation: serves to broaden and deepen the joint cavity.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient presents with a midshaft clavicle fracture. The lateral fragment is displaced inferiorly and anteriorly. Which factor is most responsible for the inferior displacement (depression) of this fragment?
optionExplanations: SCM elevates the medial fragment.
Trapezius is too weak to counteract the weight of the arm once the strut is broken.
The weight of the upper limb naturally depresses the lateral fragment once the clavicular support is lost.
Subclavius may pull it slightly medially but weight is the primary depressor.
Serratus anterior acts on the scapula, not directly on the clavicular fragment.
citation: lateral fragment is depressed by the weight of the arm and is pulled medially and forward by the strong adductor muscles

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 12-year-old boy is brought to the ER after falling from a bicycle. He landed directly on his right shoulder and is now holding his arm against his chest. Radiographs confirm a fracture at the junction of the middle and outer thirds of the collar bone. What is the most likely mechanism of this injury?
optionExplanations: A direct blow to the sternum would more likely cause sternal injury or sternoclavicular dislocation.
The text explicitly states that clavicle fractures usually occur due to a fall on the shoulder or outstretched hand.
Hyperextension of the neck is associated with cervical spine injuries, not clavicle fractures.
Repetitive overhead lifting is a cause of tendinitis, not acute clavicle fractures.
Forced adduction is not the standard mechanism for transmitting force to break the clavicle strut.
citation: The fracture usually occurs as a result of a fall on the shoulder or outstretched hand.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: During a physical examination of a patient with a mid-shaft clavicular fracture, the physician notes that the proximal fragment of the bone is significantly elevated. Which muscle is primarily responsible for this upward displacement?
optionExplanations: The trapezius attaches to the lateral third and would not elevate the medial fragment.
The pectoralis major pulls the lateral fragment medially and forward, not the medial fragment upward.
The text states the medial end is tilted upward by the sternocleidomastoid muscle after a fracture.
The subclavius muscle actually tends to depress the clavicle.
The deltoid attaches to the lateral fragment and contributes to its downward displacement.
citation: The medial end is tilted upward by the sternocleidomastoid muscle.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A rugby player sustains a clavicular fracture. On examination, the lateral fragment of the bone is depressed and displaced anteriorly. Which muscle is the main driver of this medial and forward pull on the distal fragment?
optionExplanations: The serratus anterior acts on the scapula, not directly on the fractured clavicle fragments.
The text specifies that the lateral fragment is pulled medially and forward by the pectoralis major.
While an adductor, the latissimus dorsi does not have the specific anatomical pull to displace the clavicle forward.
Teres major is an adductor but is not cited as the primary force for this specific clavicular displacement.
The sternohyoid is a neck muscle and does not influence clavicular fragment displacement.
citation: is pulled medially and forward by the strong adductor muscles of the shoulder joint, especially the pectoralis major.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Which of the following is a unique developmental feature of the clavicle compared to other bones in the human body?
options: It is the last bone to ossify
It is the first bone to begin ossification
It only has one primary center
correctOption: It is the first bone to begin ossification
optionExplanations: The clavicle is the first, not the last, to begin the process.
The clavicle undergoes intramembranous ossification, unlike most long bones.
The text explicitly identifies the clavicle as the first bone to begin ossification.
While it has unique features, being the 'first to ossify' is the primary fact mentioned in the text.
The text does not focus on the number of centers, but rather the timing of the start of the process.
citation: It is the first bone to begin ossification.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient presents with a deformity where the medial border of the scapula protrudes significantly from the posterior thoracic wall when pushing against a wall. This 'winging' is most likely due to the paralysis of which muscle?
optionExplanations: Rhomboids retract the scapula; their loss does not typically cause classic winging.
Levator scapulae elevates the scapula.
Trapezius paralysis causes a 'dropped shoulder,' not winging.
The text states that a winged scapula is caused by paralysis of the serratus anterior.
Pectoralis minor pulls the scapula forward and downward but is not responsible for keeping the medial border against the ribs.
citation: winged scapula (Fig. 3.4), caused by paralysis of the serratus anterior.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 30-year-old woman undergoes a lymph node biopsy in the posterior triangle of the neck. Post-operatively, she complains of an inability to shrug her shoulder, and the shoulder on the affected side appears lower than the other. What is the most likely diagnosis?
optionExplanations: Winging involves the medial border protruding, not just a lower shoulder height.
The text defines 'dropped shoulder' as the result of trapezius paralysis.
A clavicle fracture would present with localized pain and bony deformity, not just a 'drop'.
Dislocation would involve loss of the rounded shoulder contour and severe pain.
Rotator cuff tears affect abduction and rotation, not the resting height of the shoulder girdle.
citation: dropped shoulder, which occurs with paralysis of the trapezius.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Regarding the superior transverse scapular ligament that bridges the scapular notch, which structure normally passes inferior to the ligament (through the notch)?
optionExplanations: The suprascapular artery passes superior to the ligament ('Army over the bridge').
The text states the suprascapular nerve passes inferior to the ligament ('Navy under the bridge').
The circumflex scapular artery is related to the lateral border/axillary border of the scapula.
The dorsal scapular nerve runs along the medial border of the scapula.
The subscapular artery is a branch of the third part of the axillary artery.
citation: the suprascapular nerve passes inferior to it

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: The inferior angle of the scapula is an important clinical landmark. At which vertebral level is it typically located in a living subject?
optionExplanations: T2 corresponds to the superior angle of the scapula.
T3 corresponds to the level where the spine of the scapula meets the medial border.
T5 is between the spine and the inferior angle.
The text explicitly states the inferior angle marks the level of the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra (T7).
T9 is well below the scapular framework.
citation: marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A clinician is attempting to locate the coracoid process of the scapula for an injection. Where should they apply deep pressure to palpate this structure?
options: Through the anterior part of the deltoid
correctOption: Through the anterior part of the deltoid
optionExplanations: The acromion is lateral and posterior to the coracoid process.
The sternoclavicular joint is at the medial end of the clavicle, far from the coracoid.
The text states the coracoid process can be palpated via deep pressure through the anterior part of the deltoid muscle.
The supraspinous fossa is on the posterior surface of the scapula.
The vertebral border is the medial edge, opposite the side of the coracoid process.
citation: It can be palpated via deep pressure through the anterior part of the deltoid muscle

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A newborn is diagnosed with Poland syndrome, which includes the congenital absence of the pectoralis major muscle. Which movements of the shoulder joint will be most significantly weakened in this child?
optionExplanations: Abduction is performed by the deltoid and supraspinatus.
Elevation and retraction are functions of the trapezius and rhomboids.
The text notes that absence of the pectoralis major causes weakness in adduction and medial rotation.
Protraction is primarily a function of the serratus anterior.
Extension is primarily handled by the latissimus dorsi and posterior deltoid.
citation: this causes weakness in adduction and medial rotation of the shoulder joint.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Following a radical mastectomy, a patient notices that she can no longer reach the high shelves in her kitchen with her affected arm. Physical exam reveals the medial border of the scapula is displaced from the chest wall. Which nerve was likely damaged during surgery?
optionExplanations: The thoracodorsal nerve supplies the latissimus dorsi; injury causes weak extension/adduction.
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid; injury causes weak abduction.
The text mentions the long thoracic nerve is at risk during radical mastectomy, leading to winged scapula and inability to rotate the scapula for high reaching.
The suprascapular nerve supplies the supraspinatus and infraspinatus.
The dorsal scapular nerve supplies the rhomboids and levator scapulae.
citation: trauma to the upper lateral chest wall (e.g., during the surgical procedure of radical mastectomy) may injure the long thoracic nerve

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: At which anatomical level does the spine of the scapula meet its medial (vertebral) border?
optionExplanations: T1 is too high for the scapular spine junction.
T2 is the level of the superior angle.
The text states the spine joins the medial border at the level of the third thoracic spine (T3).
T4 is below the junction point.
T5 is further down the medial border.
citation: medial border of the scapula, which it joins at the level of the third thoracic spine

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient presents with a complete fracture of the clavicle. The entire shoulder girdle on that side appears to have collapsed medially and inferiorly. This occurs because the clavicle has lost its primary mechanical role as a:
optionExplanations: The biceps does not use the clavicle as a lever.
While it provides some protection, its primary mechanical role is structural support.
The text describes the clavicle as a light strut connecting the upper limb to the thorax, allowing free movement.
The deltoid originates from the clavicle but does not use it as a pulley.
The clavicle transmits forces but is not primarily a 'shock absorber for the neck'.
citation: The clavicle forms a light strut that connects the upper limb to the thorax

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Six weeks after a mid-shaft clavicular fracture, a patient complains of persistent, nagging pain over the side of the neck. What is the most likely anatomical explanation for this late-onset symptom?
optionExplanations: Brachial plexus compression usually causes symptoms in the arm/hand, not the neck.
Arterial issues would present with vascular deficits (pallor, pulselessness).
The text notes that supraclavicular nerves can be involved in callus formation, causing persistent neck pain.
A muscle tear would be an acute injury, not a symptom developing during healing.
Non-union causes instability and localized bone pain, not necessarily referred neck pain.
citation: The close relationship of the supraclavicular nerves to the clavicle may result in their involvement in callus formation

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Which of the following best describes the general morphological shape of the clavicle?
optionExplanations: A C-shape does not account for the double curve of the clavicle.
L-shaped bones are not found in the shoulder girdle.
The text explicitly describes the clavicle as roughly S-shaped.
The clavicle is curved to act as a strut and transmit forces.
The scapula is triangular, but the clavicle is a curved long bone.
citation: It is roughly S-shaped and resembles a large, old-style key.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient is unable to lift his arm above the level of his shoulder (90 degrees) despite having a functional deltoid muscle. This deficit is most likely due to an inability to perform which scapular movement?
optionExplanations: Retraction is pulling the scapula toward the spine.
Elevation is shrugging.
The text explains that paralysis of the serratus anterior results in the inability to rotate the scapula for abduction above 90 degrees.
Depression is moving the scapula downward.
Adduction of the scapula is the same as retraction.
citation: inability to rotate the scapula during abduction of the arm above a right angle.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Where does the pectoralis minor muscle insert?
optionExplanations: The acromion is the insertion for the trapezius and origin for the deltoid.
The greater tuberosity is the insertion for three rotator cuff muscles.
The text states the pectoralis minor inserts into the coracoid process of the scapula.
The medial border is the insertion for the rhomboids and serratus anterior.
The subclavius inserts into the clavicle, not the pectoralis minor.
citation: runs upward and laterally to insert into the coracoid process of the scapula.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Which nerve provides the motor supply to the subclavius muscle?
optionExplanations: The long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior.
The lateral pectoral nerve supplies the pectoralis major.
The text explicitly states the nerve to the subclavius supplies the subclavius muscle.
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid and teres minor.
The suprascapular nerve supplies the supraspinatus and infraspinatus.
citation: The nerve to the subclavius (C5, 6) supplies the subclavius muscle

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Which part of the scapula articulates with the head of the humerus to form the shoulder joint?
optionExplanations: The coracoid process is for muscle and ligament attachments.
The acromion articulates with the clavicle.
The text identifies the glenoid cavity as the articular surface for the head of the humerus.
The subscapular fossa is the anterior surface of the scapula.
The supraspinous fossa is the area above the scapular spine.
citation: The lateral surface of the head forms a shallow articular surface, the glenoid cavity or fossa

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A victim of an automobile crash is found to have a fractured scapula. The surgeon decides on conservative management rather than surgery. What anatomical factor allows most scapular fractures to require little direct treatment?
options: The bone has a very high density
The presence of the glenoid labrum
optionExplanations: While not weight-bearing in the traditional sense, this is not the reason for stable healing.
Scapular bone is actually quite thin in the fossae.
The text states that muscles on the anterior and posterior surfaces adequately splint the fragments.
The labrum is part of the joint, not the bone shaft stability.
Skin does not provide enough structural support to splint a fractured bone.
citation: the muscles on the anterior and posterior surfaces adequately splint the fragments.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A gymnast falls and lands on her outstretched hand. She immediately feels a snap in her collar region. Anatomically, why is the clavicle so susceptible to fracture in this scenario?
options: It is the only bone in the shoulder
It transmits all forces to the trunk
It is held by weak ligaments
It is composed of spongy bone only
correctOption: It transmits all forces to the trunk
optionExplanations: The scapula is also part of the shoulder girdle.
The clavicle has a periosteum like other bones.
The text states the clavicle is the sole link to the axial skeleton and transmits all forces from the limb to the trunk.
The ligaments of the clavicle (coracoclavicular) are actually very strong.
The clavicle is a long bone with a compact bone shell.
citation: transmits all forces from the upper limb to the trunk.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient presents with numbness in the hand and a weakened radial pulse when the shoulder is depressed. The physician suspects compression of the neurovascular bundle. Between which two structures is this compression most likely occurring?
optionExplanations: The acromion is lateral to the neurovascular bundle.
The text states the interval between the clavicle and the first rib may become narrowed, compressing nerves and vessels.
The coracoid-humeral space is related to the biceps tendon and rotator cuff.
The neurovascular bundle passes over the first rib, not between the first and second.
The scapulohumeral space is the joint cavity.
citation: The interval between the clavicle and the first rib in some patients may become narrowed

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Which border of the scapula is also known as the vertebral border?
optionExplanations: The superior border is the shortest and thinnest edge.
The lateral border is also called the axillary border.
The axillary border is the lateral border.
The text explicitly identifies the medial border as the vertebral border.
The inferior angle is a point, not a border.
citation: The medial (vertebral) border is the long, medial edge of the scapula, located closest to the vertebral column.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient has difficulty retracting their scapula (pulling the shoulders back). A nerve conduction study shows a lesion of the dorsal scapular nerve. Which muscles are affected?
optionExplanations: Trapezius is supplied by CN XI; Deltoid by the axillary nerve.
Serratus anterior is supplied by the long thoracic nerve.
The text states the dorsal scapular nerve supplies the levator scapulae and rhomboid muscles.
These are supplied by the suprascapular nerve.
These are supplied by the pectoral nerves.
citation: It supplies the levator scapulae and rhomboid muscles.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient presents with a 'dropped shoulder' following a deep laceration to the posterior triangle of the neck. The physician suspects damage to a cranial nerve. Which nerve is responsible for the motor supply of the muscle that maintains the shoulder's height?
optionExplanations: The vagus nerve does not supply shoulder girdle muscles.
The hypoglossal nerve supplies the tongue.
The text states the accessory nerve (CN XI) provides motor fibers to the trapezius.
The phrenic nerve supplies the diaphragm.
The facial nerve supplies muscles of facial expression.
citation: The accessory nerve provides motor fibers to the trapezius

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 19-year-old rugby player is brought to the ER after a heavy tackle. He is holding his right arm against his chest, and a deformity is noted at the mid-shaft of his collar bone. Which of the following most likely describes the mechanism of this injury?
optionExplanations: The text explicitly states that clavicle fractures usually result from a fall on the shoulder or an outstretched hand.
Direct blows to the sternum are less common causes of clavicle fractures compared to shoulder impacts.
Neck hyperextension does not typically transmit the forces required to break the clavicle.
Traction injuries are more likely to cause dislocations or nerve avulsions than mid-shaft clavicle fractures.
Overhead lifting is associated with chronic stress but not acute mid-shaft fractures.
citation: The fracture usually occurs as a result of a fall on the shoulder or outstretched hand.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 45-year-old man presents with a fracture at the junction of the middle and outer thirds of the clavicle. Physical examination reveals that the medial fragment of the bone is significantly elevated. Which muscle is primarily responsible for this upward displacement?
optionExplanations: The sternocleidomastoid muscle attaches to the medial part of the clavicle and pulls it upward when the bone is fractured.
The pectoralis major pulls the lateral fragment medially and forward, not the medial fragment upward.
The trapezius attaches to the lateral third and does not tilt the medial fragment upward.
The subclavius is a weak depressor and would not cause upward tilting.
The deltoid attaches to the lateral fragment and contributes to its depression.
citation: The medial end is tilted upward by the sternocleidomastoid muscle.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Following a high-velocity motor vehicle accident, a patient is diagnosed with a displaced clavicular fracture. The lateral fragment is found to be pulled medially and forward. Which muscle is the primary driver of this specific displacement?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis major is a strong adductor that pulls the lateral fragment medially and forward after a fracture.
The sternocleidomastoid acts on the medial fragment, not the lateral one.
The serratus anterior acts on the scapula and does not directly displace clavicular fragments.
While the latissimus dorsi is an adductor, the text specifically highlights the pectoralis major's role in this displacement.
The teres major is an adductor but is not the primary muscle cited for this specific clavicular displacement.
citation: The lateral fragment is depressed by the weight of the arm and is pulled medially and forward by the strong adductor muscles of the shoulder joint, especially the pectoralis major.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Which of the following bones is the very first to begin the process of ossification in the human fetus?
optionExplanations: The clavicle is unique as the first bone in the body to start ossifying.
The scapula begins ossification later than the clavicle.
Long bones like the humerus follow the clavicle in the ossification sequence.
The femur is a major long bone but is not the first to ossify.
The sternum ossifies much later from multiple centers.
citation: The clavicle... is the first bone to begin ossification.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient who recently underwent a radical mastectomy for breast cancer now complains that her shoulder blade protrudes backward when she pushes against a wall. This 'winging' of the scapula indicates damage to the nerve supplying which muscle?
optionExplanations: Winging of the scapula is the classic clinical sign of serratus anterior paralysis.
Trapezius paralysis leads to a dropped shoulder, not typical winging.
Rhomboid paralysis causes weakness in retraction but not the classic winged appearance.
Latissimus dorsi paralysis affects arm extension and adduction.
Levator scapulae paralysis affects scapular elevation.
citation: winged scapula (Fig. 3.4), caused by paralysis of the serratus anterior.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 22-year-old man is brought to the clinic with a deep laceration in the posterior triangle of his neck. On examination, his right shoulder is noticeably lower than the left, and he has difficulty shrugging. Which clinical condition is most likely present?
optionExplanations: A dropped shoulder is the result of trapezius paralysis, often due to spinal accessory nerve injury in the neck.
Winged scapula involves the serratus anterior, not the trapezius.
Ape hand is a median nerve injury affecting the thumb.
Wrist drop is a radial nerve injury.
Claw hand is typically an ulnar nerve injury.
citation: dropped shoulder, which occurs with paralysis of the trapezius

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: In a living subject, the inferior angle of the scapula serves as a surface landmark for which of the following structures?
optionExplanations: The inferior angle is at the level of the 7th rib and T7 spine.
The superior angle is closer to the level of the 2nd rib.
The spine of the scapula typically aligns with the 3rd thoracic vertebra.
The 5th rib is superior to the inferior angle of the scapula.
The 1st thoracic vertebra is far superior to the inferior angle.
citation: The inferior angle of the scapula... marks the level of the seventh rib and the spine of the seventh thoracic vertebra.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: During a surgical exploration of the superior border of the scapula, a surgeon identifies the superior transverse scapular ligament. To avoid injuring the suprascapular nerve, where should the surgeon look for it in relation to this ligament?
optionExplanations: The suprascapular nerve passes through the notch, which is inferior to the bridging ligament.
The suprascapular artery, not the nerve, passes superior to the ligament.
The nerve is medial to the coracoid process, within the scapular notch.
The medial border is on the opposite side of the scapula.
The subscapular fossa is on the anterior surface, while the notch is on the superior border.
citation: Normally, the suprascapular artery passes superior to this ligament, whereas the suprascapular nerve passes inferior to it

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Where is the conoid tubercle of the clavicle anatomically located?
optionExplanations: The conoid tubercle is found on the inferior surface near the lateral (acromial) end.
The superior surface of the clavicle is relatively smooth.
The sternal end is medial and does not possess the conoid tubercle.
The tubercle is on the inferior, not superior, surface.
The mid-shaft does not contain this specific ligamentous attachment landmark.
citation: The conoid tubercle (cono- is Greek for “pine cone”) is a small, roughened elevation on the inferior surface, near the acromial end.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: The name of which scapular process is derived from the Greek word for a 'crow's beak'?
optionExplanations: Coracoid comes from 'korax', meaning crow, referring to its beak-like shape.
Acromion means 'point of the shoulder'.
The spine is a ridge, not a beak-like projection.
The glenoid refers to a 'pit' or 'socket'.
The styloid process is found on the radius and ulna, not the scapula.
citation: The coracoid process (coraco- is Greek for “like a crow’s beak”) is a thick, beaklike structure

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 30-year-old weightlifter presents with numbness in his right hand and a weakened radial pulse when his arm is abducted. Imaging suggests a narrowing of the space between his collar bone and the highest rib. Which structure is most likely being compressed?
optionExplanations: The interval between the clavicle and the first rib contains the brachial plexus and subclavian vessels.
The axillary nerve is located deeper in the axilla, distal to this interval.
The long thoracic nerve is posterior to the plexus and less likely to be the primary cause of hand numbness in this context.
The phrenic nerve is in the neck and does not pass through this specific interval to the limb.
The vagus nerve does not enter the upper limb.
citation: The interval between the clavicle and the first rib in some patients may become narrowed and thus is responsible for compression of nerves and blood vessels.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A newborn is noted to have a lack of the normal anterior axillary fold on the left side. As the child grows, which pair of shoulder movements will likely be weaker compared to the unaffected side?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis major is a primary adductor and medial rotator; its absence causes weakness in these actions.
Abduction and lateral rotation are performed by the deltoid and rotator cuff muscles.
Elevation and retraction are functions of the trapezius and rhomboids.
Protraction is primarily the serratus anterior's role.
Supination is a function of the biceps and supinator, not the pectoralis major.
citation: The sternocostal origin is the most commonly missing part, and this causes weakness in adduction and medial rotation of the shoulder joint.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 55-year-old professional painter presents with chronic shoulder pain that worsens when he works overhead. An MRI confirms a tear in one of the rotator cuff tendons. Statistically, which muscle is most likely involved?
optionExplanations: The supraspinatus is the most frequently injured muscle of the rotator cuff due to its position under the acromion.
Infraspinatus tears are less common than supraspinatus tears.
Teres minor is the least commonly injured member of the rotator cuff.
Subscapularis tears occur but are less frequent than supraspinatus tears.
The teres major is not part of the rotator cuff.
citation: The supraspinatus is the most commonly injured muscle in the rotator cuff.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient is unable to begin moving his arm away from his side. However, if the examiner moves the arm to about 20 degrees of abduction, the patient can then lift it the rest of the way using his deltoid. This finding is diagnostic for a rupture of which tendon?
optionExplanations: The supraspinatus initiates the first 15-20 degrees of abduction; without it, the deltoid cannot effectively start the movement.
The pectoralis minor does not participate in abduction.
Serratus anterior paralysis affects rotation of the scapula, usually above 90 degrees.
The subscapularis is a medial rotator, not an abductor.
The long head of the biceps is a weak flexor and stabilizer, not the primary initiator of abduction.
citation: The patient with a ruptured supraspinatus tendon is unable to initiate abduction of the arm.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient presents with a 'dropped shoulder' and an inability to shrug against resistance following a lymph node biopsy in the neck. Which muscle has lost its motor supply?
optionExplanations: The spinal accessory nerve (CN XI) provides the motor supply to the trapezius.
The deltoid is supplied by the axillary nerve.
The levator scapulae is supplied by the dorsal scapular nerve and cervical nerves.
The rhomboids are supplied by the dorsal scapular nerve.
The serratus anterior is supplied by the long thoracic nerve.
citation: The spinal accessory nerve... provides motor fibers to the trapezius

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: During an axillary lymph node dissection, a nerve is identified descending on the superficial surface of the serratus anterior muscle. Injury to this nerve would result in which of the following?
options: Loss of sensation over the deltoid
optionExplanations: The long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior; its injury causes winged scapula.
Forearm extension is a radial nerve function.
Thumb adduction is an ulnar nerve function.
Deltoid sensation is an axillary nerve function.
Elbow flexion is primarily a musculocutaneous nerve function.
citation: The long thoracic nerve (C5 to 7) arises from the roots of the brachial plexus in the neck and enters the axilla by passing down over the lateral border of the first rib

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: What is the site of insertion for the pectoralis minor muscle?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis minor inserts into the coracoid process of the scapula.
The acromion is the insertion for the trapezius and origin for the deltoid.
The greater tubercle is the insertion for several rotator cuff muscles.
The medial border is the insertion for the rhomboids and serratus anterior.
The bicipital groove is the insertion for the pectoralis major, latissimus dorsi, and teres major.
citation: The pectoralis minor... arises from the third, fourth, and fifth ribs and runs upward and laterally to insert into the coracoid process of the scapula.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A baseball pitcher is diagnosed with an isolated tear of the subscapularis tendon. Which of the following actions will be most significantly impaired?
optionExplanations: The subscapularis is a powerful medial rotator of the humerus.
Lateral rotation is performed by the infraspinatus and teres minor.
Abduction is performed by the supraspinatus and deltoid.
Elevation of the scapula is performed by the levator scapulae and trapezius.
Supination is a forearm movement, not a shoulder movement.
citation: The subscapularis... Medially rotates arm and stabilizes shoulder joint

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient presents with weakness when trying to pull their arm toward their body (adduction) and rotating it inward. If the latissimus dorsi is intact, which other muscle of the scapular region likely shares these actions and might be injured?
optionExplanations: The teres major adducts and medially rotates the arm, similar to the latissimus dorsi.
The teres minor laterally rotates the arm.
The supraspinatus abducts the arm.
The infraspinatus laterally rotates the arm.
The serratus anterior protracts the scapula.
citation: The teres major... Adducts and medially rotates arm

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: An athlete complains of difficulty 'squaring' his shoulders. On examination, he has trouble pulling his shoulder blades toward the midline. Which muscles are primarily responsible for this retraction?
optionExplanations: The rhomboids are the primary retractors of the scapula.
The serratus anterior protracts the scapula (pulls it away from the midline).
The pectoralis minor depresses and protracts the scapula.
The subclavius steadies the clavicle.
The deltoid acts on the humerus, not primarily on scapular retraction.
citation: The rhomboid major and minor... Retract scapula

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient has a rare isolated nerve injury to the subclavius muscle. While functional loss is minimal, which of the following describes the primary anatomical role of this muscle?
optionExplanations: The subclavius depresses and steadies the clavicle during shoulder girdle movements.
It attaches to the first rib but its primary action is on the clavicle.
Retraction is a rhomboid function.
Lateral rotation is a rotator cuff function.
It depresses the clavicle rather than protracting it.
citation: The subclavius... Depresses clavicle and steadies this bone during movements of shoulder girdle

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient with an axillary nerve injury shows significant wasting of the shoulder contour. In addition to loss of abduction, which other set of movements would be weakened due to the loss of the anterior and posterior fibers of this muscle?
optionExplanations: The deltoid's anterior fibers flex the arm and its posterior fibers extend it.
The deltoid is an abductor, not an adductor.
Protraction and retraction are scapular movements, not primary deltoid actions.
Supination and pronation occur at the radioulnar joints.
Elevation and depression are scapular movements.
citation: The deltoid... Abducts arm; anterior fibers flex and medially rotate arm; posterior fibers extend and laterally rotate arm

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A rock climber finds it difficult to pull her body upward. This 'climbing' motion requires powerful extension and adduction of the humerus. Which muscle is the primary driver of this action?
optionExplanations: The latissimus dorsi is known as the 'climbing muscle' because it extends and adducts the arm.
The supraspinatus is an abductor.
The trapezius acts on the scapula, not primarily on humerus extension.
The serratus anterior protracts the scapula.
The levator scapulae elevates the scapula.
citation: The latissimus dorsi... Extends, adducts, and medially rotates arm

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A patient presents with isolated wasting of the infraspinatus muscle, but the supraspinatus muscle appears normal. A cyst is suspected to be compressing a nerve. At which anatomical location is the compression most likely occurring?
optionExplanations: Compression at the spinoglenoid notch affects the nerve after it has already supplied the supraspinatus, sparing it but affecting the infraspinatus.
Compression at the suprascapular notch would affect both the supraspinatus and infraspinatus.
The quadrangular space contains the axillary nerve.
The triangular space contains the circumflex scapular artery.
The bicipital groove contains the long head of the biceps tendon.
citation: The suprascapular nerve... supplies the supraspinatus muscle and then continues through the spinoglenoid notch into the infraspinous fossa

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: Six weeks after a mid-shaft clavicle fracture, a patient complains of persistent, sharp pain over the side of his neck and the point of his shoulder. This is most likely due to the entrapment of which nerves in the healing bone?
optionExplanations: The supraclavicular nerves are subcutaneous and cross the clavicle; they can become trapped in the healing callus.
The suprascapular nerve is deeper and passes through the scapular notch.
The axillary nerve is in the axilla, far from the clavicular mid-shaft.
The long thoracic nerve is on the chest wall.
The phrenic nerve is deep in the neck and does not cross the clavicle superficially.
citation: The close relationship of the supraclavicular nerves to the clavicle may result in their involvement in callus formation after fracture of the bone.

category: Bones of Upper Limb: Clavicle & Scapula
statement: A 40-year-old construction worker is rushed to the emergency department after falling from a scaffolding. He complains of intense pain in his upper back. Physical examination shows localized swelling and crepitus over the right shoulder blade, and imaging reveals a comminuted fracture of the scapula. Based on the anatomical position of this bone, which of the following associated injuries must the physician immediately rule out?
optionExplanations: The scapula lies on the posterior chest wall between the second and seventh ribs; high-energy trauma sufficient to fracture the scapula frequently causes concurrent fractures of the underlying thoracic cage.
The radial collateral ligament is located at the elbow joint and is not anatomically related to scapular trauma.
The distal radioulnar joint is located at the wrist and is not a typical associated injury in posterior thoracic trauma.
Scaphoid fractures typically result from a fall on an outstretched hand (FOOSH), whereas scapular fractures involve direct high-energy trauma to the back.
The flexor retinaculum is a structure of the carpal tunnel at the wrist, far removed from the scapular region.
citation: Injuries are usually associated with fractured ribs.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A 19-year-old rugby player is tackled from the side and presents to the ER with a visible protrusion at the root of his neck. Physical examination reveals the medial end of his collarbone is displaced. Which of the following is the most likely clinical finding?
options: Superior displacement of the first rib
optionExplanations: In an anterior dislocation, the medial end of the clavicle projects forward and is easily palpable under the skin.
Posterior displacement is less common and involves the clavicle moving toward the trachea.
Lateral displacement of the acromion refers to acromioclavicular joint injuries.
Inferior sagging of the humerus is seen in shoulder dislocations or nerve palsies.
The first rib is firmly anchored and does not typically displace superiorly in this context.
citation: Anterior dislocation results in the medial end of the clavicle projecting forward beneath the skin

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A 25-year-old male is brought to the trauma center following a high-speed motor vehicle accident. He complains of severe pain at the base of his neck and is experiencing significant difficulty breathing and swallowing. Which anatomical complication is most likely occurring?
optionExplanations: Posterior dislocation of the sternoclavicular joint is dangerous because the clavicle can compress vital structures like the trachea and esophagus.
The axillary artery is located more laterally in the axilla.
The brachial plexus is lateral to the sternoclavicular joint.
Surgical neck fractures affect the humerus, not the root of the neck.
Radial head dislocation occurs at the elbow.
citation: the displaced clavicle may press on the trachea, the esophagus, and major blood vessels

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: What is the structural classification of the sternoclavicular joint?
optionExplanations: The sternoclavicular joint is classified as a synovial double-plane joint due to the presence of an articular disc.
The glenohumeral joint is a ball-and-socket joint.
The humeroulnar joint is a hinge joint.
Primary cartilaginous joints (synchondroses) are found in growth plates.
A syndesmosis is a fibrous joint like the middle radioulnar joint.
citation: Type: Synovial double-plane joint.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: During a forensic autopsy, a medical examiner opens the sternoclavicular joint. He observes a structure that completely separates the joint cavity into medial and lateral spaces. What is the composition of this structure?

Hyaline cartilage covers the articulating surfaces of the bones but does not form the disc.
Elastic cartilage is found in the external ear and epiglottis.
Bone does not divide the joint cavity internally.
Adipose tissue may form fatty pads but not the structural articular disc.
citation: A flat fibrocartilaginous disc lies within the joint and divides the joint’s interior into two compartments

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint

The costoclavicular space is an area between the rib and clavicle, not a joint compartment.
The suprasternal notch is a landmark on the manubrium.
The subclavicular fossa is a surface landmark.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint

Long thoracic and thoracodorsal nerves supply muscles (serratus anterior and latissimus dorsi).
Median and ulnar nerves supply the forearm and hand.
Phrenic and vagus nerves supply the diaphragm and viscera.
citation: Nerve supply: Supraclavicular nerve and the nerve to the subclavius muscle.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint

The interclavicular ligament connects the two clavicles across the notch.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint

optionExplanations: The joint involves the sternal end of the clavicle, the manubrium of the sternum, and the first costal cartilage.
The xiphoid process is the inferior-most part of the sternum.
The acromion and coracoid are parts of the scapula.
The 2nd rib articulates at the sternal angle.
The first costal cartilage is a key component of the SC joint articulation.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint

The costal cartilage forms part of the surface but not a separate compartment.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint

There is no superior compartment in the SC joint.
There is no inferior compartment in the SC joint.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A 12-year-old boy falls onto his outstretched hand. He presents with severe pain in the mid-shaft of his collarbone. Why is a fracture more likely than a sternoclavicular dislocation in this scenario?

The sternum is part of the stable axial skeleton.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint

Manubrium fractures are rare and present differently.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint

statement: What is the structural classification of the sternoclavicular joint?

citation: It articulates with the clavicular notch of the sternum through a compound synovial joint containing an articular disc.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A patient with a congenital absence of the clavicles has shoulders that can be brought together in the midline. This demonstrates the loss of the clavicle's function as what type of mechanical structure?
optionExplanations: The clavicle acts as a lever for muscles, but its primary girdle role is a strut.
The acromion can act as a fulcrum in dislocations.
The clavicle acts as a strut connecting the limb to the thorax.
The trochlea of the humerus acts as a pulley.
The elbow is a hinge joint.
citation: The clavicle forms a light strut that connects the upper limb to the thorax and allows the limb to move freely from the trunk.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A 40-year-old woman falls on her outstretched hand. The force is transmitted through the radius, ulnar, humerus, and scapula. Which joint serves as the final link for transmitting this force to the axial skeleton?
optionExplanations: The glenohumeral joint is the first link in the girdle.
The acromioclavicular joint is an intermediate link.
The sternoclavicular joint is the sole link between the upper limb and the axial skeleton.
The scapulothoracic is a functional, not anatomical, joint.
The xiphisternal joint is part of the sternum itself.
citation: It is the sole link between the upper limb and the axial skeleton, and so transmits all forces from the upper limb to the trunk.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: Following a mid-shaft clavicular fracture, a patient's medial bone fragment is visibly displaced superiorly. Which muscle's attachment to the sternal end causes this tilting?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis minor attaches to the coracoid process.
The subclavius muscle depresses the clavicle.
The sternocleidomastoid muscle tilts the medial end of the clavicle upward.
The sternohyoid is posterior and does not cause this displacement.
The omohyoid attaches to the superior border of the scapula.
citation: The medial end is tilted upward by the sternocleidomastoid muscle.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: During a surgical repair of the sternoclavicular joint, the surgeon identifies the articular disc. To which structure is the outer edge (circumference) of this disc attached?
optionExplanations: The disc is not directly attached to the manubrium surface.
It is attached to the superior margin of the clavicle, but the circumference is capsule-related.
The circumference of the articular disc is attached to the interior of the joint capsule.
The disc is attached to the first costal cartilage, not the rib periosteum.
The interclavicular ligament is external to the capsule.
citation: The circumference is attached to the interior of the joint capsule

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A patient presents with a rare isolated tear of the costoclavicular ligament. Where does this ligament originate before inserting onto the inferior surface of the clavicle?
optionExplanations: The sternal angle is too inferior.
The costoclavicular ligament runs from the junction of the first rib and its costal cartilage.
The coracoid process is the origin of the coracoclavicular ligament.
The suprasternal notch is the superior margin of the manubrium.
The second rib is not involved in this ligament.
citation: The costoclavicular ligament is a strong ligament that runs from the junction of the first rib with the first costal cartilage to the inferior surface of the sternal end of the clavicle

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: Which of the following structures are involved in the articulation of the sternoclavicular joint?
options: Clavicle, manubrium, and first costal cartilage
Clavicle, sternal body, and first rib
correctOption: Clavicle, manubrium, and first costal cartilage
optionExplanations: The sternoclavicular joint involves the sternal end of the clavicle, the manubrium sterni, and the first costal cartilage.
The xiphoid process and second rib are not part of this specific articulation.
The acromion is part of the acromioclavicular joint, not the sternoclavicular joint.
The coracoid process is a feature of the scapula and does not articulate with the sternum.
The articulation is specifically with the manubrium, not the body of the sternum.
citation: Articulation: Occurs between the sternal end of the clavicle, the manubrium sterni, and the first costal cartilage

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: What is the anatomical classification of the sternoclavicular joint?
optionExplanations: The sternoclavicular joint is classified as a synovial double-plane joint.
It is a synovial joint, not a primary cartilaginous joint.
It is a highly mobile synovial joint, not a fibrous syndesmosis.
The shoulder joint is ball-and-socket; the sternoclavicular is double-plane.
The elbow is a hinge joint; the sternoclavicular is double-plane.
citation: Type: Synovial double-plane joint.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: How does the articular disc affect the internal structure of the sternoclavicular joint?
options: Divides it into medial and lateral compartments
Divides it into superior and inferior compartments
Fuses the clavicle to the manubrium
Eliminates the need for synovial fluid
correctOption: Divides it into medial and lateral compartments
optionExplanations: The fibrocartilaginous disc divides the joint interior into medial and lateral compartments.
The division is medial and lateral, not superior and inferior.
The disc allows movement and compartmentalization rather than fusing the bones.
As a synovial joint, it still requires synovial fluid in both compartments.
The disc actually facilitates complex movements within the compartments.
citation: A flat fibrocartilaginous disc lies within the joint and divides the joint’s interior into two compartments, medial and lateral

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: Which nerves provide the sensory and motor innervation related to the sternoclavicular joint?
optionExplanations: The joint is supplied by the supraclavicular nerve and the nerve to the subclavius.
Axillary and suprascapular nerves supply the glenohumeral joint.
Long thoracic and thoracodorsal nerves supply muscles, not this joint.
Phrenic and vagus nerves are not the primary supply for this joint.
Pectoral nerves supply the pectoralis muscles, not the sternoclavicular joint.
citation: Nerve supply: Supraclavicular nerve and the nerve to the subclavius muscle.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: Where does the costoclavicular ligament attach on the clavicle?
options: Inferior surface of the sternal end
Superior surface of the acromial end
correctOption: Inferior surface of the sternal end
optionExplanations: The costoclavicular ligament attaches to the inferior surface of the sternal (medial) end of the clavicle.
The acromial end is the lateral end, involved in the acromioclavicular joint.
The conoid tubercle is on the lateral third of the clavicle.
The deltoid tuberosity is a feature of the humerus, not the clavicle.
The trapezoid line is located near the lateral end of the clavicle.
citation: The costoclavicular ligament is a strong ligament that runs from the junction of the first rib with the first costal cartilage to the inferior surface of the sternal end of the clavicle

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A 22-year-old wrestler presents to the ER after a heavy fall onto his shoulder. Physical examination reveals a prominent bony lump just lateral to the suprasternal notch. The lump appears to move superiorly when he tilts his head. What is the most likely diagnosis?
optionExplanations: Anterior dislocation causes the medial clavicle to project forward under the skin and be pulled up by the SCM.
Posterior dislocation would result in the clavicle being driven backward, not projecting forward.
A midshaft fracture would present with a deformity in the middle of the bone, not at the sternal end.
Acromioclavicular separation occurs at the lateral end of the shoulder.
A sternal body fracture would be central and lower than the clavicular articulation.
citation: Anterior dislocation results in the medial end of the clavicle projecting forward beneath the skin; it may also be pulled upward by the sternocleidomastoid muscle.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A construction worker is struck by a falling beam directly on the front of his medial shoulder. He is brought to the ER with severe pain and difficulty breathing (dyspnea). An X-ray confirms a posterior sternoclavicular dislocation. Which structure is most likely being compressed to cause his dyspnea?
optionExplanations: Posterior dislocation is serious because the clavicle can compress the trachea, causing breathing difficulties.
Compression of the esophagus would cause difficulty swallowing (dysphagia), not primarily dyspnea.
The axillary artery is located laterally in the axilla, far from the sternoclavicular joint.
While the thoracic duct is in the neck, tracheal compression is the classic cause of acute dyspnea here.
While vessels can be compressed, the trachea is the primary airway structure at risk.
citation: This type is the more serious one because the displaced clavicle may press on the trachea, the esophagus, and major blood vessels in the root of the neck.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: Following a high-speed motor vehicle accident, a patient presents with a posterior sternoclavicular dislocation. In addition to chest pain, she complains of significant difficulty swallowing. Which structure is likely compressed by the backward displacement of the clavicle?
optionExplanations: The esophagus lies posterior to the joint and can be compressed in posterior dislocations, causing dysphagia.
Tracheal compression causes respiratory distress, not primarily swallowing difficulty.
The brachial plexus is more lateral and inferior to the immediate posterior relation of this joint.
The subclavian groove is a bony feature, not a soft tissue structure that causes dysphagia.
While the vagus nerve is in the neck, esophageal compression is the direct cause of dysphagia.
citation: This type is the more serious one because the displaced clavicle may press on the trachea, the esophagus, and major blood vessels in the root of the neck.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A patient is asked to shrug his shoulders during a physical exam. In which specific part of the sternoclavicular joint does the resulting elevation of the clavicle primarily occur?
optionExplanations: Elevation and depression of the clavicle occur specifically in the lateral compartment of the joint.
The medial compartment is involved in forward and backward movements.
The costoclavicular ligament is an accessory structure, not a joint compartment.
The acromioclavicular joint is at the other end of the clavicle.
Movement occurs within the synovial compartments, not just the capsule.
citation: Elevation and depression of the clavicle take place in the lateral compartment

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: During a reaching task where the patient protracts his scapula, the medial end of the clavicle moves forward. This specific movement occurs in which anatomical area of the sternoclavicular joint?
optionExplanations: Forward and backward movements of the clavicle take place in the medial joint compartment.
The lateral compartment is reserved for elevation and depression.
The interclavicular ligament strengthens the joint but is not a compartment where movement occurs.
There is no 'subclavius bursa' within the sternoclavicular joint compartments.
The acromial extremity is the lateral end of the clavicle.
citation: Forward and backward movement of the clavicle takes place in the medial joint compartment.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A clinician observes that in a patient with a sternoclavicular injury, the medial fragment of the bone is noticeably displaced superiorly. Which muscle is responsible for this specific upward tilt?
optionExplanations: The sternocleidomastoid muscle attaches to the medial clavicle and pulls it upward when the joint is disrupted.
The pectoralis major pulls the lateral fragment medially and forward in fractures.
The subclavius muscle actually acts to depress the clavicle.
The trapezius acts on the lateral end and spine of the scapula.
The sternohyoid is a posterior relation but does not cause this displacement.
citation: The medial end is tilted upward by the sternocleidomastoid muscle.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: An orthopedic surgeon successfully reduces a sternoclavicular dislocation, but the joint remains highly unstable and immediately redislocates upon releasing pressure. Complete rupture of which ligament most likely explains this instability?
optionExplanations: The costoclavicular ligament is the primary stabilizer; its rupture makes maintaining reduction very difficult.
The coracoclavicular ligament stabilizes the acromioclavicular joint, not the sternoclavicular.
The acromioclavicular ligament is at the lateral end of the clavicle.
The supraspinatus is part of the rotator cuff at the glenohumeral joint.
While the interclavicular ligament helps, the costoclavicular is the key anchor to the first rib.
citation: If the costoclavicular ligament ruptures completely, it is difficult to maintain the normal position of the clavicle once reduction has been accomplished.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A patient sustains a penetrating trauma just posterior to the right sternoclavicular joint. Which major vascular structure is at the highest risk of direct injury in this specific location?
optionExplanations: On the right side, the brachiocephalic artery is a direct posterior relation to the sternoclavicular joint.
The left common carotid is a posterior relation on the left side, not the right.
The axillary vein is located much further laterally in the axilla.
The subclavian artery is lateral to the brachiocephalic bifurcation.
The internal thoracic artery is inferior to the joint's posterior aspect.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: During a surgical approach to the sternoclavicular joint from the front, which muscular fibers must be reflected or incised after the skin and fascia?
optionExplanations: The sternocleidomastoid and pectoralis major have fibers located anterior to the joint.

Sternohyoid and sternothyroid are posterior relations, not anterior.
Trapezius and deltoid are related to the lateral end of the clavicle.
Serratus anterior and subscapularis are related to the scapula and axilla.
citation: Anteriorly: Skin and some fibers of the sternocleidomastoid and pectoralis major muscles

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A radiologist notes a small avulsion at the superior margin of the medial clavicle articular surface. Which intra-articular structure is most likely involved in this injury?

The costoclavicular ligament attaches to the inferior surface, not the superior margin.
The sternohyoid is a muscle and does not attach to the articular margin.
The interclavicular ligament connects the two clavicles but is not the primary intra-articular attachment described.
citation: The circumference is attached to the interior of the joint capsule, but it is also strongly attached to the superior margin of the articular surface of the clavicle above and to the first costal cartilage below.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A patient with a congenital deformity resulting in the absence of both clavicles (cleidocranial dysostosis) is able to touch his shoulders together in front of his chest. This is because the clavicle normally functions as a:
optionExplanations: The clavicle acts as a strut that holds the arm away from the trunk to allow free movement.
The clavicle is not a weight-bearing column like the femur.
While it provides some protection, its primary mechanical role is a strut.
The clavicle is a bone, not a muscle (flexor).
It is a mobile link, not a static anchor.
citation: The clavicle forms a light strut that connects the upper limb to the thorax and allows the limb to move freely from the trunk.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A gymnast falls from the high bar and lands on her outstretched hand. The force travels through the radius, ulna, humerus, and scapula. Which joint serves as the final link for transmitting this force to the axial skeleton?
optionExplanations: The sternoclavicular joint is the sole bony link between the upper limb and the axial skeleton.
The acromioclavicular joint is a link within the shoulder girdle, not to the axial skeleton.
The glenohumeral joint connects the arm to the girdle, not the girdle to the trunk.
The scapulothoracic interface is a functional 'joint' but not a bony link.
The xiphisternal joint is part of the sternum and not involved in upper limb force transmission.
citation: It is the sole link between the upper limb and the axial skeleton, and so transmits all forces from the upper limb to the trunk.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: In a case of septic arthritis of the sternoclavicular joint, where would the inflammatory fluid and pus primarily accumulate within the joint structure?

The disc is solid fibrocartilage and does not hold fluid internally.

The sternohyoid is an external muscle relation.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A patient presents with a minor sprain of the sternoclavicular joint. The physician explains that the joint is naturally stable because the capsule is thickened anteriorly and posteriorly. These thickenings are known as the:
