The interclavicular ligament connects the two clavicles across the notch.
The costoclavicular ligament is an accessory ligament, not a capsular reinforcement.
Radiate ligaments are found in the ribs/sternum, not this joint.
Coracoclavicular ligaments are at the lateral end of the clavicle.
citation: The capsule is reinforced in front of and behind the joint by the strong sternoclavicular ligaments.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: During a physical examination, a doctor palps the medial end of the clavicle to check for symmetry. This specific part of the bone is anatomically described as the:
optionExplanations: The medial end of the clavicle is the sternal extremity.
The acromial extremity is the lateral end.
The conoid tubercle is on the inferior surface of the lateral third.
The coracoid process is part of the scapula.
The deltoid tubercle is a feature of the humerus.
citation: The sternal extremity (Fig. 3.1) is the blunt, thickened, proximal (medial) end of the clavicle.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A patient with a left-sided posterior sternoclavicular dislocation is found to have a diminished left carotid pulse and swelling in the neck. Which structure is most likely being compressed?
optionExplanations: The left common carotid artery is a posterior relation on the left side.
The brachiocephalic artery is on the right side.
The right subclavian is on the opposite side of the injury.
Compression of the thoracic duct would cause lymph issues, not a diminished carotid pulse.
The axillary artery is too far lateral to be affected by this joint.
citation: on the left, the left brachiocephalic vein and the left common carotid artery

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A patient suffers a fracture of the first costal cartilage. Which intra-articular component of the sternoclavicular joint is most likely to lose its inferior anchor as a result?
optionExplanations: The articular disc is strongly attached to the first costal cartilage below.
The synovial membrane is a lining, not a structural anchor to the cartilage.
The interclavicular ligament attaches to the manubrium, not the costal cartilage.
The sternoclavicular ligaments reinforce the capsule but the disc has the specific cartilage attachment.
The subclavius is a muscle, not an intra-articular component.
citation: strongly attached to the superior margin of the articular surface of the clavicle above and to the first costal cartilage below.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: When a patient is asked to depress his shoulders (as if reaching for the floor), in which specific anatomical compartment of the sternoclavicular joint does this movement occur?

The interclavicular space is not a joint compartment.
There is no 'substernal bursa' involved in this joint's mechanics.
The acromioclavicular space is at the lateral end of the shoulder girdle.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint

The lateral compartment is for elevation and depression.
The costoclavicular ligament limits movement but is not the compartment where it occurs.
The sternal notch is a landmark on the manubrium, not a joint compartment.
The acromial end is the lateral end of the clavicle.
citation: Forward and backward movement of the clavicle takes place in the medial joint compartment.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: The sternoclavicular joint is described as a 'compound' joint because it contains which of the following?
optionExplanations: The presence of an articular disc that divides the joint makes it a compound synovial joint.

All synovial joints contain synovial fluid.
Hyaline cartilage is standard for synovial joints.
citation: It articulates with the clavicular notch of the sternum through a compound synovial joint containing an articular disc.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
coreConcept: Articulating surfaces of the Sternoclavicular Joint

options: Clavicle, manubrium, and first costal cartilage
Clavicle, xiphoid process, and first rib
Clavicle, body of sternum, and rib
correctOption: Clavicle, manubrium, and first costal cartilage
optionExplanations: The sternoclavicular joint involves the sternal end of the clavicle, the manubrium, and the first costal cartilage.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint

optionExplanations: The articular disc is fibrocartilaginous and divides the joint into medial and lateral compartments.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint

The long thoracic and thoracodorsal nerves supply muscles (serratus anterior and latissimus dorsi).
The phrenic nerve supplies the diaphragm; intercostal nerves supply the chest wall.
Pectoral nerves supply the pectoralis major and minor muscles.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint

Anterior displacement of the medial clavicle
optionExplanations: In posterior dislocations, the clavicle moves backward and can compress the esophagus, causing dysphagia.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint

The coracoclavicular ligament is located at the lateral end of the clavicle.
Sternoclavicular ligaments reinforce the joint capsule anteriorly and posteriorly.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A physical therapist is assessing the range of motion of the shoulder girdle. When the patient shrugs their shoulders, movement occurs at the sternoclavicular joint. In which specific anatomical compartment does this elevation occur?
optionExplanations: Elevation and depression of the clavicle occur in the lateral compartment of the sternoclavicular joint.

The joint is divided into medial and lateral compartments, not superior/inferior.
The joint is divided into medial and lateral compartments, not superior/inferior.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint

optionExplanations: The sternocleidomastoid muscle attaches to the medial clavicle and pulls it upward during a dislocation.

The subclavius muscle depresses the clavicle.
The trapezius acts on the lateral end and spine of the scapula.

citation: Anterior dislocation results in the medial end of the clavicle projecting forward beneath the skin; it may also be pulled upward by the sternocleidomastoid muscle.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A radiologist is reviewing an MRI of the sternoclavicular joint. They observe the articular disc and need to verify its attachments to ensure no avulsion has occurred. Where should the disc be anchored superiorly and inferiorly?

The coracoid process is far lateral to the sternoclavicular joint.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: During a reaching task where a patient moves their shoulder forward (protraction), the medial end of the clavicle glides on the articular disc. In which specific joint compartment does this anteroposterior movement occur?
optionExplanations: Forward and backward movements occur in the medial compartment of the SC joint.
Elevation and depression occur in the lateral compartment.
The joint is divided into medial and lateral, not superior/inferior.
The joint is divided into medial and lateral, not superior/inferior.
The subacromial space is related to the glenohumeral joint.
citation: Forward and backward movement of the clavicle takes place in the medial joint compartment.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
coreConcept: Posterior relations of the Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A surgeon is performing a deep dissection near the left sternoclavicular joint. Which major vascular structure must be protected as it lies immediately posterior to the joint on the left side?
optionExplanations: On the left side, the left brachiocephalic vein and left common carotid artery are posterior relations.
The brachiocephalic artery is a posterior relation on the right side.
The superior vena cava is located deeper in the mediastinum.
The subclavian artery is lateral to these immediate posterior relations.
The internal thoracic artery arises lower and more laterally.
citation: Posteriorly: Sternohyoid muscle; on the right, the brachiocephalic artery; on the left, the left brachiocephalic vein and the left common carotid artery

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
coreConcept: Accessory ligaments of the Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A patient suffers a traumatic injury that forces the clavicle upward. Despite a tear in the joint capsule, the clavicle remains largely tethered to the thoracic cage. Which accessory ligament is primarily providing this stability?
optionExplanations: The costoclavicular ligament is the strong accessory ligament anchoring the clavicle to the first rib.
The coracoacromial ligament is part of the shoulder joint complex.
Geno-humeral ligaments are part of the shoulder joint capsule.
The transverse humeral ligament holds the biceps tendon in the bicipital groove.
The suprascapular ligament bridges the scapular notch.
citation: Accessory ligament: The costoclavicular ligament is a strong ligament that runs from the junction of the first rib with the first costal cartilage to the inferior surface of the sternal end of the clavicle

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
coreConcept: Articulating surfaces of the Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: Anatomy students are examining a prosected cadaver. They are identifying the specific site on the sternum where the medial end of the clavicle attaches. What is this landmark called?
optionExplanations: The clavicle articulates with the clavicular notch of the manubrium sterni.
The jugular (suprasternal) notch is the superior border between the two clavicular notches.
The sternal angle is the junction between the manubrium and the body.
Costal facets are for articulation with the ribs.
There is no 'xiphoid notch' for the clavicle.
citation: It articulates with the clavicular notch of the sternum through a compound synovial joint containing an articular disc.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A patient with a nerve injury is unable to elevate their shoulder girdle effectively. Which group of muscles, acting on the sternoclavicular joint, is likely affected?
optionExplanations: Elevation is performed by the trapezius, sternocleidomastoid, levator scapulae, and rhomboids.
Pectoralis muscles are involved in depression and protraction.
Subclavius is a depressor; serratus anterior is a protractor.
These muscles are primarily adductors and extensors of the humerus.
These are abductors of the glenohumeral joint.
citation: Elevation: Trapezius, sternocleidomastoid, levator scapulae, and rhomboid muscles

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A clinician is injecting a local anesthetic into the sternoclavicular joint. To ensure the needle is within the joint space, they must pierce the fibrous layer that encloses the articulation. Where is this layer attached?
optionExplanations: The joint capsule is attached to the margins of the articular surfaces.
The capsule is localized to the joint ends, not the shaft.
The coracoid process is not part of the sternoclavicular joint.
The sternal angle is inferior to the joint.
The joint involves the first costal cartilage, not the second.
citation: Capsule: Surrounds the joint and is attached to the margins of the articular surfaces.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
coreConcept: Posterior relations of the Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A patient presents with a right-sided posterior sternoclavicular dislocation. The physician is concerned about potential arterial compression. Which specific vessel is at risk on the right side?
optionExplanations: The brachiocephalic artery is a direct posterior relation of the right sternoclavicular joint.
The left common carotid is a relation on the left side.
The left brachiocephalic vein is a relation on the left side.
The pulmonary artery is located much deeper and inferiorly.
The aortic arch is located inferior to the level of the sternoclavicular joints.
citation: Posteriorly: Sternohyoid muscle; on the right, the brachiocephalic artery

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A 30-year-old athlete has a degenerative tear of the internal structure of the sternoclavicular joint. This structure normally acts as a shock absorber and partitions the joint. What is this structure?
optionExplanations: The articular disc is the internal fibrocartilage of the sternoclavicular joint.
The glenoid labrum is in the shoulder joint.
The acetabular labrum is in the hip joint.
Menisci are found in the knee joint.
The interosseous membrane connects the radius and ulna.
citation: A flat fibrocartilaginous disc lies within the joint and divides the joint’s interior into two compartments

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
coreConcept: Nerve supply of the Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A patient undergoes a nerve block of the supraclavicular nerves. While this provides significant anesthesia to the skin of the shoulder, they still feel deep pressure at the sternoclavicular joint. Which other nerve provides innervation to this joint?
optionExplanations: The nerve to the subclavius muscle provides additional innervation to the sternoclavicular joint.
The long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior muscle.
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid and teres minor.
The phrenic nerve supplies the diaphragm.
The thoracodorsal nerve supplies the latissimus dorsi.
citation: Nerve supply: Supraclavicular nerve and the nerve to the subclavius muscle.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A trauma surgeon is assessing the integrity of the sternoclavicular joint capsule. Which specific ligaments provide the primary reinforcement to the anterior and posterior aspects of the capsule?
optionExplanations: The anterior and posterior sternoclavicular ligaments reinforce the joint capsule.
The costoclavicular ligament is an accessory ligament, not a direct capsular reinforcement.
The interclavicular ligament connects the two clavicles.
The coracoclavicular ligament is at the lateral end of the clavicle.
The acromioclavicular ligament is at the lateral end of the clavicle.
citation: Ligaments: Capsule is reinforced in front of and behind the joint by the strong sternoclavicular ligaments.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A patient is instructed to perform 'shoulder blade squeezes' and downward movements of the shoulder girdle. Which muscle is a primary contributor to the depression of the clavicle at the sternoclavicular joint?
optionExplanations: The subclavius muscle is a primary depressor of the clavicle.
The levator scapulae elevates the scapula/clavicle.
The rhomboids elevate the scapula/clavicle.
The sternocleidomastoid elevates the clavicle.
The upper fibers of the trapezius elevate the clavicle.
citation: Depression: Pectoralis major (lower fibers), subclavius, pectoralis minor, and trapezius (lower fibers) muscles

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
coreConcept: Articulating surfaces of the Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: Which of the following structures is involved in the articulation of the sternoclavicular joint?
optionExplanations: The first costal cartilage is one of the three articulating components of the SC joint.
The second costal cartilage articulates at the sternal angle.
The xiphoid process is the most inferior part of the sternum.
The sternal angle is the junction of the manubrium and body.
The body of the sternum articulates with ribs 2-7.
citation: Articulation: Occurs between the sternal end of the clavicle, the manubrium sterni, and the first costal cartilage

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
coreConcept: Nerve supply of the Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: Which nerve provides sensory innervation to the sternoclavicular joint?
optionExplanations: The supraclavicular nerve (C3, 4) supplies the sternoclavicular joint.
The axillary nerve supplies the shoulder joint.
The suprascapular nerve supplies the shoulder and acromioclavicular joints.
The long thoracic nerve is a motor nerve to the serratus anterior.
The thoracodorsal nerve is a motor nerve to the latissimus dorsi.
citation: Nerve supply: Supraclavicular nerve and the nerve to the subclavius muscle.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
coreConcept: Articular disc of the Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: What structure divides the interior of the sternoclavicular joint into two separate compartments?
optionExplanations: A fibrocartilaginous articular disc divides the SC joint cavity.
The synovial membrane lines the cavity but does not divide it.
The interclavicular ligament is extracapsular.
The costoclavicular ligament is an accessory ligament outside the joint.
Hyaline cartilage covers the bone ends but is not a dividing disc.
citation: A flat fibrocartilaginous disc lies within the joint and divides the joint’s interior into two compartments

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
coreConcept: Joint type of the Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: The sternoclavicular joint is anatomically classified as which type of joint?
optionExplanations: The SC joint is a synovial double-plane joint due to the presence of the disc.
Hinge joints allow flexion/extension (e.g., elbow).
Pivot joints allow rotation (e.g., proximal radioulnar).
Sutures are immovable fibrous joints in the skull.
Symphyses are secondary cartilaginous joints.
citation: Type: Synovial double-plane joint.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
coreConcept: Accessory ligaments of the Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: Which ligament connects the inferior surface of the sternal end of the clavicle to the first rib?
optionExplanations: The costoclavicular ligament connects the clavicle to the first rib/cartilage.
Sternoclavicular ligaments are part of the joint capsule.
The interclavicular ligament connects the two clavicles.
The coracoclavicular ligament is at the lateral end of the clavicle.
The acromioclavicular ligament is at the lateral end of the clavicle.
citation: Accessory ligament: The costoclavicular ligament is a strong ligament that runs from the junction of the first rib with the first costal cartilage to the inferior surface of the sternal end of the clavicle

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: Which of the following structures participate in the formation of the sternoclavicular joint?
options: Clavicle, manubrium, and first costal cartilage
correctOption: Clavicle, manubrium, and first costal cartilage
optionExplanations: The sternoclavicular joint is formed by the medial end of the clavicle, the clavicular notch of the manubrium, and the first costal cartilage.
The xiphoid process is too inferior to participate in this joint.
The acromion articulates with the lateral end of the clavicle at the AC joint.
The coracoid process is part of the scapula and does not articulate with the sternum.
The body of the sternum is inferior to the manubrium where the clavicle attaches.
citation: Occurs between the sternal end of the clavicle, the manubrium sterni, and the first costal cartilage

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: Anatomically, the sternoclavicular joint is classified as which type of joint?
optionExplanations: The text explicitly classifies the sternoclavicular joint as a synovial double-plane joint.
Primary cartilaginous joints (synchondroses) involve hyaline cartilage, unlike this synovial joint.
A syndesmosis is a fibrous joint, such as the middle radioulnar joint.
The shoulder joint is ball-and-socket, but the SC joint is plane-type.
Secondary cartilaginous joints (symphyses) are in the midline, like the manubriosternal joint.
citation: Synovial double-plane joint.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A 19-year-old rugby player is rushed to the ER after a direct blow to the front of his chest. He presents with severe neck pain, dyspnea, and dysphagia. Imaging reveals a posterior sternoclavicular dislocation. Which structures are at immediate risk of compression?
options: Vagus nerve and internal jugular vein
optionExplanations: Posterior displacement of the medial clavicle is a medical emergency because it can compress the airway (trachea), food pipe (esophagus), and great vessels.
The brachial plexus and axillary artery are located more laterally in the axilla.
While nearby, the trachea and esophagus are the primary structures mentioned as being at risk of compression.
The vagus nerve is deeper, but the trachea and esophagus are the classic clinical concerns.
The aorta is deeper in the thorax, though the brachiocephalic vessels are at risk.
citation: The displaced clavicle may press on the trachea, the esophagus, and major blood vessels in the root of the neck.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: During a dissection of the root of the neck, a student opens the sternoclavicular joint capsule. He observes a structure that completely separates the joint space. What is the composition and arrangement of this structure?
options: Membranous septum with a central perforation
optionExplanations: The sternoclavicular joint contains a fibrocartilaginous articular disc that divides the cavity into medial and lateral compartments.
Hyaline cartilage covers the bone ends but does not divide the joint into compartments.
The disc is a structural divider, not a simple bridging ligament.
Fat pads are found in the elbow joint fossae, not as dividers in the SC joint.
The disc is typically solid and fibrocartilaginous, not a perforated membrane.
citation: A flat fibrocartilaginous disc lies within the joint and divides the joint’s interior into two compartments, medial and lateral

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: Which nerves provide the sensory and vasomotor supply to the sternoclavicular joint?
optionExplanations: The SC joint is innervated by the supraclavicular nerve (C3-C4) and the nerve to the subclavius (C5-C6).
The long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior.
The axillary and suprascapular nerves supply the glenohumeral joint.
Pectoral nerves supply the pectoralis muscles.
The phrenic nerve supplies the diaphragm, though it may receive a branch from the nerve to subclavius.
citation: Supraclavicular nerve and the nerve to the subclavius muscle.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A 25-year-old female falls onto her shoulder. On examination, there is a visible, palpable lump at the root of her neck on the right side, just lateral to the suprasternal notch. The lump moves when she shrugs. What is the most likely diagnosis?
optionExplanations: Anterior dislocation causes the medial end of the clavicle to move forward, becoming prominent under the skin.
A rib fracture would not typically present as a mobile lump at the sternal notch.
Posterior dislocation would result in the medial end being non-palpable and potentially causing respiratory distress.
AC joint separation occurs at the lateral end of the clavicle, near the shoulder tip.
A lymph node would not typically move specifically with clavicular shrugging in this manner.
citation: Anterior dislocation results in the medial end of the clavicle projecting forward beneath the skin

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A patient presents with a sternoclavicular joint injury. The examiner notes that despite a tear in the joint capsule, the clavicle remains relatively anchored to the first rib. Which ligament is primarily responsible for this stability?
optionExplanations: The costoclavicular ligament is the strong accessory ligament that anchors the medial clavicle to the first rib/cartilage.
The coracoclavicular ligament anchors the lateral clavicle to the scapula.
The interclavicular ligament connects the two clavicles across the notch.
The sternoclavicular ligaments reinforce the capsule but are not the primary rib-anchor.
The suprascapular ligament is located on the superior border of the scapula.
citation: The costoclavicular ligament is a strong ligament that runs from the junction of the first rib with the first costal cartilage to the inferior surface of the sternal end of the clavicle

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A gymnast performing a routine experiences sharp pain at the base of his neck during a high-impact landing where he forcefully depressed his shoulders. If the injury is localized to the sternoclavicular joint, which specific functional area is most likely involved?
optionExplanations: The text states that elevation and depression of the clavicle occur specifically within the lateral compartment of the SC joint.
The medial compartment is associated with forward and backward movements.
The costoclavicular ligament is an accessory structure, not a compartment.
The interclavicular ligament is superior to the joint.
There is no 'subclavius bursa' within the SC joint structure.
citation: Elevation and depression of the clavicle take place in the lateral compartment

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A patient is asked to move their shoulders forward (protraction) and backward (retraction). In which part of the sternoclavicular joint does the majority of this specific gliding motion occur?
optionExplanations: Forward and backward movements (protraction/retraction) are localized to the medial compartment of the SC joint.
The lateral compartment handles elevation and depression.
Gliding occurs in the compartments, not specifically in a 'capsular fold'.
Synovial recesses are not the primary site of these functional movements.
The costoclavicular interval refers to the space between the bone and rib, not the joint interior.
citation: Forward and backward movement of the clavicle takes place in the medial joint compartment.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: To which structures is the articular disc of the sternoclavicular joint most firmly attached?
options: Superior clavicle and first costal cartilage
correctOption: Superior clavicle and first costal cartilage
optionExplanations: The disc is anchored to the superior margin of the clavicle's medial end and the first costal cartilage.
The manubrium is part of the articulation, but the disc's primary anchors are the clavicle and cartilage.
The attachment is to the superior margin, not the inferior surface.
The coracoid process is too far lateral.
The xiphoid process is at the inferior end of the sternum.
citation: strongly attached to the superior margin of the articular surface of the clavicle above and to the first costal cartilage below.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: In a patient with an anterior dislocation of the sternoclavicular joint, the medial end of the clavicle is often displaced superiorly. Which muscle is primarily responsible for this upward displacement?
optionExplanations: The sternocleidomastoid muscle attaches to the medial clavicle and pulls the fragment upward when the joint is dislocated.
The pectoralis major pulls the clavicle anteriorly and medially, but not primarily upward in this context.
The subclavius muscle actually pulls the clavicle downward.
The trapezius attaches to the lateral third of the clavicle.
The sternohyoid lies posterior to the joint and does not cause this displacement.
citation: it may also be pulled upward by the sternocleidomastoid muscle.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A surgeon is performing a procedure near the left sternoclavicular joint. Which vascular structures must be carefully protected immediately posterior to the joint on the left side?
options: Left brachiocephalic vein and common carotid
Internal thoracic artery and azygos vein
Superior vena cava and left atrium
correctOption: Left brachiocephalic vein and common carotid
optionExplanations: On the left side, the left brachiocephalic vein and left common carotid artery are the immediate posterior relations.
The brachiocephalic artery is a posterior relation on the right side, not the left.
The internal thoracic artery is lateral to the sternum, not directly posterior to the SC joint.
The aortic arch is deeper in the mediastinum.
The superior vena cava is formed on the right side of the mediastinum.
citation: Posteriorly: Sternohyoid muscle; on the right, the brachiocephalic artery; on the left, the left brachiocephalic vein and the left common carotid artery

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: Where does the fibrous capsule of the sternoclavicular joint attach?
optionExplanations: The capsule of the SC joint attaches to the perimeter of the articulating surfaces of the clavicle and sternum.
The surgical neck is a feature of the humerus, not the clavicle.
The SC joint involves the first rib/cartilage, not the second rib.
The coracoid process is at the lateral end of the shoulder girdle.
The xiphoid process is far inferior to the SC joint.
citation: Surrounds the joint and is attached to the margins of the articular surfaces.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A 15-year-old boy falls on his outstretched hand. He presents with severe pain in the collarbone region. Why is a clavicular fracture more common than a sternoclavicular dislocation in this scenario?
options: The joint ligaments are extremely strong
The clavicle has no medullary cavity
The first rib acts as a shock absorber
The articular disc prevents all dislocation
correctOption: The joint ligaments are extremely strong
optionExplanations: The sternoclavicular joint is reinforced by very strong ligaments (like the costoclavicular), making the bone more likely to break than the joint to dislocate.
The clavicle does have a structure that allows for transmission of force, but its strength relative to the ligaments is the key.
The manubrium is a solid, stable part of the axial skeleton.
The first rib transmits force but does not prevent fracture.
The disc facilitates movement and absorbs shock but cannot prevent dislocation from violent force.
citation: Violent forces directed along the long axis of the clavicle usually result in fracture of that bone, but dislocation of the sternoclavicular joint takes place occasionally.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: What is the extent of the synovial membrane in the sternoclavicular joint?
options: Lines capsule to articular cartilage margins
Extends down to the third rib
Lines the interior of the subclavius
correctOption: Lines capsule to articular cartilage margins
optionExplanations: The synovial membrane lines the fibrous capsule and stops at the edges of the hyaline articular cartilage.
The articular disc is fibrocartilaginous and is not entirely covered by synovial membrane.
The joint only involves the first rib/cartilage.
The subclavius is a muscle outside the joint space.
The costoclavicular ligament is an extrinsic accessory ligament.
citation: Lines the joint capsule and is attached to the margins of the cartilage covering the articular surfaces.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: Which ligament is considered the primary accessory ligament of the sternoclavicular joint?
optionExplanations: The costoclavicular ligament is the major accessory ligament providing medial stability to the clavicle.
The coracoacromial ligament is part of the shoulder joint complex.
Glenohumeral ligaments are in the shoulder joint capsule.
The transverse humeral ligament holds the biceps tendon in the bicipital groove.
The conoid ligament is part of the coracoclavicular ligament at the lateral end.
citation: The costoclavicular ligament is a strong ligament that runs from the junction of the first rib with the first costal cartilage to the inferior surface of the sternal end of the clavicle

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A patient with a nerve injury is unable to shrug his shoulders. Which group of muscles, acting on the sternoclavicular joint, is likely affected?
optionExplanations: Elevation (shrugging) is produced by the trapezius, SCM, levator scapulae, and rhomboids.
Pectoralis major and latissimus dorsi are primarily adductors/extensors of the arm.
Subclavius and pectoralis minor produce depression of the clavicle.
Serratus anterior protracts the scapula.
Biceps and coracobrachialis act on the elbow and glenohumeral joints.
citation: Elevation: Trapezius, sternocleidomastoid, levator scapulae, and rhomboid muscles

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: During a physical therapy session, a patient is asked to actively depress their shoulders. Which muscles are the primary effectors of this movement at the sternoclavicular joint?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis minor and subclavius muscles pull the shoulder girdle downward (depression).
Trapezius and rhomboids elevate or retract the scapula.
SCM and levator scapulae are elevators.
Serratus anterior moves the scapula forward.
Teres muscles act on the humerus at the glenohumeral joint.
citation: Depression: Pectoralis minor and subclavius muscles

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A patient with chronic inflammation of the sternoclavicular joint experiences referred pain to the skin over the point of the shoulder. This is most likely due to shared spinal segments with which nerve?
optionExplanations: The supraclavicular nerve (C3-C4) supplies both the SC joint and the skin over the shoulder, explaining the referred pain pattern.
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid skin, but not the SC joint.
The long thoracic nerve is purely motor to the serratus anterior.
The phrenic nerve (C3-C5) can cause referred shoulder pain from the diaphragm, but the supraclavicular nerve is the direct supply to the SC joint.
The thoracodorsal nerve supplies the latissimus dorsi.
citation: Supraclavicular nerve and the nerve to the subclavius muscle.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: Which costal structure is directly involved in the sternoclavicular joint articulation?
optionExplanations: The first costal cartilage is a direct articulating surface for the medial end of the clavicle.
The second costal cartilage articulates at the sternal angle (Angle of Louis).
The tubercle of the first rib articulates with the T1 vertebra.
The sternal angle is the junction of the manubrium and body, inferior to the SC joint.
The xiphoid process is the most inferior part of the sternum.
citation: articulation occurs between the sternal end of the clavicle, the manubrium sterni, and the first costal cartilage

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A physician reduces an anterior sternoclavicular dislocation. However, as soon as the pressure is released, the medial end of the clavicle pops back up. This instability is primarily due to the constant tone of which muscle?
optionExplanations: The sternocleidomastoid (SCM) exerts a superior pull on the medial clavicle, making reduction difficult to maintain.
The pectoralis major pulls medially and anteriorly, but the upward 'pop' is SCM-driven.
The subclavius pulls the clavicle down, which would actually help keep it reduced.
The sternohyoid is posterior and does not have a significant upward pull on the clavicle.
The omohyoid acts on the scapula and hyoid bone, not the medial clavicle.
citation: medial end of the clavicle projecting forward beneath the skin; it may also be pulled upward by the sternocleidomastoid muscle.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A patient undergoes a biopsy of a mass located directly behind the right sternoclavicular joint. Which major artery is the surgeon most likely to encounter in this specific location?
optionExplanations: On the right side, the brachiocephalic artery (innominate artery) lies directly posterior to the SC joint.
The left common carotid is a posterior relation on the left side.
The subclavian vein is a vein, and the question asks for an artery.
The internal thoracic artery is lateral to the sternal margin.
The aorta is located more centrally and inferiorly in the thorax.
citation: Posteriorly: Sternohyoid muscle; on the right, the brachiocephalic artery

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: The presence of an articular disc that divides the joint cavity into two distinct spaces classifies the sternoclavicular joint as which of the following?
optionExplanations: The 'double' in double-plane joint refers to the two compartments created by the articular disc.
While some texts call it a saddle joint functionally, this specific text classifies it as a synovial double-plane joint.
A hinge joint (like the elbow) allows movement in only one plane.
A pivot joint (like the atlantoaxial) allows rotation.
A condyloid joint (like the wrist) allows flexion, extension, abduction, and adduction.
citation: Type: Synovial double-plane joint.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A radiologist is reviewing an MRI of the sternoclavicular joint. He notes thickening of the fibrous tissue on the anterior and posterior aspects of the joint capsule. These structures are known as the:
optionExplanations: The anterior and posterior sternoclavicular ligaments are direct thickenings of the joint capsule.
The costoclavicular ligament is an accessory ligament, not a capsular reinforcement.
The interclavicular ligament is a single band superior to the joint.
Coracoclavicular ligaments are at the lateral end of the clavicle.
There is no 'radioclavicular' ligament.
citation: Capsule is reinforced in front of and behind the joint by the strong sternoclavicular ligaments.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A patient presents with a 'choking' sensation and difficulty swallowing following a high-speed motor vehicle accident where his chest hit the steering wheel. Physical exam shows a depression where the medial clavicle should be. What is the most likely anatomical cause?
optionExplanations: Posterior dislocation drives the clavicle into the mediastinum, compressing the trachea (choking) and esophagus (difficulty swallowing).
Anterior dislocation would cause a protrusion, not a depression, and rarely affects swallowing.
A manubrial fracture is possible but the specific 'depression' of the clavicle points to dislocation.
Pectoralis rupture would cause bruising and weakness in arm adduction, not swallowing issues.

There is no posterior compartment.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A 19-year-old rugby player is brought to the ER after a heavy collision. Physical examination reveals the medial end of his right clavicle is visibly projecting forward and is tilted superiorly. Which muscle is primarily responsible for the superior displacement of the bone fragment?

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A 25-year-old male is involved in a high-speed motor vehicle accident. He presents with severe pain at the root of the neck and significant difficulty swallowing (dysphagia). category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A 30-year-old wrestler suffers a sternoclavicular injury. After successful manual reduction of the joint, the clinician finds that the clavicle repeatedly displaces from its normal position. Complete rupture of which ligament is the most likely cause of this instability?

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint

citation: Trapezius, sternocleidomastoid, levator scapulae, and rhomboid muscles

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: Which muscles are responsible for the depression of the clavicle at the sternoclavicular joint?
optionExplanations: Depression is performed by the pectoralis major, subclavius, pectoralis minor, and serratus anterior.
Trapezius and SCM are elevators.
Rhomboids are elevators of the scapula/clavicle.
Levator scapulae is an elevator.
Biceps and coracobrachialis act on the arm/elbow.
citation: Pectoralis major, subclavius, pectoralis minor, and serratus anterior muscles

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A patient is asked to reach forward (protraction). Which muscles produce this forward movement of the clavicle at the sternoclavicular joint?
optionExplanations: Forward movement (protraction) is produced by the serratus anterior, pectoralis major, and pectoralis minor.
Trapezius and rhomboids produce backward movement (retraction).
SCM and levator scapulae are primarily elevators.
Latissimus dorsi is a retractor/depressor of the humerus.
Infraspinatus and teres minor are lateral rotators of the humerus.
citation: Serratus anterior, pectoralis major, and pectoralis minor muscles

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: During a rowing exercise, the patient pulls their shoulders back. Which muscles are primarily responsible for this backward movement at the sternoclavicular joint?
optionExplanations: Backward movement (retraction) is performed by the trapezius and rhomboid muscles.
Serratus anterior and pectoralis major are protractors.
SCM is an elevator; subclavius is a depressor.
Pectoralis minor is a protractor.
Coracobrachialis and brachialis act on the arm and elbow.
citation: Trapezius and rhomboid muscles

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: While palpating the suprasternal notch, a student notes a bony prominence just lateral to the notch. What is the normal anatomical finding at this location?
options: First rib is superior to clavicle
Sternal angle is at this level
optionExplanations: It is normal for the medial end of the clavicle to project slightly above the margin of the manubrium sterni.
The first rib is inferior to the clavicle at the SC joint.
The coracoid process is lateral, near the deltopectoral triangle.
The sternal angle is about 2 inches inferior to the suprasternal notch.
The xiphoid process is at the inferior end of the sternum.
citation: medial end of the clavicle projects above the margin of the manubrium sterni.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A pathology report describes a 'circumferential tear' of the sternoclavicular articular disc. To which structure is the circumference of this disc normally attached?
options: Sternal end of the second rib
optionExplanations: The circumference of the articular disc is attached to the interior of the joint capsule.
It is an internal structure, not attached to the external manubrium.
The costoclavicular ligament is an accessory ligament outside the joint cavity.
The interclavicular ligament is superior and external to the joint.
The SC joint involves the first costal cartilage, not the second rib.
citation: attached to the interior of the joint capsule

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: Where does the fibrous capsule of the sternoclavicular joint attach?
optionExplanations: The capsule surrounds the joint and attaches to the margins of the articular surfaces.
The clavicle does not have a 'surgical neck'; that term applies to the humerus.
The capsule attaches to the first costal cartilage, not the body of the rib.
The sternal angle is inferior to the joint.
The coracoid process is on the scapula, far lateral to this joint.
citation: Surrounds the joint and is attached to the margins of the articular surfaces.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A patient with a posterior sternoclavicular dislocation presents with stridor and respiratory distress. What is the most likely anatomical cause?
options: Rupture of the internal jugular vein
optionExplanations: The displaced clavicle in a posterior dislocation can press directly on the trachea, causing airway obstruction.
While possible in trauma, stridor specifically suggests upper airway compression by the clavicle.
Venous rupture causes hematoma but not typically immediate stridor.
Diaphragmatic paralysis (phrenic nerve) causes different respiratory signs, not stridor.
Esophageal pressure causes dysphagia, not stridor.
citation: displaced clavicle may press on the trachea

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A clinician is examining a patient after a fall. They note a prominent 'lump' at the root of the neck that moves when the patient shrugs. The lump is lateral to the suprasternal notch. What is the most likely finding?
optionExplanations: An anterior SC dislocation presents as the medial end of the clavicle projecting forward beneath the skin.
A lymph node would not typically move in direct synchronization with clavicular shrugging.
A thyroid nodule is usually more medial and moves with swallowing.
A fractured rib is deep and rarely presents as a prominent moving lump at the SC joint.
An aneurysm would be pulsatile and not move specifically with shoulder shrugging.
citation: medial end of the clavicle projecting forward beneath the skin

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A patient has a 'grade 1' strain of the sternoclavicular joint. Which structure is primarily responsible for preventing the medial end of the clavicle from displacing superiorly out of its socket?
optionExplanations: The costoclavicular ligament is the strongest stabilizer anchoring the clavicle down to the first rib/cartilage.
The disc is internal and helps with movement/fit but is not the primary anchor.
The SCM actually pulls the clavicle upward if the ligaments fail.
The interclavicular ligament prevents lateral displacement but is weaker than the costoclavicular.
Capsular ligaments provide support but the costoclavicular is the 'strong' anchor mentioned in the text.
citation: The strong costoclavicular ligament firmly holds the medial end of the clavicle to the first costal cartilage.

category: Bones of thorax, Joints of Upper Limb: Sternoclavicular Joint
statement: A driver is involved in a head-on collision and his chest hits the steering wheel directly over the sternum and medial clavicles. He is now unable to swallow properly. What mechanism of injury occurred?
options: Indirect force from falling on hand
optionExplanations: Direct trauma to the front of the joint drives the clavicle backward (posterior dislocation), risking esophageal compression.
Falling on an outstretched hand usually causes anterior dislocation or clavicular fracture.
The SCM pulls the clavicle upward, not backward.
The pectoralis major pulls the clavicle forward and medially.
Neck hyperextension does not typically cause posterior SC dislocation.
citation: usually follows direct trauma applied to the front of the joint that drives the clavicle backward.

coreConcept: Axillary nerve relation to surgical neck
statement: A 65-year-old woman falls and sustains a displaced fracture of the proximal humerus. On examination, she has lost sensation over the lower half of the deltoid muscle. Which nerve is most likely damaged?
optionExplanations: The radial nerve is related to the midshaft of the humerus in the spiral groove.
The ulnar nerve is related to the medial epicondyle of the humerus.
The axillary nerve has a close relationship with the surgical neck of the humerus and is frequently damaged in fractures at this site.
The median nerve does not have a close relationship with the surgical neck of the humerus.
The musculocutaneous nerve is protected deep to the biceps brachii and is not related to the surgical neck.
citation: The surgical neck has important relations with the axillary nerve

coreConcept: Axillary lymph node drainage of the breast
statement: A 45-year-old woman is diagnosed with a malignant tumor in the upper lateral quadrant of her breast. The surgeon explains that the cancer is most likely to spread first to a specific group of axillary lymph nodes. Which group is this?
optionExplanations: The lateral group receives most of the lymph vessels from the upper limb.
The posterior group receives lymph from the back down to the iliac crests.
The anterior (pectoral) group of axillary nodes receives lymph from the lateral quadrants of the breast.
The central group receives lymph from the anterior, posterior, and lateral groups.
The apical group receives efferent vessels from all other axillary nodes.
citation: The lateral quadrants of the breast drain into the anterior axillary or pectoral group of nodes

coreConcept: Axillary sheath and brachial plexus block
statement: An anesthesiologist performs a brachial plexus block by injecting anesthetic into the axillary sheath. Which structure is also contained within this sheath and can be used to verify its position by palpation?
optionExplanations: The axillary vein is medial to the artery and its pulsations are not typically used for verification.
The axillary artery is enclosed within the axillary sheath along with the brachial plexus; its pulsations identify the sheath's location.
The cephalic vein is superficial and not contained within the axillary sheath.
The basilic vein is superficial in the arm and joins the venae comitantes to form the axillary vein.
The long thoracic nerve lies on the superficial surface of the serratus anterior, outside the axillary sheath.
citation: The position of the sheath can be verified by feeling the pulsations of the third part of the axillary artery.

coreConcept: Axillary artery parts and pectoralis minor
statement: During a surgical dissection of the axilla, the surgeon uses a specific muscle as a landmark to divide the axillary artery into three parts for descriptive purposes. Which muscle is this?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis major forms the anterior wall but does not divide the artery into three parts.
The subclavius is part of the anterior wall but is located superiorly.
The pectoralis minor muscle crosses the axillary artery, dividing it into proximal (1st), deep (2nd), and distal (3rd) parts.
The serratus anterior forms the medial wall of the axilla.
The teres major marks the termination of the axillary artery, where it becomes the brachial artery.
citation: The pectoralis minor muscle crosses in front of the axillary artery and divides it into three parts

statement: A 30-year-old woman undergoes a radical mastectomy. Postoperatively, she is unable to rotate her scapula to raise her arm above her head, and her scapula protrudes posteriorly. Which nerve was likely injured?
optionExplanations: Axillary nerve injury causes deltoid paralysis, affecting abduction up to 90 degrees.
Suprascapular nerve injury affects the initiation of abduction.
The long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior; its injury causes 'winged scapula' and inability to rotate the scapula for overhead abduction.
Thoracodorsal nerve injury affects the latissimus dorsi, primarily impacting extension and adduction.
Dorsal scapular nerve injury affects the rhomboids and levator scapulae.
citation: Paralysis of the serratus anterior results in the inability to rotate the scapula during abduction of the arm above a right angle.

statement: A patient presents with weakness in shoulder abduction and loss of sensation over the lateral arm. Imaging shows a mass compressing the contents of the quadrangular space. Which nerve is affected?
optionExplanations: The radial nerve passes through the triangular interval, not the quadrangular space.
The axillary nerve, along with the posterior circumflex humeral vessels, passes through the quadrangular space.
The suprascapular nerve passes through the suprascapular notch.
The thoracodorsal nerve descends in the axilla to the latissimus dorsi.
The long thoracic nerve descends on the superficial surface of the serratus anterior.
citation: the axillary nerve and the posterior circumflex humeral vessels emerge through this space

statement: A medical student is asked to identify the formation of the axillary vein during a dissection. Which vein joins the venae comitantes of the brachial artery to form it?
optionExplanations: The cephalic vein drains into the axillary vein near its termination, not its formation.
The axillary vein is formed by the union of the basilic vein and the venae comitantes of the brachial artery.
The median cubital vein is a superficial vein of the elbow region.
The subclavian vein is the continuation of the axillary vein.
The internal thoracic vein drains into the brachiocephalic vein.
citation: The axillary vein is formed at the lower border of the teres major muscle by the union of the venae comitantes of the brachial artery and the basilic vein

statement: During an axillary clearance for breast cancer, the surgeon identifies an artery running along the lower border of the pectoralis minor muscle. Which artery is this?
optionExplanations: The thoracoacromial artery arises from the second part but is superior to the pectoralis minor.
The highest thoracic artery arises from the first part, superior to the pectoralis minor.
The lateral thoracic artery is a branch of the second part of the axillary artery and runs along the lower border of the pectoralis minor.
The subscapular artery arises from the third part of the axillary artery.
The brachial artery begins at the lower border of the teres major.
citation: The lateral thoracic artery runs along the lower (lateral) border of the pectoralis minor

statement: A surgeon is tracing the branches of the third part of the axillary artery. They identify a large vessel descending along the axillary border of the scapula. Which artery is this?
optionExplanations: The anterior circumflex humeral artery winds around the surgical neck of the humerus.
The posterior circumflex humeral artery passes through the quadrangular space.
The subscapular artery is the largest branch of the third part and descends along the axillary border of the scapula.
The lateral thoracic artery is a branch of the second part of the axillary artery.
The thoracoacromial artery is a branch of the second part of the axillary artery.
citation: The subscapular artery is a large vessel that descends along the lower (axillary) border of the scapula.

statement: A radiologist is interpreting an MRI of the axilla. They note that the cords of the brachial plexus are named based on their relationship to a specific part of the axillary artery. Which part is this?
optionExplanations: All three cords lie lateral and superior to the first part of the axillary artery.
The cords (lateral, medial, posterior) are named based on their position relative to the second part of the axillary artery.
The terminal branches of the cords are related to the third part of the axillary artery.
The brachial artery is distal to the brachial plexus cords.
The subclavian artery is proximal to the brachial plexus cords.
citation: the cords of the plexus are named according to their positional relationship to the second part of the axillary artery.

statement: A patient sustains a deep stab wound to the medial wall of the axilla. Which muscle, covering the upper ribs in this area, is most likely damaged?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis major forms the anterior wall of the axilla.
The pectoralis minor forms part of the anterior wall of the axilla.
The serratus anterior muscle covers the upper ribs and intercostal spaces, forming the medial wall of the axilla.
The subscapularis forms part of the posterior wall of the axilla.
The latissimus dorsi forms part of the posterior wall of the axilla.
citation: Medial wall: By the upper four or five ribs and the intercostal spaces covered by the serratus anterior muscle

statement: During an anterior approach to the shoulder, the surgeon identifies the muscles forming the anterior wall of the axilla. Which muscle forms the lower border of this wall, creating the anterior axillary fold?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis minor is deep to the pectoralis major and does not form the axillary fold.
The subclavius is a small muscle located superiorly under the clavicle.
The lower border of the pectoralis major muscle forms the anterior axillary fold.
The serratus anterior forms the medial wall of the axilla.
The teres major forms part of the posterior axillary fold.
citation: Anterior wall: By the pectoralis major, subclavius, and pectoralis minor muscles

statement: A patient presents with a laceration to the posterior axillary fold. Which muscle's tendon forms this fold along with the teres major?
optionExplanations: The subscapularis forms the upper part of the posterior wall but not the fold.
The posterior axillary fold is formed by the tendons of the latissimus dorsi and teres major muscles.
The pectoralis major forms the anterior axillary fold.
The pectoralis minor is part of the anterior wall.
The serratus anterior forms the medial wall.
citation: posterior axillary fold (formed by the tendons of the latissimus dorsi and teres major muscles)

statement: A 50-year-old woman notices a lump in her armpit. Examination reveals it is a normal extension of her breast tissue that has pierced the deep fascia to enter the axilla. What is this extension called?
optionExplanations: Suspensory ligaments (Cooper's) separate the lobes of the gland.
Lactiferous ducts open on the summit of the nipple.
The axillary tail (of Spence) is an extension of the breast that pierces the deep fascia to enter the axilla.
The areola is the colored area of skin surrounding the nipple.
The retromammary space lies deep to the breast and superficial to the pectoral muscles.
citation: An extension of the gland called the axillary tail... pierces the deep fascia

statement: A surgeon needs to ligate the axillary artery due to severe trauma. To maintain adequate collateral circulation to the upper limb via the scapular anastomosis, the ligation must be performed proximal to which major branch?
optionExplanations: Ligation proximal to the lateral thoracic artery is safe but not the specific landmark for the scapular anastomosis.
The thoracoacromial artery is too proximal to be the critical landmark for this anastomosis.
Ligation must be proximal to the subscapular artery to allow blood from the scapular anastomosis to flow retrograde into the axillary artery.
The highest thoracic artery is a small branch of the first part.
Ligating the brachial artery would cut off all main supply to the forearm.
citation: it is critical to ligate the axillary artery proximal to the subscapular artery

statement: During a surgical procedure, the surgeon pierces the clavipectoral fascia to reach the axilla. Which artery's branches are typically found piercing this fascia along with the cephalic vein?
optionExplanations: The lateral thoracic artery runs along the lower border of the pectoralis minor.
Branches of the thoracoacromial artery, the cephalic vein, and the lateral pectoral nerve pierce the clavipectoral fascia.
The subscapular artery is a branch of the third part and does not pierce this fascia.
The highest thoracic artery is a branch of the first part and does not pierce this fascia.
The main trunk of the axillary artery lies deep to the clavipectoral fascia.
citation: branches of the thoracoacromial artery... pierce the clavipectoral fascia

statement: A patient has a severe infection in the hand that has spread to the lateral group of axillary lymph nodes. These nodes are located along which structure?
optionExplanations: The anterior group lies along the lower border of the pectoralis minor.
The posterior group lies in front of the subscapularis muscle.
The lateral group of axillary lymph nodes lies along the medial side of the axillary vein.
The lateral nodes are pressed against the vein, not primarily the artery.
The apical group lies at the lateral border of the first rib.
citation: Lateral group: Lying along the medial side of the axillary vein

statement: During an autopsy, the pathologist identifies a group of lymph nodes lying in front of the subscapularis muscle on the posterior wall of the axilla. Which group is this?
optionExplanations: The anterior group lies along the pectoralis minor.
The posterior (subscapular) group lies in front of the subscapularis muscle.
The lateral group lies along the axillary vein.
The central group lies in the axillary fat.
The apical group lies at the apex of the axilla.
citation: Posterior (subscapular) group: Lying in front of the subscapularis muscle

statement: A clinician is palpating the axilla for enlarged lymph nodes. They feel nodes located in the axillary fat deep to the pectoralis minor muscle. Which group is this?
optionExplanations: The anterior group is more superficial, along the pectoralis minor's lower border.
The posterior group is on the posterior wall.
The lateral group is along the axillary vein.
The central group lies in the center of the axilla, deep to the pectoralis minor.
The apical group is at the very top of the axilla.
citation: Central group: Lying in the center of the axilla, in the axillary fat deep to the pectoralis minor

statement: A patient has advanced breast cancer with metastasis to the apical axillary lymph nodes. These nodes are located at the lateral border of which structure?
optionExplanations: The clavicle forms the anterior boundary of the apex.
The scapula forms the posterior boundary of the apex.
The apical group of axillary lymph nodes is located at the lateral border of the first rib.
The humerus forms the lateral wall of the axilla, not the apex.
The pectoralis minor is a landmark for the central and anterior groups.
citation: Apical group: Lying at the apex of the axilla at the lateral border of the first rib

statement: What structure forms the medial boundary of the apex of the axilla?
optionExplanations: The clavicle forms the anterior boundary of the apex.
The upper border of the scapula forms the posterior boundary of the apex.
The outer border of the first rib forms the medial boundary of the axillary apex.
The serratus anterior forms the medial wall of the axilla proper, not the apex.
The pectoralis minor forms part of the anterior wall.
citation: medially by the outer border of the first rib

statement: What are the spinal root values of the axillary nerve?
optionExplanations: The axillary nerve is derived from the C5 and C6 roots of the brachial plexus.
C7 and C8 are not the primary roots for the axillary nerve.
C8 and T1 are the roots for the ulnar nerve.
C5, 6, and 7 are the roots for the musculocutaneous nerve.
C6, 7, and 8 are roots contributing to the radial nerve.
citation: The axillary nerve (C5 to 6)

statement: Which structure forms the medial boundary of the quadrangular space?
optionExplanations: The surgical neck of the humerus forms the lateral boundary.
The teres major muscle forms the inferior boundary.
The long head of the triceps brachii forms the medial boundary of the quadrangular space.
The teres minor forms part of the superior boundary.
The subscapularis forms part of the superior boundary.
citation: It is bounded medially by the long head of the triceps

statement: Which branch typically arises from the first part of the axillary artery?
optionExplanations: The thoracoacromial artery arises from the second part.
The lateral thoracic artery arises from the second part.
The highest (superior) thoracic artery is the only branch of the first part of the axillary artery.
The subscapular artery arises from the third part.
The circumflex scapular artery is a branch of the subscapular artery.
citation: The highest thoracic artery is small and extremely variable.

statement: At which landmark does the axillary vein terminate and become the subclavian vein?
optionExplanations: The axillary vein begins at the lower border of the teres major.
The pectoralis minor is a landmark for the parts of the axillary artery.
The axillary vein ends at the lateral border of the first rib, where it continues as the subclavian vein.
The medial border of the first rib is not the standard anatomical landmark for this transition.
The vein passes posterior to the clavicle but the rib is the specific landmark.
citation: ends at the lateral border of the first rib by becoming the subclavian vein

statement: A 24-year-old athlete is brought to the ER after a heavy fall on his shoulder during a rugby match. Radiographs reveal a fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus. Physical examination shows a loss of skin sensation over the lower half of the deltoid muscle. The nerve likely injured in this patient passes through a space that has which of the following medial boundaries?
optionExplanations: The long head of the triceps forms the medial boundary of the quadrangular space, which transmits the axillary nerve.
The lateral head of the triceps is not a boundary of the quadrangular space.
The teres minor muscle forms the superior boundary of the quadrangular space.
The teres major muscle forms the inferior boundary of the quadrangular space.
The coracobrachialis muscle is located in the lateral wall of the axilla and is not a boundary of the quadrangular space.
citation: It is bounded medially by the long head of the triceps and laterally by the surgical neck of the humerus.

coreConcept: Axillary nerve injury in humerus fracture
statement: A 70-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department after falling on her outstretched hand. Radiographs reveal a fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus. Which of the following structures is most likely at risk of injury?
optionExplanations: The axillary nerve winds around the surgical neck of the humerus and is frequently damaged in fractures at this site.
The radial nerve is related to the midshaft of the humerus in the spiral groove.
The ulnar nerve is related to the medial epicondyle of the humerus.
The median nerve does not have a close relationship with the surgical neck of the humerus.
The musculocutaneous nerve enters the coracobrachialis muscle and is not related to the surgical neck.
citation: The axillary nerve and circumflex humeral blood vessels have close relations to the surgical neck and can be readily damaged in association with a fracture here

coreConcept: Long thoracic nerve and winged scapula
statement: A 35-year-old patient undergoes a radical mastectomy. Postoperatively, the surgeon notices that the vertebral border and inferior angle of the scapula protrude posteriorly when the patient pushes against a wall. Which muscle is paralyzed?
optionExplanations: Paralysis of the serratus anterior, often due to long thoracic nerve injury, results in the characteristic 'winged scapula'.
Pectoralis major paralysis causes weakness in adduction and medial rotation but not winging.
Latissimus dorsi paralysis causes weakness in extension and adduction.
Subscapularis is a medial rotator and does not maintain the scapula against the chest wall.
Teres major is an adductor and medial rotator.
citation: winged scapula (Fig. 3.4), caused by paralysis of the serratus anterior

coreConcept: Axillary lymph node drainage of the breast
statement: A 50-year-old woman presents with a hard, painless lump in the upper lateral quadrant of her left breast. If malignant cells have metastasized to the primary draining lymph nodes, which group will be involved first?
optionExplanations: The anterior or pectoral group of axillary nodes receives the majority of lymph from the lateral quadrants of the breast.
The posterior group drains the skin of the back down to the iliac crests.
The lateral group primarily drains the upper limb.
The central group receives lymph from the anterior, posterior, and lateral groups.
The apical group is the final common pathway for all axillary lymph drainage.
citation: The lateral quadrants of the breast drain into the anterior axillary or pectoral group of nodes

statement: An anesthesiologist prepares to perform a regional nerve block for a forearm surgery. He palpates the pulsations of the third part of the axillary artery to identify the injection site. Which anatomical structure allows the anesthetic to bathe the entire plexus?
optionExplanations: The axillary sheath is a fascial envelope that contains the axillary vessels and brachial plexus, allowing anesthetic to spread around the nerves.
The clavipectoral fascia lies deep to the pectoralis major but does not enclose the neurovascular bundle.
The pretracheal fascia is located in the neck and is not part of the axilla.
The suspensory ligament is a continuation of the clavipectoral fascia to the axillary floor.
The bicipital aponeurosis is located in the cubital fossa.
citation: Because the axillary sheath encloses the axillary vessels and the brachial plexus, a brachial plexus nerve block can easily be obtained

statement: A baseball pitcher experiences sharp pain in the posterior shoulder during high-velocity throws. MRI suggests hypertrophy of the long head of the triceps compressing structures in the quadrangular space. Which vessel is likely compressed?
optionExplanations: The posterior circumflex humeral vessels and the axillary nerve are the primary contents of the quadrangular space.
The anterior circumflex humeral artery winds around the surgical neck but does not pass through the quadrangular space.
The subscapular artery descends along the lower border of the subscapularis.
The thoracoacromial artery is a branch of the second part of the axillary artery.
The profunda brachii artery travels with the radial nerve in the spiral groove.
citation: the axillary nerve and the posterior circumflex humeral vessels emerge through this space

coreConcept: Axillary artery ligation and collateral circulation
statement: A surgeon must ligate the axillary artery due to a severe traumatic injury. To ensure the survival of the upper limb via the scapular anastomosis, the ligature must be placed proximal to the origin of which artery?
optionExplanations: Ligating proximal to the subscapular artery allows blood from the scapular anastomosis to flow retrograde into the subscapular artery and then distally into the limb.
The lateral thoracic artery is part of the second part and does not provide the primary collateral pathway.
The thoracoacromial artery is too proximal to be the deciding factor for the subscapular bypass.
The highest thoracic artery is a small branch of the first part.
The profunda brachii is a branch of the brachial artery, distal to the axillary artery.
citation: it is critical to ligate the axillary artery proximal to the subscapular artery in order to maintain adequate collateral flow

statement: A 22-year-old weightlifter presents with sudden swelling, heaviness, and a bluish tint of his right upper limb after an intense training session involving heavy overhead presses. What is the most likely diagnosis?
optionExplanations: Spontaneous axillary vein thrombosis (Paget-Schroetter syndrome) can occur after strenuous, unaccustomed arm activity.
An aneurysm would present with a pulsatile mass and potentially distal ischemia, not limb-wide venous congestion.
Brachial plexus avulsion presents with motor and sensory loss, not acute vascular swelling.
Lymphangitis presents with red streaks and tender nodes, usually following an infection.
A muscle tear would cause localized pain and bruising, not global limb edema.
citation: Spontaneous thrombosis of the axillary vein occasionally occurs after excessive and unaccustomed movements of the arm

coreConcept: Pectoralis minor as a landmark for the axillary artery
statement: During a surgical dissection of the axilla, the resident is asked to identify the second part of the axillary artery. Which muscle should be used as the primary landmark to locate this specific segment?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis minor muscle is the standard anatomical landmark used to divide the axillary artery into three parts.
The pectoralis major forms the anterior wall but does not define the three segments of the artery.
The subclavius is part of the anterior wall but is located superiorly.
The serratus anterior forms the medial wall.
The coracobrachialis forms part of the lateral wall.
citation: The pectoralis minor muscle crosses in front of the axillary artery and divides it into three parts

statement: A clinician is performing a physical examination of the axilla. He asks the patient to press his hands against his hips to make the anterior axillary fold prominent. This fold is primarily composed of which muscle?
optionExplanations: The lower border of the pectoralis major muscle forms the anterior axillary fold.
The pectoralis minor lies deep to the pectoralis major and does not form the fold.
The latissimus dorsi forms the posterior axillary fold.
The teres major contributes to the posterior axillary fold.
The serratus anterior forms the medial wall of the axilla.
citation: The anterior axillary fold is formed by the lower margin of the pectoralis major muscle

statement: While examining a patient's axilla, the physician grasps the thick fleshy fold forming the back of the armpit. Which muscle's tendon is the primary component of this posterior axillary fold?
optionExplanations: The latissimus dorsi tendon, winding around the teres major, forms the posterior axillary fold.
The pectoralis major forms the anterior fold.
The subscapularis forms the posterior wall but is not the primary component of the palpable fold.
The serratus anterior is on the medial wall.

statement: A physician is palpating for enlarged lymph nodes in a patient with suspected breast cancer. To reach the apical group of axillary nodes, the fingers must be pushed high up toward which bony landmark?

statement: A patient presents with a deep laceration in the posterior wall of the axilla. The surgeon identifies a large artery descending along the lower border of the subscapularis muscle. This vessel typically divides into which two branches?

statement: During an axillary lymph node dissection, the surgeon identifies an artery running along the lateral border of the pectoralis minor muscle. Which artery is this?

statement: A patient presents with a 'winged scapula' after a stab wound to the lateral chest wall. The nerve involved is particularly vulnerable because it lies on the superficial surface of which muscle?

statement: A 45-year-old woman notices a firm mass in her armpit that becomes tender during her menstrual cycle. Anatomical knowledge suggests this mass is likely an extension of the mammary gland. This extension pierces the deep fascia at the lower border of which muscle?

coreConcept: First part of the axillary artery branch
statement: Which artery typically arises from the first part of the axillary artery?

statement: A 65-year-old woman presents to the ER after a fall on her outstretched hand. Radiographs reveal a fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus. Which nerve is most likely at risk of injury in this patient?

statement: A 55-year-old woman with advanced breast cancer presents with palpable lymph nodes at the very apex of her axilla, near the lateral border of the first rib. These nodes represent the final common pathway for axillary drainage. Which group is being described?

Coracobrachialis forms the lateral wall.
