statement: An examiner asks a student to palpate the posterior boundary of the axillary base. The student identifies a thick fold of tissue. Which two muscles contribute to the formation of this posterior axillary fold?
optionExplanations: Subscapularis forms the posterior wall but not the fold itself.
The posterior axillary fold is formed by the tendons of the latissimus dorsi and teres major muscles.
Subscapularis is not part of the fold.
Teres minor is not part of the fold.
Infraspinatus is located on the posterior surface of the scapula.
citation: The posterior axillary fold is formed by the tendons of the latissimus dorsi and teres major muscles

statement: Which structures constitute the narrow lateral wall of the axilla?
optionExplanations: Serratus anterior forms the medial wall.
Subscapularis and latissimus dorsi form the posterior wall.
The lateral wall is formed by the coracobrachialis and biceps brachii muscles in the bicipital groove of the humerus.
Pectoralis muscles form the anterior wall.
The deltoid is not a boundary of the axilla.
citation: The lateral wall: By the coracobrachialis and biceps brachii muscles in the bicipital groove of the humerus

statement: What anatomical structures form the medial wall of the axillary space?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis muscles form the anterior wall.
Subscapularis forms the posterior wall.
The medial wall is formed by the upper four or five ribs and the intercostal spaces covered by the serratus anterior muscle.
Coracobrachialis forms the lateral wall.
The clavicle and first rib form part of the apex.
citation: The medial wall: By the upper four or five ribs and the intercostal spaces covered by the serratus anterior muscle

statement: An anesthesiologist performs a nerve block by injecting local anesthetic into a connective tissue wrapping in the axilla. This wrapping is continuous with the prevertebral fascia of the neck. What is the name of this structure that contains the neurovascular bundle?
optionExplanations: Clavipectoral fascia is deep to the pectoralis major.
Axillary fascia forms the floor of the axilla.
The axillary sheath encloses the axillary vessels and the brachial plexus.
The suspensory ligament is a continuation of the clavipectoral fascia.
Pretracheal fascia is in the neck but not the axilla.
citation: The axillary sheath encloses the axillary vessels and the brachial plexus

statement: A surgeon is operating on the posterior wall of the axilla. They identify a large arterial branch arising from the third part of the axillary artery that descends along the axillary border of the scapula. Which artery is this?
optionExplanations: The lateral thoracic artery arises from the second part.
The thoracoacromial artery arises from the second part.
The subscapular artery is a large vessel that descends along the lower (axillary) border of the scapula.
The highest thoracic artery arises from the first part.
The profunda brachii arises from the brachial artery.
citation: The subscapular artery is a large vessel that descends along the lower (axillary) border of the scapula

statement: During a physical examination, a clinician palpates for lymph nodes by pressing laterally against the pulsating axillary artery and the adjacent vein. Which specific group of axillary nodes are they attempting to feel?
optionExplanations: Pectoral nodes are felt against the pectoralis major.
Subscapular nodes are felt against the subscapularis.
The lateral nodes may be palpated against the medial side of the axillary vein.
Central nodes are in the center of the axillary fat.
Apical nodes are high up at the outer border of the first rib.
citation: The lateral nodes may be palpated against the medial side of the axillary vein

statement: A patient has a severe infection on their upper back. Which group of axillary lymph nodes, located on the posterior wall of the axilla, would most likely be enlarged and tender?
optionExplanations: The anterior group drains the breast and abdominal wall.
The posterior (subscapular) group lies in front of the subscapularis and drains the back.
The lateral group drains the arm.
The central group receives lymph from other groups.
The apical group is the final drainage point.
citation: The posterior (subscapular) group: Lying in front of the subscapularis muscle

statement: A 40-year-old woman presents with a suspicious lump in the lateral quadrant of her breast. The physician palpates for enlarged lymph nodes along the lower border of the pectoralis minor muscle. Which group is being examined?
optionExplanations: The anterior (pectoral) group lies along the lower border of the pectoralis minor.
Subscapular nodes are on the posterior wall.
Lateral nodes are along the axillary vein.
Central nodes are deep in the axillary fat.
Apical nodes are at the apex of the axilla.
citation: The anterior (pectoral) group: Lying along the lower border of the pectoralis minor

statement: A 19-year-old wrestler suffers an inferior dislocation of the shoulder. He subsequently develops paralysis of the deltoid muscle and loss of sensation over the lateral aspect of the upper arm. Which nerve was most likely damaged?
optionExplanations: Radial nerve injury causes wrist drop.
Ulnar nerve injury causes claw hand.
Median nerve injury causes ape hand.
The axillary nerve can be injured in dislocations of the glenohumeral (shoulder) joint.
Musculocutaneous nerve injury affects elbow flexion.
citation: The axillary nerve can be injured in dislocations of the glenohumeral (shoulder) joint

statement: A fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus results in a large hematoma in the shoulder region. The surgeon suspects a tear in the artery that travels through the quadrangular space. Which vessel is most likely involved?
optionExplanations: The anterior circumflex humeral is smaller and does not pass through the quadrangular space.
The posterior circumflex humeral artery passes through the quadrangular space with the axillary nerve.
The subscapular artery descends along the scapula.
The profunda brachii passes through the triangular interval.
The thoracoacromial artery is in the anterior axilla.
citation: The posterior circumflex humeral artery... passes through the quadrangular space with the axillary nerve

statement: A medical student is asked to trace the cephalic vein from the deltopectoral triangle. After piercing the clavipectoral fascia, into which vessel does the cephalic vein normally empty?
optionExplanations: The basilic vein joins the brachial veins to form the axillary vein.
The brachial veins are deep veins.
The cephalic vein terminates by draining into the axillary vein.
The axillary vein becomes the subclavian vein at the first rib.
The internal thoracic vein is inside the chest.
citation: The cephalic vein... terminates by draining into the axillary vein

statement: At which anatomical landmark does the axillary artery terminate and continue as the brachial artery?
optionExplanations: This is the origin of the axillary artery.
The axillary artery ends at the lower border of the teres major muscle.
This marks the start of the second part.
This marks the start of the third part.
The artery passes the surgical neck but does not end there.
citation: The axillary artery... ends at the lower border of the teres major muscle

statement: During an axillary dissection, a surgeon identifies several structures piercing a strong sheet of connective tissue deep to the pectoralis major. Which of the following sets of structures is correctly identified as piercing this clavipectoral fascia?
options: Medial pectoral nerve and basilic vein
Lateral pectoral nerve and cephalic vein
Long thoracic nerve and lateral thoracic artery
correctOption: Lateral pectoral nerve and cephalic vein
optionExplanations: The medial pectoral nerve pierces the pectoralis minor.
The lateral pectoral nerve, cephalic vein, and thoracoacromial branches pierce the clavipectoral fascia.
The axillary nerve does not pierce this fascia.
The long thoracic nerve is on the medial wall.
The thoracodorsal nerve is on the posterior wall.
citation: The lateral pectoral nerve, cephalic vein, branches of the thoracoacromial artery... pierce the clavipectoral fascia

statement: A 70-year-old woman falls and sustains a fracture of the proximal humerus. Radiographs show a displaced fracture at the surgical neck. Which nerve is most likely to be compressed or damaged due to its close relationship with the medial side of this bony landmark?
optionExplanations: The radial nerve is related to the midshaft (spiral groove).
The axillary nerve comes into close relationship with the medial side of the surgical neck of the humerus.
The ulnar nerve is related to the medial epicondyle.
The median nerve has no close relationship with the surgical neck.
The musculocutaneous nerve pierces the coracobrachialis.
citation: The axillary nerve... comes into close relationship with... the medial side of the surgical neck of the humerus

statement: A student is confused about why the cords of the brachial plexus are named 'lateral', 'medial', and 'posterior'. What anatomical structure serves as the reference point for this nomenclature?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis minor divides the artery but is not the reference for cord names.
The cords lie lateral to the first part.
The cords of the plexus are named according to their positional relationship to the second part of the axillary artery.
The cords have already branched into terminal nerves by the third part.
The clavicle divides the plexus into supra- and infraclavicular parts.
citation: The cords of the plexus are named according to their positional relationship to the second part of the axillary artery

statement: A surgeon is performing an axillary clearance for metastatic melanoma. Which of the following represents the standard anatomical contents found within the axillary fat that must be carefully managed?
options: Brachial artery and basilic vein only
optionExplanations: The axilla contains the axillary artery, vein, brachial plexus, and lymph nodes.
The brachial artery begins after the axilla.
The cephalic vein is superficial; the radial nerve is only one part of the plexus.
Subclavian vessels are in the neck/thorax.
Internal thoracic vessels are in the chest wall.
citation: The axilla contains the axillary artery and its branches... the axillary vein and its tributaries... and lymph vessels and lymph nodes

statement: A 70-year-old woman is brought to the ER after falling on her outstretched hand. Radiographs reveal a fracture of the humerus. The physician is concerned about potential nerve damage due to the proximity of a specific bony landmark. Which anatomical relationship is most critical in this case?
options: Axillary nerve at the surgical neck
Median nerve at the supracondylar ridge
correctOption: Axillary nerve at the surgical neck
optionExplanations: The radial nerve is related to the midshaft spiral groove.
The ulnar nerve is related to the medial epicondyle.
The surgical neck of the humerus is closely related to the axillary nerve, making it vulnerable in fractures at this site.
The median nerve is related to the supracondylar region but not as a primary risk in surgical neck fractures.
The musculocutaneous nerve pierces the coracobrachialis distal to the surgical neck.
citation: The surgical neck has important relations with the axillary nerve

statement: A 45-year-old woman undergoes a radical mastectomy for breast cancer. Postoperatively, she notices that her right scapula protrudes posteriorly when she pushes against a wall. The surgeon suspects nerve damage. Why was this nerve particularly vulnerable during the axillary dissection?
options: It lies superficial to its muscle
It passes through the quadrangular space
It is enclosed in the axillary sheath
It runs deep to the subscapularis
correctOption: It lies superficial to its muscle
optionExplanations: The medial pectoral nerve pierces the pectoralis minor.
The long thoracic nerve is unusual because it descends over the superficial surface of the serratus anterior, making it prone to trauma during axillary surgery.
The axillary nerve passes through the quadrangular space.
The long thoracic nerve is not part of the axillary sheath contents.
The subscapular nerves run deep in the axilla.
citation: It descends over the superficial surface of the serratus anterior muscle, which it supplies

statement: During a surgical exploration of the axilla to control hemorrhage, the surgeon uses a specific muscle as a landmark to identify the three segments of the axillary artery. Which muscle serves this purpose?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis major forms the anterior wall but does not divide the artery into parts.
The serratus anterior forms the medial wall.
The pectoralis minor muscle crosses the axillary artery, dividing it into proximal (1st), deep (2nd), and distal (3rd) parts.
The subclavius is part of the anterior wall but is superior to the divisions.
The latissimus dorsi forms part of the posterior wall.
citation: The pectoralis minor muscle crosses in front of the axillary artery and divides it into three parts

statement: A baseball pitcher presents with deep shoulder pain and weakness in abduction. An MRI suggests compression of structures within the intermuscular space located immediately below the glenohumeral joint. Which structures are likely affected?
options: Axillary nerve and posterior circumflex humeral
Long thoracic nerve and lateral thoracic
correctOption: Axillary nerve and posterior circumflex humeral
optionExplanations: The radial nerve and profunda brachii are in the spiral groove.
The quadrangular space transmits the axillary nerve and the posterior circumflex humeral vessels.
The ulnar nerve is not related to this space.
The long thoracic nerve is on the medial wall of the axilla.
The suprascapular nerve passes through the scapular notch.
citation: the axillary nerve and the posterior circumflex humeral vessels emerge through this space

statement: A patient is diagnosed with a venous thrombosis in the axilla. The radiologist notes the clot begins exactly where the axillary vein is formed. At which anatomical landmark and by which vessels does this formation occur?
optionExplanations: The axillary vein ends at the first rib.
The axillary vein is formed at the lower border of the teres major by the union of the brachial artery's venae comitantes and the basilic vein.
The pectoralis minor divides the artery, not the vein formation.
The teres minor is the superior boundary of the quadrangular space.
The cephalic vein drains into the axillary vein much higher up.
citation: formed at the lower border of the teres major muscle by the union of the venae comitantes of the brachial artery and the basilic vein

statement: A 55-year-old woman discovers a lump in the lateral quadrant of her breast. During the physical exam, the physician palpates for the anterior group of axillary lymph nodes. Where should the physician's fingers be directed to find these specific nodes?
options: Outer border of the first rib
optionExplanations: The lateral group lies along the axillary vein.
The anterior (pectoral) group is situated just posterior to the lower border of the pectoralis major muscle.
The posterior (subscapular) group lies in front of the subscapularis.
The infraclavicular nodes lie in the deltopectoral groove.
The apical nodes lie at the outer border of the first rib.
citation: situated just posterior to the lower border of the pectoralis major muscle

statement: An anesthesiologist is performing a brachial plexus block. To ensure the anesthetic reaches the target, the needle is inserted into the proximal part of a specific fascial compartment. What structures are contained within this sheath?
optionExplanations: The long thoracic nerve is superficial to the serratus anterior, outside the sheath.
The axillary sheath encloses the brachial plexus and the axillary artery and vein.
The axillary nerve leaves the sheath to enter the quadrangular space.
The cephalic vein pierces the clavipectoral fascia to enter the sheath.
The thoracodorsal nerve is a branch of the posterior cord but is not the primary content described.
citation: the brachial plexus and the axillary artery and vein are enclosed together in a connective tissue wrapping termed the axillary sheath

statement: A surgeon is operating deep to the pectoralis minor muscle. To identify the posterior cord of the brachial plexus, where should they look in relation to the axillary artery?
options: In front of the second part
optionExplanations: All cords are lateral/superior to the first part.
The medial cord is medial to the second part.
The cords are named based on their relationship to the second part of the axillary artery; the posterior cord lies behind it.
No cord lies in front of the second part.
The cords are lateral to the first part.
citation: The posterior cord lies behind the second part of the artery

statement: A patient presents with a deep laceration along the lateral chest wall. The physician notes heavy bleeding from an artery that typically supplies the breast and follows the inferior margin of the smaller pectoral muscle. Which artery is likely damaged?
optionExplanations: The highest thoracic is at the upper border of pectoralis minor.
The thoracoacromial is a short trunk from the second part.
The lateral thoracic artery runs along the lower (lateral) border of the pectoralis minor.
The subscapular artery is from the third part.
The posterior circumflex humeral is from the third part.
citation: runs along the lower (lateral) border of the pectoralis minor along the lateral chest wall

statement: During an axillary lymph node dissection, the surgeon identifies a large arterial branch arising from the third part of the axillary artery. It is seen descending along the axillary margin of the shoulder blade. What is this vessel?
optionExplanations: The lateral thoracic is from the second part.
The thoracodorsal is a branch of the subscapular.
The subscapular artery is a large vessel from the third part that descends along the lower (axillary) border of the scapula.
The circumflex scapular is a branch of the subscapular.
The highest thoracic is from the first part.
citation: large vessel that descends along the lower (axillary) border of the scapula

statement: A patient with a shoulder dislocation is tested for sensory loss. The physician checks the skin over the lower half of the deltoid muscle. Which nerve's integrity is being evaluated?
optionExplanations: The radial nerve supplies the back of the arm.
The suprascapular nerve is purely motor to the spinati and sensory to the joint.
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid and the skin covering its lower part.
The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the lateral forearm.
The medial cutaneous nerve supplies the medial arm.
citation: supplies the deltoid and the skin that covers its lower part

statement: A medical student is asked to identify the structures forming the medial boundary of the axillary space during a dissection. Which of the following should they point to?
optionExplanations: These form the anterior wall.
These form the posterior wall.
The medial wall is formed by the upper four or five ribs and intercostal spaces covered by the serratus anterior.
These form the lateral wall.
These form the apex.
citation: upper four or five ribs and the intercostal spaces covered by the serratus anterior muscle

statement: A patient has a deep abscess in the axilla. The surgeon notes the infection is limited posteriorly by a group of muscles. Which muscles form this posterior boundary?
optionExplanations: These are anterior.
These are medial.
The posterior wall is formed by the subscapularis, latissimus dorsi, and teres major muscles.
These are lateral.
These are anterior.
citation: By the subscapularis, latissimus dorsi, and teres major muscles

statement: A patient presents with 'Thoracic Outlet Syndrome' where nerves are compressed as they enter the axilla. The physician explains the anatomy of the axillary inlet (apex). What are its boundaries?
optionExplanations: The sternum is not a boundary of the apex.
The apex is bounded by the clavicle (front), upper border of scapula (behind), and outer border of the first rib (medially).
The humerus is not part of the apex.
The pectoralis minor is part of the anterior wall, not the apex.
The acromion and coracoid are lateral to the apex.
citation: bounded in front by the clavicle, behind by the upper border of the scapula, and medially by the outer border of the first rib

statement: A woman presents with a palpable mass in her armpit. The physician explains that breast tissue can sometimes extend into this region. What is the specific pathway of this extension?
optionExplanations: It does not go deep to the subclavius.
The cephalic vein pierces the clavipectoral fascia.
The axillary tail pierces the deep fascia at the lower border of the pectoralis major to enter the axilla.
It is anterior to the serratus anterior.
It is medial to the humerus.
citation: pierces the deep fascia at the lower border of the pectoralis major muscle, and enters the axilla

statement: In a patient with advanced breast cancer, the surgeon is most concerned about the 'apical' group of axillary lymph nodes. Why are these nodes considered the final common pathway for axillary drainage?
options: They receive all other axillary lymph
They drain directly into the heart
They are the first to enlarge
correctOption: They receive all other axillary lymph
optionExplanations: The lateral group drains the arm.
The posterior group drains the back.
The apical nodes receive the efferent lymph vessels from all the other axillary node groups.
They drain into the subclavian trunk, not the heart directly.
The pectoral nodes are often the first to enlarge in breast cancer.
citation: receive the efferent lymph vessels from all the other axillary nodes

statement: A patient has a severe infection on their hand, and the physician finds enlarged lymph nodes in the axilla. These nodes are located along the medial side of the main axillary vein. Which group is this?
optionExplanations: The pectoral group is along the pectoralis minor.
The subscapular group is on the posterior wall.
The lateral group lies along the medial side of the axillary vein and drains the upper limb.
The central group is in the axillary fat.
The apical group is at the apex.
citation: Lying along the medial side of the axillary vein, these nodes receive most of the lymph vessels of the upper limb

statement: During surgery, a short arterial trunk is identified arising from the second part of the axillary artery, just medial to the pectoralis minor. It quickly fans out into multiple branches. What is this vessel?
optionExplanations: The lateral thoracic is a long, single vessel.
The subscapular is from the third part.
The thoracoacromial artery is a short trunk that immediately divides into four terminal branches (pectoral, deltoid, acromial, clavicular).
The highest thoracic is from the first part.
The brachial is the continuation of the axillary.
citation: short trunk that immediately divides into four terminal branches

statement: A surgeon is performing a posterior approach to the shoulder. They identify the quadrangular space to protect the axillary nerve. What structures form the medial and lateral boundaries of this space?
optionExplanations: These are the inferior and superior boundaries.
The quadrangular space is bounded medially by the long head of the triceps and laterally by the surgical neck of the humerus.
The deltoid is not a boundary.
The scapula is not a direct boundary.
The lateral head of the triceps is not a boundary.
citation: bounded medially by the long head of the triceps and laterally by the surgical neck of the humerus

statement: A patient presents with weakness in shoulder abduction and loss of sensation over the lateral arm. The physician suspects a lesion of the nerve that enters the quadrangular space. Where does this nerve originate?
optionExplanations: The lateral cord gives the musculocutaneous nerve.
The medial cord gives the ulnar nerve.
The axillary nerve arises from the posterior cord of the brachial plexus.
The upper trunk gives the suprascapular nerve.
The middle trunk has no branches.

statement: What is the root value of the axillary nerve?
optionExplanations: The axillary nerve is derived from the C5 and C6 spinal roots.
C7, 8 are not the primary roots for this nerve.
C8, T1 form the medial cord.
C5, 6, 7 form the musculocutaneous nerve.
C6, 7, 8 are related to the radial nerve.
citation: The axillary nerve (C5 to 6) arises from the termination of the posterior cord

statement: Which artery typically arises from the first part of the axillary artery?
optionExplanations: Thoracoacromial is from the second part.
Lateral thoracic is from the second part.
The highest (superior) thoracic artery is the only branch from the first part.
Subscapular is from the third part.
Circumflex humeral arteries are from the third part.
citation: The highest thoracic artery is small and extremely variable.

statement: At which anatomical landmark does the axillary vein terminate and change its name?
optionExplanations: This is where it begins.
This is a landmark for the artery parts.
The axillary vein ends at the lateral border of the first rib, where it becomes the subclavian vein.
This is not a termination point.
The vein passes behind the clavicle but terminates at the rib.
citation: ends at the lateral border of the first rib by becoming the subclavian vein

statement: Which group of muscles forms the anterior wall of the axilla?
optionExplanations: These are posterior.
Serratus is medial.
The anterior wall is formed by the pectoralis major, subclavius, and pectoralis minor muscles.
These are lateral.
These are posterior/superior.
citation: By the pectoralis major, subclavius, and pectoralis minor muscles

statement: Which muscles are located in the bicipital groove and form the lateral wall of the axilla?
optionExplanations: These are anterior.
These are posterior.
The lateral wall is formed by the coracobrachialis and biceps brachii muscles in the bicipital groove.
These are medial/anterior.
Latissimus is posterior.
citation: By the coracobrachialis and biceps brachii muscles in the bicipital groove of the humerus

The median cubital vein is located in the cubital fossa.
The external jugular vein is in the neck.
The internal thoracic vein is inside the thoracic cavity.

coreConcept: Long thoracic nerve relation and injury
statement: Following a radical mastectomy, a patient notices that her right scapula protrudes posteriorly when she pushes against a wall. The nerve damaged during surgery was vulnerable because it descends on the superficial surface of which muscle?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis minor is related to the pectoral nerves.

citation: The highest thoracic artery is small and extremely variable.

statement: A patient with a shoulder dislocation is tested for nerve damage. The physician checks for sensation in the 'regimental badge' area. Loss of sensation here indicates injury to which nerve?
optionExplanations: The axillary nerve (via the upper lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm) supplies the skin over the lower half of the deltoid.
The radial nerve supplies the posterior arm and forearm.
Supraclavicular nerves supply the skin over the point of the shoulder.
This nerve supplies the medial side of the arm.
This nerve supplies the skin of the armpit and medial arm.
citation: consequently, there is a loss of skin sensation over the lower half of the deltoid muscle.

statement: A patient presents with symptoms of axillary nerve entrapment. The clinician considers a space bounded by the humerus laterally and the teres muscles. What structure forms the medial boundary of this space?
optionExplanations: The long head of the triceps brachii forms the medial boundary of the quadrangular space.
The lateral head is not a boundary of this space.
The medial head is located deeper and more distally on the humerus.
The coracobrachialis is in the lateral wall of the axilla.
The serratus anterior is on the medial wall of the axilla.
citation: It is bounded medially by the long head of the triceps

statement: A 70-year-old woman falls in the bathroom and sustains a fracture of the humerus. Radiographs confirm a displaced fracture at the surgical neck. Which nerve is most likely at risk of injury in this patient?
optionExplanations: The radial nerve is related to the midshaft of the humerus.
The ulnar nerve is related to the medial epicondyle.
The axillary nerve winds around the surgical neck of the humerus, making it vulnerable during fractures at this site.
The median nerve has no direct contact with the proximal humerus.
The musculocutaneous nerve pierces the coracobrachialis and is not related to the surgical neck.
citation: The surgical neck has important relations with the axillary nerve

statement: An anesthesiologist is performing a regional nerve block for a patient undergoing forearm surgery. The needle is inserted into the proximal part of a connective tissue wrapping in the axilla to bathe the nerves in anesthetic. What is the name of this structure?
optionExplanations: The clavipectoral fascia lies deep to the pectoralis major but does not enclose the plexus.
The axillary sheath is a fascial extension that encloses the axillary artery, vein, and brachial plexus cords.
The prevertebral fascia is continuous with the sheath but is located in the neck.
The suspensory ligament is a continuation of the clavipectoral fascia.
The bicipital aponeurosis is located in the cubital fossa.
citation: The axillary sheath encloses the axillary vessels and the brachial plexus

statement: A 35-year-old man is brought to the ER with a deep laceration on the medial wall of his axilla. During surgical exploration, the surgeon notes damage to the muscle covering the upper ribs in this region. Which muscle is most likely affected?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis minor forms part of the anterior wall.
Subscapularis forms part of the posterior wall.
The serratus anterior muscle covers the upper ribs and intercostal spaces, forming the medial wall of the axilla.
Latissimus dorsi forms part of the posterior wall.
Teres major forms part of the posterior wall.
citation: The medial wall is formed by the upper four or five ribs and the intercostal spaces covered by the serratus anterior muscle

statement: A 50-year-old woman is diagnosed with a malignant tumor in the upper lateral quadrant of her right breast. Which group of axillary lymph nodes is most likely to be the first site of metastasis?
optionExplanations: The posterior group drains the back down to the iliac crests.
The lateral group drains most of the upper limb.
The anterior (pectoral) group receives lymph from the lateral quadrants of the breast.
The apical group receives efferents from all other axillary nodes.
The infraclavicular group drains the lateral side of the hand and forearm.
citation: The lateral quadrants of the breast drain into the anterior axillary or pectoral group of nodes

statement: During a dissection of the axilla, a medical student is asked to identify the second part of the axillary artery. Which anatomical landmark should the student use to locate this specific segment?
optionExplanations: The clavicle is related to the apex but not the division of the artery.
The pectoralis major is superficial to the entire axilla.
The pectoralis minor muscle crosses the artery, defining the first (proximal), second (deep), and third (distal) parts.
The teres major marks the termination of the axillary artery.
The first rib marks the beginning of the axillary artery.
citation: The pectoralis minor muscle crosses in front of the axillary artery and divides it into three parts

statement: A patient presents with weakness in shoulder abduction and loss of sensation over the lower deltoid. An MRI shows a tumor compressing the contents of the quadrangular space. Which muscle forms the inferior boundary of this space?
optionExplanations: Teres minor forms the superior boundary.
Subscapularis forms the superior boundary.
The teres major muscle forms the inferior boundary of the quadrangular space.
The long head of triceps forms the medial boundary.
The surgical neck of the humerus forms the lateral boundary.
citation: The quadrangular space is bounded below by the teres major muscle

statement: A surgeon is performing a vascular repair in the lower axilla. He identifies a large vein being formed by the junction of the brachial venae comitantes and another vessel. What is this other vessel?
optionExplanations: The cephalic vein drains into the axillary vein much higher up in the deltopectoral triangle.
The basilic vein joins the brachial venae comitantes at the lower border of the teres major to form the axillary vein.
The subclavian vein is the continuation of the axillary vein.
The internal thoracic vein is not a tributary of the axillary vein.
The lateral thoracic vein is a tributary but does not form the main trunk.
citation: The axillary vein is formed at the lower border of the teres major muscle by the union of the venae comitantes of the brachial artery and the basilic vein

statement: A patient with a stab wound to the axilla presents with an inability to abduct the arm beyond 15 degrees. The physician suspects damage to the axillary nerve. From which part of the brachial plexus does this nerve originate?
optionExplanations: The lateral cord gives rise to the musculocutaneous nerve.
The medial cord gives rise to the ulnar nerve.
The axillary nerve is a terminal branch of the posterior cord.
Trunks do not directly give off the axillary nerve.
The middle trunk continues as divisions, not terminal branches.
citation: The axillary nerve (C5 to 6) arises from the termination of the posterior cord of the brachial plexus

statement: A 22-year-old athlete suffers a penetrating injury to the lateral aspect of the axilla. The emergency physician notes that the injury has penetrated the bicipital groove. Which muscle forming the lateral wall is likely damaged?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis major forms the anterior wall.
Latissimus dorsi forms the posterior wall.
The coracobrachialis and biceps brachii muscles form the narrow lateral wall of the axilla.
Serratus anterior forms the medial wall.
Subscapularis forms the posterior wall.
citation: The lateral wall is formed by the coracobrachialis and biceps brachii muscles in the bicipital groove of the humerus

statement: A patient presents with an enlarged, tender lymph node in the posterior fold of the axilla. He recently had a severe skin infection on his upper back. Which group of nodes is most likely involved?
optionExplanations: The pectoral group drains the anterior chest wall and breast.
The lateral group drains the upper limb.
The posterior or subscapular group drains the skin of the back down to the iliac crests.
The central group receives lymph from other primary groups.
The apical group is the final common pathway for axillary drainage.
citation: The posterior (subscapular) group of nodes receive superficial lymph vessels from the back

statement: During surgery to repair a ruptured pectoralis major muscle, the surgeon identifies a short arterial trunk arising from the second part of the axillary artery. This vessel provides the primary blood supply to the pectoral region. What is this artery?
optionExplanations: The lateral thoracic artery runs along the lower border of pectoralis minor.
The subscapular artery arises from the third part.
The thoracoacromial artery arises from the second part and divides into four branches (pectoral, deltoid, acromial, clavicular).
The highest thoracic artery arises from the first part.
The circumflex scapular artery is a branch of the subscapular artery.
citation: The thoracoacromial artery is a short trunk that immediately divides into four terminal branches

statement: A patient presents with a shoulder injury. Physical examination reveals weakness in lateral rotation of the arm. Electromyography shows denervation of the teres minor muscle. Which nerve is damaged?
optionExplanations: The suprascapular nerve supplies supraspinatus and infraspinatus.
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid and teres minor muscles.
The thoracodorsal nerve supplies latissimus dorsi.
The lower subscapular nerve supplies subscapularis and teres major.
The radial nerve supplies the triceps and forearm extensors.
citation: The posterior terminal branch gives off a branch to the teres minor muscle

statement: A patient with Thoracic Outlet Syndrome has compression of the neurovascular bundle as it enters the axillary apex. Which structure forms the medial boundary of this narrow passage?
optionExplanations: The clavicle forms the anterior boundary of the apex.
The upper border of the scapula forms the posterior boundary.
The outer border of the first rib forms the medial boundary of the axillary apex (inlet).
The coracoid process is not a boundary of the apex.
The manubrium is medial to the first rib and not a direct boundary.
citation: The apex is bounded medially by the outer border of the first rib

statement: A 25-year-old man is stabbed in the posterior aspect of the shoulder. The knife enters the quadrangular space. Which artery is most likely to be severed in this location?
optionExplanations: The profunda brachii artery travels in the radial groove.
The subscapular artery does not enter the quadrangular space.
The posterior circumflex humeral artery travels through the quadrangular space alongside the axillary nerve.
The anterior circumflex humeral artery winds around the front of the surgical neck.
The thoracodorsal artery descends along the latissimus dorsi.
citation: The posterior circumflex humeral artery passes through the quadrangular space with the axillary nerve

statement: During a radical mastectomy, the surgeon removes a group of lymph nodes embedded in the axillary fat deep to the pectoralis minor. These nodes receive drainage from the pectoral, subscapular, and lateral groups. What is this group called?
optionExplanations: The apical group receives lymph from the central group.
The central group of nodes is located in the center of the axilla and receives lymph from the anterior, posterior, and lateral groups.
The infraclavicular group is located outside the axilla.
The supratrochlear node is located near the elbow.
The internal thoracic nodes are located inside the thoracic cavity.
citation: The central group of nodes receive lymph from the above three groups

statement: A patient undergoing an axillary lymph node dissection for breast cancer develops 'winged scapula' postoperatively. The nerve damaged in this procedure enters the axilla by passing over the first rib. What is its root value?
optionExplanations: C5, 6 is the root value for the axillary and suprascapular nerves.
C8, T1 is the root value for the ulnar nerve.
The long thoracic nerve, which supplies the serratus anterior, has a root value of C5, 6, and 7.
C6, 7, 8 is not the standard root value for this nerve.
C3, 4, 5 is the root value for the phrenic nerve.
citation: The long thoracic nerve (C5 to 7) arises from the roots of the brachial plexus in the neck and enters the axilla

statement: At which anatomical landmark does the axillary artery terminate and become the brachial artery?
optionExplanations: This is where the axillary artery begins.
This marks the start of the second part of the artery.
This marks the start of the third part of the artery.
The axillary artery ends at the lower border of the teres major muscle.
The artery passes the surgical neck but does not end there.
citation: The axillary artery ends at the lower border of the teres major muscle

statement: Into which vessel does the cephalic vein typically drain after piercing the clavipectoral fascia?
optionExplanations: The basilic vein helps form the axillary vein.
The brachial veins are deep venae comitantes.
The cephalic vein terminates by draining into the axillary vein in the deltopectoral triangle.
The axillary vein becomes the subclavian vein at the first rib.
The cephalic vein may occasionally communicate with the external jugular, but it is not the primary termination.
citation: The cephalic vein terminates by draining into the axillary vein

statement: Which terminal branch of the brachial plexus arises directly from the lateral cord?
optionExplanations: The ulnar nerve arises from the medial cord.
The axillary nerve arises from the posterior cord.
The radial nerve arises from the posterior cord.
The musculocutaneous nerve is the main terminal branch of the lateral cord.
The long thoracic nerve arises from the roots (C5-7).
citation: The lateral cord gives rise to the musculocutaneous nerve

statement: A patient with an axillary nerve injury has a patch of sensory loss over the lower half of the deltoid muscle. Which specific cutaneous nerve is responsible for carrying sensation from this area?
options: Upper lateral cutaneous nerve of arm
Lower lateral cutaneous nerve of arm
correctOption: Upper lateral cutaneous nerve of arm
optionExplanations: The medial cutaneous nerve of the arm supplies the medial side of the arm.
The intercostobrachial nerve supplies the skin of the armpit.
The upper lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm is the cutaneous branch of the axillary nerve.
The lower lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm is a branch of the radial nerve.
The posterior cutaneous nerve of the arm is a branch of the radial nerve.
citation: The posterior terminal branch emerges from the posterior border of the deltoid as the upper lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm

statement: What is the root value of the axillary nerve?
optionExplanations: The axillary nerve is derived from the C5 and C6 spinal segments.
C6, 7 is not the root value for the axillary nerve.
C7, 8 is not the root value for the axillary nerve.
C8, T1 is the root value for the ulnar nerve.
C5, 6, 7 is the root value for the long thoracic and musculocutaneous nerves.
citation: The axillary nerve (C5 to 6) arises from the termination of the posterior cord

statement: Which structure forms the medial boundary of the quadrangular space?
optionExplanations: Teres major forms the inferior boundary.
Teres minor forms the superior boundary.
The long head of the triceps brachii forms the medial boundary of the quadrangular space.
The surgical neck of the humerus forms the lateral boundary.
The lateral head of the triceps is not a boundary of this space.
citation: It is bounded medially by the long head of the triceps

statement: Which branch typically arises from the first part of the axillary artery?
optionExplanations: The thoracoacromial artery arises from the second part.
The lateral thoracic artery arises from the second part.
The subscapular artery arises from the third part.
The highest (superior) thoracic artery is the only branch of the first part.
The circumflex humeral arteries arise from the third part.
citation: The highest thoracic artery is small and extremely variable. It runs along the upper border of the pectoralis minor

statement: At which anatomical location is the axillary vein formed?
optionExplanations: This is where the axillary vein ends and becomes the subclavian vein.
The axillary vein is formed at the lower border of the teres major muscle.
This is a landmark for the second part of the axillary artery.
The apex is where the vein leaves the axilla.
The deltopectoral triangle is where the cephalic vein enters the axillary vein.
citation: The axillary vein... is formed at the lower border of the teres major muscle

statement: What structure forms the medial boundary of the axillary inlet (apex)?
optionExplanations: The clavicle forms the anterior boundary.
The scapula forms the posterior boundary.
The outer border of the first rib forms the medial boundary.
The coracoid process is not a boundary of the apex.
The humerus is lateral to the axilla.
citation: medially by the outer border of the first rib

statement: A 65-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department after falling on her shoulder. Radiographs reveal a fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus. Which of the following structures is most likely at risk of injury?
optionExplanations: The radial nerve is related to the midshaft of the humerus.
The ulnar nerve is related to the medial epicondyle.
The axillary nerve winds around the surgical neck of the humerus and is frequently injured in fractures at this site.
The median nerve does not have a close relationship with the surgical neck.
The musculocutaneous nerve is protected deep to the biceps brachii.
citation: The surgical neck has important relations with the axillary nerve

statement: A 30-year-old woman undergoes a radical mastectomy for breast cancer. Postoperatively, she notices that her right scapula protrudes posteriorly when she pushes against a wall. Which nerve was likely damaged during the surgery?
optionExplanations: Injury to the thoracodorsal nerve would affect the latissimus dorsi.
The long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior; its paralysis leads to winging of the scapula.
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid and teres minor.
The suprascapular nerve supplies the supraspinatus and infraspinatus.
The dorsal scapular nerve supplies the rhomboids and levator scapulae.
citation: winged scapula (Fig. 3.4), caused by paralysis of the serratus anterior

statement: An anesthesiologist is performing a brachial plexus block for a patient undergoing hand surgery. To ensure the anesthetic solution reaches the plexus, it must be injected into which of the following structures?
optionExplanations: The prevertebral fascia is continuous with the sheath but is in the neck.
The axillary sheath is a connective tissue wrapping that encloses the axillary vessels and the brachial plexus.
The clavipectoral fascia lies deep to the pectoralis major but does not enclose the plexus.
The pectoral fascia is superficial to the pectoralis major.
The suspensory ligament is a continuation of the clavipectoral fascia.
citation: axillary sheath encloses the axillary vessels and the brachial plexus

statement: A 50-year-old woman presents with a hard, painless lump in the upper lateral quadrant of her left breast. Which group of axillary lymph nodes is most likely to be the first site of metastasis?
optionExplanations: The lateral group receives lymph from the upper limb.
The posterior group receives lymph from the back.
The pectoral (anterior) group receives lymph from the lateral quadrants of the breast.
The central group receives lymph from the pectoral, lateral, and posterior groups.
The apical group is the final common pathway for all axillary nodes.
citation: lateral quadrants of the breast drain into the anterior axillary or pectoral group of nodes

statement: During a surgical dissection of the axilla, a surgeon uses a specific muscle as a landmark to identify the three parts of the axillary artery. Which muscle is this?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis major forms the anterior wall but does not divide the artery into parts.
The subclavius is part of the anterior wall but is too superior.
The pectoralis minor crosses the axillary artery and is the standard landmark for its three parts.
The serratus anterior forms the medial wall.
The coracobrachialis forms part of the lateral wall.
citation: pectoralis minor muscle crosses in front of the axillary artery and divides it into three parts

statement: A 20-year-old athlete presents with weakness in shoulder abduction and loss of sensation over the lower deltoid. An MRI suggests compression of a neurovascular bundle in the quadrangular space. Which vessel is likely involved?
optionExplanations: The anterior circumflex humeral artery does not pass through the quadrangular space.
The posterior circumflex humeral artery and the axillary nerve pass through the quadrangular space.
The subscapular artery descends along the axillary border of the scapula.
The thoracoacromial artery is a branch of the second part of the axillary artery.
The lateral thoracic artery runs along the lower border of the pectoralis minor.
citation: axillary nerve and the posterior circumflex humeral vessels emerge through this space

statement: At which anatomical landmark is the axillary vein formed by the union of the basilic vein and the venae comitantes of the brachial artery?
optionExplanations: The axillary vein ends at the lateral border of the first rib.
The axillary vein is formed at the lower border of the teres major muscle.
The upper border of pectoralis minor marks the start of the second part of the artery.
The lower border of pectoralis minor marks the start of the third part of the artery.
The apex is where the vein becomes the subclavian vein.
citation: axillary vein (see Fig. 3.11) is formed at the lower border of the teres major muscle

statement: A patient suffers a deep laceration to the medial wall of the axilla. Which muscle, responsible for keeping the scapula applied to the chest wall, is most likely damaged?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis major forms the anterior wall.
The subscapularis forms the posterior wall.
The serratus anterior covers the upper ribs and forms the medial wall of the axilla.
The latissimus dorsi forms the posterior wall.
The teres major forms the posterior wall.
citation: medial wall: By the upper four or five ribs and the intercostal spaces covered by the serratus anterior muscle

statement: A 55-year-old man presents with a stab wound to the posterior aspect of his armpit. Which of the following muscles contributes to the formation of the posterior wall of the axilla?
optionExplanations: The pectoralis minor is in the anterior wall.
The serratus anterior is in the medial wall.
The latissimus dorsi, along with subscapularis and teres major, forms the posterior wall.
The coracobrachialis is in the lateral wall.
The subclavius is in the anterior wall.
citation: posterior wall: By the subscapularis, latissimus dorsi, and teres major muscles

statement: A 19-year-old male is diagnosed with a fracture involving the bicipital groove of the humerus. This structure forms which boundary of the axilla?
optionExplanations: The anterior wall is formed by pectoral muscles.
The posterior wall is formed by subscapularis and latissimus dorsi.
The medial wall is formed by the serratus anterior.
The lateral wall is formed by the coracobrachialis and biceps brachii in the bicipital groove.
The apex is the axillary inlet.
citation: lateral wall: By the coracobrachialis and biceps brachii muscles in the bicipital groove of the humerus

statement: A patient presents with axillary nerve palsy. While the deltoid is obviously affected, the clinician also tests for the function of another muscle supplied by the posterior terminal branch of this nerve. Which muscle is it?
optionExplanations: The teres major is supplied by the lower subscapular nerve.
The supraspinatus is supplied by the suprascapular nerve.
The infraspinatus is supplied by the suprascapular nerve.
The axillary nerve's posterior terminal branch supplies the teres minor.
The subscapularis is supplied by the upper and lower subscapular nerves.
citation: posterior terminal branch gives off a branch to the teres minor muscle

statement: A 50-year-old man has difficulty climbing a ladder because he cannot powerfully extend and medially rotate his humerus. Examination reveals injury to a nerve arising from the posterior cord. Which muscle is paralyzed?
optionExplanations: The serratus anterior is supplied by the long thoracic nerve.
The pectoralis major is supplied by pectoral nerves.
The thoracodorsal nerve supplies the latissimus dorsi, which is essential for climbing.
The teres minor is supplied by the axillary nerve.
The deltoid is supplied by the axillary nerve.
citation: thoracodorsal nerve arises from the posterior cord of the brachial plexus and runs downward into the axilla to supply the latissimus dorsi muscle

statement: A 60-year-old woman with advanced breast cancer has palpable nodes at the very top of her axilla, near the lateral border of the first rib. These nodes represent the final common pathway for axillary drainage. What are they called?
optionExplanations: Central nodes receive lymph from pectoral, lateral, and posterior groups.
Lateral nodes receive lymph from the upper limb.
Pectoral nodes receive lymph from the breast.
The apical nodes receive efferents from all other axillary groups and are located at the apex.
Subscapular nodes receive lymph from the back.
citation: apical nodes receive the efferent lymph vessels from all the other axillary nodes

statement: Which of the following is the only branch typically arising from the first part of the axillary artery?
optionExplanations: The thoracoacromial artery arises from the second part.
The lateral thoracic artery arises from the second part.
The highest (superior) thoracic artery is the branch of the first part.
The subscapular artery arises from the third part.
The posterior circumflex humeral artery arises from the third part.
citation: highest thoracic artery is small and extremely variable

statement: Which branch of the second part of the axillary artery provides blood supply to the pectoral muscles and the acromioclavicular region?
optionExplanations: The highest thoracic artery is from the first part.
The thoracoacromial artery is a branch of the second part and supplies these regions.
The subscapular artery is from the third part.
The anterior circumflex humeral artery is from the third part.
The brachial artery is the continuation of the axillary artery.
citation: thoracoacromial artery is a short trunk that immediately divides into four terminal branches

statement: Which is the largest branch of the third part of the axillary artery?
optionExplanations: The thoracoacromial artery is from the second part.
The lateral thoracic artery is from the second part.
The subscapular artery is the largest branch and arises from the third part.
The highest thoracic artery is from the first part.
The anterior circumflex humeral artery is a smaller branch of the third part.
citation: subscapular artery is a large vessel that descends along the lower (axillary) border of the scapula

statement: A surgeon is performing an open reduction and internal fixation of a humeral fracture. To avoid damaging the axillary nerve, they must be particularly careful when dissecting near which part of the bone?
optionExplanations: The anatomic neck is proximal to the surgical neck and rarely fractured.
The surgical neck is the site where the axillary nerve and circumflex vessels are closely related.
The deltoid tuberosity is on the midshaft.
The radial groove houses the radial nerve.
The medial epicondyle is related to the ulnar nerve.
citation: axillary nerve and circumflex humeral blood vessels have close relations to the surgical neck

statement: During an axillary exploration, a surgeon identifies a nerve cord passing posterior to the first part of the axillary artery to reach its medial side. Which cord is this?
optionExplanations: The lateral cord lies on the lateral side of the second part.
The posterior cord lies behind the second part.
The medial cord crosses behind the first part to reach the medial side of the second part.
Trunks are located in the neck, not the axilla.
Trunks are located in the neck, not the axilla.
citation: medial cord crosses behind the artery to reach the medial side of the second part of the artery

statement: A radiologist is interpreting an ultrasound of the axilla. They identify a nerve bundle immediately lateral to the second part of the axillary artery. This bundle is most likely which of the following?
optionExplanations: The medial cord is medial to the second part.
The posterior cord is posterior to the second part.
The lateral cord is named for its position lateral to the second part of the axillary artery.
The ulnar nerve arises from the medial cord.
The radial nerve arises from the posterior cord.
citation: lateral cord lies on the lateral side of the second part of the artery

statement: The axillary vein terminates and changes its name to the subclavian vein at which anatomical landmark?
optionExplanations: The axillary vein begins at the lower border of the teres major.
The pectoralis minor divides the artery, not the vein's termination.
The axillary vein becomes the subclavian vein at the lateral border of the first rib.
The medial border is not the standard landmark for this transition.
The clavicle overlies the transition but the rib is the specific landmark.
citation: ends at the lateral border of the first rib by becoming the subclavian vein

statement: Which of the following structures forms the medial boundary of the quadrangular space?
optionExplanations: The surgical neck forms the lateral boundary.
The teres major forms the inferior boundary.
The teres minor forms the superior boundary.
The long head of the triceps forms the medial boundary.
The lateral head of the triceps is not a boundary.
citation: bounded medially by the long head of the triceps

statement: What is the root value of the axillary nerve?
optionExplanations: C5, 6, 7 is the root value for the long thoracic nerve.
C8, T1 is the root value for the ulnar nerve.
The axillary nerve is derived from the C5 and C6 roots.
C6, 7, 8 is part of the radial nerve value.
C7, 8, T1 is part of the median nerve value.
citation: axillary nerve (C5 to 6)

statement: Which bone forms the anterior boundary of the apex (inlet) of the axilla?
optionExplanations: The first rib forms the medial boundary of the apex.
The upper border of the scapula forms the posterior boundary.
The clavicle forms the anterior boundary of the axillary apex.
The humerus is not a boundary of the apex.
The sternum is medial to the axillary inlet.
citation: bounded in front by the clavicle

statement: Where are the central group of axillary lymph nodes specifically located?
optionExplanations: The pectoral nodes lie along the lateral thoracic artery.
The lateral nodes lie along the axillary vein.
The central nodes are embedded in the axillary fat in the center of the axilla.
The infraclavicular nodes lie in the deltopectoral groove.
The posterior nodes lie in front of the subscapularis.
citation: Lying in the center of the axilla, in the axillary fat

statement: A 35-year-old weightlifter presents with sudden swelling and bluish discoloration of his right arm after an intense workout. Spontaneous thrombosis is suspected. Which vessel is most likely affected?
optionExplanations: The cephalic vein is a superficial tributary.
The basilic vein is a superficial vein that helps form the axillary vein.
Spontaneous thrombosis of the axillary vein can occur after excessive arm movements.
The subclavian vein is the continuation of the axillary vein.
The brachial veins are deep veins of the arm.
citation: Spontaneous thrombosis of the axillary vein occasionally occurs after excessive and unaccustomed movements

statement: A 65-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department after falling on her shoulder. Radiographs reveal a fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus. Which of the following structures is most likely to be damaged in this patient?
optionExplanations: The axillary nerve winds around the surgical neck of the humerus and is highly vulnerable in fractures at this site.
The radial nerve is related to the midshaft of the humerus in the spiral groove.
The ulnar nerve is related to the medial epicondyle at the distal end.
The median nerve does not have a direct contact relation with the surgical neck.
The musculocutaneous nerve pierces the coracobrachialis and is not directly related to the surgical neck.
citation: The surgical neck has important relations with the axillary nerve and the anterior and posterior circumflex humeral vessels.

statement: In the anatomical position, how is the head of the humerus oriented to articulate with the glenoid cavity?
optionExplanations: The humeral head is directed medially, superiorly, and posteriorly to fit into the glenoid fossa.
Lateral orientation would face away from the scapula.
Inferior orientation would not align with the glenoid cavity.
Lateral and inferior orientation is incorrect for the proximal articulation.
The head is oriented slightly posteriorly, not anteriorly.
citation: It forms about one third of a sphere and is oriented medially, superiorly, and slightly posteriorly.

statement: A 28-year-old man sustains a midshaft fracture of the humerus during a motor vehicle accident. Physical examination reveals an inability to extend the wrist (wrist drop). Which structure related to the radial groove was likely injured?
optionExplanations: The radial nerve travels in the radial (spiral) groove and supplies the extensors of the wrist.
The axillary nerve is related to the surgical neck, not the midshaft.
The ulnar nerve is related to the medial epicondyle.
The profunda brachii artery is in the groove, but its injury wouldn't cause wrist drop.
The median nerve is not directly related to the spiral groove.

statement: A 12-year-old boy falls on his elbow and sustains a fracture of the medial epicondyle of the humerus. He subsequently complains of numbness in his little finger. Which nerve is most likely involved?

The radial nerve is related to the lateral side of the distal humerus.
The median nerve passes anterior to the elbow joint.
The axillary nerve is located at the shoulder.
The musculocutaneous nerve is not related to the medial epicondyle.

statement: Which feature of the humerus is defined as the constricted region immediately surrounding the articular margin of the head?

The radial groove is on the shaft.
The intertubercular groove is between the tubercles.
The deltoid tuberosity is on the lateral aspect of the shaft.

statement: A baseball pitcher feels a sharp pain in his shoulder during a high-velocity throw. An MRI shows an avulsion fracture of the greater tuberosity of the humerus. Which group of muscles would lose their distal attachment?

The biceps long head originates from the supraglenoid tubercle, not the humerus.

statement: A patient is diagnosed with an isolated fracture of the lesser tuberosity of the humerus following a posterior shoulder dislocation. Which muscle's function will be most significantly impaired?
optionExplanations: The subscapularis is the only rotator cuff muscle that inserts on the lesser tuberosity.
The supraspinatus inserts on the greater tuberosity.
The infraspinatus inserts on the greater tuberosity.
The teres minor inserts on the greater tuberosity.
The deltoid inserts on the deltoid tuberosity of the shaft.
citation: The bone fragment receives the insertion of the subscapularis tendon, a part of the rotator cuff.

statement: With which structure does the capitulum of the humerus articulate?
optionExplanations: The capitulum is the lateral articular surface for the radial head.
The trochlea articulates with the trochlear notch.
The coronoid process fits into the coronoid fossa.
The olecranon process fits into the olecranon fossa.
The styloid process is at the distal end of the radius.

Posterior extension is not the primary displacement force here.
Medial rotation is more likely than lateral rotation due to the adductors.

statement: A 35-year-old professional tennis player presents with chronic pain on the lateral aspect of her elbow. The clinician suspects 'tennis elbow'. This condition involves inflammation at the origin of which humeral landmark?

The deltoid tuberosity is for the deltoid muscle insertion.
The greater tubercle is at the shoulder.
The capitulum is an articular surface, not a site for the common extensor origin.

The radial fossa is anterior and used in flexion.
The ulnar sulcus houses the ulnar nerve.
The spiral groove is on the shaft for the radial nerve.

statement: Which part of the ulna articulates with the pulley-shaped trochlea of the humerus?

statement: Where is the deltoid tuberosity located on the humerus?

statement: A 7-year-old boy is rushed to the ER after falling from a tree onto his outstretched hand. X-rays show a supracondylar fracture of the humerus. The clinician is most concerned about Volkmann’s ischemic contracture due to injury of which vessel?

statement: Which structure is contained within the intertubercular (bicipital) groove of the humerus?

statement: During a violent shoulder dislocation, a patient sustains a compression fracture of the humeral head. Which structure is responsible for producing this specific fracture according to the mechanism of injury?

The greater tuberosity is part of the humerus itself.

statement: A young athlete suffers a valgus stress injury to the elbow (forced abduction of the forearm). Radiographs show a small bone fragment displaced from the medial side of the distal humerus. Which ligament caused this avulsion?

statement: A patient presents with a humeral shaft fracture located in the distal third of the bone, well below the deltoid tuberosity. What is the characteristic displacement of the distal fragment?

Adduction is not the primary force on the distal fragment here.
Rotation is not the primary displacement described.

statement: A child with a supracondylar fracture of the humerus is being monitored for nerve damage. Which of the following nerves is at risk of injury in this specific fracture?

Long thoracic and thoracodorsal nerves are in the axilla.

statement: What are the three primary morphological divisions of the humerus?

optionExplanations: The text explicitly divides the bone into these three major regions.
These are sub-features of the proximal extremity.
These are sub-features of the distal extremity.
These are specific landmarks, not the three main regions.

citation: The humerus can be divided into three main regions: (1) proximal extremity, (2) body or shaft, and (3) distal extremity.

statement: A student accidentally strikes the medial side of his elbow against a desk, feeling a sharp 'electric' shock radiating to his pinky finger. Which humeral landmark was involved in this 'funny bone' sensation?

statement: A 72-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department after falling on her shoulder. Radiographs reveal a fracture of the humerus just inferior to the greater and lesser tubercles. Which nerve is most likely to be damaged in this patient?

statement: In side determination of the humerus, what is the correct anatomical orientation of the head?

The head is oriented slightly posteriorly, not anteriorly.

statement: A 24-year-old man sustains a midshaft fracture of the humerus during a motor vehicle accident. Physical examination reveals an inability to extend the wrist. Which structure related to the spiral groove was likely injured?
optionExplanations: The radial nerve travels in the radial (spiral) groove of the humerus.
The axillary nerve is related to the surgical neck.
The ulnar nerve is related to the medial epicondyle.
The profunda brachii artery, not the main brachial artery, is in the groove.

statement: A student accidentally strikes the medial aspect of his elbow against a hard chair. He experiences a sharp, tingling sensation radiating to his little finger. Which humeral landmark is involved in this 'funny bone' response?

statement: A baseball pitcher presents with an avulsion fracture of the greater tuberosity of the humerus. Which of the following muscles would lose its primary attachment to the humerus in this injury?

statement: Which structure is contained within the bicipital groove of the humerus?

statement: Where is the deltoid tuberosity located on the humerus?

The teres minor inserts onto the greater tubercle.

statement: During a physical examination, a clinician notes that a patient's elbow flexion is limited. Which anterior depression on the distal humerus is responsible for accommodating the ulna during this movement?

The radial fossa receives the radius, not the ulna.
The radial groove is on the shaft.

The olecranon fossa is for extension.
The ulnar sulcus is posterior.

statement: A 25-year-old male sustains a humeral shaft fracture just above the deltoid tuberosity. On X-ray, the proximal fragment is significantly adducted. Which muscle is primarily responsible for this displacement?

statement: Which humeral feature is defined as the constricted area immediately surrounding the articular cartilage of the head?
optionExplanations: The anatomical neck is the line of attachment of the joint capsule around the head.

The radial groove is on the shaft.
The bicipital groove is between the tubercles.

statement: During an attempt to reduce an anterior shoulder dislocation, the physician finds the joint is 'locked' and reduction is impossible. A fracture of the humeral head is suspected. What structure is likely jammed in the humeral defect?

statement: Which humeral landmark serves as the common origin for the superficial flexor muscles of the forearm?

coreConcept: Humeral shaft fracture and radial nerve
statement: A patient with a midshaft humeral fracture presents with 'wrist drop.' The physician explains that the nerve was injured while it was protected by a specific muscle. Which muscle covers the nerve in the spiral groove?

statement: A 65-year-old woman falls on her outstretched hand and sustains a fracture at the constricted area immediately inferior to the humeral tubercles. Which structures are most at risk of injury due to their close relationship with this site?

The median nerve and brachial artery are related to the supracondylar region.
The musculocutaneous nerve pierces the coracobrachialis, not the surgical neck.

statement: A young man is brought to the ER after a motorcycle accident with a midshaft humeral fracture. Physical examination reveals an inability to extend the wrist. Which anatomical landmark on the humerus is likely involved in this nerve injury?

statement: In side determination of the humerus, how is the smooth, rounded proximal head normally oriented in the anatomical position?

Lateral orientation would face away from the scapula.

statement: A 7-year-old boy falls on his outstretched hand and sustains a supracondylar fracture of the humerus. Several hours later, he develops severe pain and absent capillary refill in the nail beds. What is the most likely consequence if this is not treated immediately?

Ape hand is caused by median nerve injury.

statement: A patient presents with tingling sensations in the fifth digit after striking the medial side of their elbow against a hard table. Which part of the humerus was likely involved in stimulating the nerve?

The capitulum articulates with the radius.
The greater tubercle is at the proximal end.
citation: The ulnar nerve crosses the posterior surface of this epicondyle in the shallow ulnar sulcus and is susceptible to injury here

statement: An athlete experiences a violent contraction of the shoulder muscles, resulting in an avulsion fracture of the lateral proximal humerus. Which muscle is most likely responsible for pulling off the bone fragment?
optionExplanations: Subscapularis attaches to the lesser tuberosity.
Pectoralis major attaches to the lateral lip of the bicipital groove.
The supraspinatus attaches to the greater tuberosity and can cause avulsion.
Biceps brachii (long head) passes through the groove but doesn't attach to the tuberosity.
Teres major attaches to the medial lip of the bicipital groove.
citation: The greater tuberosity of the humerus can be fractured by direct trauma, displaced by the glenoid labrum during dislocation of the shoulder joint, or avulsed by violent contractions of the supraspinatus muscle.

statement: Which structure of the forearm articulates with the rounded, half-spherical process on the distal lateral end of the humerus?
optionExplanations: The trochlear notch articulates with the trochlea.
The coronoid process fits into the coronoid fossa.
The olecranon process fits into the olecranon fossa.
The capitulum articulates specifically with the head of the radius.
The styloid process is at the distal end of the radius.
citation: The capitulum articulates with the head of the radius.

statement: A radiograph shows a small fracture of the lesser tuberosity of the humerus following a posterior shoulder dislocation. Which rotator cuff muscle's function will be most compromised by this injury?
optionExplanations: Supraspinatus inserts on the greater tuberosity.
Infraspinatus inserts on the greater tuberosity.
Teres minor inserts on the greater tuberosity.
The subscapularis is the only rotator cuff muscle inserting on the lesser tuberosity.
The deltoid inserts on the deltoid tuberosity of the shaft.
citation: The bone fragment receives the insertion of the subscapularis tendon, a part of the rotator cuff.

statement: What is the primary function of the deep depression located on the distal posterior surface of the humerus?
options: Receives the radial head during flexion
Receives the coronoid process during flexion
Holds the olecranon process during extension
correctOption: Holds the olecranon process during extension
optionExplanations: The radial fossa receives the radial head.
The coronoid fossa receives the coronoid process.
The olecranon fossa holds the olecranon process of the ulna during extension.
The ulnar nerve lies in the ulnar sulcus.
The brachialis originates from the anterior shaft.
citation: This holds the apex of the olecranon process of the ulna when the elbow is extended.

statement: A patient sustains a humeral shaft fracture located between the surgical neck and the deltoid tuberosity. In which direction will the proximal fragment most likely be displaced?
optionExplanations: The deltoid abducts the proximal fragment only if the fracture is distal to its insertion.
If the fracture is proximal to the deltoid insertion, the pectoralis major and others adduct the proximal fragment.
The triceps pulls the distal fragment, not the proximal.
Lateral rotation is not the primary displacement described for this site.
The biceps pulls the distal fragment proximally.
citation: When the fracture line is proximal to the deltoid insertion, the pectoralis major, latissimus dorsi, and teres major muscles adduct the proximal fragment

statement: Which structure is contained within the intertubercular groove of the humerus?
optionExplanations: The short head originates from the coracoid process.
The intertubercular (bicipital) groove houses the long head of the biceps tendon.
Coracobrachialis attaches to the coracoid process and humeral shaft.
The axillary nerve is related to the surgical neck.
The radial nerve is in the spiral groove.
citation: It houses the tendon of the long head of the biceps brachii muscle and extends into the upper third of the shaft of the humerus.

statement: A professional tennis player complains of pain on the outer aspect of her elbow. Tenderness is localized to the small, roughened projection on the distal lateral humerus. What is the clinical name for this condition?
optionExplanations: Golfer's elbow involves the medial epicondyle.
Funny bone syndrome refers to ulnar nerve irritation.
Lateral epicondylitis is commonly known as tennis elbow.
Student's elbow is olecranon bursitis.
Nursemaid's elbow is radial head subluxation.
citation: Inflammation of this tendon is termed “lateral epicondylitis” (“tennis elbow”).

statement: Where does the articular capsule of the shoulder joint attach on the humerus?
optionExplanations: The surgical neck is distal to the capsule attachment.
The deltoid tuberosity is on the shaft.
The capsule attaches along the edge of the anatomical neck.
The bicipital groove is an attachment for muscles, not the joint capsule.
The radial groove is on the posterior shaft.
citation: The articular capsule of the glenohumeral joint attaches along the inferior edge of the anatomical neck.

statement: Which specific part of the ulna articulates with the pulley-shaped process on the distal medial end of the humerus?
optionExplanations: The radial notch articulates with the radial head.
The coronoid process is a projection, not the primary notch.
The olecranon process is the proximal point of the elbow.
The trochlea articulates with the trochlear notch of the ulna.
The ulnar tuberosity is an insertion for the brachialis.
citation: The trochlea articulates with the trochlear notch of the ulna.

statement: During a shoulder reduction procedure, the physician finds it extremely difficult to return the humeral head to the glenoid cavity. A radiograph reveals a humeral head fracture. What structure likely caused the fracture and is now obstructing the reduction?
optionExplanations: The acromion roofs the joint but doesn't typically jam the head.
The coracoid is anterior to the joint.
The glenoid labrum can produce the fracture and become jammed in the defect.
The triceps supports the joint inferiorly.
The bicipital tendon passes through the joint but doesn't cause this specific jamming.
citation: The fibrocartilaginous glenoid labrum of the scapula produces the fracture, and the labrum can become jammed in the defect, making reduction of the shoulder joint difficult.

statement: Where is the deltoid tuberosity located on the humerus?
optionExplanations: The medial surface lacks this tuberosity.
It is not on the distal end.
The deltoid tuberosity is on the anterolateral midshaft.
The posterior midshaft contains the radial groove.
The anterior proximal end contains the lesser tubercle.
citation: The deltoid tuberosity is the roughened triangular elevation on the anterolateral surface of the midshaft of the humerus.

statement: What is the anatomical purpose of the radial fossa of the humerus?
options: Receives the coronoid process during flexion
Receives the radial head during flexion
correctOption: Receives the radial head during flexion
optionExplanations: The olecranon fossa holds the olecranon.
The coronoid fossa receives the coronoid process.
The radial fossa receives the radial head margin during full flexion.
Brachioradialis originates from the supracondylar ridge.
The radial nerve is in the spiral groove.
citation: This receives the margin of the head of the radius when the elbow is fully flexed.

statement: A teenager is brought to the clinic after a wrestling match where his forearm was forcibly abducted. Radiographs show a displaced medial epicondyle. Which structure most likely caused this avulsion?
optionExplanations: The ulnar nerve is related but doesn't pull the bone off.
The common extensor tendon attaches to the lateral epicondyle.
The medial collateral ligament can avulse the medial epicondyle during forced abduction.
The brachialis inserts on the ulna.
The annular ligament surrounds the radial head.
citation: The medial collateral ligament of the elbow can avulse the medial epicondyle if the forearm is forcibly abducted.

statement: A patient has a humeral fracture located in the distal third of the shaft. Physical examination shows the proximal bone fragment is tilted away from the midline. Which muscle is responsible for this abduction?
optionExplanations: Supraspinatus initiates abduction but the deltoid is the primary abductor of the shaft fragment here.
Pectoralis major is an adductor.
When the fracture is distal to its insertion, the deltoid abducts the proximal fragment.
Coracobrachialis is an adductor.
Teres major is an adductor.
citation: When the fracture is distal to the deltoid insertion, the proximal fragment is abducted by the deltoid

statement: Which of the following is the longest bone found in the human upper limb?
optionExplanations: The clavicle is a small strut bone.
The scapula is a flat bone.
The radius is shorter than the humerus.
