The ulna is shorter than the humerus.
The humerus is the longest bone of the upper limb.
citation: The humerus (humer- is Latin for “shoulder”) is located in the arm (brachium) and is the longest bone of the upper limb.

statement: A patient with a posterior humeral shaft injury presents with weakness in elbow extension and wristdrop. The surgeon notes the injury is deep to the triceps muscle. Which humeral feature is the nerve most likely contacting at this site?
optionExplanations: The surgical neck is proximal to the triceps cover.
The radial nerve lies in the spiral (radial) groove under the triceps.
The olecranon fossa is at the distal end.
The trochlea is an articular surface.
The medial ridge is not related to the radial nerve.
citation: The radial nerve can be damaged where it lies in the spiral groove on the posterior surface of the humerus under cover of the triceps muscle.

statement: What is the function of the coronoid fossa of the humerus?
options: Receives the radial head during flexion
Receives the coronoid process during flexion
correctOption: Receives the coronoid process during flexion
optionExplanations: The olecranon fossa receives the olecranon.
The radial fossa receives the radial head.
The coronoid fossa receives the coronoid process of the ulna during flexion.
The brachial artery is anterior but not in a fossa.
The triceps originates from the posterior humerus.
citation: This receives the coronoid process of the ulna when the elbow is fully flexed.

statement: A midshaft humeral fracture results in a significant hematoma. The surgeon is concerned about a specific artery that travels with the radial nerve along the posterior aspect of the bone. Which vessel is this?
optionExplanations: The brachial artery is anterior.
The ulnar artery is in the forearm.
The profunda brachii travels in the radial groove with the radial nerve.
The superior ulnar collateral travels with the ulnar nerve.
The circumflex humeral vessels are at the surgical neck.
citation: It has important relations with the radial nerve and the profunda brachii vessels.

statement: A child is diagnosed with a displaced supracondylar fracture. Which nerves are most commonly at risk of injury in this specific distal humeral location?
optionExplanations: Axillary is proximal.
Supracondylar fractures can injure the median, radial, and ulnar nerves.
Long thoracic is in the axilla.
Suprascapular is at the scapula.
Phrenic is in the neck.
citation: Injuries to the median, radial, and ulnar nerves are not uncommon, although function usually quickly returns after reduction of the fracture.

statement: With which specific part of the scapula does the head of the humerus articulate?
optionExplanations: The acromion articulates with the clavicle.
The coracoid is an attachment point.
The humeral head articulates with the glenoid cavity.
The supraspinous fossa is an origin for a muscle.
The infraspinous fossa is an origin for a muscle.
citation: It articulates with the glenoid cavity of the scapula to form the glenohumeral joint in the shoulder joint complex.

statement: A 70-year-old woman falls in her kitchen and sustains a fracture at the constricted area immediately inferior to the humeral tubercles. During surgical repair, the surgeon must be particularly careful to avoid damaging which nerve in this specific region?
optionExplanations: The radial nerve is related to the midshaft spiral groove, not the surgical neck.
The ulnar nerve is related to the medial epicondyle.
The surgical neck of the humerus is closely related to the axillary nerve and circumflex humeral vessels.
The median nerve has no direct contact with the proximal humerus.
The musculocutaneous nerve pierces the coracobrachialis and is not related to the surgical neck.
citation: The surgical neck has important relations with the axillary nerve and the anterior and posterior circumflex humeral vessels.

statement: An 18-year-old male is brought to the ER with a midshaft humeral fracture following a motorcycle accident. Physical examination reveals an inability to extend the wrist. Which artery is most likely at risk of injury alongside the affected nerve in the spiral groove?
optionExplanations: The brachial artery is in the anterior compartment.
The ulnar artery begins in the cubital fossa.
The radial groove (spiral groove) houses both the radial nerve and the profunda brachii vessels.
The axillary artery ends at the lower border of the teres major.
The superior ulnar collateral artery accompanies the ulnar nerve.
citation: It has important relations with the radial nerve and the profunda brachii vessels.

statement: A young boy is treated for a fracture of the medial epicondyle of the humerus. He later complains of a 'pins and needles' sensation in his fifth digit. Which nerve was likely irritated by the irregular bony surface of the callus?
optionExplanations: The radial nerve is lateral at the elbow.
The median nerve passes anterior to the elbow joint.
The ulnar nerve passes posterior to the medial epicondyle in the ulnar sulcus.
The axillary nerve is related to the proximal humerus.
The lateral cutaneous nerve is a sensory branch of the musculocutaneous nerve.
citation: The ulnar nerve crosses the posterior surface of this epicondyle in the shallow ulnar sulcus and is susceptible to injury here

statement: In side determination of the humerus, how is the smooth articular head normally oriented in the anatomical position?
optionExplanations: The head must face medially to articulate with the glenoid cavity.
The text specifies the head is oriented medially, superiorly, and slightly posteriorly.
The head is oriented superiorly, not inferiorly.
The head is medial, not lateral.
The head has a slight posterior orientation, not strictly anterior.
citation: It forms about one third of a sphere and is oriented medially, superiorly, and slightly posteriorly.

statement: A 7-year-old girl falls from a jungle gym onto her outstretched hand with her elbow flexed. Radiographs confirm a supracondylar fracture of the humerus. Which vascular structure is most commonly at risk of compression due to tissue swelling in this region?
optionExplanations: The radial artery begins distal to the supracondylar region.
The ulnar artery begins distal to the supracondylar region.
Damage to or pressure on the brachial artery can occur at the time of a supracondylar fracture.
The profunda brachii is related to the midshaft.
The axillary artery is proximal to the arm.
citation: Supracondylar fractures are common in children and occur when the child falls on the outstretched hand with the elbow partially flexed.

statement: A middle-aged man presents with severe shoulder pain after a fall. An X-ray shows an avulsion fracture of the lateral proximal elevation of the humerus. Which muscle's violent contraction is most likely responsible for pulling this bone fragment away?
optionExplanations: The subscapularis attaches to the lesser tuberosity.
The pectoralis major attaches to the lateral lip of the bicipital groove.
The greater tuberosity (tuberosity) can be avulsed by the supraspinatus muscle.
The long head of the biceps passes through the groove but does not attach to the tubercle.
The teres major attaches to the medial lip of the bicipital groove.
citation: The greater tuberosity of the humerus can be... avulsed by violent contractions of the supraspinatus muscle.

statement: Which structure is contained within the deep groove on the anterior surface of the humerus that separates the greater and lesser tubercles?
options: Long head of biceps brachii tendon
correctOption: Long head of biceps brachii tendon
optionExplanations: The short head of the biceps originates from the coracoid process.
The intertubercular (bicipital) groove houses the tendon of the long head of the biceps brachii.
The radial nerve is in the spiral groove.
The axillary nerve is related to the surgical neck.
The coracobrachialis attaches to the coracoid process and midshaft.
citation: It houses the tendon of the long head of the biceps brachii muscle and extends into the upper third of the shaft of the humerus.

statement: A professional tennis player complains of pain on the lateral side of his elbow. The physician diagnoses 'tennis elbow.' Which humeral landmark is the site of inflammation for the common origin of the affected muscles?
optionExplanations: The medial epicondyle is the common flexor origin.
The lateral epicondyle is the attachment site for the common extensor tendon.
The deltoid tuberosity is on the midshaft.
The trochlea is an articular surface for the ulna.
The capitulum is an articular surface for the radius.
citation: The common extensor tendon (tendon of origin for several superficial forearm extensor muscles) attaches here.

statement: A patient is diagnosed with a posterior dislocation of the shoulder accompanied by a fracture of the lesser tuberosity. Which rotator cuff muscle's function will be most directly compromised by this specific bone fragment displacement?
optionExplanations: The supraspinatus attaches to the greater tuberosity.
The infraspinatus attaches to the greater tuberosity.
The teres minor attaches to the greater tuberosity.
The lesser tuberosity receives the insertion of the subscapularis tendon.
The deltoid attaches to the deltoid tuberosity on the shaft.
citation: The bone fragment receives the insertion of the subscapularis tendon, a part of the rotator cuff.

statement: Where is the deltoid tuberosity located on the humerus?
options: Posterior surface of the distal end
Medial surface of the proximal end
Anterior surface of the medial epicondyle
Lateral surface of the surgical neck
optionExplanations: The distal posterior surface contains the olecranon fossa.
The deltoid tuberosity is on the anterolateral surface of the midshaft.
The proximal medial aspect contains the head.
The medial epicondyle is at the distal end.
The surgical neck is proximal to the tuberosity.
citation: The deltoid tuberosity... is the roughened triangular elevation on the anterolateral surface of the midshaft of the humerus.

statement: A 22-year-old rugby player receives a high-impact direct blow to the lateral aspect of his shoulder. He is unable to abduct his arm, and there is a loss of sensation over the lower deltoid. Which part of the humerus was most likely fractured?
optionExplanations: Fractures of the anatomical neck are rare.
The surgical neck is commonly fractured by direct blows to the lateral shoulder and is related to the axillary nerve.
The deltoid tuberosity is on the shaft, not the shoulder region.
The medial epicondyle is at the elbow.
The trochlea is at the distal articular surface.
citation: The surgical neck of the humerus... can be fractured by a direct blow on the lateral aspect of the shoulder or in an indirect manner by falling on the outstretched hand.

statement: With which specific structure does the pulley-shaped articular process of the distal medial humerus articulate?
optionExplanations: The head of the radius articulates with the capitulum.
The radius does not have a coronoid process.
The trochlea of the humerus articulates with the trochlear notch of the ulna.
The olecranon fossa is a depression on the humerus itself.
The glenoid cavity articulates with the humeral head.
citation: The trochlea articulates with the trochlear notch of the ulna.

statement: A student accidentally strikes the medial side of his elbow against a desk, experiencing a sharp tingling sensation that radiates to his pinky finger. This 'funny bone' effect involves a nerve related to which humeral feature?
optionExplanations: The lateral epicondyle is related to the radial nerve.
The medial epicondyle is where the ulnar nerve can be palpated and rolled, causing the 'funny bone' response.
The radial groove is on the posterior shaft.
The surgical neck is related to the axillary nerve.
The coronoid fossa is an anterior depression.
citation: Stimulation of the nerve by contact against the epicondyle elicits the characteristic “funny bone” response

statement: What is the functional significance of the radial fossa of the humerus?
options: It receives the radial head during flexion
It provides attachment for the deltoid
correctOption: It receives the radial head during flexion
optionExplanations: The ulnar nerve is in the ulnar sulcus.
The olecranon process is received by the olecranon fossa during extension.
The radial fossa receives the margin of the head of the radius during full elbow flexion.
The deltoid attaches to the deltoid tuberosity.
The trochlea is an articular process, not a fossa.
citation: This receives the margin of the head of the radius when the elbow is fully flexed.

statement: A patient suffers a humerus shaft fracture located just above the deltoid tuberosity. In which direction will the proximal bone fragment most likely be displaced due to muscle pull?
optionExplanations: The supraspinatus is a weak abductor compared to the adductors here.
If the fracture is proximal to the deltoid, the pectoralis major, latissimus dorsi, and teres major adduct the proximal fragment.
The triceps pulls the distal fragment proximally.
Lateral rotation is not the primary displacement described.
The deltoid pulls the distal fragment proximally in this scenario.
citation: When the fracture line is proximal to the deltoid insertion, the pectoralis major, latissimus dorsi, and teres major muscles adduct the proximal fragment

statement: A radiograph of a midshaft humeral fracture shows the proximal fragment is significantly abducted. Based on this displacement, where is the fracture line relative to the deltoid tuberosity?
optionExplanations: If proximal to the insertion, the fragment would be adducted.
When the fracture is distal to the deltoid insertion, the proximal fragment is abducted by the deltoid.
Surgical neck fractures have different displacement patterns.
Anatomical neck fractures are rare and proximal to the shaft.
The medial epicondyle is at the distal extremity.
citation: When the fracture is distal to the deltoid insertion, the proximal fragment is abducted by the deltoid

statement: Which humeral feature is defined as the constricted region immediately surrounding the articular head?
optionExplanations: The surgical neck is inferior to the tubercles.
The anatomical neck is the constriction surrounding the head's articular surface.
The bicipital groove is between the tubercles.
The radial groove is on the shaft.
The deltoid tuberosity is on the shaft.
citation: The anatomical neck is the slightly constricted region surrounding the articular surface of the head.

statement: A patient is unable to fully straighten their arm following a distal humeral injury. The physician suspects a bony obstruction in the deep posterior depression of the humerus. Which structure normally occupies this space during full extension?
optionExplanations: The coronoid process enters the anterior coronoid fossa during flexion.
The radial head enters the anterior radial fossa during flexion.
The olecranon fossa holds the apex of the olecranon process during elbow extension.
The trochlea is an articular surface.
The capitulum is an articular surface.
citation: This holds the apex of the olecranon process of the ulna when the elbow is extended.

statement: The rounded, half-spherical articular process on the distal lateral humerus articulates with which bone?
optionExplanations: The ulna articulates with the trochlea.
The scapula articulates with the humeral head.
The capitulum (lateral distal process) articulates with the head of the radius.
The clavicle does not articulate with the humerus.
The humerus does not articulate with itself.
citation: The capitulum articulates with the head of the radius.

statement: A patient presents with 'wrist drop' after a deep laceration to the posterior arm that penetrated the triceps muscle. The surgeon identifies damage to a nerve lying directly against the humeral shaft. Which nerve is this?
optionExplanations: The ulnar nerve is medial.
The median nerve is anterior.
The radial nerve lies in the spiral groove on the posterior humerus.
The axillary nerve is proximal at the surgical neck.
The musculocutaneous nerve is in the anterior compartment.
citation: The radial nerve can be damaged where it lies in the spiral groove on the posterior surface of the humerus under cover of the triceps muscle.

statement: Which humeral feature forms the lower medial border of the bone, extending upward from the medial epicondyle?
optionExplanations: The lateral ridge is on the opposite side.
The medial supracondylar ridge runs proximally from the medial epicondyle.
The deltoid tuberosity is on the midshaft.
The anatomical neck is proximal.
The spiral groove is on the posterior shaft.
citation: The medial supracondylar ridge is the narrow ridge running proximally from the medial epicondyle

statement: An elderly man is diagnosed with a displaced fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus. Which clinical finding would most strongly suggest that the nerve related to this humeral landmark has been damaged?
options: Loss of sensation in the palm
optionExplanations: Thumb flexion is median nerve function.
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid; damage leads to paralysis and wasting.
Little finger tingling is ulnar nerve related.
Wrist drop is radial nerve related.
Palmar sensation is median/ulnar related.
citation: The axillary nerve... is particularly vulnerable to trauma from downward displacement of the humeral head in shoulder dislocations or fractures of the surgical neck of the humerus.

statement: Approximately what proportion of a sphere is formed by the articular head of the humerus?
optionExplanations: The capitulum is half-spherical, not the head.
One quarter is too small.
The text explicitly states the head forms about one third of a sphere.
Two thirds is too large.
Three quarters is too large.
citation: The head... forms about one third of a sphere

statement: Following a severe supracondylar fracture of the humerus, a child develops a permanent flexion deformity of the wrist and fingers. This condition, resulting from brachial artery interference, is known as:
optionExplanations: Erb's palsy is a brachial plexus injury.
Klumpke's is a lower brachial plexus injury.
Volkmann’s ischemic contracture results from brachial artery damage associated with humeral fractures.
Dupuytren involves the palmar aponeurosis.
Tennis elbow is lateral epicondylitis.
citation: damage to or pressure on the brachial artery can occur at the time of the fracture... leading to Volkmann’s ischemic contracture

statement: Which bone serves as the primary skeletal definition of the 'arm' (brachium) in human anatomy?
optionExplanations: The scapula is part of the shoulder girdle.
The clavicle is part of the shoulder girdle.
The radius is in the forearm.
The ulna is in the forearm.
The humerus defines the arm (brachium).
citation: The humerus defines the arm, whereas the radius and ulna delineate the forearm.

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A 22-year-old rugby player is brought to the ER after a heavy collision. Radiographs show a fracture involving the primary articulating surface of the shoulder joint. Which structures are involved in this articulation?
optionExplanations: The shoulder joint is formed by the humeral head and the glenoid cavity of the scapula.
The acromion does not articulate with the humerus directly in the shoulder joint.
The coracoid process is a site for ligamentous and muscular attachment, not articulation.
The acromion forms the acromioclavicular joint, not the glenohumeral joint.
The anatomical neck is the site of capsule attachment, not the articulating surface.
citation: This occurs between the rounded head of the humerus and the shallow, pear-shaped glenoid cavity of the scapula.

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: What is the anatomical classification of the glenohumeral joint?
optionExplanations: The shoulder joint is a multiaxial ball-and-socket synovial joint.
The elbow is a hinge joint.
The acromioclavicular joint is a plane joint.
The proximal radioulnar joint is a pivot joint.
The wrist joint is an ellipsoid joint.
citation: Type: Synovial ball-and-socket joint.

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A 30-year-old weightlifter complains of a 'catching' sensation in his shoulder. An MRI reveals a tear in the fibrocartilaginous structure that normally increases the depth of the socket. What is this structure?
optionExplanations: The glenoid labrum is a fibrocartilaginous rim that deepens the glenoid cavity.
Articular discs are found in the sternoclavicular joint, not the shoulder.
The coracoacromial ligament forms a protective arch, not a rim.
The synovial membrane lines the capsule but does not deepen the cavity.
Capsular ligaments strengthen the joint but do not deepen the socket.
citation: the glenoid cavity is deepened by the presence of a fibrocartilaginous rim termed the glenoid labrum

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: During a surgical procedure to repair a shoulder capsule tear, the surgeon identifies the lateral attachment site of the fibrous capsule. Where is this attachment located on the humerus?
optionExplanations: The shoulder joint capsule attaches to the anatomic neck of the humerus.
The surgical neck is distal to the capsule attachment and is a common site for fractures.
The deltoid tuberosity is on the shaft of the humerus.
The greater tuberosity is a site for rotator cuff muscle insertions.
The bicipital groove houses the biceps tendon, not the capsule attachment.
citation: laterally, it is attached to the anatomic neck of the humerus

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A patient presents with recurrent shoulder subluxation. The physician explains that the stability of this joint is primarily maintained by a group of four muscles whose tendons fuse with the capsule. What is this functional group called?
optionExplanations: The rotator cuff (SITS muscles) provides the primary stability for the shoulder joint.
The shoulder girdle refers to the clavicle and scapula bones.
There is no specific 'axillary group' of muscles for stabilization.
The pectoral muscles are primarily for movement, not intrinsic stabilization.
The deltoid is a major mover but is not part of the stabilizing cuff.
citation: The cuff plays a very important role in stabilizing the glenohumeral (shoulder) joint.

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A 45-year-old woman is unable to lift her arm sideways to a 90-degree angle after a fall. Which part of the deltoid muscle is primarily responsible for this movement?
optionExplanations: The middle fibers of the deltoid are the primary abductors of the shoulder.
Anterior fibers are primarily for flexion and medial rotation.
Posterior fibers are primarily for extension and lateral rotation.
The deltoid does not have 'sternal' fibers; that refers to the pectoralis major.
Clavicular fibers are the anterior fibers, which flex the arm.
citation: The middle fibers of the deltoid, assisted by the supraspinatus, are involved.

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: Which muscle is responsible for the first 15 degrees of abduction at the shoulder joint?
optionExplanations: The supraspinatus initiates abduction for the first 15 degrees.
The deltoid takes over abduction after the first 15 degrees.
The infraspinatus is a lateral rotator.
The teres minor is a lateral rotator.
The subscapularis is a medial rotator.
citation: The supraspinatus muscle initiates the movement of abduction

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A physical therapist is measuring the range of motion in a patient. If the patient abducts their arm to a total of 90 degrees, how many degrees of this movement occurred specifically at the glenohumeral joint according to the scapulo-humeral mechanism?
optionExplanations: Using the 2:1 ratio, 2/3 of the 90 degrees (60 degrees) occurs at the glenohumeral joint.
30 degrees (1/3) would be the scapular rotation component.
45 degrees would imply a 1:1 ratio, which is incorrect.
90 degrees would imply no scapular rotation, which is impossible for full abduction.
15 degrees is only the initiation phase.
citation: For every 3° of abduction of the arm, a 2° abduction occurs in the shoulder joint and a 1° abduction occurs by rotation of the scapula.

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: During abduction of the arm, what bony contact prevents further movement at the glenohumeral joint beyond approximately 120 degrees?
optionExplanations: The greater tuberosity hits the lateral edge of the acromion at 120 degrees.
The lesser tuberosity does not contact the coracoid during abduction.
The humeral head rotates within the glenoid but contact with the rim is not the primary limit for abduction.
The surgical neck is too distal to contact the acromion.
The deltoid tuberosity is on the shaft and does not contact the clavicle.
citation: At about 120° of abduction of the arm, the greater tuberosity of the humerus comes into contact with the lateral edge of the acromion.

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A 19-year-old athlete falls on his outstretched arm while it is in a fully abducted position. He presents with a squared-off shoulder. What is the most likely mechanism of this injury?
optionExplanations: Abduction tilts the head onto the weak inferior capsule, leading to anterior-inferior dislocation.
Posterior dislocations are rare and usually caused by direct violence to the front.
Superior subluxation is prevented by the coracoacromial arch.
AC separation involves the clavicle and acromion, not the humeral head.
Sternoclavicular displacement involves the medial end of the clavicle.
citation: Sudden violence applied to the humerus with the joint fully abducted tilts the humeral head downward onto the inferior weak part of the capsule

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: Following a shoulder dislocation, a patient is found to have a loss of sensation over the lower half of the deltoid muscle. Which nerve was likely compressed by the humeral head?
optionExplanations: The axillary nerve passes through the quadrangular space and is vulnerable during dislocation.
The radial nerve is in the spiral groove, not the quadrangular space.
The suprascapular nerve is superior to the joint.
The musculocutaneous nerve is anterior and medial to the joint.
The long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior on the chest wall.
citation: A subglenoid displacement of the head of the humerus into the quadrangular space can cause damage to the axillary nerve

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A 50-year-old tennis player reports sharp pain only when his arm is between 60 and 120 degrees of abduction. What clinical condition does this 'painful arc' suggest?
optionExplanations: Pain in the middle range of abduction is classic for supraspinatus tendinitis/impingement.
Biceps tendon rupture usually presents with a 'Popeye' deformity and pain in the groove.
Adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder) causes pain and restriction in all planes.
Deltoid strain would cause pain throughout the entire range of contraction.
Subscapularis tears affect medial rotation more than abduction.
citation: It is characterized by the presence of a spasm of pain in the middle range of abduction, when the diseased area impinges on the acromion.

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A patient is unable to start lifting his arm from his side. However, if the examiner passively lifts the arm to 20 degrees, the patient can then continue the abduction himself. Which muscle is likely ruptured?
optionExplanations: Loss of initiation of abduction is the hallmark of a supraspinatus tendon rupture.
The deltoid can continue abduction once the first 15-20 degrees are achieved.
Teres major is an adductor, not an abductor.
Pectoralis major is an adductor and flexor.
Serratus anterior rotates the scapula for abduction above 90 degrees.
citation: The patient with a ruptured supraspinatus tendon is unable to initiate abduction of the arm.

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A 70-year-old woman suffers a fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus. Which muscle's function must be carefully evaluated due to its nerve supply's proximity to the fracture site?
optionExplanations: The axillary nerve, which supplies the deltoid, is closely related to the surgical neck.
The biceps is supplied by the musculocutaneous nerve.
The triceps is supplied by the radial nerve.
The coracobrachialis is supplied by the musculocutaneous nerve.
The brachialis is supplied by the musculocutaneous and radial nerves.
citation: The surgical neck has important relations with the axillary nerve and the anterior and posterior circumflex humeral vessels.

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: Which artery contributes to the anastomosis around the scapula by supplying both the supraspinous and infraspinous fossae?
optionExplanations: The suprascapular artery supplies both fossae on the posterior scapula.
The lateral thoracic artery supplies the chest wall.
The thoracoacromial artery supplies the pectoral and acromial regions.
The brachial artery is in the arm.
The profunda brachii is in the posterior compartment of the arm.
citation: The suprascapular artery is distributed to the supraspinous and infraspinous fossae of the scapula.

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A surgeon needs to ligate the axillary artery due to a traumatic injury. To ensure the survival of the upper limb via the scapular anastomosis, where should the ligature be placed relative to the subscapular artery?
options: At the lower border of teres major
Distal to the circumflex humeral arteries
At the origin of the brachial artery
optionExplanations: Ligating proximal to the subscapular artery allows blood to reach the limb via the scapular anastomosis.
Ligating distal to the subscapular artery cuts off the main collateral pathway to the distal limb.
The lower border of teres major is where the axillary artery becomes the brachial artery.
Ligating distal to the circumflex arteries is too far distal to utilize the scapular anastomosis.
Ligating the brachial artery origin would result in limb ischemia.
citation: it is critical to ligate the axillary artery proximal to the subscapular artery in order to maintain adequate collateral flow to the upper limb

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: Which part of the deltoid muscle is the primary agonist for shoulder flexion?
optionExplanations: The anterior fibers of the deltoid flex the shoulder.
The middle fibers abduct the shoulder.
The posterior fibers extend the shoulder.
The deltoid does not have a distinct 'inferior' part.
Lateral fibers usually refer to the middle (acromial) fibers.
citation: Normal flexion is about 90° and is performed by the anterior fibers of the deltoid

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: Which part of the deltoid muscle acts as an antagonist to the anterior fibers during shoulder movement?
optionExplanations: The posterior fibers extend the shoulder, opposing the flexion of the anterior fibers.
The middle fibers are for abduction.
Acromial fibers are the middle fibers.
Clavicular fibers are the anterior fibers.
The deltoid does not have 'medial' fibers.
citation: Normal extension is about 45° and is performed by the posterior fibers of the deltoid

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A patient presents with a deep abscess in the posterior shoulder region. The surgeon is concerned about structures passing through the quadrangular space. Which nerve and vessel pair are at risk?
options: Axillary nerve and posterior circumflex humeral artery
Radial nerve and profunda brachii artery
Ulnar nerve and superior ulnar collateral artery
correctOption: Axillary nerve and posterior circumflex humeral artery
optionExplanations: The axillary nerve and posterior circumflex humeral artery occupy the quadrangular space.
The radial nerve and profunda brachii are in the triangular interval/spiral groove.
The suprascapular structures are in the suprascapular notch.
The ulnar nerve is in the medial compartment of the arm.
The median nerve and brachial artery are in the anterior compartment.
citation: the axillary nerve and the posterior circumflex humeral vessels emerge through this space

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: What is the primary function of the coracoacromial ligament?
options: Protect the superior aspect of the joint
Hold the biceps tendon in place
correctOption: Protect the superior aspect of the joint
optionExplanations: The coracoacromial ligament and arch protect the superior aspect of the shoulder joint.
The glenoid labrum deepens the cavity.
The transverse humeral ligament holds the biceps tendon.
The glenohumeral ligaments prevent anterior dislocation.
The supraspinatus muscle initiates abduction.
citation: The coracoacromial ligament extends between the coracoid process and the acromion... Its function is to protect the superior aspect of the joint.

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A patient with chronic shoulder arthritis experiences pain that radiates to the skin over the lower deltoid. This is explained by the fact that the joint and the overlying skin share a common nerve supply. Which nerve is responsible for this referred pain?
optionExplanations: The axillary nerve supplies both the shoulder joint and the skin over the lower deltoid.
The radial nerve supplies the skin of the posterior arm and forearm.
The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the skin of the lateral forearm.
The ulnar nerve supplies the medial hand.
The median nerve supplies the lateral palm.
citation: Nerve supply: Axillary and suprascapular nerves.

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A patient who underwent a radical mastectomy now finds it impossible to raise her arm above her head (180 degrees), although she can reach 90 degrees. Which muscle's nerve was likely damaged during surgery?
optionExplanations: The serratus anterior is required to rotate the scapula for abduction above 90 degrees.
The deltoid can abduct to 90 degrees but cannot rotate the scapula.
The supraspinatus initiates abduction but does not rotate the scapula.
The pectoralis minor depresses the shoulder.
The latissimus dorsi is an adductor and extensor.
citation: Paralysis of the serratus anterior results in the inability to rotate the scapula during abduction of the arm above a right angle.

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A midshaft humeral fracture occurs just proximal to the insertion of the deltoid. The distal fragment is pulled upward and laterally. At which bony landmark does the deltoid insert?
optionExplanations: The deltoid muscle inserts into the deltoid tuberosity on the lateral humerus.
The greater tuberosity is the insertion for the supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and teres minor.
The lesser tuberosity is the insertion for the subscapularis.
The radial groove is a depression for the radial nerve.
The medial epicondyle is an origin for flexor muscles.
citation: The deltoid tuberosity... serves as the attachment area for the deltoid muscle.

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A 25-year-old baseball pitcher feels a 'pop' and subsequent snapping sensation in the front of his shoulder during a throw. The physician suspects the long head of the biceps tendon has slipped out of its groove. Which ligament is likely torn?
optionExplanations: The transverse humeral ligament holds the long head of the biceps tendon in the bicipital groove.
The coracohumeral ligament strengthens the superior capsule.
The glenohumeral ligaments are internal thickenings of the capsule.
The coracoacromial ligament forms the superior arch.
The costo-clavicular ligament is at the sternoclavicular joint.
citation: The transverse humeral ligament... bridges the gap between the two tuberosities, and holds the tendon of the long head of the biceps muscle in place.

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: Which two arteries form a vascular ring around the surgical neck of the humerus to supply the shoulder joint?
options: Anterior and posterior circumflex humeral arteries
Highest thoracic and lateral thoracic arteries
correctOption: Anterior and posterior circumflex humeral arteries
optionExplanations: The circumflex humeral arteries encircle the surgical neck.
The suprascapular and subscapular arteries are part of the scapular anastomosis.
The lateral thoracic and thoracoacromial arteries supply the pectoral region.
The profunda brachii is distal to the shoulder joint.
The highest thoracic artery is a branch of the first part of the axillary artery.
citation: The anterior and posterior circumflex humeral arteries wind around the front and the back of the surgical neck of the humerus, respectively, and form an anastomosing circle.

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
coreConcept: Axillary nerve injury in shoulder dislocation
statement: A 22-year-old rugby player is brought to the ER after a heavy tackle. Physical examination reveals a loss of the rounded contour of the right shoulder and a subglenoid displacement of the humeral head. Which nerve is most likely at risk of injury in this patient?
optionExplanations: The radial nerve is related to the radial groove of the humerus, not the inferior capsule.
The ulnar nerve passes behind the medial epicondyle at the elbow.
The axillary nerve passes through the quadrangular space, making it vulnerable to downward displacement of the humeral head.
The musculocutaneous nerve is protected deep to the biceps brachii.
The suprascapular nerve is superior to the joint and less likely to be injured in inferior dislocations.
citation: axillary nerve can be injured in dislocations of the glenohumeral (shoulder) joint

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A 45-year-old painter presents with difficulty lifting his arm to paint ceilings. He can lift his arm if someone helps him for the first 15 degrees, after which he can complete the movement himself. Which muscle is most likely damaged?
optionExplanations: The deltoid completes abduction but cannot initiate it effectively without the supraspinatus.
The supraspinatus initiates the first 15 degrees of abduction and holds the humeral head in the glenoid fossa.
The infraspinatus is primarily a lateral rotator.
The teres minor is primarily a lateral rotator.
The subscapularis is a medial rotator.
citation: supraspinatus muscle initiates the movement of abduction

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: What is the anatomical classification of the glenohumeral joint?
optionExplanations: The elbow is a hinge joint.
The proximal radioulnar joint is a pivot joint.
The acromioclavicular joint is a plane joint.
The shoulder joint allows multi-axial movement, characteristic of a ball-and-socket variety.
The carpometacarpal joint of the thumb is a saddle joint.
citation: Synovial ball-and-socket joint

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: During a physical therapy session, a patient abducts their arm to 90 degrees. According to the normal scapula-humeral mechanism, how many degrees of this movement occurred specifically at the glenohumeral joint?
optionExplanations: 30 degrees would be the scapular contribution in a 90-degree total movement.
45 degrees is half, which does not follow the 2:1 ratio.
In a 3:2:1 ratio, for 90 degrees total, 60 degrees occurs at the shoulder joint and 30 degrees occurs via scapular rotation.
90 degrees would mean no scapular rotation occurred.
120 degrees exceeds the total movement described.
citation: For every 3° of abduction of the arm, a 2° abduction occurs in the shoulder joint and a 1° abduction occurs by rotation of the scapula

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A patient is unable to perform powerful flexion of the shoulder joint after a trauma. Which part of the deltoid muscle is most likely involved in this deficit?
optionExplanations: Middle (acromial) fibers are responsible for abduction.
Posterior fibers are responsible for extension and lateral rotation.
Anterior fibers of the deltoid act as powerful flexors of the shoulder joint.
Acromial fibers is another name for the middle fibers.
Spinal fibers is another name for the posterior fibers.
citation: anterior fibers of the deltoid, pectoralis major, biceps, and coracobrachialis muscles

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: Which nerves provide the primary sensory innervation to the glenohumeral joint?
optionExplanations: The radial and ulnar nerves supply the elbow and hand.
The median and musculocutaneous nerves supply the forearm and arm muscles.
The axillary and suprascapular nerves are the primary sources of innervation for the shoulder joint capsule.
The long thoracic and thoracodorsal nerves supply the serratus anterior and latissimus dorsi.
The phrenic nerve can cause referred pain but does not directly innervate the joint.
citation: Axillary and suprascapular nerves

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A 60-year-old woman with chronic shoulder instability is found to have significant muscle wasting. The clinician explains that the joint's strength depends on the tone of specific muscles. Which group of muscles is the clinician referring to?
optionExplanations: Pectoral muscles are primarily for adduction and medial rotation.
The rotator cuff (SITS) provides dynamic stability by holding the humeral head in the glenoid cavity.
Triceps and biceps provide minimal support compared to the cuff.
Rhomboids stabilize the scapula, not the glenohumeral joint directly.
The deltoid is for movement; the trapezius stabilizes the shoulder girdle.
citation: tone of the short rotator cuff muscles that cross in front, above, and behind the joint

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A patient presents with 'impingement syndrome' where the supraspinatus tendon is compressed during abduction. Which structure forms the superior boundary of the space through which this tendon passes?
optionExplanations: Glenohumeral ligaments are part of the joint capsule.
The transverse humeral ligament bridges the bicipital groove.
The coracoacromial ligament, along with the acromion and coracoid, forms a protective arch over the joint.
There is no such structure as the costoacromial ligament.
The conoid ligament is part of the coracoclavicular ligament.
citation: coracoacromial ligament extends between the coracoid process and the acromion

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: Which ligament is responsible for holding the tendon of the long head of the biceps brachii in the intertubercular groove?
optionExplanations: The coracohumeral ligament strengthens the superior capsule.
Glenohumeral ligaments are intrinsic to the capsule.
The transverse humeral ligament bridges the greater and lesser tuberosities to secure the biceps tendon.
The coracoacromial ligament forms an arch above the joint.
The trapezoid ligament is part of the coracoclavicular ligament.
citation: transverse humeral ligament strengthens the capsule, bridges the gap between the two tuberosities

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A patient is brought to the trauma center after a high-speed motorcycle accident where his chest hit the handlebars. Radiographs show a rare posterior dislocation of the shoulder. What was the most likely mechanism of injury?
optionExplanations: Fall on an outstretched hand usually causes anterior-inferior dislocation.
Forced abduction typically leads to anterior-inferior dislocation.
Posterior dislocations are rare and typically result from direct force applied to the anterior aspect of the joint.
Lateral rotation is a component of anterior dislocations.
Upward traction is not a standard mechanism for posterior dislocation.
citation: Posterior dislocations are rare and are usually caused by direct violence to the front of the joint

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A 30-year-old weightlifter complains of pain when performing lateral raises (abducting the arm to 90 degrees). Which part of the deltoid is the primary mover for this specific exercise?
optionExplanations: Anterior fibers flex and medially rotate.
Posterior fibers extend and laterally rotate.
The middle (acromial) fibers are the multipennate, powerful abductors of the shoulder.
Clavicular fibers are the anterior fibers.
Spinal fibers are the posterior fibers.
citation: middle fibers of the deltoid, assisted by the supraspinatus, are involved

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: Which artery, originating from the subclavian artery, contributes to the anastomosis on the posterior surface of the scapula?
optionExplanations: The lateral thoracic artery supplies the chest wall.
The thoracoacromial artery supplies the pectoral region.
The suprascapular artery (from the thyrocervical trunk of the subclavian) passes over the transverse scapular ligament to the fossae.
The subscapular artery is a branch of the axillary artery, not the subclavian.
The brachial artery is the continuation of the axillary artery in the arm.
citation: suprascapular artery is distributed to the supraspinous and infraspinous fossae

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: Three months after a surgical neck fracture of the humerus, a patient's shoulder appears bony and square rather than rounded. On palpation, the greater tuberosity is very prominent. What is the most likely cause?
optionExplanations: Supraspinatus tear affects abduction initiation but not the rounded contour.
Biceps rupture causes a 'Popeye' deformity in the arm.
Atrophy of the deltoid (due to axillary nerve injury) removes the muscle bulk covering the greater tuberosity.
Pectoralis major fibrosis would not cause a square shoulder.
Teres major hypertrophy would not compensate for deltoid loss.
citation: paralyzed deltoid wastes rapidly, and the underlying greater tuberosity can be readily palpated

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A patient who underwent a radical mastectomy now finds she cannot comb her hair because she cannot raise her arm above her head. Physical exam shows winging of the scapula. Which component of the abduction mechanism is failed?
optionExplanations: Initiation is likely intact if she can start the movement.
The deltoid can abduct to 90 degrees, but hair combing requires higher elevation.
Raising the arm above the head requires the serratus anterior to rotate the scapula, tilting the glenoid cavity upward.
Adduction is the opposite of the required movement.
Lateral rotation is necessary but not the primary reason for the inability to reach overhead in this case.
citation: Paralysis of the serratus anterior results in the inability to rotate the scapula during abduction

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: What is the primary function of the glenoid labrum in the shoulder joint?
optionExplanations: Synovial fluid is secreted by the synovial membrane.
The labrum is a fibrocartilaginous ring that increases the depth and surface area of the shallow glenoid cavity.
It does not specifically limit medial rotation.
The capsule attaches to the anatomical neck, not the labrum to the surgical neck.
The axillary nerve is inferior to the joint, not related to the labrum.
citation: glenoid cavity is deepened by the presence of a fibrocartilaginous rim termed the glenoid labrum

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A patient is asked to place the back of his hand on his lower back (internal/medial rotation). He finds this movement extremely weak. Which muscle is a primary medial rotator of the shoulder?
optionExplanations: Infraspinatus is a lateral rotator.
Supraspinatus is an abductor.
The subscapularis is the only rotator cuff muscle that medially rotates the humerus.
Teres minor is a lateral rotator.
Trapezius acts on the scapula, not the humerus.
citation: subscapularis, latissimus dorsi, teres major, and the anterior fibers of the deltoid muscle perform this

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A professional baseball pitcher is diagnosed with a tear in his lateral rotators. Which muscle is most likely affected?
optionExplanations: Subscapularis is a medial rotator.
Pectoralis major is a medial rotator.
The infraspinatus is a key lateral rotator of the shoulder.
Latissimus dorsi is a medial rotator.
Teres major is a medial rotator.
citation: infraspinatus, teres minor, and the posterior fibers of the deltoid muscle perform this

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: Where does the articular capsule of the glenohumeral joint attach on the humerus?
optionExplanations: The surgical neck is distal to the capsule attachment and is a common site for fractures.
The capsule attaches to the anatomic neck, which is the line separating the head from the tuberosities.
The deltoid tuberosity is on the shaft of the humerus.
The greater tuberosity provides attachment for muscles, not the primary circumferential capsule.
The bicipital groove houses the biceps tendon.
citation: laterally, it is attached to the anatomic neck of the humerus

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A patient has a condition that prevents any rotation of the scapula. During physical examination, what is the maximum degree of abduction the patient can likely achieve at the shoulder joint before bony contact occurs?
optionExplanations: 30 degrees is far below the limit.
60 degrees is below the limit.
90 degrees is often cited as a functional limit, but the anatomical limit is higher.
Pure glenohumeral abduction is limited to 120 degrees when the greater tuberosity hits the acromion.
180 degrees requires full scapular rotation.
citation: At about 120° of abduction of the arm, the greater tuberosity of the humerus comes into contact with the lateral edge of the acromion

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A 55-year-old woman complains of a 'painful arc' when she abducts her arm between 60 and 120 degrees. The doctor suspects inflammation of a structure that normally reduces friction between the deltoid and the supraspinatus tendon. What is this structure?
optionExplanations: The glenoid labrum is inside the joint cavity.
The subscapular bursa is anterior, under the subscapularis muscle.
The subacromial bursa lies between the acromion/deltoid and the supraspinatus tendon to prevent friction.
Synovial sheaths surround tendons, but this specific large bursa is the subacromial.
The shoulder joint does not have an internal articular disc.
citation: friction is reduced to a minimum by the large subacromial bursa

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: Which nerve provides motor supply to the teres major muscle?
optionExplanations: The upper subscapular nerve supplies only the subscapularis.
The lower subscapular nerve supplies both the subscapularis and the teres major.
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid and teres minor.
The suprascapular nerve supplies the supraspinatus and infraspinatus.
The thoracodorsal nerve supplies the latissimus dorsi.
citation: lower subscapular nerve supplies the teres major muscle

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A patient is recovering from a nerve injury and is practicing bringing his arm from an abducted position back toward his trunk against resistance. Which muscle is a primary contributor to this adduction?
optionExplanations: Supraspinatus is an abductor.
The deltoid is the primary abductor.
The latissimus dorsi is a powerful adductor, extensor, and medial rotator.
The serratus anterior rotates the scapula.
The levator scapulae elevates the scapula.
citation: pectoralis major, latissimus dorsi, teres major, and teres minor muscles perform this

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: During an axillary nerve block, the posterior terminal branch is successfully anesthetized. Besides the deltoid, which muscle's function will be directly affected?
optionExplanations: Teres major is supplied by the lower subscapular nerve.
The axillary nerve (specifically its posterior branch) innervates the teres minor.
Subscapularis is supplied by the subscapular nerves.
Infraspinatus is supplied by the suprascapular nerve.
Supraspinatus is supplied by the suprascapular nerve.
citation: posterior terminal branch gives off a branch to the teres minor muscle

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: Which arteries form an anastomotic circle around the surgical neck of the humerus?
optionExplanations: These contribute to the scapular anastomosis.
These are in the arm and radial groove.
The circumflex humeral arteries encircle the surgical neck and are at risk in fractures there.
These supply the lateral chest wall.
These are branches of the 1st and 2nd parts of the axillary artery.
citation: anterior and posterior circumflex humeral arteries wind around the front and the back of the surgical neck

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A 19-year-old wrestler presents with an obvious deformity after his shoulder 'popped out'. The shoulder looks flat and 'squared off'. Why has the shoulder lost its normal rounded appearance?
options: Greater tuberosity is no longer bulging
correctOption: Greater tuberosity is no longer bulging
optionExplanations: An acromion fracture would cause localized pain but not the classic 'squared' look of dislocation.
Clavicle displacement affects the root of the neck.
In dislocation, the humeral head moves out of place, so the greater tuberosity no longer bulges laterally under the deltoid.
A deltoid tear would cause weakness but not the immediate 'squared' deformity of a dislocation.
Coracoid fracture is rare and does not define the shoulder's outer contour.
citation: rounded appearance of the shoulder is seen to be lost because the greater tuberosity of the humerus is no longer bulging

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: Which of the following best describes the anatomical variety of the glenohumeral joint?
optionExplanations: Hinge joints allow movement in one plane, like the elbow.
Pivot joints allow rotation, like the radioulnar joint.
The shoulder joint is a multiaxial ball-and-socket joint allowing wide range of motion.
Saddle joints are found at the base of the thumb.
Plane joints allow gliding, like intercarpal joints.
citation: Synovial ball-and-socket joint

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A 22-year-old athlete presents to the ER after a fall during a basketball game. He is holding his arm in slight abduction and lateral rotation, and the normal rounded contour of his shoulder is lost. Given that the shoulder joint is the most commonly dislocated large joint, which position is the humeral head most likely to occupy?
optionExplanations: Subglenoid is the initial position before muscles pull it elsewhere.
In anterior-inferior dislocations, muscles usually pull the head into the subcoracoid position.
Subacromial is not a standard dislocation position for the humeral head.
Retroglenoid refers to rare posterior dislocations.
Supraclavicular is not a site for humeral head displacement.
citation: shoulder joint is the most commonly dislocated large joint

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A 65-year-old retired laborer presents with inability to lift his right arm. On examination, he cannot start the movement of abduction, but if the examiner passively lifts the arm to 20 degrees, he can then raise it further using his deltoid. This confirms that the supraspinatus muscle initiates the movement of abduction. Which structure is likely ruptured?
optionExplanations: Deltoid takes over after the first 15-20 degrees.
The supraspinatus is essential for the first 15 degrees of abduction.
Infraspinatus is primarily a lateral rotator.
Teres major is an adductor and medial rotator.
Subscapularis is a medial rotator.
citation: supraspinatus muscle initiates the movement of abduction

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: During the clinical assessment of arm abduction, the examiner notes the coordinated movement of the humerus and scapula. What is the standard ratio of glenohumeral to scapulothoracic movement?
optionExplanations: 1:1 would imply equal movement, which is incorrect.
The text states for every 3 degrees, 2 are at the joint and 1 is at the scapula (2:1).
3:1 overestimates the joint contribution.
1:2 would mean the scapula moves twice as much as the joint.
1:3 is anatomically incorrect for normal rhythm.
citation: for every 3° of abduction of the arm, a 2° abduction occurs in the shoulder joint and a 1° abduction occurs by rotation of the scapula

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A patient with a fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus shows signs of deltoid muscle wasting and loss of sensation over the lower half of the deltoid. The examiner recalls that the axillary nerve and the posterior circumflex humeral vessels emerge through this space. Which space is being described?
optionExplanations: The triangular space transmits the circumflex scapular artery.
The quadrangular space transmits the axillary nerve and posterior circumflex humeral vessels.
The suprascapular notch transmits the suprascapular nerve.
The bicipital groove houses the long head of biceps tendon.
The clavipectoral triangle contains the cephalic vein.
citation: axillary nerve and the posterior circumflex humeral vessels emerge through this space

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: Which nerves provide the primary sensory innervation to the capsule and synovial membrane of the shoulder joint?
optionExplanations: Radial and ulnar nerves supply the forearm and hand.
Median and musculocutaneous nerves supply the arm and forearm.
The axillary and suprascapular nerves are the primary supply to the shoulder joint.
Long thoracic and thoracodorsal nerves supply muscles (serratus anterior/latissimus dorsi).
Phrenic and accessory nerves are not the primary supply to the joint capsule.
citation: Axillary and suprascapular nerves

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A 45-year-old tennis player complains of severe shoulder pain when serving. On examination, the pain is absent during the first 60 degrees of abduction but becomes intense between 60 and 120 degrees. This is characterized as pain in the middle range of abduction, when the diseased area impinges on the acromion. What is the most likely diagnosis?
optionExplanations: Biceps rupture causes pain in the groove, not a painful arc.
Supraspinatus tendinitis causes the 'painful arc' as the tendon hits the acromion.
Adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder) limits all movements, not just a specific arc.
Deltoid strain would cause pain throughout the contraction.
Serratus anterior paralysis causes winging, not a painful arc.
citation: pain in the middle range of abduction, when the diseased area impinges on the acromion

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A surgeon is operating on a patient with a high-velocity gunshot wound to the axilla. To maintain collateral circulation to the upper limb, the surgeon knows it is critical to ligate the axillary artery proximal to the subscapular artery. Which artery from the subclavian system primarily contributes to this anastomosis?
optionExplanations: Internal thoracic artery supplies the chest wall.
Vertebral artery supplies the brain.
The suprascapular artery (from subclavian) anastomoses with the subscapular artery (from axillary).
Superior thyroid artery supplies the thyroid gland.
Costocervical trunk supplies the upper intercostal spaces.
citation: critical to ligate the axillary artery proximal to the subscapular artery

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: While testing the different parts of the deltoid muscle, the examiner asks the patient to laterally rotate the arm against resistance. The posterior fibers of the deltoid muscle perform this action. Which other movement is also performed by these specific fibers?
optionExplanations: Flexion is performed by the anterior fibers.
Medial rotation is performed by the anterior fibers.
The posterior fibers of the deltoid perform extension and lateral rotation.
Initiation of abduction is the supraspinatus.
Protraction is a scapular movement.
citation: posterior fibers of the deltoid muscle perform this

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A 30-year-old man falls from a height and catches a ledge with his hand, resulting in a sudden downward pull on his abducted arm. He suffers a dislocation because the least supported part of the joint lies in the inferior location. Why is this area weak?
options: Attachment of the long head of biceps
Support from the long head of triceps
optionExplanations: The coracoacromial ligament supports the superior aspect.
The rotator cuff (SITS) covers the front, top, and back, but not the bottom.
Glenohumeral ligaments strengthen the anterior capsule.
Biceps tendon is intracapsular but superior.
Triceps gives only weak support and does not prevent inferior weakness.
citation: least supported part of the joint lies in the inferior location

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: At approximately 120 degrees of arm abduction, pure glenohumeral movement is halted. This occurs because the greater tuberosity of the humerus comes into contact with the lateral edge of the acromion. How is further elevation achieved?
optionExplanations: Medial rotation would not clear the tuberosity.
Further elevation above 120 degrees requires scapular rotation on the chest wall.
Elbow flexion does not affect shoulder elevation.
Clavicular elevation, not depression, accompanies this.
Scapular rotation (upward) is required, not simple adduction.
citation: greater tuberosity of the humerus comes into contact with the lateral edge of the acromion

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: What is the primary functional purpose of the fibrocartilage ring known as the glenoid labrum?
optionExplanations: Synovial membrane secretes fluid.
The labrum increases the surface area and depth of the shallow glenoid fossa.
It does not specifically limit medial rotation.
The capsule attaches to the anatomical neck.
It has no relation to the axillary nerve.
citation: serves to broaden and deepen the joint cavity

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A patient presents with 'impingement syndrome.' The clinician explains that the supraspinatus tendon is being squeezed against a protective 'roof' formed by a ligament and two bony processes. The coracoacromial ligament extends between the coracoid process and the acromion to form this roof. What is the primary function of this arch?
optionExplanations: Anterior dislocation is prevented by the subscapularis and ligaments.
The arch prevents the humeral head from being displaced superiorly out of the fossa.
It has no motor function.
The labrum deepens the fossa.
It is superior to the musculocutaneous nerve.
citation: coracoacromial ligament extends between the coracoid process and the acromion

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A patient is unable to medially rotate his arm after a deep laceration to the anterior axillary wall. The examiner notes that the subscapularis, latissimus dorsi, teres major, and the anterior fibers of the deltoid muscle perform this action. Which of these muscles is also a component of the rotator cuff?
optionExplanations: Latissimus dorsi is a back muscle.
Teres major is not part of the cuff (Teres minor is).
Deltoid is a superficial shoulder muscle.
Subscapularis is the anterior component of the rotator cuff.
Pectoralis major is a chest muscle.
citation: subscapularis, latissimus dorsi, teres major, and the anterior fibers of the deltoid muscle perform this

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: What is the specific function of the transverse humeral ligament?
optionExplanations: Coracoclavicular ligament connects scapula to clavicle.
Labrum deepens the cavity.
It bridges the bicipital groove to retain the long head of biceps.
The inferior capsule has no specific ligamentous reinforcement.
It does not primarily limit rotation.
citation: holds the tendon of the long head of the biceps muscle in place

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A 50-year-old woman presents with sharp pain over the point of her right shoulder. However, shoulder movements are full and painless. The physician suspects gallbladder disease. Irritation of the diaphragmatic pleura or peritoneum can produce referred pain to this region. Which nerve shares the C3-C5 segments responsible for this referral?
optionExplanations: Axillary nerve is C5-C6.
Suprascapular nerve is C5-C6.
The phrenic nerve (C3-C5) irritates the diaphragm, referring pain to the supraclavicular nerves (C3-C4).
Long thoracic is C5-C7.
Radial nerve is C5-T1.
citation: Irritation of the diaphragmatic pleura or peritoneum can produce referred pain

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: An X-ray of a 19-year-old male shows a displaced fracture of the midshaft of the humerus. The proximal fragment is pulled laterally. The radiologist notes the fracture is just distal to a roughened triangular elevation on the anterolateral surface which serves as the attachment area for the deltoid muscle. What is this landmark?
optionExplanations: Greater tubercle is on the proximal end.
Lesser tubercle is on the anterior proximal end.
The deltoid tuberosity is located on the midshaft.
Spiral groove is for the radial nerve.
Anatomical neck is just distal to the head.
citation: serves as the attachment area for the deltoid muscle

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A 33-year-old professional swimmer presents with chronic shoulder instability. The doctor explains that the tendons of the subscapularis, supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and teres minor muscles are fused to the underlying capsule to provide stability. Which of these muscles is responsible for lateral rotation and is tested by having the patient push their hand outward against resistance?
optionExplanations: Subscapularis is a medial rotator.
Supraspinatus is an abductor.
Infraspinatus (and teres minor) are the primary lateral rotators of the cuff.
Teres major is not part of the rotator cuff.
Anterior deltoid is a medial rotator.
citation: tendons of the subscapularis, supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and teres minor muscles are fused to the underlying capsule

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: Where is the fibrous capsule of the shoulder joint attached on the humerus?
optionExplanations: The surgical neck is distal to the tuberosities and is a common fracture site.
The capsule attaches to the anatomic neck, which surrounds the head.
Deltoid tuberosity is on the shaft.
Greater tuberosity is an insertion site for muscles, not the primary capsule line.
Bicipital groove is bridged by the transverse ligament.
citation: attached laterally to the anatomic neck of the humerus

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A patient with a lesion of the long thoracic nerve is unable to raise his arm above the horizontal level. The physician explains that elevation of the arm above the head is accomplished by rotating the scapula. Which two muscles are primarily responsible for this upward rotation?
optionExplanations: Deltoid and supraspinatus abduct at the glenohumeral joint.
Serratus anterior and trapezius rotate the scapula upward to allow full elevation.
Levator scapulae and rhomboids rotate the scapula downward.
Pectoral muscles adduct and medially rotate.
Latissimus and teres major adduct and extend.
citation: Elevation of the arm above the head is accomplished by rotating the scapula

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A 70-year-old man with severe osteoarthritis of the shoulder feels a sudden 'snap' while lifting a grocery bag, followed by a 'Popeye' deformity in his arm. The examiner notes that the tendon of the long head of the biceps brachii muscle passes through the joint and is prone to erosion. Where does this tendon attach proximally?
optionExplanations: Short head of biceps attaches to the coracoid.
Long head of triceps attaches to the infraglenoid tubercle.
The long head of biceps attaches to the supraglenoid tubercle inside the joint.
Supraspinatus attaches to the greater tuberosity.
The capsule attaches to the anatomic neck.
citation: tendon of the long head of the biceps brachii muscle passes through the joint

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: The glenohumeral joint is classified as which type of synovial joint?
optionExplanations: Hinge joints allow flexion/extension only.
Pivot joints allow rotation only.
Ball-and-socket joints allow multiaxial movement.
Condyloid joints allow biaxial movement.
Plane joints allow gliding.
citation: Synovial ball-and-socket joint

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: During a dissection of the axilla, a student identifies a large branch of the third part of the axillary artery that descends along the axillary border of the scapula. This vessel divides into the circumflex scapular and thoracodorsal arteries. What is this parent vessel?
optionExplanations: Lateral thoracic is a branch of the second part.
Thoracoacromial is a branch of the second part.
The subscapular artery is the largest branch of the third part and gives these two branches.
Profunda brachii is a branch of the brachial artery.
Highest thoracic is a branch of the first part.
citation: divides into the circumflex scapular and thoracodorsal arteries

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A patient presents with a 'waiter's tip' posture following a birth injury. The clinician notes that the axillary nerve (C5 to 6) arises from the termination of the posterior cord and is likely damaged. Which muscle's paralysis primarily contributes to the loss of abduction in this case?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis major is an adductor.
The deltoid is the main abductor and is supplied by the axillary nerve.
Teres major is an adductor.
Biceps is a flexor/supinator.
Coracobrachialis is a flexor.
citation: axillary nerve (C5 to 6) arises from the termination of the posterior cord

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: Which of the following statements regarding the glenohumeral ligaments is correct?
optionExplanations: They are described as weak, not strong.
The text explicitly calls them three weak bands of fibrous tissue.
They strengthen the anterior, not inferior, capsule.
They connect the glenoid to the humerus.
They are intrinsic thickenings of the capsule.
citation: glenohumeral ligaments are three weak bands of fibrous tissue

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A 22-year-old rugby player is brought to the ER after a heavy tackle where his arm was forced into full abduction. On inspection, the normal rounded contour of his shoulder is lost. Which joint is most likely affected given that it is the most frequently dislocated large joint in the body?
optionExplanations: This joint is stable and rarely dislocates compared to the shoulder.
Dislocation here is termed shoulder separation, not glenohumeral dislocation.
The glenohumeral (shoulder) joint is the most commonly dislocated large joint due to its shallow glenoid cavity.
This is a stable hinge joint of the elbow.
This is part of the elbow complex and less prone to isolated dislocation.
citation: The shoulder joint is the most commonly dislocated large joint.

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: An elderly woman falls and sustains a fracture of the proximal humerus. Radiographs confirm a fracture at the constricted area just inferior to the tuberosities. Which nerve is at greatest risk of injury due to its close anatomical relationship with this specific site?
optionExplanations: The radial nerve is related to the midshaft (spiral groove).
The ulnar nerve is related to the medial epicondyle.
The median nerve has no close bony relations in the proximal humerus.
The axillary nerve winds around the surgical neck of the humerus.
The musculocutaneous nerve pierces the coracobrachialis and is not related to the surgical neck.
citation: The surgical neck has important relations with the axillary nerve

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: What is the structural classification of the glenohumeral joint?
optionExplanations: The elbow is a hinge joint.
The radioulnar joints are pivot joints.
The carpometacarpal joint of the thumb is a saddle joint.
The shoulder joint is a multiaxial synovial ball-and-socket joint.
The acromioclavicular joint is a plane joint.
citation: Type: Synovial ball-and-socket joint.

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A 45-year-old painter complains that he can no longer start the movement of lifting his arm away from his side, though he can hold it up if someone lifts it for him first. Which muscle is likely ruptured, preventing the initiation of this movement?
optionExplanations: The deltoid takes over abduction after the first 15 degrees.
The supraspinatus initiates the first 0-15 degrees of abduction.
The infraspinatus is a lateral rotator.
The teres minor is a lateral rotator.
The subscapularis is a medial rotator.
citation: The supraspinatus muscle initiates the movement of abduction

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: During a physical therapy session, a student is asked why the humerus usually displaces downwards during a shoulder dislocation. The instructor explains that the rotator cuff reinforces the joint capsule on almost all sides except one. Where is the cuff anatomically deficient?
optionExplanations: The supraspinatus reinforces the joint superiorly.
The subscapularis reinforces the joint anteriorly.
The infraspinatus and teres minor reinforce the joint posteriorly.
The rotator cuff is deficient inferiorly, making it the weakest area.
The medial aspect is the glenoid cavity itself.
citation: The cuff is deficient inferiorly, and this is a site of potential weakness.

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: Which part of the deltoid muscle is primarily responsible for the abduction of the humerus at the shoulder joint?
optionExplanations: Anterior fibers perform flexion and medial rotation.
Posterior fibers perform extension and lateral rotation.
The multipennate middle fibers are the primary abductors.
There are no 'sternal' fibers of the deltoid.
Clavicular fibers are the anterior fibers.
citation: The middle fibers of the deltoid, assisted by the supraspinatus, are involved.

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A patient is unable to raise his arm fully above his head despite having a functional deltoid muscle. The clinician notes that the humerus stops abducting at 120 degrees when the greater tuberosity hits the acromion. What mechanism must occur to allow the final 60 degrees of elevation?
optionExplanations: Medial rotation does not clear the acromion for further abduction.
Clavicle elevation, not depression, accompanies arm elevation.
Rotation of the scapula by the serratus anterior and trapezius allows elevation above 120 degrees.
The labrum is a fixed fibrocartilaginous rim.
Adduction is the opposite of the required movement.
citation: Further elevation of the arm above the head is accomplished by rotating the scapula.

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A young man presents to the clinic following a motorcycle accident with a suspected axillary nerve injury. To confirm the diagnosis, the physician tests for sensory loss. In which specific area would anesthesia be expected?
optionExplanations: The tip of the shoulder is supplied by supraclavicular nerves (C3-4).
The medial arm is supplied by the medial cutaneous nerve of the arm.
The axillary nerve (upper lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm) supplies the skin over the lower deltoid.
The posterior scapular skin is supplied by dorsal rami.
The root of the neck is supplied by cervical nerves.
citation: loss of skin sensation over the lower half of the deltoid muscle

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: What is the primary function of the glenoid labrum in the shoulder joint?
options: Connecting the humerus to the acromion
optionExplanations: Synovial fluid is secreted by the synovial membrane.
The glenoid labrum is a fibrocartilaginous rim that deepens and broadens the glenoid cavity.
The coracoacromial ligament connects the coracoid to the acromion.
It does not specifically limit medial rotation.
It has no protective role for the axillary nerve.
citation: glenoid cavity is deepened by the presence of a fibrocartilaginous rim termed the glenoid labrum

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A professional tennis player experiences sharp shoulder pain only when her arm is between 60 and 120 degrees of abduction. Outside of this range, the pain subsides. What is the most likely clinical diagnosis for this 'painful arc'?
optionExplanations: Frozen shoulder (adhesive capsulitis) causes pain in all ranges of motion.
Axillary nerve palsy causes weakness and sensory loss, not a painful arc.
Supraspinatus tendinitis causes pain when the tendon impinges on the acromion during the middle range of abduction.
Serratus anterior paralysis causes winged scapula.
Teres major is an adductor and medial rotator, not involved in the abduction arc.
citation: characterized by the presence of a spasm of pain in the middle range of abduction

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: A patient with a humerus fracture shows signs of internal bleeding and a weakened pulse in the upper limb. The fracture is located at the surgical neck. Besides the axillary nerve, which vascular structures are most likely damaged?
optionExplanations: The brachial artery begins at the lower border of the teres major.
The profunda brachii is related to the midshaft (radial groove).
The anterior and posterior circumflex humeral vessels wind around the surgical neck.
The subscapular artery is a branch of the third part of the axillary artery but is not at the surgical neck.
The thoracoacromial artery is related to the second part of the axillary artery.
citation: The surgical neck... can be readily damaged in association with a fracture here.

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
statement: Which two nerves provide the primary sensory innervation to the glenohumeral joint capsule and synovial membrane?
optionExplanations: Radial and ulnar nerves supply the elbow and hand.
Median and musculocutaneous nerves supply the forearm and arm muscles.
The axillary and suprascapular nerves supply the shoulder joint.
These nerves supply the serratus anterior and latissimus dorsi.
These are not primary nerves for shoulder joint sensation.
citation: Nerve supply: Axillary and suprascapular nerves.

category: Joints of Upper Limb: Shoulder Joint
