Anterior dislocations are common and usually caused by abduction/external rotation.
Inferior displacement is the first step of a common anterior dislocation.
Superior displacement is blocked by the acromion.
Medial displacement is not possible due to the scapula.
citation: Posterior dislocations are rare and are usually caused by direct violence to the front of the joint.

statement: A patient is diagnosed with 'subacromial bursitis.' Which rotator cuff muscle is most likely to develop secondary degenerative changes due to its location immediately deep to this bursa?
optionExplanations: The supraspinatus tendon lies directly under the subacromial bursa and is most affected by its pathology.
The infraspinatus is posterior to the main part of the subacromial bursa.
The subscapularis is anterior and has its own bursa.
The teres minor is too inferior/posterior to be primarily affected by subacromial bursitis.
The teres major is not related to the subacromial bursa.
citation: Under normal conditions, the amount of friction is reduced to a minimum by the large subacromial bursa

statement: An ER physician is struggling to reduce a dislocated shoulder despite adequate sedation and traction. What anatomical structure is most likely obstructing the return of the humeral head to the glenoid cavity?
optionExplanations: A torn or detached labrum can get caught in the joint space, preventing successful reduction.
The triceps may be stretched but rarely acts as a mechanical block to reduction.
The coracoacromial ligament is superior and doesn't usually block the path of reduction.
Muscle spasm can hinder reduction, but a 'jammed' defect usually implies a structural block like the labrum.
The acromion is a fixed bone and doesn't 'jam' into the joint defect.
citation: The labrum can become jammed in the defect, making reduction of the shoulder joint difficult.

statement: A patient presents with a fracture of the lesser tuberosity of the humerus. Which specific movement of the shoulder will be most significantly weakened due to the loss of the associated rotator cuff muscle?
optionExplanations: The subscapularis inserts on the lesser tuberosity and is a powerful medial rotator.
Lateral rotation is performed by infraspinatus and teres minor (greater tuberosity).
Abduction is initiated by supraspinatus (greater tuberosity).
While subscapularis assists in adduction, medial rotation is its primary specific cuff action.
Extension is primarily performed by latissimus dorsi and teres major.
citation: The lesser tuberosity fracture... receives the insertion of the subscapularis tendon, a part of the rotator cuff.

statement: A medical student is asked why the shoulder joint is so much more prone to dislocation than the hip joint. Which anatomical feature of the scapula contributes most to this instability?
optionExplanations: The glenoid fossa is very shallow, providing little mechanical containment for the humeral head.
The coracoid process provides ligamentous attachment but not socket depth.
The acromion provides a roof but doesn't stabilize the socket itself.
The scapular spine is for muscle attachment and doesn't affect joint stability.
The inferior angle is far from the joint and has no role in stability.
citation: The shallow n e s s   o f   t h e   g l e n o i d   f o s s a   o f   t h e   s c a p u l a   a n d   t h e   l a c k   o f   s u p p o r t   p r o v i d e d   b y   w e a k   l i g a m e n t s   m a k e   t h i s   j o i n t   a n   u n s t a b l e   s t r u c t u r e .

statement: A 32-year-old professional baseball pitcher presents with chronic shoulder pain. Physical examination reveals that he experiences sharp pain specifically when his arm is between 60 and 120 degrees of abduction. What is the most likely clinical diagnosis?
optionExplanations: The painful arc syndrome is a classic sign of supraspinatus tendinitis where the tendon impinges on the acromion.
Teres major is not part of the rotator cuff and does not cause a painful arc.
Bicipital groove issues typically present with anterior shoulder pain during elbow flexion.
Serratus anterior paralysis causes winging of the scapula, not a painful arc.
Subclavius hypertrophy does not typically present with abduction-related shoulder pain.
citation: It is characterized by the presence of a spasm of pain in the middle range of abduction, when the diseased area impinges on the acromion.

statement: What is the primary factor responsible for the stability of the glenohumeral joint?
optionExplanations: Atmospheric pressure is a minor factor compared to muscle tone.
The glenohumeral ligaments are described as weak bands.
The glenoid fossa is shallow, which contributes to instability.
The tone of the rotator cuff muscles is the primary stabilizer of this joint.
The coracoclavicular ligament stabilizes the acromioclavicular joint, not the glenohumeral joint.
citation: The strength of the joint almost entirely depends on the tone of the short muscles that bind the upper end of the humerus to the scapula

statement: A 22-year-old rugby player is tackled while his arm is fully abducted and laterally rotated. He feels a sudden 'pop' and intense pain. In the ER, his shoulder has lost its rounded contour. What is the most likely mechanism of this injury?
options: Humeral head tilting onto inferior capsule
correctOption: Humeral head tilting onto inferior capsule
optionExplanations: A blow to the acromion usually causes acromioclavicular separation.
In full abduction, the humeral head is forced against the weak inferior capsule, leading to anterior-inferior dislocation.
Coracoid avulsion is rare and not the primary mechanism of dislocation.
Conoid ligament rupture is associated with shoulder separation (AC joint).
Anatomical neck fractures are rare and usually seen in the elderly, not as a result of this mechanism.
citation: Sudden violence applied to the humerus with the joint fully abducted tilts the humeral head downward onto the inferior weak part of the capsule, which tears, and the humeral head comes to lie inferior to the glenoid fossa.

statement: Following a subglenoid shoulder dislocation, a patient is unable to abduct his arm and has numbness over the lateral aspect of the upper arm. Which nerve was likely compressed in the quadrangular space?
optionExplanations: The radial nerve is in the spiral groove, not the quadrangular space.
The suprascapular nerve passes through the suprascapular notch.
The axillary nerve passes through the quadrangular space and is frequently injured in shoulder dislocations.
The musculocutaneous nerve pierces the coracobrachialis.
The long thoracic nerve descends on the superficial surface of the serratus anterior.
citation: A subglenoid displacement of the head of the humerus into the quadrangular space can cause damage to the axillary nerve, as indicated by paralysis of the deltoid muscle and loss of skin sensation over the lower half of the deltoid

statement: A 65-year-old man with a history of rotator cuff tendinitis suddenly feels a snap while lifting a heavy box. He can no longer start the movement of lifting his arm out to the side, but if someone lifts it slightly for him, he can complete the motion. Which structure is ruptured?
optionExplanations: Infraspinatus is primarily a lateral rotator.
Teres minor is primarily a lateral rotator.
The supraspinatus initiates the first 15 degrees of abduction; its rupture prevents the initiation of this movement.
Subscapularis is a medial rotator.
The long head of triceps supports the joint inferiorly but does not initiate abduction.
citation: The patient with a ruptured supraspinatus tendon is unable to initiate abduction of the arm. However, if the arm is passively assisted for the first 15° of abduction, the deltoid can then take over

statement: Which of the following muscles is NOT a member of the rotator cuff?
optionExplanations: Subscapularis is part of the rotator cuff.
Supraspinatus is part of the rotator cuff.
Infraspinatus is part of the rotator cuff.
Teres minor is part of the rotator cuff.
Teres major is not part of the rotator cuff as its tendon does not fuse with the joint capsule.
citation: The tendons of the subscapularis, supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and teres minor muscles are fused to the underlying capsule of the shoulder joint.

statement: A 45-year-old woman presents with sharp pain in her right shoulder. However, the shoulder joint has a full range of motion and no tenderness. She also mentions abdominal discomfort after fatty meals. Irritation of which nerve is likely referring pain to the shoulder?
optionExplanations: Axillary nerve irritation causes localized shoulder pain.
The phrenic nerve (C3-C5) shares roots with the supraclavicular nerves, referring pain from the diaphragm/gallbladder to the shoulder.
Suprascapular nerve supplies the joint directly.
Radial nerve supplies the posterior arm and forearm.
Intercostobrachial nerve supplies the axilla and medial arm skin.
citation: Pleurisy, peritonitis, subphrenic abscess, or gallbladder disease may therefore be responsible for shoulder pain.

statement: In which anatomical direction is the rotator cuff naturally deficient, making the shoulder joint most vulnerable to dislocation?
optionExplanations: The supraspinatus protects the joint superiorly.
The subscapularis protects the joint anteriorly.
The infraspinatus and teres minor protect the joint posteriorly.
The rotator cuff does not cover the inferior aspect of the joint capsule, leaving it weak.
The medial aspect is the glenoid cavity itself.
citation: The cuff is deficient inferiorly, and this is a site of potential weakness.

statement: Which structure primarily protects the supraspinatus tendon from friction against the acromion during abduction?
optionExplanations: The subscapular bursa is associated with the subscapularis muscle.
The glenoid labrum deepens the socket but doesn't reduce acromial friction.
The subacromial bursa acts as a cushion between the supraspinatus tendon and the acromion.
The coracohumeral ligament strengthens the capsule superiorly.
The transverse humeral ligament holds the biceps tendon in place.
citation: the amount of friction is reduced to a minimum by the large subacromial bursa, which extends laterally beneath the deltoid.

statement: Which two nerves provide the primary sensory innervation to the capsule and ligaments of the glenohumeral joint?
optionExplanations: Radial and ulnar nerves do not supply the shoulder joint capsule.
The axillary and suprascapular nerves are the main sensory supply to the joint structures.
Median and musculocutaneous nerves supply the arm and forearm.
Long thoracic and thoracodorsal nerves supply the serratus anterior and latissimus dorsi.
Phrenic refers pain but doesn't innervate the joint capsule directly.
citation: The axillary and suprascapular nerves innervate the synovial membrane, capsule, and ligaments of the shoulder joint.

statement: A 19-year-old athlete falls and violently contracts his shoulder muscles to break the fall. Radiographs show a small bone fragment pulled away from the lateral aspect of the proximal humerus. Which muscle most likely caused this avulsion fracture?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis major inserts into the lateral lip of the bicipital groove.
Subscapularis inserts into the lesser tuberosity.
The supraspinatus inserts into the greater tuberosity and can avulse it during violent contraction.
Biceps brachii (long head) originates from the supraglenoid tubercle.
Latissimus dorsi inserts into the floor of the bicipital groove.
citation: The greater tuberosity of the humerus can be fractured by direct trauma, displaced by the glenoid labrum during dislocation of the shoulder joint, or avulsed by violent contractions of the supraspinatus muscle.

statement: A patient is brought to the emergency department with a suspected shoulder dislocation. On inspection, the shoulder appears 'squared-off' rather than rounded. This loss of normal contour is due to the displacement of which structure?
optionExplanations: The coracoid process is medial and deep.
The lesser tuberosity is anterior and does not form the lateral bulge.
The greater tuberosity normally bulges laterally under the deltoid; its displacement causes the loss of the rounded shoulder contour.
The acromion becomes more prominent but is not the structure that is displaced.
The clavicle is not involved in the rounded contour of the deltoid region.
citation: the rounded appearance of the shoulder is seen to be lost because the greater tuberosity of the humerus is no longer bulging laterally beneath the deltoid muscle.

statement: A 50-year-old office clerk presents with shoulder pain and a painful arc during abduction. He denies any history of sports or trauma. Which of the following is true regarding his condition?
options: It is likely a nerve tumor
It can occur spontaneously with age
It is caused by a cervical rib
correctOption: It can occur spontaneously with age
optionExplanations: Fractures usually follow significant trauma.
Nerve tumors are a much less common cause of painful arcs.
Rotator cuff tendinitis can occur spontaneously, often related to age-related wear.
The text explicitly states many cases appear spontaneously, not just in athletes.
A cervical rib causes thoracic outlet syndrome, not typically a painful arc.
citation: Overused overhead movements of the upper limb, such as seen in baseball pitchers, tennis players, and swimmers, may be the cause of tendinitis, although many cases appear spontaneously.

statement: What is the function of the glenoid labrum in the shoulder joint?
optionExplanations: The synovial membrane secretes fluid.
The glenoid labrum is a fibrocartilaginous rim that deepens the shallow glenoid cavity to improve stability.
It does not specifically limit medial rotation.
The deltoid attaches to the humerus and scapular spine/acromion.
It has no protective relationship with the axillary nerve.
citation: the glenoid cavity is deepened by the presence of a fibrocartilaginous rim termed the glenoid labrum

statement: During a physical exam, a clinician tests the strength of a patient's internal rotation of the shoulder. This specifically evaluates which rotator cuff muscle's contribution to joint stability?
optionExplanations: Supraspinatus is an abductor.
Infraspinatus is a lateral rotator.
Teres minor is a lateral rotator.
Subscapularis is the only rotator cuff muscle that performs medial (internal) rotation.
Teres major is a medial rotator but is not part of the rotator cuff.
citation: The tone of these muscles assists in holding the head of the humerus in the glenoid cavity of the scapula during movements at the shoulder joint.

statement: Which characteristic of the shoulder joint capsule allows for its extensive mobility but contributes to its instability?
optionExplanations: A thick capsule would limit movement.
The capsule is fibrous, not primarily elastic.
The thin and lax nature of the capsule is essential for the wide range of motion but provides little structural support.
The capsule is fused with rotator cuff tendons, not the deltoid.
An ossified capsule would prevent all movement.
citation: The capsule is thin and lax, allowing a wide range of movement.

statement: In a classic anterior-inferior shoulder dislocation occurring during forced abduction, which bony structure acts as the fulcrum that levers the humeral head out of the glenoid cavity?
optionExplanations: The coracoid process is not the fulcrum in this mechanism.
As the arm is abducted, the humerus hits the acromion, which then acts as a fulcrum to lever the head downwards.
The labrum is often torn during the process but is not the fulcrum.
The greater tuberosity is part of the lever, not the fulcrum.
The clavicle is not involved in the lever mechanism of the humerus.
citation: During this movement, the acromion has acted as a fulcrum.

statement: A patient with acute shoulder joint inflammation presents with his arm held tightly against his chest and very limited movement. What is the physiological purpose of the muscle spasms observed in this patient?
optionExplanations: Spasms do not primarily aim to increase blood flow.
Spasms have nothing to do with nerve regeneration.
Reflex spasms occur to naturally immobilize the painful joint and prevent further irritation.
Spasms aim to stabilize, not cause dislocation.
Spasms actually shorten the muscles, not stretch the ligaments.
citation: The muscles surrounding the joint undergo reflex spasm in response to pain originating in the joint, which in turn serves to immobilize the joint and thus reduce the pain.

statement: What is the typical cause of the relatively rare posterior shoulder dislocation?
options: Direct violence to the front of the joint
correctOption: Direct violence to the front of the joint
optionExplanations: Forced abduction causes anterior-inferior dislocation.
Falling on an outstretched hand usually causes anterior dislocation or fractures.
Direct force applied to the anterior aspect of the shoulder can drive the humeral head posteriorly.
Triceps contraction does not typically cause posterior dislocation.
Swimming is associated with tendinitis, not acute posterior dislocation.
citation: Posterior dislocations are rare and are usually caused by direct violence to the front of the joint.

statement: A radiograph of a patient with a posterior shoulder dislocation reveals a fracture of the lesser tuberosity. Which rotator cuff muscle's function will be most compromised by this specific fracture?
optionExplanations: Supraspinatus inserts on the greater tuberosity.
Infraspinatus inserts on the greater tuberosity.
Teres minor inserts on the greater tuberosity.
The subscapularis is the only rotator cuff muscle that inserts onto the lesser tuberosity.
The deltoid inserts onto the deltoid tuberosity on the humeral shaft.
citation: Occasionally, a lesser tuberosity fracture accompanies posterior dislocation of the shoulder joint. The bone fragment receives the insertion of the subscapularis tendon

statement: A medical student is comparing the hip and shoulder joints. She notes that the shoulder is much more prone to dislocation. Which anatomical feature of the shoulder joint best explains this inherent instability?
options: The long head of the biceps
optionExplanations: The coracoacromial ligament provides superior protection, not instability.
The shallow glenoid fossa provides very little 'socket' for the humeral head, making it unstable.
The rotator cuff is the main stabilizer; its presence helps, not hinders.
The biceps tendon provides some stability but is not the cause of instability.
The capsule is thin and lax, which contributes to instability, but the shallow fossa is a primary factor.
citation: The shallowness of the glenoid fossa of the scapula and the lack of support provided by weak ligaments make this joint an unstable structure.

statement: A 55-year-old painter has been diagnosed with chronic subacromial bursitis. He is now developing signs of a rotator cuff tear. According to the typical progression of this condition, which structure is likely to undergo degenerative changes next?
optionExplanations: The acromion may show spurs but doesn't 'degenerate' like soft tissue.
Degeneration of the subacromial bursa often leads directly to degeneration of the supraspinatus tendon lying immediately beneath it.
The labrum is deeper in the joint and not directly affected by subacromial bursitis.
The coracoid process is not involved in this specific degenerative pathway.
Teres major is not part of the rotator cuff or the subacromial space.
citation: Degenerative changes in the bursa are followed by degenerative changes in the underlying supraspinatus tendon

coreConcept: Shoulder Joint Dislocation and Nerve Damage
statement: A patient with an inferior shoulder dislocation is found to have 'wrist drop' during the neurological exam. Which nerve was likely stretched during the downward displacement of the humerus?
optionExplanations: Axillary nerve damage causes deltoid paralysis, not wrist drop.
Median nerve damage affects thumb opposition and forearm pronation.
Ulnar nerve damage causes claw hand.
The radial nerve can be stretched in inferior dislocations, leading to paralysis of extensors and wrist drop.
Musculocutaneous nerve damage affects elbow flexion.
citation: Downward displacement of the humerus can also stretch and damage the radial nerve

statement: A 60-year-old woman has a small, chronic tear of her rotator cuff. She has moderate pain but can still perform daily activities. What is the recommended initial management for her condition?
optionExplanations: Mastectomy is for breast cancer.
Surgery is usually reserved for extensive acute tears.
Conservative management with anti-inflammatories and physical therapy is preferred for small chronic injuries.
Joint replacement is for advanced arthritis.
Complete immobilization would lead to 'frozen shoulder' and disuse atrophy.
citation: Small chronic cuff injuries are best managed without surgery using nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs and muscle exercises.

statement: Which muscle has a tendon that actually travels through the internal cavity of the shoulder joint?
optionExplanations: The short head of biceps is extracapsular.
The long head of triceps is extracapsular (though it supports the joint).
The long head of the biceps brachii is unique in that its tendon is intracapsular.
Pectoralis minor is not related to the joint cavity.
Coracobrachialis is extracapsular.
citation: The tendon of the long head of the biceps brachii muscle passes through the joint and emerges beneath the transverse humeral ligament

statement: In the formation of the brachial plexus, which spinal nerve root continues independently to form the middle trunk?
optionExplanations: C5 joins C6 to form the upper trunk.
C6 joins C5 to form the upper trunk.
The root of C7 continues as the middle trunk without joining other roots initially.
C8 joins T1 to form the lower trunk.
T1 joins C8 to form the lower trunk.
citation: The roots of C5 and 6 unite to form the upper trunk, the root of C7 continues as the middle trunk, and the roots of C8 and T1 unite to form the lower trunk.

statement: A 25-year-old man is brought to the ER after a motorcycle accident where he fell on his shoulder, causing excessive displacement of the head to the opposite side. Physical examination reveals the right upper limb hanging limply, medially rotated, and pronated. What is the most likely clinical description of this posture?
optionExplanations: Claw hand results from lower brachial plexus or ulnar nerve lesions.
Ape hand is characteristic of median nerve injury.
Wristdrop is caused by radial nerve injury.
Waiter's tip posture is the classic presentation of Erb-Duchenne palsy involving C5-C6 roots.
Winged scapula results from long thoracic nerve injury.
citation: The position of the upper limb in this condition has been likened to that of a porter or waiter hinting for a tip and so is often referred to as “waiter’s tip posture”

statement: Which of the following muscles is directly innervated by the musculocutaneous nerve?
optionExplanations: Triceps is supplied by the radial nerve.
Deltoid is supplied by the axillary nerve.
The coracobrachialis is one of the three muscles in the anterior compartment supplied by the musculocutaneous nerve.
Pectoralis minor is supplied by the medial pectoral nerve.
Serratus anterior is supplied by the long thoracic nerve.
citation: The musculocutaneous nerve is the motor nerve to the anterior compartment of the arm (i.e., to the coracobrachialis, biceps brachii, and brachialis muscles

statement: A construction worker falls from a scaffold and manages to catch a beam with one hand to break his fall. He later presents with weakness in the small muscles of the hand and a 'clawed' appearance. Which spinal nerve root was most likely torn during this traction injury?
optionExplanations: C5 is involved in upper plexus injuries.
C6 is involved in upper plexus injuries.
C7 forms the middle trunk and is rarely isolated in these mechanisms.
While C8 can be involved, T1 is the primary root torn in hyperabduction traction injuries.
T1 is the root usually torn in Klumpke's palsy, leading to loss of intrinsic hand muscle function.
citation: Lower lesions of the brachial plexus are usually traction injuries caused by excessive abduction of the arm, as occurs in the case of a person falling from a height clutching at an object to save himself or herself.

statement: Following a radical mastectomy, a patient notices that her scapula protrudes backward when she pushes against a wall. Damage to which nerve during surgery would explain this finding?
optionExplanations: Axillary nerve damage causes deltoid paralysis.
Suprascapular nerve damage affects supraspinatus/infraspinatus.
The long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior; its injury causes 'winged scapula'.
Thoracodorsal nerve damage affects the latissimus dorsi.
Musculocutaneous nerve damage affects elbow flexion.
citation: The vertebral border and inferior angle of the scapula will no longer be kept closely applied to the chest wall and will protrude posteriorly in a condition known as “winged scapula”

statement: Which components of the brachial plexus trunks unite to form the posterior cord?
options: Anterior divisions of upper and middle
Posterior divisions of upper and middle
optionExplanations: Anterior divisions form the lateral and medial cords.
The posterior cord is uniquely formed by the posterior divisions of the upper, middle, and lower trunks.
These form the lateral cord.
This continues as the medial cord.
The lower trunk's posterior division must also be included.
citation: the posterior divisions of all three trunks join to form the posterior cord.

statement: During an axillary dissection, a surgeon identifies a nerve originating from the lateral cord that pierces the coracobrachialis muscle. Which nerve is being identified?
optionExplanations: The median nerve does not pierce muscles in the arm.
The ulnar nerve passes medially and does not pierce the coracobrachialis.
The radial nerve passes posteriorly into the spiral groove.
The axillary nerve passes through the quadrangular space.
The musculocutaneous nerve is characterized by piercing the coracobrachialis muscle.
citation: The musculocutaneous nerve (C5 to 7) originates from the lateral cord of the brachial plexus in the axilla (see Figs. 3.51 and 3.54). It runs downward and laterally, pierces the coracobrachialis muscle

coreConcept: Anterior Arm Muscles: Biceps Brachii Action
statement: A man is using a manual screwdriver to drive a screw into a wooden plank with his right hand. Which muscle is primarily responsible for the powerful supination required for this task?
optionExplanations: Brachialis is a pure flexor of the elbow.
Coracobrachialis is a weak flexor/adductor of the shoulder.
The biceps brachii is the most powerful supinator of the forearm, especially against resistance.
Triceps is an extensor.
Anconeus is a small extensor.
citation: the biceps brachii is a powerful supinator, especially during supination against resistance.

statement: Which nerves provide cutaneous sensation to the skin over the point of the shoulder and the upper half of the deltoid muscle?
optionExplanations: The axillary nerve supplies the lower half of the deltoid skin.
The supraclavicular nerves (C3-C4) supply the shoulder point and upper deltoid region.
This supplies the axilla and medial arm.
This supplies the medial side of the arm.
This supplies the back of the arm.
citation: The supraclavicular nerves (C3, 4 from the cervical plexus) provide the sensory nerve supply to the skin over the point of the shoulder to halfway down the deltoid muscle.

statement: Which parts of the brachial plexus are considered supraclavicular and are located in the posterior triangle of the neck?
optionExplanations: Cords are infraclavicular (axillary).
Cords are infraclavicular.
Cords are infraclavicular.
Roots, trunks, and divisions are the supraclavicular components located in the neck.
Branches are mostly infraclavicular.
citation: The roots, trunks, and divisions of the brachial plexus reside in the upper portion, in the lower part of the posterior triangle of the neck.

statement: What is the terminal cutaneous branch of the musculocutaneous nerve?
options: Upper lateral cutaneous nerve of arm
Lower lateral cutaneous nerve of arm
optionExplanations: This arises from the medial cord.
This arises from the radial nerve.
The musculocutaneous nerve continues into the forearm as the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm.
This arises from the axillary nerve.
This arises from the radial nerve.
citation: It then runs down the lateral aspect of the forearm as the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm

statement: While the musculocutaneous nerve is the primary supply to the brachialis, which other nerve provides a small contribution to this muscle?
optionExplanations: The median nerve does not supply arm muscles.
The ulnar nerve does not supply arm muscles.
The radial nerve provides a small sensory/motor branch to the lateral part of the brachialis.
The axillary nerve supplies deltoid and teres minor.
The long thoracic nerve supplies serratus anterior.
citation: the brachialis muscle is greatly weakened (the latter muscle is also supplied by the radial nerve).

statement: A newborn presents with an upper brachial plexus injury (C5-C6) following a difficult delivery. The infant's forearm is fixed in a pronated position. The loss of which muscle's action primarily accounts for this specific forearm posture?
optionExplanations: Brachialis is a flexor, not a supinator.
Coracobrachialis acts on the shoulder.
Loss of the biceps brachii (a powerful supinator) leads to unopposed pronation.
While supinator is affected, the text emphasizes the loss of biceps action.
Pronator teres is intact (C6-C7) and causes the pronation.
citation: the forearm will be pronated because of loss of the action of the biceps.

statement: Which of the following nerves is a direct branch of the lateral cord of the brachial plexus?
optionExplanations: Ulnar nerve comes from the medial cord.
Axillary nerve comes from the posterior cord.
Radial nerve comes from the posterior cord.
The musculocutaneous nerve is a terminal branch of the lateral cord.
Thoracodorsal nerve comes from the posterior cord.
citation: The lateral cord gives rise to the musculocutaneous nerve and contributes to the formation of the median nerve.

statement: Which nerve, originating from the second intercostal nerve, provides cutaneous innervation to the axilla and medial arm?
optionExplanations: This comes from the medial cord (T1).
This comes from the medial cord (C8-T1).
The intercostobrachial nerve is the lateral cutaneous branch of T2.
This is a motor nerve.
This is a motor nerve.
citation: the intercostobrachial nerve (a branch of the T2 intercostal nerve) supply the skin of the armpit and the medial side of the arm.

statement: In the axilla, what is the anatomical relationship of the medial cord to the second part of the axillary artery?
optionExplanations: The lateral cord is lateral.
The medial cord is located on the medial side of the second part of the artery.
No cord is consistently superior to the second part.
The pectoralis minor is anterior.
The posterior cord is posterior.
citation: The medial cord crosses behind the artery to reach the medial side of the second part of the artery

statement: Where does the tendon of the biceps brachii muscle insert?
optionExplanations: This is an origin site for the short head.
This is the insertion for the triceps.
This is the insertion for the brachialis.
The biceps brachii inserts into the radial tuberosity.
This is the insertion for the deltoid.
citation: The radial tuberosity is the raised, mostly roughened area on the anteromedial, proximal aspect of the radius, just distal to the neck. This is the insertion site of the biceps brachii muscle.

statement: A patient presents with a fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus. On examination, there is a small patch of anesthesia over the lower part of the deltoid muscle. Which nerve is most likely injured?
optionExplanations: Radial nerve supplies the back of the arm.
Median nerve supplies the palm.
The axillary nerve supplies the skin over the lower half of the deltoid via the upper lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm.
Musculocutaneous nerve supplies the lateral forearm.
Suprascapular nerve is primarily motor and articular.
citation: consequently, there is a loss of skin sensation over the lower half of the deltoid muscle

statement: From which part of the brachial plexus does the suprascapular nerve originate?
optionExplanations: Only dorsal scapular and long thoracic arise from roots.
The suprascapular nerve is a branch of the upper trunk.
The middle trunk has no branches.
The lateral cord gives lateral pectoral, musculocutaneous, and median root.
The posterior cord gives subscapular, thoracodorsal, axillary, and radial.
citation: The suprascapular nerve (C5 to 6) arises in the posterior triangle in the neck and runs downward and laterally to reach the upper edge of the scapula.

statement: What are the primary actions of the coracobrachialis muscle?
optionExplanations: It is a flexor, not an extensor.
It is an adductor, not an abductor.
The coracobrachialis flexes and adducts the arm at the shoulder.
It is a flexor, not an extensor.
It has minimal effect on rotation.
citation: Coracobrachialis... Flexes and adducts arm at shoulder joint

statement: Which nerve is formed by contributions from both the lateral and medial cords?
optionExplanations: Ulnar is medial cord only.
Radial is posterior cord only.
The median nerve is formed by the union of the lateral root (lateral cord) and medial root (medial cord).
Axillary is posterior cord only.
Musculocutaneous is lateral cord only.

statement: After piercing the coracobrachialis, the musculocutaneous nerve travels in the plane between which two muscles?
optionExplanations: Triceps is in the posterior compartment.
It has already left the coracobrachialis.
The nerve runs in the interval between the biceps (superficial) and brachialis (deep).
Deltoid is not in the anterior compartment of the arm.
Pectoralis major is in the pectoral region.
citation: passes downward between the biceps and brachialis muscles

statement: A patient with a confirmed C5-C6 root avulsion (Erb's Palsy) is being mapped for sensory deficits. In which area is sensation most likely to be absent?
optionExplanations: Medial arm is T1-T2.

Axilla is T2.

statement: Which two major terminal nerves are the final divisions of the posterior cord?
optionExplanations: Median/Ulnar are from lateral/medial cords.
Musculocutaneous is from the lateral cord.
The posterior cord terminates by dividing into the axillary and radial nerves.
Ulnar is from the medial cord.
Musculocutaneous is from the lateral cord.
citation: The posterior cord divides into the axillary and radial nerves.

statement: What is the primary functional role of the brachialis muscle?
optionExplanations: Biceps is the supinator.
Pronators are in the forearm.
The brachialis is a powerful and pure flexor of the elbow joint.
Triceps is the extensor.
Deltoid/Supraspinatus are abductors.

statement: A 24-year-old motorcyclist is brought to the ER after a high-speed collision. Physical examination reveals that his right arm hangs limply by his side, is medially rotated, and the forearm is pronated. The mechanism of injury involved excessive displacement of the head to the opposite side and depression of the shoulder on the same side. Which spinal nerve roots are most likely torn?
optionExplanations: This mechanism causes traction on the upper roots (C5-C6) of the brachial plexus.

C8 and T1 involvement results in Klumpke's palsy, not Erb's.

T1 and T2 are not part of the upper lesion mechanism.

statement: A construction worker was admitted after a person falling from a height clutching at an object to save himself or herself. He now presents with a 'clawed' appearance of the hand and loss of sensation along the medial side of the arm. Which component of the brachial plexus was most likely injured?
optionExplanations: Upper trunk injury leads to Erb's palsy, not claw hand.
Middle trunk injury is rare and presents differently.
Excessive abduction of the arm typically tears the T1 root, leading to Klumpke's palsy.
Lateral cord injury would affect the musculocutaneous and median nerves.
Posterior cord injury would cause wrist drop, not a claw hand.

statement: In the formation of the brachial plexus, which of the following describes the origin of the lateral cord?
options: Anterior division of the lower trunk
Anterior divisions of upper and middle trunks
Union of C5 and C6 roots
correctOption: Anterior divisions of upper and middle trunks
optionExplanations: Posterior divisions form the posterior cord.
The anterior division of the lower trunk forms the medial cord.
The lateral cord is formed by the anterior divisions of the upper and middle trunks.
C5 and C6 roots form the upper trunk.
The middle trunk continues as divisions, not directly as a cord.
citation: anterior divisions of the upper and middle trunks unite to form the lateral cord

statement: During a surgical exploration of the axilla, a surgeon identifies a nerve that supplies the coracobrachialis and leaves the axilla by piercing that muscle. If this nerve is accidentally transected at this point, which movement will be most significantly weakened?
optionExplanations: Shoulder abduction is primarily axillary and suprascapular nerves.
Elbow extension is the radial nerve.
The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the biceps, a powerful supinator.

Thumb adduction is the ulnar nerve.

statement: A physician is performing a neurological exam on a patient with suspected cervical disc herniation. He elicits flexion of the elbow joint by tapping the biceps tendon. Which spinal segments is he primarily testing?
optionExplanations: C3-C4 supply the neck and diaphragm.

C6-C7 is more associated with the brachioradialis reflex.
C7-C8 is associated with the triceps reflex.
C8-T1 supply the intrinsic hand muscles.
citation: flexion of the elbow joint by tapping the biceps tendon

statement: What is the anatomical origin of the musculocutaneous nerve?
optionExplanations: The medial cord gives rise to the ulnar nerve.
The posterior cord gives rise to the radial and axillary nerves.
The musculocutaneous nerve originates from the lateral cord.
Trunks divide into divisions before forming cords.
The middle trunk contributes to the lateral and posterior cords via divisions.

statement: An elderly woman falls and sustains a fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus. On examination, she has difficulty abducting her arm. Which sensory finding would confirm an injury to the nerve related to this fracture site?

Loss of sensation over the little finger
correctOption: Loss of skin sensation over the lower half of the deltoid muscle
optionExplanations: Medial arm numbness suggests medial cutaneous nerve of arm injury.
Thumb sensation is median/radial nerve territory.
The axillary nerve supplies the skin over the lower deltoid.
Posterior forearm sensation is the radial nerve.
Little finger sensation is the ulnar nerve.
citation: loss of skin sensation over the lower half of the deltoid muscle

statement: A patient is found unconscious with his arm draped over the back of a hard chair for several hours. Upon waking, he cannot extend his wrist or fingers. optionExplanations: Axillary compression (crutch palsy) would also affect the triceps.
Compression in the radial groove (Saturday night palsy) spares the triceps but causes wrist drop.
Lateral epicondyle injury usually affects the deep branch only.
Cubital fossa injury would not cause full wrist drop.
Carpal tunnel affects the median nerve, not radial.
citation: Wristdrop (flexion of the wrist; Fig. 3.63) occurs as a result of the action of the unopposed flexor muscles

statement: Which components of the brachial plexus are considered part of its supraclavicular portion?
optionExplanations: Cords and branches are infraclavicular.
The roots, trunks, and divisions are located above the clavicle.
Cords are infraclavicular.
Cords are infraclavicular.
Cords are infraclavicular.
citation: roots, trunks, and divisions of the brachial plexus reside in the upper portion

statement: Following a radical mastectomy, a patient notices that her scapula protrudes backward when she pushes against a wall. She also demonstrates an inability to rotate the scapula during abduction of the arm above a right angle. Which nerve was likely damaged during the surgery?

Suprascapular nerve supplies supraspinatus/infraspinatus.

statement: A patient presents with localized paresthesia after a superficial laceration to the medial aspect of the mid-arm. The nerve involved originates from the medial cord and supplies the skin on the medial side of the arm. Which nerve is this?

The medial cutaneous nerve of the arm provides this specific sensory supply.
The medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm supplies the forearm skin.
The intercostobrachial nerve supplies the armpit and upper medial arm.
The radial nerve supplies the posterior arm.

statement: Which muscle of the anterior compartment of the arm receives a small part of its nerve supply from the radial nerve in addition to its primary supply?

The brachialis is dually supplied by the musculocutaneous and radial nerves.
Triceps is in the posterior compartment.

statement: A carpenter complains of weakness when trying to drive a screw into wood using a manual screwdriver with his right hand. He has no trouble with elbow flexion. The biceps brachii is a powerful supinator, especially during supination against resistance. Which nerve is likely affected?

The median nerve does not supply supinators.
The ulnar nerve does not supply supinators.
The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the biceps brachii.
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid.
citation: biceps brachii is a powerful supinator, especially during supination against resistance

statement: A surgeon is operating in the axilla and identifies a muscle that is pierced by a major nerve. The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the coracobrachialis muscle and leaves the axilla by piercing that muscle. What is the primary action of this muscle?
optionExplanations: Elbow flexion is biceps/brachialis.
Supination is biceps/supinator.
Coracobrachialis flexes and adducts the shoulder.
Abduction is deltoid/supraspinatus.
Extension is triceps.

statement: Which of the following correctly describes the formation of the posterior cord of the brachial plexus?
options: Union of C5 and C6 roots

Anterior divisions of all three trunks
Union of the middle and lower trunks

optionExplanations: Roots form trunks, not cords directly.
The lower trunk anterior division forms the medial cord.
The posterior cord is formed by the posterior divisions of all three trunks.
Anterior divisions form the lateral and medial cords.
Trunks must divide into divisions before forming cords.

statement: A newborn presents with the right arm adducted and medially rotated following a difficult breech delivery. The axillary nerve supplies the lateral shoulder.

Clavipectoral fascia is anterior to the sheath.
Axillary fascia forms the floor of the axilla.

statement: From which part of the brachial plexus does the nerve to the subclavius arise?

statement: A patient undergoing axillary lymph node dissection for breast cancer reports numbness in her armpit post-operatively. The intercostobrachial nerve (a branch of the T2 intercostal nerve) supply the skin of the armpit. This nerve is unique because it does not arise from which structure?

optionExplanations: Median and ulnar are from medial/lateral cords.

Musculocutaneous is from the lateral cord.

Musculocutaneous is from the lateral cord.

The pectoralis major does not attach to the coracoid.

statement: A newborn presents with a right upper limb that hangs limply by the side, medially rotated and pronated, following a difficult breech delivery. Which of the following best describes this clinical appearance?

statement: A 25-year-old man falls from a tree and catches a branch with one hand to break his fall. He later presents with wasting of all small muscles of the hand. What is the most likely anatomical description of his hand deformity?

statement: Which components of the brachial plexus unite to form the lateral cord?

correctOption: Anterior divisions of upper and middle
optionExplanations: Posterior divisions form the posterior cord.
The lateral cord is formed by the union of the anterior divisions of the upper and middle trunks.
The anterior division of the lower trunk forms the medial cord.
Roots of C5 and C6 form the upper trunk.

statement: A 70-year-old man with chronic shoulder pain suddenly feels a 'snap' while lifting a heavy box, followed by a prominent bulge in the distal anterior arm. Which anatomical mechanism likely caused this?

statement: Which muscle is typically pierced by the musculocutaneous nerve as it leaves the axilla?

statement: A patient is brought to the ER after a fall on their shoulder. Radiographs show a fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus. Which nerve is most at risk?

statement: Which parts of the brachial plexus are considered supraclavicular and located in the posterior triangle of the neck?
optionExplanations: Cords are infraclavicular.
The roots, trunks, and divisions are located above the clavicle in the neck.
Cords are located in the axilla.
Cords are infraclavicular.
Cords are infraclavicular.
citation: The roots, trunks, and divisions of the brachial plexus reside in the upper portion, in the lower part of the posterior triangle of the neck.

statement: A 10-year-old boy suffers a supracondylar fracture of the humerus. On examination, he cannot flex the distal phalanges of his index finger and his thumb. Which nerve was likely injured?

statement: A patient experiencing a myocardial infarction reports referred pain radiating to the axilla and the medial aspect of the left arm. Which nerve carries this sensation from the arm?

Direct axillary blows usually affect cords.

statement: Which nerve arises from the posterior cord to innervate the latissimus dorsi?

coreConcept: Radial Nerve Injury and Wrist Drop
statement: A patient who fell asleep with his arm over the back of a chair ('Saturday night palsy') presents with an inability to extend his wrist. What is the clinical name for this deformity?

statement: Which spinal nerve roots unite to form the lower trunk of the brachial plexus?

statement: A patient presents with a deep laceration to the anterior wrist. Physical exam reveals a loss of thumb opposition and a noticeably flattened area at the base of the thumb. Which nerve was severed?

statement: What is the origin of the medial cutaneous nerve of the arm?
optionExplanations: Lateral cord gives the musculocutaneous.

statement: A patient is unable to reach for an object on a high shelf despite having a functional deltoid muscle. Examination shows the scapula fails to rotate upward. Which muscle is paralyzed?

statement: From which specific part of the brachial plexus does the suprascapular nerve arise?
optionExplanations: Roots give the long thoracic nerve.

The lateral cord gives the lateral pectoral nerve.
The posterior cord gives the subscapular nerves.

Dorsum of hand is primarily radial/ulnar.
Axilla is T2.
citation: In addition, sensation will be lost over the lower half of the deltoid and down the lateral side of the forearm.

statement: A patient presents with significant weakness in elbow flexion and loss of sensation on the lateral forearm after a stab wound to the axilla. Which nerve is most likely damaged?
optionExplanations: Median nerve does not supply arm muscles.
Ulnar nerve does not supply arm muscles.
Radial nerve supplies the posterior compartment.
The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the anterior arm muscles and lateral forearm skin.
Axillary nerve supplies the deltoid.
citation: The musculocutaneous nerve is the motor nerve to the anterior compartment of the arm

statement: A newborn presents with the right arm hanging limply by the side, medially rotated, and the forearm pronated following a difficult delivery. The physician suspects traction injury to the part of the brachial plexus where specific roots unite. Which structure is primarily damaged?
optionExplanations: The upper trunk is formed by the union of C5 and C6 roots; its injury leads to Erb-Duchenne palsy.
The middle trunk is the continuation of the C7 root.
The lower trunk is formed by the union of C8 and T1 roots.
The lateral cord is formed by the anterior divisions of the upper and middle trunks.
The posterior cord is formed by the posterior divisions of all three trunks.
citation: The roots of C5 and 6 unite to form the upper trunk

statement: A 24-year-old man falls from a tree and clutches a branch to save himself. He later presents with significant wasting of the small muscles of the hand and a specific deformity. Which of the following best describes the resulting clinical appearance?

Ape hand results from median nerve injury.
Klumpke's palsy (T1 injury) causes a clawed hand due to loss of intrinsic muscle function.

Winged scapula results from long thoracic nerve injury.
citation: The hand has a clawed appearance caused by hyperextension of the metacarpophalangeal joints and flexion of the interphalangeal joints.

statement: Following a radical mastectomy, a patient notices that her scapula protrudes posteriorly when she pushes against a wall. The nerve responsible for this condition arises from which part of the brachial plexus?
optionExplanations: The long thoracic nerve arises directly from the C5, C6, and C7 roots.
Trunks give rise to the suprascapular nerve and nerve to subclavius.
The divisions of the brachial plexus give off no branches.
Cords give rise to branches like the pectoral and subscapular nerves.
Terminal branches include the median, ulnar, and radial nerves.
citation: The long thoracic nerve (C5 to 7) arises from the roots of the brachial plexus in the neck

statement: Which components of the brachial plexus unite to form the lateral cord?
optionExplanations: The lateral cord is formed by the anterior divisions of the upper and middle trunks.
The posterior cord is formed by the posterior divisions of all three trunks.
The medial cord is the continuation of the anterior division of the lower trunk.
The upper trunk is formed by C5 and C6 roots.
The lower trunk is formed by C8 and T1 roots.
citation: The anterior divisions of the upper and middle trunks unite to form the lateral cord

statement: A surgeon is exploring the axilla and identifies a nerve that pierces the coracobrachialis muscle. If this nerve is accidentally transected at this point, which function will be most severely impaired?

Shoulder abduction is primarily axillary and suprascapular nerve function.

statement: A carpenter complains of weakness when using a manual screwdriver with his right hand. Which muscle is the primary power source for this specific movement?

The biceps brachii is the most powerful supinator, especially against resistance.

statement: From which part of the brachial plexus does the musculocutaneous nerve originate?

The medial cord gives rise to the ulnar nerve.
The posterior cord gives rise to the radial and axillary nerves.

statement: An elderly woman falls and sustains a fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus. She later exhibits wasting of the deltoid muscle. The injured nerve originates from which cord of the brachial plexus?
optionExplanations: The lateral cord gives rise to the musculocutaneous nerve.
The medial cord gives rise to the ulnar nerve.
The axillary nerve is a terminal branch of the posterior cord.
There is no 'anterior cord' in the brachial plexus.
There is no 'superior cord' in the brachial plexus.
citation: The axillary nerve (C5 to 6) arises from the termination of the posterior cord of the brachial plexus in the axilla

statement: A patient presents with 'wrist drop' after sleeping with his arm over the back of a chair. The nerve affected in this 'Saturday night palsy' carries fibers from which spinal segments?

statement: A patient with coronary artery disease experiences referred pain to the medial aspect of the left upper arm during exercise. Which nerves are responsible for carrying this sensation from the skin of that region?

The lateral cutaneous nerve supplies the lateral aspect of the arm.
The posterior cutaneous nerve supplies the back of the arm.
Supraclavicular nerves supply the skin over the shoulder.
The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the lateral forearm skin.
citation: The medial cutaneous nerve of the arm (T1) and the intercostobrachial nerve (a branch of the T2 intercostal nerve) supply the skin of the armpit and the medial side of the arm.

statement: Which nerve provides the motor supply to the muscles of the anterior compartment of the arm?
optionExplanations: The median nerve supplies most anterior forearm muscles.
The ulnar nerve supplies 1.5 muscles in the forearm and most hand muscles.
The radial nerve supplies the posterior compartment of the arm.
The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the biceps, brachialis, and coracobrachialis.
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid and teres minor.

statement: A 5-year-old boy falls from a swing and lands on his shoulder, causing a wide separation between his neck and shoulder. He now presents with his arm adducted and medially rotated. What is the common clinical name for this posture?

statement: Which spinal nerve anterior rami constitute the roots of the brachial plexus?
optionExplanations: C3-C4 contribute to the cervical plexus.

T1-T5 are thoracic nerves not part of the brachial plexus roots.
citation: The roots are the anterior rami of spinal nerves C5 to T1.

statement: Which components of the brachial plexus are located in the supraclavicular region (posterior triangle of the neck)?
optionExplanations: The roots, trunks, and divisions are supraclavicular.
Cords and terminal branches are infraclavicular (axillary).

Trunks are only one part of the supraclavicular portion.

statement: A weightlifter experiences a sudden 'pop' in his shoulder while performing curls. He is still able to flex his elbow, but it is significantly weaker. Which muscle is likely ruptured?

statement: A patient has a complete lesion of the musculocutaneous nerve in the axilla. Surprisingly, he can still perform weak elbow flexion. This is possible because a small part of the brachialis muscle receives innervation from which other nerve?

The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid.
The long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior.

statement: A patient presents with numbness on the lateral aspect of the forearm following a deep laceration just above the elbow. Which nerve is the parent trunk of the affected cutaneous branch?

The musculocutaneous nerve terminates as the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm.
The axillary nerve supplies the skin over the deltoid.

statement: How is the posterior cord of the brachial plexus formed?

Union of upper and middle trunks

C8 and T1 roots form the lower trunk.

statement: During an axillary nerve block, the clinician uses the pulsations of a major vessel as a landmark. The cords of the brachial plexus are named based on their relationship to which part of this vessel?
optionExplanations: The cords are lateral to the first part.

statement: A patient presents with a 'dropped shoulder' and an inability to retract the scapula. If the rhomboid muscles are paralyzed, the lesion is likely at which specific part of the brachial plexus?

statement: A newborn presents with an upper limb that hangs limply by the side, medially rotated and pronated, following a difficult delivery. The examiner notes the injury likely resulted from which mechanism?

Wrist strain does not cause elbow extension weakness.
citation: pressure of the upper end of a badly fitting crutch pressing up into the armpit and compressing the nerve

statement: A newborn presents with an upper limb that hangs limply by the side, medially rotated and pronated following a difficult breech delivery. The examiner notes the 'waiter’s tip posture'. Which spinal nerve roots are most likely involved in this injury?
optionExplanations: Upper lesions of the brachial plexus (Erb-Duchenne palsy) typically involve the C5 and C6 roots.
C8 and T1 involvement characterizes Klumpke’s palsy, not Erb-Duchenne.
C7 root injury alone would not produce the classic waiter's tip deformity.
T1 and T2 are not the primary roots for the upper brachial plexus.
While C6 is involved, C5 is the other primary root affected in this traction injury.
citation: The position of the upper limb in this condition has been likened to that of a porter or waiter hinting for a tip and so is often referred to as “waiter’s tip posture”

statement: A 25-year-old man falls from a tree and clutches a branch to save himself. He later presents with a 'clawed appearance' of the hand. Which specific spinal segment was most likely torn during this excessive abduction of the arm?
optionExplanations: C5 is involved in upper plexus injuries, not lower.
C6 is involved in upper plexus injuries.
C7 forms the middle trunk and is rarely the sole root involved in claw hand.
While C8 contributes to the lower trunk, T1 is the root usually torn in this mechanism.
Lower lesions (Klumpke’s palsy) usually involve the T1 anterior ramus being torn during forced abduction.
citation: The hand has a clawed appearance caused by hyperextension of the metacarpophalangeal joints and flexion of the interphalangeal joints.

statement: Following a radical mastectomy, a patient notices that her scapula protrudes posteriorly when she pushes against a wall. Physical examination confirms a 'winged scapula'. Which muscle is paralyzed in this patient?
optionExplanations: Pectoralis major adducts and medially rotates the arm; it does not prevent winging.
The serratus anterior, supplied by the long thoracic nerve, keeps the scapula applied to the chest wall.
Latissimus dorsi is supplied by the thoracodorsal nerve and does not cause winging.
Rhomboids are supplied by the dorsal scapular nerve and retract the scapula.
Trapezius paralysis causes a dropped shoulder, not classic winging.
citation: The vertebral border and inferior angle of the scapula will no longer be kept closely applied to the chest wall and will protrude posteriorly in a condition known as “winged scapula”

statement: Which muscle does the musculocutaneous nerve typically pierce to exit the axilla and enter the arm?
optionExplanations: The nerve passes between the biceps and brachialis but does not pierce the biceps to exit the axilla.
The nerve lies anterior to the brachialis but does not pierce it to leave the axilla.
The musculocutaneous nerve characteristically pierces the coracobrachialis muscle.
The pectoralis minor is a landmark for the axillary artery, not pierced by this nerve.
The deltoid is supplied by the axillary nerve.
citation: The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the coracobrachialis muscle and leaves the axilla by piercing that muscle

statement: The lateral, medial, and posterior cords of the brachial plexus are named based on their relationship to which structure?
optionExplanations: The cords lie lateral and superior to the first part, but are not named by it.
The cords are named (lateral, medial, posterior) relative to the second part of the axillary artery.
The cords have already begun to branch by the third part of the artery.
The clavicle divides the plexus into supra- and infraclavicular parts, not the cords.
The pectoralis minor overlies the second part, but the artery is the reference point.
citation: the cords of the plexus are named according to their positional relationship to the second part of the axillary artery.

statement: A 40-year-old woman suffers a fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus. On examination, she has lost sensation over the lower half of her deltoid muscle. Which nerve is most likely damaged?
optionExplanations: The radial nerve supplies the posterior cutaneous sensation of the arm.
The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the lateral forearm, not the deltoid region.
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid muscle and the skin over its lower half.
The suprascapular nerve supplies the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles.
The median nerve has no cutaneous distribution in the arm.
citation: The cutaneous branches of the axillary nerve, including the upper lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm, are functionless, and consequently, there is a loss of skin sensation over the lower half of the deltoid muscle.

statement: A carpenter is using a manual screwdriver with his right hand to drive a screw into hard wood. Which muscle is providing the primary power for this supination movement?
optionExplanations: Brachialis is a pure flexor of the elbow.
Coracobrachialis flexes and adducts the shoulder.
The biceps brachii is the most powerful supinator, especially against resistance.
The supinator muscle acts during slow, unresisted supination.
The brachioradialis can assist in returning the forearm to midprone position.
citation: the biceps brachii is a powerful supinator, especially during supination against resistance.

statement: A patient presents with a deep laceration in the axilla. Physical examination reveals a total loss of elbow flexion and a loss of sensation on the lateral aspect of the forearm. Which nerve has been severed?
optionExplanations: Radial nerve injury would cause wrist drop and loss of elbow extension.
Median nerve injury affects forearm flexors and hand intrinsics.
Ulnar nerve injury affects hand intrinsics and medial forearm flexors.
Axillary nerve injury affects shoulder abduction.
The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the anterior arm flexors and the lateral forearm skin.
citation: The musculocutaneous nerve is the motor nerve to the anterior compartment of the arm (i.e., to the coracobrachialis, biceps brachii, and brachialis muscles)

statement: Which spinal nerve segments constitute the roots of the brachial plexus?
optionExplanations: C4 is a frequent contributor but not a standard root segment.
The brachial plexus is formed by the anterior rami of C5, C6, C7, C8, and T1.
T2 is not a standard root of the brachial plexus.
This range is incomplete; it excludes C8 and T1.
C1 to C4 form the cervical plexus.
citation: The roots are the anterior rami of spinal nerves C5 to T1.

statement: Where does the tendon of the biceps brachii muscle insert?
optionExplanations: The brachialis muscle inserts on the coronoid process/ulnar tuberosity.
The triceps brachii inserts on the olecranon process.
The biceps brachii inserts into the radial tuberosity.
No major arm muscle inserts on the radial styloid process.
The deltoid muscle inserts on the deltoid tuberosity of the humerus.
citation: The radial tuberosity is the raised, mostly roughened area on the anteromedial, proximal aspect of the radius, just distal to the neck. This is the insertion site of the biceps brachii muscle.

statement: During surgery to remove axillary lymph nodes, a surgeon accidentally damages a nerve arising from the posterior cord. Postoperatively, the patient has difficulty extending, adducting, and medially rotating the humerus. Which nerve was injured?
optionExplanations: Axillary nerve injury primarily affects abduction.
Radial nerve injury affects elbow and wrist extension.
The thoracodorsal nerve supplies the latissimus dorsi, which extends, adducts, and medially rotates the arm.
Long thoracic nerve injury causes winged scapula.
Lower subscapular nerve supplies the subscapularis and teres major.
citation: The thoracodorsal nerve arises from the posterior cord of the brachial plexus and runs downward into the axilla to supply the latissimus dorsi muscle

statement: A patient with a medial cord lesion of the brachial plexus is likely to experience sensory loss in which specific region of the arm?
optionExplanations: The lateral side is supplied by the axillary and radial nerves.
The posterior surface is supplied by the radial nerve.
The medial side of the arm is supplied by the medial cutaneous nerve of the arm (from the medial cord).
The point of the shoulder is supplied by supraclavicular nerves (C3,4).
The lower deltoid is supplied by the axillary nerve.
citation: The medial cutaneous nerve of the arm (T1) arises from the medial cord of the brachial plexus and is joined by the intercostobrachial nerve (lateral cutaneous branch of the second intercostal nerve). It supplies the skin on the medial side of the arm.

statement: A patient has a complete lesion of the musculocutaneous nerve in the axilla. While elbow flexion is severely weakened, it is not completely lost. Which anatomical fact explains the remaining flexion strength?
optionExplanations: The biceps is supplied only by the musculocutaneous nerve.
The brachialis is primarily supplied by the musculocutaneous nerve but also receives a small branch from the radial nerve.
The coracobrachialis only acts on the shoulder joint.
The ulnar nerve does not supply muscles in the arm.
The median nerve does not supply muscles in the arm.
citation: the brachialis muscle is also supplied by the radial nerve

statement: Which components of the brachial plexus are located in the supraclavicular part (posterior triangle of the neck)?
optionExplanations: Cords and branches are infraclavicular (in the axilla).
The roots, trunks, and divisions are supraclavicular.
Cords are infraclavicular.
Branches are infraclavicular.
Cords are infraclavicular.
citation: The roots, trunks, and divisions of the brachial plexus reside in the upper portion, in the lower part of the posterior triangle of the neck.

statement: A physician performs a deep tendon reflex test by tapping the tendon in the cubital fossa, resulting in elbow flexion. Which spinal segments are being evaluated?
optionExplanations: C3 and C4 supply the supraclavicular nerves and diaphragm.
The biceps reflex specifically tests the C5 and C6 spinal segments.
C7 and C8 are primarily tested by the triceps reflex.
C8 and T1 are involved in intrinsic hand muscle function.
T1 and T2 supply the medial arm and axilla skin.
citation: Biceps brachii tendon reflex: C5 and 6 (flexion of the elbow joint by tapping the biceps tendon)

statement: A patient undergoing axillary node dissection for breast cancer complains of numbness in the armpit and the upper medial aspect of the arm. Which nerve was most likely sacrificed?
optionExplanations: Long thoracic nerve injury causes motor loss (winging), not armpit numbness.
Thoracodorsal nerve injury causes motor loss (weak adduction).
The intercostobrachial nerve (T2) provides sensation to the axilla and medial arm.
Axillary nerve supplies the lateral shoulder.
Radial nerve supplies the posterior arm.
citation: The medial cutaneous nerve of the arm (T1) and the intercostobrachial nerve (a branch of the T2 intercostal nerve) supply the skin of the armpit and the medial side of the arm.

statement: A professional volleyball player presents with weakness in initiating abduction and lateral rotation of the shoulder. An MRI shows nerve entrapment at the suprascapular notch. Which two muscles are affected?
optionExplanations: Deltoid and teres minor are supplied by the axillary nerve.
The suprascapular nerve supplies both the supraspinatus and infraspinatus.
Subscapularis and teres major are supplied by subscapular nerves.
Pectoral muscles are supplied by pectoral nerves.
Trapezius is supplied by CN XI; rhomboids by the dorsal scapular nerve.
citation: It supplies the supraspinatus muscle and then continues through the spinoglenoid notch into the infraspinous fossa where it innervates the infraspinatus muscle.

statement: From which cord of the brachial plexus does the musculocutaneous nerve originate?
optionExplanations: The medial cord gives rise to the ulnar nerve.
The posterior cord gives rise to the radial and axillary nerves.
The musculocutaneous nerve is a terminal branch of the lateral cord.
Trunks do not directly give rise to terminal branches like the musculocutaneous.
The middle trunk continues as divisions, not terminal nerves.
citation: The musculocutaneous nerve (C5 to 7) originates from the lateral cord of the brachial plexus in the axilla

statement: A patient with gallbladder disease complains of 'referred pain' at the very top (point) of his right shoulder. Which nerves are carrying this sensation to the spinal cord?
optionExplanations: Axillary nerves supply the lower deltoid region.
Supraclavicular nerves (C3,4) supply the skin over the point of the shoulder.
Intercostobrachial nerves supply the axilla.
Medial cutaneous nerves supply the medial arm.
Radial nerves supply the posterior arm.
citation: The supraclavicular nerves (C3, 4 from the cervical plexus) provide the sensory nerve supply to the skin over the point of the shoulder to halfway down the deltoid muscle.

statement: To perform a regional nerve block for hand surgery, an anesthesiologist injects lidocaine into a connective tissue wrapping in the axilla. What is this structure called?
optionExplanations: Prevertebral fascia is continuous with the sheath but is in the neck.
The axillary sheath encloses the brachial plexus and axillary vessels.
Clavipectoral fascia is a sheet deep to the pectoralis major.
Bicipital aponeurosis is in the cubital fossa.
Investing fascia is the most superficial layer of deep fascia.
citation: Because the axillary sheath encloses the axillary vessels and the brachial plexus, a brachial plexus nerve block can easily be obtained.

statement: Which muscle is innervated by the lower subscapular nerve in addition to the subscapularis?
optionExplanations: Teres minor is supplied by the axillary nerve.
The lower subscapular nerve supplies both the subscapularis and the teres major.
Supraspinatus is supplied by the suprascapular nerve.
Infraspinatus is supplied by the suprascapular nerve.
Deltoid is supplied by the axillary nerve.
citation: In addition, the lower subscapular nerve supplies the teres major muscle.

statement: What is the secondary action of the biceps brachii muscle at the glenohumeral joint?
optionExplanations: Biceps does not extend the shoulder.
Biceps does not abduct the shoulder.
The biceps brachii acts as a weak flexor of the shoulder joint.
Biceps does not rotate the humerus laterally.
Biceps does not rotate the humerus medially.
citation: It is a powerful flexor of the elbow joint and a weak flexor of the shoulder joint.

statement: A patient using crutches incorrectly presents with 'wrist drop' and an inability to extend his elbow. Where is the most likely site of radial nerve compression?
optionExplanations: Compression in the radial groove spares the triceps (elbow extension).
Compression in the axilla affects all radial-supplied muscles, including the triceps.
Lateral epicondyle compression affects only the deep branch.
Cubital fossa compression affects only the deep branch.
Surgical neck injury typically affects the axillary nerve.
citation: The radial nerve can be injured in the axilla by the pressure of the upper end of a badly fitting crutch pressing up into the armpit

statement: What are the primary actions of the coracobrachialis muscle?
optionExplanations: It does not abduct the arm.
It is a flexor, not an extensor.
The coracobrachialis flexes the arm and acts as a weak adductor.
It is a flexor and adductor.
It has no action on the forearm (supination).

statement: Which two terminal nerves are formed by the division of the posterior cord?
optionExplanations: Median and ulnar come from medial/lateral cords.
Musculocutaneous comes from the lateral cord.
The posterior cord terminates by dividing into the axillary and radial nerves.
Ulnar comes from the medial cord.
Musculocutaneous comes from the lateral cord.

statement: A 25-year-old man is brought to the ER after a high-speed motorcycle accident where he fell forcefully on his shoulder, causing excessive displacement of his head to the opposite side. On examination, his right arm hangs limply by his side, is medially rotated, and the forearm is pronated. Which of the following best describes this clinical presentation?
optionExplanations: This is the classic description of Erb-Duchenne palsy resulting from C5-C6 root injury.
Claw hand results from lower brachial plexus (Klumpke's) or ulnar nerve injury.
Ape hand results from median nerve injury.
Wristdrop results from radial nerve injury.
Winged scapula results from long thoracic nerve injury.
citation: The position of the upper limb in this condition has been likened to that of a porter or waiter hinting for a tip and so is often referred to as “waiter’s tip posture”

statement: A construction worker falls from a ladder and instinctively clutches a nearby ledge to save himself. He immediately feels a sharp pain in his axilla. Weeks later, he presents with wasting of all small muscles of the hand and a specific deformity. What is the anatomical basis for the hyperextension seen at his metacarpophalangeal joints?
options: Injury to the C5 nerve root
optionExplanations: In Klumpke's palsy (T1), paralysis of lumbricals and interossei leaves the extensor digitorum unopposed, causing hyperextension.
Flexor digitorum superficialis is usually intact or less affected than intrinsic muscles in pure T1 lesions.
C5 injury leads to Erb's palsy, not claw hand.
Lumbrical muscles are paralyzed in this condition, not in spasm.
Klumpke's palsy involves the lower trunk/medial cord (C8-T1), not the lateral cord.
citation: The hand has a clawed appearance caused by hyperextension of the metacarpophalangeal joints and flexion of the interphalangeal joints.

statement: A 45-year-old woman undergoes a radical mastectomy for breast cancer. Postoperatively, she complains of difficulty combing her hair and notices her shoulder blade sticks out when she pushes against a wall. Which nerve was likely damaged during the clearance of axillary lymph nodes?
optionExplanations: The long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior; its injury causes winging of the scapula.
Thoracodorsal nerve injury affects the latissimus dorsi, causing weak adduction/extension.
Axillary nerve injury affects the deltoid, causing loss of abduction.
Suprascapular nerve injury affects supraspinatus and infraspinatus.
Musculocutaneous nerve injury affects elbow flexion.
citation: The vertebral border and inferior angle of the scapula will no longer be kept closely applied to the chest wall and will protrude posteriorly in a condition known as “winged scapula”

statement: A patient sustains a deep laceration to the anterior arm between the biceps and brachialis muscles. While elbow flexion is weakened, the physician is specifically checking for sensory loss to confirm musculocutaneous nerve involvement. Where would this sensory deficit be most prominent?
optionExplanations: The musculocutaneous nerve terminates as the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm.
Medial arm sensation is provided by the medial cutaneous nerve of the arm and intercostobrachial nerve.
Posterior arm sensation is provided by the radial nerve.
The palm is supplied by the median and ulnar nerves.
Skin over the deltoid is supplied by supraclavicular and axillary nerves.

statement: A handyman is using a manual screwdriver to tighten a stubborn screw with his right hand. He notices that the movement is much easier when he flexes his elbow slightly. Which muscle is primarily responsible for this powerful rotatory action in this position?
optionExplanations: The biceps brachii is the primary powerful supinator, especially when the elbow is flexed.
The supinator muscle acts during slow, unresisted supination.
The brachialis is a pure flexor of the elbow.
The brachioradialis primarily flexes the elbow in the midprone position.
The coracobrachialis flexes and adducts the shoulder.
