statement: In the formation of the brachial plexus, which spinal nerve roots unite to form the lower trunk?
optionExplanations: C8 and T1 roots unite to form the lower trunk.
C5 and C6 roots unite to form the upper trunk.
C6 does not unite with C7; C7 continues alone as the middle trunk.
C7 continues as the middle trunk; C8 joins T1.
T2 is not a standard root of the brachial plexus.
citation: The roots of C5 and 6 unite to form the upper trunk, the root of C7 continues as the middle trunk, and the roots of C8 and T1 unite to form the lower trunk.

statement: The lateral, medial, and posterior cords of the brachial plexus are named based on their anatomical relationship to which structure?
optionExplanations: The cords are named based on their relation to the second part of the axillary artery.
The first part of the artery is proximal to the cords' definitive positions.
The third part is distal to where the cords are typically named.
The clavicle divides the plexus into supra- and infraclavicular parts, but doesn't define cord names.
The pectoralis minor overlies the second part of the artery, but the artery itself is the reference.
citation: the cords of the plexus are named according to their positional relationship to the second part of the axillary artery.

statement: An anesthesiologist is performing a nerve block for a patient undergoing hand surgery. He palpates the pulsations of the axillary artery and injects local anesthetic into the surrounding fascia to ensure the entire plexus is bathed in the solution. What is this specific fascial compartment called?
optionExplanations: The axillary sheath encloses the artery, vein, and brachial plexus cords.
The clavipectoral fascia is a sheet deep to the pectoralis major.
The axillary sheath is continuous with the prevertebral fascia, but the compartment in the axilla is the sheath.
The axillary tail is breast tissue.
The suspensory ligament of the axilla is derived from clavipectoral fascia.
citation: the brachial plexus and the axillary artery and vein are enclosed together in a connective tissue wrapping termed the axillary sheath.

statement: A 60-year-old man with a history of gallstones presents with sharp pain at the 'point of his right shoulder'. The physician explains this is referred pain from gallbladder irritation of the diaphragm. Which nerves are responsible for the cutaneous sensation in this specific shoulder region?
optionExplanations: Supraclavicular nerves (C3-C4) supply the skin over the shoulder point; they share roots with the phrenic nerve.
The axillary nerve supplies the lower half of the deltoid via the upper lateral cutaneous nerve.
The medial cutaneous nerve of the arm supplies the medial arm.
The intercostobrachial nerve supplies the axilla and medial arm.
The upper lateral cutaneous nerve is a branch of the axillary nerve for the lower deltoid area.
citation: The supraclavicular nerves (C3, 4 from the cervical plexus) provide the sensory nerve supply to the skin over the point of the shoulder to halfway down the deltoid muscle.

statement: While the musculocutaneous nerve is the primary motor supply to the anterior compartment of the arm, which other nerve provides a small contribution to the brachialis muscle?
optionExplanations: The radial nerve provides a small sensory/motor branch to the lateral part of the brachialis.
The median nerve has no motor branches in the arm.
The ulnar nerve has no motor branches in the arm.
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid and teres minor.
The long thoracic nerve supplies the serratus anterior.
citation: the brachialis muscle is greatly weakened (the latter muscle is also supplied by the radial nerve).

statement: Which muscle of the anterior compartment of the arm is pierced by the musculocutaneous nerve as it leaves the axilla?
optionExplanations: The musculocutaneous nerve characteristically pierces the coracobrachialis.
It passes between the biceps and brachialis but does not pierce them to exit the axilla.
It lies anterior to the brachialis but does not pierce it.
The medial pectoral nerve pierces the pectoralis minor.
The axillary nerve passes through the quadrangular space, not through the deltoid.
citation: The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the coracobrachialis muscle and leaves the axilla by piercing that muscle

statement: From which part of the brachial plexus does the medial cutaneous nerve of the arm originate?
optionExplanations: The medial cutaneous nerve of the arm is a branch of the medial cord.
The lateral cord gives off the lateral pectoral and musculocutaneous nerves.
The posterior cord gives off subscapular, thoracodorsal, axillary, and radial nerves.
Trunks do not typically give off cutaneous nerves to the arm.
The lower trunk gives rise to divisions that eventually form the medial cord.
citation: The medial cutaneous nerve of the arm (T1) arises from the medial cord of the brachial plexus

statement: Which components of the brachial plexus trunks unite to form the posterior cord?
optionExplanations: The posterior cord is formed by the union of posterior divisions from the upper, middle, and lower trunks.
Anterior divisions do not all join together; they form lateral and medial cords.
Anterior divisions of upper and middle trunks form the lateral cord.
The posterior division of the lower trunk must also be included.
The anterior division of the lower trunk continues as the medial cord.

statement: Which divisions of the brachial plexus trunks unite to form the lateral cord?
options: Anterior divisions of upper and middle
Posterior divisions of upper and middle
Anterior divisions of middle and lower
correctOption: Anterior divisions of upper and middle
optionExplanations: The lateral cord is formed by the anterior divisions of the upper and middle trunks.
Posterior divisions form the posterior cord.
The anterior division of the lower trunk forms the medial cord.
Posterior divisions of all three form the posterior cord.
The anterior division of the lower trunk forms the medial cord.
citation: The anterior divisions of the upper and middle trunks unite to form the lateral cord

statement: Which specific division of the brachial plexus trunks forms the medial cord?
optionExplanations: The medial cord is the continuation of the anterior division of the lower trunk.
The posterior division of the lower trunk contributes to the posterior cord.
The anterior division of the middle trunk contributes to the lateral cord.
The posterior division of the upper trunk contributes to the posterior cord.
The anterior division of the upper trunk contributes to the lateral cord.

statement: Where does the suprascapular nerve typically originate within the brachial plexus structure?
optionExplanations: The suprascapular nerve is a branch of the upper trunk.
The lateral cord gives off the musculocutaneous nerve.
The posterior cord gives off the axillary and radial nerves.
The dorsal scapular nerve arises from the C5 root.
The middle trunk typically has no branches.
citation: The suprascapular nerve (C5 to 6) arises in the posterior triangle in the neck and runs downward and laterally to reach the upper edge of the scapula.

statement: The nerve supplying the serratus anterior muscle originates from which part of the brachial plexus?
optionExplanations: The long thoracic nerve arises directly from the roots (C5, C6, C7).
Trunks give rise to the suprascapular nerve and nerve to subclavius.
Divisions give off no branches.
Cords give off branches like the pectoral and subscapular nerves.
Terminal branches are the five main nerves (Median, Ulnar, etc.).
citation: The long thoracic nerve (C5 to 7) arises from the roots of the brachial plexus in the neck

statement: What is the origin of the musculocutaneous nerve?
optionExplanations: The musculocutaneous nerve is a terminal branch of the lateral cord.
The medial cord gives rise to the ulnar nerve.
The posterior cord gives rise to the radial and axillary nerves.
Trunks do not give rise to the musculocutaneous nerve.
The middle trunk continues as divisions, not terminal nerves.
citation: The musculocutaneous nerve (C5 to 7) originates from the lateral cord of the brachial plexus in the axilla

statement: During a routine neurological exam, a physician taps the distal tendon of the biceps brachii in the cubital fossa. This test primarily evaluates the integrity of which spinal cord segments?
optionExplanations: The biceps reflex tests the C5 and C6 spinal segments.
C3 and C4 supply the diaphragm and levator scapulae.
C7 is the primary root for the triceps reflex.
C8 and T1 are tested via finger flexors and intrinsic hand muscles.
T1 and T2 supply the axilla and medial arm skin.
citation: Biceps brachii tendon reflex: C5 and 6 (flexion of the elbow joint by tapping the biceps tendon)

statement: A newborn presents with an upper limb that is adducted and medially rotated at the shoulder, with the elbow extended and forearm pronated. The obstetrician notes the delivery involved significant traction on the head. Which part of the brachial plexus was most likely injured?

Posterior cord injury would cause wrist drop and deltoid paralysis but different overall posture.
Medial cord injury affects ulnar nerve distribution.
Lower trunk injury (C8-T1) leads to Klumpke's palsy.

statement: Which spinal nerve components constitute the 'roots' of the brachial plexus?
optionExplanations: The roots are the anterior (ventral) rami of C5, C6, C7, C8, and T1.
Posterior rami supply the skin and deep muscles of the back.
C1-C4 form the cervical plexus.
T1-T5 posterior rami do not form the brachial plexus.
The T1 root must be included.
citation: The roots are the anterior rami of spinal nerves C5 to T1.

statement: Which nerve is the primary motor supply for the muscles of the anterior compartment of the arm?
optionExplanations: The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the coracobrachialis, biceps, and brachialis.
The median nerve has no motor branches in the arm.
The ulnar nerve has no motor branches in the arm.
The radial nerve supplies the posterior compartment (triceps).
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid and teres minor.

statement: After piercing the coracobrachialis, where does the musculocutaneous nerve travel in the arm?
optionExplanations: The nerve runs in the plane between the biceps brachii and the brachialis.

The bicipital groove houses the long head of the biceps tendon.

statement: Which nerve provides sensory innervation to the skin on the middle of the back of the arm?
optionExplanations: The posterior cutaneous nerve of the arm is a branch of the radial nerve.
The axillary nerve supplies the lower deltoid area via the upper lateral cutaneous nerve.
The ulnar nerve has no cutaneous branches in the arm.
The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the lateral forearm.
The median nerve has no cutaneous branches in the arm.
citation: The posterior cutaneous nerve of the arm supplies the skin of the back of the arm

statement: Which trunk of the brachial plexus is formed by the union of the C8 and T1 roots?
optionExplanations: C8 and T1 form the lower trunk.
C5 and C6 form the upper trunk.
C7 forms the middle trunk.
There is no 'medial trunk'; medial refers to a cord.
There is no 'lateral trunk'; lateral refers to a cord.
citation: the roots of C8 and T1 unite to form the lower trunk.

statement: A newborn infant presents with the right upper limb hanging limply by the side, medially rotated, and the forearm pronated after a difficult delivery involving excessive displacement of the head. Which spinal nerve roots were likely injured?
optionExplanations: C3 and C4 supply the supraclavicular region, not the main plexus.
Excessive traction on C5 and C6 roots during delivery causes Erb-Duchenne palsy, resulting in the 'waiter's tip' posture.
C7 and C8 involvement would affect different muscle groups, primarily extensors.
C8 and T1 injury results in Klumpke's palsy, affecting the small muscles of the hand.
T1 and T2 are not the primary roots for the upper limb motor function described.
citation: This causes excessive traction or even tearing of the C5 and 6 roots of the plexus.

statement: A 25-year-old man falls from a tree and clutches a branch to save himself. He later presents with a 'clawed' appearance of the hand and loss of sensation along the medial side of the arm. Which part of the brachial plexus was most likely torn?
optionExplanations: C5 injury causes proximal limb deficits, not a claw hand.
C7 injury primarily affects elbow and wrist extension.
Excessive abduction of the arm, as in clutching an object while falling, typically tears the T1 ramus, leading to Klumpke's palsy.
Upper trunk injury (C5-C6) leads to Erb's palsy.
Lateral cord injury would not cause the specific claw hand deformity associated with T1.
citation: The T1 anterior ramus is usually torn.

statement: Following a radical mastectomy, a patient is unable to rotate her scapula to raise her arm above her head. Physical examination reveals the medial border of the scapula protruding posteriorly. Which nerve was likely damaged during surgery?
optionExplanations: Axillary nerve damage affects the deltoid, not scapular stability against the chest wall.
Suprascapular nerve damage affects supraspinatus and infraspinatus.
Thoracodorsal nerve damage affects the latissimus dorsi.
The long thoracic nerve (C5-C7) supplies the serratus anterior; its injury causes 'winged scapula'.
Musculocutaneous nerve damage affects the anterior compartment of the arm.
citation: The long thoracic nerve (C5 to 7) arises from the roots of the brachial plexus

statement: A surgeon is exploring the axilla and identifies a nerve that pierces the coracobrachialis muscle. If this nerve is accidentally transected at this point, which functional deficit will be most prominent?
options: Loss of sensation on medial forearm
optionExplanations: Wrist extension is controlled by the radial nerve.
The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the biceps and brachialis; its loss severely weakens elbow flexion.
Thumb opposition is controlled by the median nerve.
Shoulder abduction is primarily axillary and suprascapular nerves.
Medial forearm sensation is provided by the medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm.
citation: The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the coracobrachialis muscle and leaves the axilla by piercing that muscle

statement: A carpenter complains of difficulty using a manual screwdriver to drive screws into wood with his right hand. Which muscle is primarily responsible for this powerful movement?
optionExplanations: Brachialis is a pure flexor of the elbow.
Coracobrachialis flexes and adducts the shoulder.
The biceps brachii is the primary powerful supinator used in movements like using a screwdriver.
Triceps brachii is an elbow extensor.
Pronator teres performs the opposite action (pronation).
citation: The biceps brachii is a powerful supinator, especially during supination against resistance.

statement: An elderly woman falls and sustains a fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus. She later complains of numbness over the lower half of the deltoid muscle. Which nerve is most likely involved?
optionExplanations: Radial nerve is related to the midshaft (spiral groove).
Ulnar nerve is related to the medial epicondyle.
Median nerve is not closely related to the surgical neck.
The axillary nerve winds around the surgical neck of the humerus and is frequently damaged in fractures there.
Musculocutaneous nerve is deeper and less likely to be injured by this specific fracture.
citation: The axillary nerve (C5 to 6) arises from the termination of the posterior cord

statement: An anesthesiologist performs a nerve block by injecting local anesthetic into the connective tissue wrapping that surrounds the axillary artery and the cords of the brachial plexus. What is this wrapping called?
optionExplanations: Prevertebral fascia is continuous with the sheath but is in the neck.
Clavipectoral fascia lies deep to the pectoralis major but does not enclose the neurovascular bundle.
The axillary sheath encloses the axillary vessels and the brachial plexus cords.
Deep fascia of the arm is distal to the axilla.
Pectoral fascia covers the pectoralis major.
citation: The brachial plexus and the axillary artery and vein are enclosed together in a connective tissue wrapping termed the axillary sheath.

statement: A patient with gallbladder disease complains of sharp pain at the 'point of the shoulder.' This referred pain is mediated by nerves that also supply the diaphragm. Which nerves provide this cutaneous sensation?
optionExplanations: Axillary nerve supplies the lower deltoid skin.
Supraclavicular nerves (C3-C4) supply the skin over the shoulder point; pain is referred here from the diaphragm (phrenic nerve C3-C5).
Intercostobrachial nerve supplies the armpit.
Medial cutaneous nerves supply the medial arm/forearm.
Dorsal scapular nerve is a motor nerve to the rhomboids.
citation: The supraclavicular nerves (C3, 4 from the cervical plexus) provide the sensory nerve supply to the skin over the point of the shoulder

statement: A patient undergoing an axillary lymph node dissection for breast cancer reports numbness in the armpit and the medial side of the proximal arm postoperatively. Which nerve was likely sacrificed?
optionExplanations: Long thoracic nerve is motor to serratus anterior.
Thoracodorsal nerve is motor to latissimus dorsi.
The intercostobrachial nerve (T2) supplies the skin of the axilla and medial arm.
Radial nerve supplies the posterior arm.
Ulnar nerve supplies the medial hand and forearm.
citation: The medial cutaneous nerve of the arm (T1) and the intercostobrachial nerve (a branch of the T2 intercostal nerve) supply the skin of the armpit

statement: From which specific part of the brachial plexus does the musculocutaneous nerve originate?
optionExplanations: The medial cord gives rise to the ulnar nerve.
The posterior cord gives rise to the radial and axillary nerves.
The musculocutaneous nerve is a terminal branch of the lateral cord.
Trunks do not directly give rise to the musculocutaneous nerve.
The middle trunk continues as divisions, not terminal nerves.
citation: The musculocutaneous nerve (C5 to 7) originates from the lateral cord of the brachial plexus

statement: Which spinal nerve components form the roots of the brachial plexus?
optionExplanations: Posterior rami supply the skin and muscles of the back.
Anterior rami C1-C4 form the cervical plexus.
The brachial plexus is formed by the anterior rami of C5, C6, C7, C8, and T1.
Posterior rami of C1-C4 supply the back of the neck.
Anterior rami T2-T6 form intercostal nerves.
citation: The roots are the anterior rami of spinal nerves C5 to T1.

statement: How is the posterior cord of the brachial plexus formed?
options: Posterior division of lower trunk only
Anterior division of lower trunk only
optionExplanations: Anterior divisions form the lateral and medial cords.
The posterior cord is uniquely formed by the union of the posterior divisions of the upper, middle, and lower trunks.
This union forms the lateral cord.
The posterior division of the lower trunk is only one of three contributors.
The anterior division of the lower trunk forms the medial cord.
citation: The posterior divisions of all three trunks join to form the posterior cord.

statement: During a dissection of the infraclavicular part of the brachial plexus, a student observes two nerve roots joining to form a single large nerve trunk anterior to the axillary artery. Which nerve is being formed?
optionExplanations: The ulnar nerve arises solely from the medial cord.
The radial nerve arises solely from the posterior cord.
The median nerve is formed by the union of a lateral root (from the lateral cord) and a medial root (from the medial cord).
The axillary nerve arises from the posterior cord.
The musculocutaneous nerve is a direct branch of the lateral cord.
citation: The lateral cord gives rise to the musculocutaneous nerve and contributes to the formation of the median nerve.

statement: Which group of muscles constitutes the anterior compartment of the arm?
optionExplanations: These are posterior compartment muscles.
The anterior compartment contains the biceps brachii, coracobrachialis, and brachialis.
These are shoulder region muscles.
These are forearm compartment muscles.
These are pectoral region muscles.
citation: The biceps brachii, coracobrachialis, and brachialis muscles

statement: What is the primary motor nerve supply to the muscles of the anterior compartment of the arm?
optionExplanations: Median nerve supplies most anterior forearm muscles.
Ulnar nerve supplies most intrinsic hand muscles.
Radial nerve supplies the posterior compartment of the arm.
The musculocutaneous nerve supplies all muscles in the anterior arm compartment.
Axillary nerve supplies the deltoid and teres minor.
citation: The musculocutaneous nerve is the motor nerve to the anterior compartment of the arm

statement: A patient suffers a high injury to the musculocutaneous nerve. Although the biceps is paralyzed, the patient can still perform weak elbow flexion. Which other nerve contributes to the innervation of the brachialis muscle?
optionExplanations: The median nerve does not supply the brachialis.
The ulnar nerve does not supply the brachialis.
The brachialis has a dual nerve supply: the musculocutaneous nerve and a small branch from the radial nerve.
The axillary nerve is restricted to the shoulder.
The long thoracic nerve is restricted to the serratus anterior.
citation: the brachialis muscle is greatly weakened (the latter muscle is also supplied by the radial nerve).

statement: A patient presents with a loss of sensation on the lateral aspect of the forearm following a deep laceration just above the elbow, lateral to the biceps tendon. Which nerve is the continuation of the musculocutaneous nerve in this region?
optionExplanations: This arises from the medial cord.
This arises from the radial nerve.
The musculocutaneous nerve terminates by becoming the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm.
This supplies the dorsum of the hand.
This arises from the median or ulnar nerves.
citation: It then runs down the lateral aspect of the forearm as the lateral cutaneous nerve of the forearm

statement: A college student falls asleep with his arm draped over the back of a hard chair ('Saturday night palsy'). He wakes up unable to extend his wrist. What is the clinical name for this deformity?
optionExplanations: Claw hand is caused by ulnar nerve injury.
Ape hand is caused by median nerve injury.
Wristdrop is the characteristic result of radial nerve compression or injury, leading to paralysis of extensors.
Waiter's tip is Erb's palsy (C5-C6).
Winged scapula is long thoracic nerve injury.
citation: Wristdrop (flexion of the wrist; Fig. 3.63) occurs as a result of the action of the unopposed flexor muscles

statement: A patient has a localized loss of sensation strictly on the skin of the middle of the back of the arm. Which nerve, arising in the axilla from the radial nerve, is responsible for this area?
optionExplanations: This is a branch of the axillary nerve.
This is a more distal branch of the radial nerve.
This arises from the medial cord.
The posterior cutaneous nerve of the arm is a branch of the radial nerve that supplies the back of the arm.
This supplies the axilla and medial arm.
citation: The posterior cutaneous nerve of the arm supplies the skin of the back of the arm

statement: A physician taps the tendon of the biceps brachii to elicit a reflex. Which spinal cord segments are primarily being tested?
optionExplanations: These are supraclavicular segments.
The biceps reflex specifically tests the C5 and C6 segments of the spinal cord.
C7 is primarily tested by the triceps reflex.
These segments supply the hand muscles.
These segments supply the medial arm and hand.
citation: Biceps brachii tendon reflex: C5 and 6

statement: An anatomical variation is noted where the middle trunk of the brachial plexus fails to provide an anterior division. Which cord would be most directly affected in its formation?
optionExplanations: The medial cord is formed by the anterior division of the lower trunk.
The posterior cord is formed by posterior divisions.
The lateral cord is formed by the union of the anterior divisions of the upper and middle trunks.
Trunks are proximal to divisions.
Trunks are proximal to divisions.
citation: The anterior divisions of the upper and middle trunks unite to form the lateral cord

statement: A lesion specifically involving the anterior division of the lower trunk of the brachial plexus would result in functional loss in which cord?
optionExplanations: Lateral cord comes from upper and middle trunks.
The anterior division of the lower trunk is the sole contributor that continues as the medial cord.
Posterior cord comes from posterior divisions.
Trunks are proximal to divisions.
Trunks are proximal to divisions.
citation: the anterior division of the lower trunk continues as the medial cord

statement: A patient presents with a loss of sensation over the lower half of the deltoid muscle. This area is specifically supplied by which nerve?
optionExplanations: This supplies the lower part of the arm, not the deltoid area.
The upper lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm (a branch of the axillary nerve) supplies the skin over the lower deltoid.
This supplies the medial arm.
This supplies the back of the arm.
This supplies the point of the shoulder.
citation: upper lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm

statement: A patient has a lesion of the musculocutaneous nerve. In addition to weak elbow flexion, they have slight weakness in which shoulder movement?
optionExplanations: Extension is performed by the posterior compartment.
Abduction is performed by deltoid/supraspinatus.
Lateral rotation is performed by infraspinatus/teres minor.
The coracobrachialis, supplied by the musculocutaneous nerve, acts as a flexor of the shoulder joint.
Adduction is primarily pectoralis major/latissimus dorsi.
citation: the coracobrachialis (flexes the shoulder)

statement: Which spinal nerve root continues directly to form the middle trunk of the brachial plexus?
optionExplanations: C5 joins C6 to form the upper trunk.
C6 joins C5 to form the upper trunk.
The C7 root continues independently as the middle trunk.
C8 joins T1 to form the lower trunk.
T1 joins C8 to form the lower trunk.
citation: the root of C7 continues as the middle trunk

statement: A 22-year-old athlete sustains a deep penetrating injury to the middle of his anterior arm. On examination, he has marked weakness of elbow flexion and loss of sensation along the lateral border of the forearm. During debridement, the surgeon identifies a damaged nerve situated in the interval between the biceps brachii and the brachialis muscles. Which nerve is most likely involved?
optionExplanations: The median nerve typically travels medial to the brachial artery and does not occupy the plane between the biceps and brachialis in the mid-arm.
The ulnar nerve descends on the medial side of the arm before piercing the medial intermuscular septum to enter the posterior compartment.
The radial nerve is found in the spiral groove and later between the brachialis and brachioradialis muscles, rather than between the biceps and brachialis.
The musculocutaneous nerve pierces the coracobrachialis and then descends specifically between the biceps brachii and the brachialis muscles.
The axillary nerve terminates in the shoulder region after passing through the quadrangular space and does not extend into the mid-arm anterior compartment.
citation: It runs downward and laterally, pierces the coracobrachialis muscle (see Fig. 3.13), and then passes downward between the biceps and brachialis muscles (see Fig. 3.25).

statement: At which anatomical landmark does the brachial artery begin as a continuation of the axillary artery?
optionExplanations: This is the starting point of the axillary artery.
This marks the division between the first and second parts of the axillary artery.
The brachial artery officially begins at the lower border of the teres major muscle.
This is the termination point where the brachial artery bifurcates.
This is a common site for humeral fractures but not the origin of the brachial artery.
citation: The brachial artery (Fig. 3.67; also see Figs. 3.25, 3.29, and 3.64) begins at the lower border of the teres major muscle as a continuation of the axillary artery.

statement: A 10-year-old boy is brought to the ER with a supracondylar fracture of the humerus. The surgeon is concerned about the integrity of the brachial artery at its bifurcation. At which level does this artery normally terminate?
options: Opposite the neck of the radius
correctOption: Opposite the neck of the radius
optionExplanations: The radial tuberosity is distal to the bifurcation point.
The brachial artery terminates by dividing into its terminal branches opposite the neck of the radius.
The lateral epicondyle is a landmark for the radial nerve division, not the brachial artery termination.
The artery lies on the brachialis but terminates further distal.
The coronoid process is part of the ulna; the landmark for termination is the radius.
citation: It terminates opposite the neck of the radius by dividing into the radial and ulnar arteries.

coreConcept: Median Nerve Relation to Brachial Artery
statement: During a surgical exploration of the mid-arm to repair a lacerated vessel, the surgeon identifies the brachial artery. Which nerve is expected to be found crossing the middle part of this artery from the lateral to the medial side?
optionExplanations: The ulnar nerve remains medial to the artery in the upper arm before piercing the septum.
The radial nerve passes posteriorly into the spiral groove.
The musculocutaneous nerve remains lateral to the biceps.
The median nerve characteristically crosses the middle part of the brachial artery.
The axillary nerve is located in the axilla and does not reach the mid-arm.
citation: the median nerve crosses its middle part

statement: A 45-year-old man sustains a midshaft fracture of the humerus. Radiographs show a displaced fracture line along the spiral groove. Which artery is most likely to be damaged alongside the radial nerve in this location?
optionExplanations: This artery follows the ulnar nerve behind the medial epicondyle.
The profunda brachii artery accompanies the radial nerve through the spiral groove.
This artery passes anterior to the medial epicondyle.
The nutrient artery enters the bone but does not travel the length of the spiral groove.
This is a branch of the subscapular artery in the scapular region.
citation: The profunda brachii (deep brachial) artery arises near the origin of the brachial artery, accompanies the radial nerve through the radial (spiral) groove of the humerus

statement: What is the primary functional action of the triceps brachii muscle at the elbow joint?
optionExplanations: Flexion is performed by the anterior compartment muscles.
Supination is primarily the role of the biceps and supinator.
Pronation is performed by the pronator teres and quadratus.
The triceps is the chief extensor of the elbow joint.
Abduction is a movement of the shoulder or digits, not the elbow.
citation: The triceps and anconeus muscles perform extension.

statement: A patient presents with an inability to extend the elbow after a high-velocity injury to the axilla. If the triceps brachii is found to be paralyzed, which nerve has most likely been injured?
optionExplanations: The median nerve supplies the anterior forearm and thenar muscles.
The ulnar nerve supplies the flexor carpi ulnaris and intrinsic hand muscles.
The radial nerve provides motor supply to the posterior compartment of the arm, including the triceps.
The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid and teres minor.
The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the anterior compartment of the arm.
citation: the radial nerve innervates the posterior compartment.

statement: Regarding the osteology of the ulna, what is the specific insertion site for the triceps brachii muscle?
optionExplanations: The coronoid process receives the brachialis muscle.
The radial notch articulates with the head of the radius.
The ulnar tuberosity is the insertion for the brachialis.
The olecranon process is the insertion site for the triceps brachii.
The styloid process is at the distal end of the ulna.
citation: It is the insertion site of the triceps brachii muscle.

statement: Which muscle is primarily supplied by the profunda brachii artery as it travels through the posterior compartment of the arm?
optionExplanations: Biceps is supplied by muscular branches of the brachial artery.
Brachialis is in the anterior compartment.
Coracobrachialis is in the anterior compartment.
The profunda brachii artery supplies the triceps muscle.
The deltoid is supplied by the posterior circumflex humeral and thoracoacromial arteries.
citation: supplies the triceps muscle.

coreConcept: Brachial Artery Relations in Cubital Fossa
statement: A medical student is attempting to palpate the brachial pulse in the cubital fossa. They find that the pulse is somewhat muffled by a flat, fibrous sheet that crosses the lower part of the artery. What is this structure?
optionExplanations: The flexor retinaculum is at the wrist.
The bicipital aponeurosis crosses the lower part of the brachial artery in the cubital fossa.
The interosseous membrane is between the radius and ulna.
The lateral septum divides the arm compartments but does not cross the artery in the fossa.
The clavipectoral fascia is in the pectoral region.
citation: the bicipital aponeurosis crosses its lower part.

statement: A patient presents with weakness in elbow extension and shoulder adduction. The physician suspects an avulsion of the long head of the triceps. From which specific scapular landmark does this head originate?
optionExplanations: The supraglenoid tubercle is the origin of the long head of the biceps.
The coracoid process is the origin of the short head of the biceps and coracobrachialis.
The infraglenoid tubercle is the origin of the long head of the triceps.
The spine of the scapula is an attachment for the deltoid and trapezius.
The acromion is an attachment for the deltoid and trapezius.
citation: A roughened area (infraglenoid tubercle) is located immediately inferior to the glenoid cavity.

statement: Which branch of the brachial artery accompanies the ulnar nerve as it passes posterior to the medial epicondyle?
optionExplanations: The profunda brachii follows the radial nerve.
The nutrient artery enters the humeral shaft.
The superior ulnar collateral artery follows the ulnar nerve behind the medial epicondyle.
The inferior ulnar collateral artery passes anterior to the medial epicondyle.
The radial recurrent artery is a branch of the radial artery.
citation: The superior ulnar collateral artery arises near the middle of the arm and follows the ulnar nerve posterior to the medial epicondyle

statement: During a neurological examination, a physician taps the tendon just proximal to the olecranon to elicit a reflex. Which spinal cord segments are primarily being tested by this maneuver?
optionExplanations: These segments supply the levator scapulae and trapezius.
These segments are tested by the biceps reflex.
The triceps tendon reflex tests segments C6, C7, and C8.
These segments supply the medial arm and hand.
These segments are associated with Klumpke's palsy and intrinsic hand muscles.
citation: Triceps tendon reflex: C6, 7, and 8 (extension of the elbow joint by tapping the triceps tendon)

statement: Which of the following muscles lies directly posterior to the brachial artery in the arm?
optionExplanations: The biceps brachii overlaps the artery anteriorly.
The brachial artery lies on the brachialis muscle posteriorly.
The pectoralis major is anterior to the axillary artery.
The deltoid is lateral to the upper humerus.
The serratus anterior is on the medial wall of the axilla.
citation: Posteriorly: The artery lies on the triceps, the coracobrachialis insertion, and the brachialis.

statement: The profunda brachii artery contributes to the collateral circulation of the elbow by anastomosing with which specific vessel?
optionExplanations: This anastomoses with the superior ulnar collateral.
This anastomoses with the inferior ulnar collateral.
The profunda brachii anastomoses with the radial recurrent artery.
The common interosseous is a branch of the ulnar artery.
This is a branch of the brachial artery itself.
citation: It anastomoses with the radial recurrent artery (a branch of the radial artery) to form part of the collateral circulation around the elbow joint

statement: Where does the medial head of the triceps brachii muscle originate?
optionExplanations: This is the origin of the long head.
The lateral head originates above the radial groove.
The medial head originates from the posterior surface of the humerus below the radial groove.
The anconeus originates here.
This is a site for the axillary nerve, not triceps origin.
citation: Medial head: Posterior surface of humerus below radial groove

statement: A nurse needs to measure a patient's blood pressure using a manual sphygmomanometer. Where should the stethoscope be placed to best palpate the brachial artery pulsations in the mid-arm?
optionExplanations: The artery is overlapped from the lateral side, making the medial side the palpation point.
The brachial artery is palpated medial to the biceps brachii muscle.
The triceps is in the posterior compartment; the artery is anterior.
The deltoid tuberosity is a bony landmark for the deltoid muscle.
The radial artery is palpated in the snuffbox.
citation: The brachial artery can be palpated in the arm as it lies on the brachialis and is overlapped from the lateral side by the biceps brachii

statement: A patient has a tear in the long head of the triceps brachii. Besides elbow extension, which shoulder movement would most likely be weakened due to this muscle's scapular attachment?
optionExplanations: Abduction is performed by the deltoid and supraspinatus.
Lateral rotation is performed by the infraspinatus and teres minor.
The long head of the triceps crosses the shoulder joint and assists in adduction.
Flexion is performed by the anterior compartment muscles.
Protraction is a movement of the scapula by the serratus anterior.
citation: Long head: Infraglenoid tubercle of scapula

statement: Which branch of the brachial artery is responsible for the primary blood supply to the internal structure of the humeral shaft?
optionExplanations: This supplies the triceps and posterior compartment.
This supplies the elbow anastomosis.
This supplies the elbow anastomosis.
The nutrient artery of the humerus arises from the brachial artery.
This is a branch of the radial artery.
citation: Nutrient artery to the humerus.

statement: A patient with a midshaft humeral fracture presents with wrist drop. However, they are still able to extend their elbow with significant strength. Why is elbow extension often preserved in spiral groove injuries?
options: Branches to triceps arise in axilla
Triceps is supplied by ulnar nerve
correctOption: Branches to triceps arise in axilla
optionExplanations: The ulnar nerve does not supply the triceps.
The median nerve does not supply the triceps.
The radial nerve gives off branches to the long and medial heads of the triceps in the axilla, proximal to the spiral groove.
The triceps is exclusively radial nerve territory.
The anconeus is a small muscle and cannot compensate for the entire triceps.
citation: The radial nerve can be damaged where it lies in the spiral groove on the posterior surface of the humerus under cover of the triceps muscle.

statement: Following a supracondylar fracture, a child develops severe pain, pallor, and pulselessness in the forearm. If left untreated, the resulting muscle necrosis and fibrosis is known as:
optionExplanations: This is a contracture of the palmar aponeurosis.
Volkmann’s ischemic contracture results from brachial artery obstruction following injury.
This is an upper brachial plexus injury.
This is a lower brachial plexus injury.
This is lateral epicondylitis.
citation: Damage to or pressure on the brachial artery can occur at the time of the fracture or from swelling of the surrounding tissues; the circulation to the forearm may be interfered with, leading to Volkmann’s ischemic contracture

statement: Where does the profunda brachii artery typically originate?
options: Near the origin of brachial artery
correctOption: Near the origin of brachial artery
optionExplanations: The axillary artery ends before the profunda brachii begins.
The profunda brachii arises from the proximal part of the brachial artery.
The brachial artery terminates in the cubital fossa.
The radial artery is a terminal branch.
The ulnar artery is a terminal branch.
citation: The profunda brachii (deep brachial) artery arises near the origin of the brachial artery

statement: What is the origin of the lateral head of the triceps brachii?
optionExplanations: This is the long head origin.
The lateral head originates from the posterior humerus above the radial groove.
This is the medial head origin.
The medial epicondyle is an origin for flexors.
The coracoid process is an origin for the short head of the biceps.
citation: Lateral head: Posterior surface of humerus above radial groove

statement: A surgeon is performing a procedure in the upper third of the arm. Which venous structure is found lying immediately medial to the brachial artery?
optionExplanations: The cephalic vein is lateral in the arm.
The basilic vein lies medial to the brachial artery in the upper arm.
The median cubital vein is in the cubital fossa.
The axillary vein is proximal to the brachial artery.
The subclavian vein is in the root of the neck.
citation: Medially: In the upper part of the arm, the ulnar nerve and the basilic vein lie medial to the artery.

statement: A patient is asked to push against a wall with their hands while keeping their arms straight. Which muscle is primarily responsible for maintaining the elbow in this extended position against resistance?
optionExplanations: Biceps is a flexor.
Brachialis is a flexor.
The triceps brachii is the primary extensor of the elbow.
Coracobrachialis is a shoulder flexor.
Pronator teres is a forearm pronator.
citation: The triceps and anconeus muscles perform extension.

statement: What are the two terminal branches of the brachial artery?
optionExplanations: These are proximal continuations.
These are side branches, not terminal.
The brachial artery bifurcates into the radial and ulnar arteries.
These are side branches.
These are branches of the common interosseous artery.
citation: It terminates opposite the neck of the radius by dividing into the radial and ulnar arteries.

statement: A 25-year-old male is brought to the ER with a deep laceration in the upper third of his arm. The surgeon notes a vessel lying medial to the coracobrachialis. Which nerve is located immediately in front of the upper part of this vessel?
optionExplanations: The median nerve crosses the middle part of the artery, not the upper part.
The ulnar nerve lies medial to the artery in the upper part of the arm.
The radial nerve lies posterior to the artery in the axilla and upper arm.
The medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm is specifically described as lying in front of the upper part of the brachial artery.
The musculocutaneous nerve lies lateral to the artery in the upper arm.
citation: The medial cutaneous nerve of the forearm lies in front of the upper part

statement: During a surgical procedure to repair a midshaft humeral fracture, the surgeon identifies a muscle originating from the posterior surface of the humerus, specifically below the radial groove. Which muscle is this?
optionExplanations: The lateral head originates above the radial groove.
The medial head of the triceps originates from the posterior surface of the humerus below the radial groove.
The long head originates from the infraglenoid tubercle of the scapula.
The brachialis originates from the anterior surface of the humerus.
The anconeus originates from the lateral epicondyle.

statement: A patient presents with a high axillary nerve lesion. While testing the triceps, the clinician notes that extension is still possible but weakened. Which head of the triceps receives its innervation highest up in the axilla, potentially sparing it in lower arm injuries?
optionExplanations: The radial nerve gives off branches to the long and medial heads of the triceps while still in the axilla.
The lateral head is typically supplied in the spiral groove.
The medial head receives multiple branches, some in the axilla and some in the groove.
The triceps does not have a 'short head'.
The triceps does not have an 'anterior head'.
citation: The radial nerve... gives off branches to the heads of the triceps

statement: A medical student is taught to palpate the arterial pulse in the mid-arm. To find the brachial artery, they should feel along the medial border of which muscle?
optionExplanations: The triceps is in the posterior compartment.
The artery lies on the brachialis, but the biceps is the overlapping landmark.
The brachial artery is overlapped from the lateral side by the biceps brachii.
The coracobrachialis is higher up in the arm.
The deltoid is in the shoulder region.
citation: The brachial artery can be palpated in the arm as it lies on the brachialis and is overlapped from the lateral side by the biceps brachii

statement: What is the primary functional role of the muscles located in the posterior compartment of the arm?
optionExplanations: Flexion is the role of the anterior compartment.
Supination is primarily performed by the biceps and supinator.
Pronation is performed by forearm muscles.
The posterior compartment muscle (triceps) produces mainly extension.
Abduction is performed by the deltoid and supraspinatus.
citation: the posterior compartment muscle producing mainly extension.

statement: A patient suffers a fracture of the medial epicondyle of the humerus. The ulnar nerve is found to be bruised. Which artery, traveling in close proximity to the ulnar nerve in this region, might also be involved?
optionExplanations: The profunda brachii follows the radial nerve.
The radial recurrent is on the lateral side of the elbow.
The superior ulnar collateral artery follows the ulnar nerve posterior to the medial epicondyle.
The inferior ulnar collateral passes anterior to the medial epicondyle.
The nutrient artery enters the humerus shaft.
citation: The superior ulnar collateral artery arises near the middle of the arm and follows the ulnar nerve posterior to the medial epicondyle

statement: During a neurological exam, a physician taps the tendon just proximal to the olecranon process. This test primarily evaluates the integrity of which spinal cord segments?
optionExplanations: C3, 4 supply the levator scapulae and trapezius.
C5, 6 are tested by the biceps reflex.
The triceps tendon reflex tests C6, 7, and 8.
C8, T1 supply the intrinsic hand muscles.
T1, 2 supply the intercostal regions.
citation: Triceps tendon reflex: C6, 7, and 8 (extension of the elbow joint by tapping the triceps tendon)

statement: A surgeon is exploring the cubital fossa. Medial to the biceps tendon, they identify the brachial artery. Where should they expect to find the median nerve in relation to the artery at this distal level?
optionExplanations: The median nerve is lateral to the artery in the upper arm.
In the lower part of the arm and cubital fossa, the median nerve lies on the medial side of the brachial artery.
The radial nerve is posterior to the artery.
The bicipital aponeurosis is superficial to the artery.
They are in the same general region but the nerve is a distinct medial relation.
citation: In the lower part of the arm, the median nerve lies on its medial side.

statement: From which vessel does the profunda brachii artery typically originate?
optionExplanations: The axillary artery becomes the brachial artery.
The subclavian artery is in the neck.
The profunda brachii arises from the brachial artery near its origin.
The radial artery is a terminal branch of the brachial.
The ulnar artery is a terminal branch of the brachial.
citation: The profunda brachii (deep brachial) artery arises near the origin of the brachial artery

statement: In which anatomical compartment is the triceps brachii muscle located?
optionExplanations: The anterior compartment contains the biceps and brachialis.
The triceps brachii is the muscle of the posterior osseofascial compartment of the arm.
The triceps is in the arm, not the forearm.
There is no 'medial compartment' in the arm; only anterior and posterior.
There is no 'superficial compartment' in the arm.
citation: the upper arm is divided into an anterior and a posterior osseofascial compartment, each having a set of muscles

statement: A patient has a blockage in the brachial artery just above the elbow. Collateral flow might reach the forearm via an anastomosis involving a vessel that passes anterior to the medial epicondyle. Which vessel is this?
optionExplanations: The superior ulnar collateral passes posterior to the medial epicondyle.
The inferior ulnar collateral arises distally and passes anterior to the medial epicondyle.
The profunda brachii is on the lateral side.
The radial recurrent is on the lateral side.
The posterior interosseous is in the forearm.
citation: The inferior ulnar collateral artery arises near the termination of the brachial artery and passes anterior to the medial epicondyle.

statement: A patient is unable to use a crutch because they cannot keep their elbow straight while bearing weight. Which muscle is likely paralyzed?
optionExplanations: Biceps brachii flexes the elbow.
Brachialis flexes the elbow.
The triceps brachii is required to extend and maintain extension of the elbow.
Coracobrachialis flexes the shoulder.
The deltoid abducts the shoulder.
citation: The triceps and anconeus muscles perform extension.

statement: A surgeon is performing a posterior approach to the humerus. After reflecting the triceps, they must be careful of the brachial artery. Which muscle lies directly between the humerus and the brachial artery in the distal half of the arm?
optionExplanations: Biceps is anterior to the artery.
Coracobrachialis insertion is in the middle of the arm.
The brachial artery lies on the brachialis muscle in the lower arm.
Anconeus is at the elbow joint.
Pronator teres is in the forearm.
citation: Posteriorly: The artery lies on the triceps, the coracobrachialis insertion, and the brachialis.

statement: A patient presents with 'wrist drop' following a humerus fracture. The physician suspects the radial nerve is damaged in the radial groove. Which vascular structure is most likely to be damaged at the same site?
optionExplanations: The brachial artery is not in the radial groove.
The profunda brachii vessels accompany the radial nerve in the radial groove.
The superior ulnar collateral follows the ulnar nerve.
The cephalic vein is superficial.
The basilic vein is superficial/medial.
citation: The profunda brachii vessels accompany the nerve in the radial groove.

statement: Which statement best describes the blood supply to the compartments of the arm?
optionExplanations: It supplies more than just the anterior compartment.
The profunda brachii (a branch) supplies the posterior compartment.
The brachial artery, through its branches, supplies the entire arm (both compartments).
It supplies all muscles in the arm, not just the biceps.
It terminates at the forearm but its primary distribution is the arm.
citation: its branches supply both the anterior and posterior compartments of the arm, and thus, the brachial artery supplies the entire arm.

statement: A patient with a radial nerve injury is asked to perform various movements. Which of the following will be most significantly impaired?
optionExplanations: Flexion is performed by the musculocutaneous nerve.
Extension of the elbow is performed by the triceps, which is supplied by the radial nerve.
Supination is primarily biceps (musculocutaneous).
Abduction of the thumb is median/radial, but elbow extension is the classic triceps test.
Adduction of fingers is ulnar nerve.
citation: The triceps and anconeus muscles perform extension.

statement: Which of the following is a direct branch of the brachial artery providing internal supply to the bone of the arm?
optionExplanations: Profunda brachii supplies the triceps.
Radial recurrent is a branch of the radial artery.
The nutrient artery to the humerus is a direct branch of the brachial artery.
Thoracoacromial is a branch of the axillary artery.
Subscapular is a branch of the axillary artery.
citation: Nutrient artery to the humerus.

statement: A 22-year-old athlete presents with a deep laceration across the front of his elbow. During surgical exploration, the physician identifies the lateral boundary of the space. Which muscle forms this specific boundary?
optionExplanations: The brachioradialis muscle forms the lateral boundary of the cubital fossa.
The pronator teres forms the medial boundary of the fossa.
The brachialis forms part of the floor, not the lateral boundary.
The supinator forms the lateral part of the floor.
The biceps brachii tendon is a content of the fossa, not a boundary.
citation: The cubital fossa is a triangular depression that lies in the anterior aspect of the elbow

statement: A 7-year-old boy is brought to the ER after falling on his outstretched hand, resulting in a supracondylar fracture of the humerus. The examiner is concerned about the most medially located structure within the cubital fossa. Which structure is this?
optionExplanations: The median nerve is the most medially located structure in the cubital fossa.
The brachial artery is lateral to the median nerve.
The biceps tendon is lateral to the brachial artery.
The radial nerve is the most lateral structure listed in the contents.
The ulnar artery is a branch of the brachial artery, not the most medial content.
citation: the median nerve, the bifurcation of the brachial artery into the ulnar and radial arteries, the tendon of the biceps muscle, and the radial nerve and its deep branch.

statement: During a difficult venipuncture in the cubital fossa, a trainee accidentally pierces too deeply through the median cubital vein. Which structure normally provides a protective barrier for the underlying brachial artery in this region?
optionExplanations: The bicipital aponeurosis separates the median cubital vein from the brachial artery, offering protection during venipuncture.
The deep fascia is reinforced by the aponeurosis but is not the specific protective structure mentioned.
The brachialis muscle lies deep to the artery, forming the floor.
The pronator teres forms the medial boundary, not the roof/protection.
The lateral intermuscular septum is located in the arm, not the cubital fossa roof.
citation: The bicipital aponeurosis protects the artery from the mistaken introduction of irritating drugs

statement: A paramedic is attempting to establish IV access in a patient with hypovolemic shock whose veins are collapsed. Where should the paramedic look for the cephalic vein at the level of the wrist?
options: Posterior to the radial styloid process
Anterior to the ulnar styloid process
In the center of the palm
correctOption: Posterior to the radial styloid process
optionExplanations: The cephalic vein is consistently found posterior to the styloid process of the radius at the wrist.
The basilic vein is related to the medial/ulnar side.
The pisiform bone is on the medial side of the wrist.
The cephalic vein is lateral to the biceps in the arm, but the question asks about the wrist.
Superficial veins for IV access are not typically accessed in the center of the palm.
citation: The cephalic vein lies fairly constantly in the superficial fascia, immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius.

statement: A clinician chooses the basilic vein over the cephalic vein for central venous catheterization. What anatomical feature makes the basilic vein the preferred choice for this procedure?
optionExplanations: The basilic vein is preferred because it is in a direct line with the axillary vein and increases in diameter.
The axillary vein actually contains valves that can be troublesome.
Both are superficial veins in the fascia.
The basilic vein increases in diameter, making it easier for catheters.
The cephalic vein, not the basilic, sometimes communicates with the external jugular.
citation: the basilic vein increases in diameter and is in direct line with the axillary vein

statement: A 45-year-old man presents with a suspected cervical disc herniation. On examination, the physician taps the biceps brachii tendon to elicit a reflex. Which spinal cord segments are primarily being tested?
optionExplanations: The biceps brachii reflex tests the C5 and C6 spinal segments.
C6 and C7 are more associated with the brachioradialis or triceps reflexes.
C7 and C8 are associated with the triceps reflex.
C3 and C4 supply the levator scapulae and trapezius, not the biceps.
T1 and T2 supply the intrinsic hand muscles and medial arm skin.
citation: Biceps brachii tendon reflex: C5 and 6

statement: A patient exhibits a diminished response when the triceps tendon is tapped just proximal to the olecranon. This finding suggests a lesion involving which spinal nerve roots?
optionExplanations: The triceps reflex tests the C6, C7, and C8 segments.
C5 and C6 are for the biceps reflex.
C4 and C5 are not the primary roots for the triceps.
T1 and T2 are too low for the triceps innervation.
C8 and T1 are associated with Klumpke's palsy and hand muscles.
citation: Triceps tendon reflex: C6, 7, and 8

statement: During a neurological exam, a physician taps the insertion of the brachioradialis tendon. What is the expected normal response and the segments tested?
optionExplanations: The brachioradialis reflex results in supination and tests C5, C6, and C7.
Flexion is the response for the biceps reflex.
Extension is the response for the triceps reflex.
Pronation is not the primary reflex response for the brachioradialis.
Abduction is a shoulder movement, not a forearm reflex.
citation: Brachioradialis tendon reflex: C5, 6, and 7

statement: Which muscles constitute the floor of the cubital fossa?
optionExplanations: The floor is formed by the supinator (laterally) and brachialis (medially).
Brachioradialis and pronator teres form the boundaries, not the floor.
Biceps brachii is a content; triceps is in the posterior compartment.
Anconeus is posterior to the elbow joint.
Coracobrachialis is in the arm and does not reach the cubital fossa floor.
citation: Floor: Supinator muscle laterally; the brachialis muscle medially

statement: What structure separates the median cubital vein from the median nerve in the cubital fossa?
optionExplanations: The bicipital aponeurosis separates the superficial vein from the deeper nerve and artery.
The brachialis muscle is deep to the median nerve.
The bicipital aponeurosis is a specific thickening of the deep fascia.
The supinator is part of the floor, much deeper.
The radial nerve is lateral to the median nerve.
citation: the median cubital vein crosses over the brachial artery and the median nerve, but it is separated from them by the bicipital aponeurosis.

statement: Where does the cephalic vein terminate?
optionExplanations: The cephalic vein drains into the axillary vein after piercing the clavipectoral fascia.
The basilic vein joins the brachial veins to form the axillary vein.
The axillary vein becomes the subclavian vein at the first rib.
The brachial veins are deep veins.
The cephalic vein may communicate with the external jugular, but it does not terminate there.
citation: It dives into the deltopectoral triangle, pierces the clavipectoral fascia, and terminates by draining into the axillary vein.

statement: What anatomical landmark forms the base of the triangular cubital fossa?
optionExplanations: The base is an imaginary horizontal line connecting the medial and lateral epicondyles.
The bicipital aponeurosis forms part of the roof.
The pronator teres forms the medial boundary.
The brachioradialis forms the lateral boundary.
The neck of the radius is deep to the fossa contents.
citation: Base: Imaginary line drawn between the two epicondyles of the humerus

statement: From which structure does the basilic vein originate?
optionExplanations: The basilic vein begins on the medial side of the dorsal venous arch.
The cephalic vein begins on the lateral side of the dorsal venous arch.
The median cubital vein is a tributary/connection, not the origin.
The dorsal venous arch is the primary origin for these superficial veins.
The basilic vein contributes to the formation of the axillary vein.
citation: The basilic vein arises from the medial side of the dorsal venous arch on the back of the hand

statement: A patient presents with severe pain and coldness in the forearm after an attempted IV injection in the cubital fossa. The physician suspects an accidental intra-arterial injection. Which structure was likely entered instead of the median cubital vein?
optionExplanations: The brachial artery lies directly deep to the median cubital vein in the fossa.
The radial artery begins at the bifurcation within the fossa but is more lateral.
The ulnar artery begins at the bifurcation but is deeper and more medial.
The profunda brachii is in the posterior compartment of the arm.
The axillary artery is proximal to the cubital fossa.
citation: In the cubital fossa, the median cubital vein crosses over the brachial artery and the median nerve

statement: A 19-year-old football player sustains a midshaft clavicle fracture. Shortly after, a massive hematoma forms over the site. Rupture of which specific venous communication is most likely responsible?
optionExplanations: A small vein communicating between the cephalic and external jugular veins crosses the clavicle and can rupture during a fracture.
The basilic to axillary connection is deeper and more medial.
The median cubital is in the elbow region.
The cephalic drains into the axillary, but this occurs in the deltopectoral triangle, not across the clavicle.
The internal jugular and subclavian union is deeper in the neck.
citation: The cephalic vein frequently communicates with the external jugular vein by a small vein that crosses in front of the clavicle. Fracture of the clavicle can result in rupture of this communicating vein

statement: A newborn presents with 'waiter's tip' posture following a difficult delivery. Which reflex would you expect to be most significantly absent or diminished in this infant?
optionExplanations: Erb-Duchenne palsy involves C5-C6, which are the roots for the biceps reflex.
The triceps reflex involves C7-C8, which are usually spared in upper lesions.
The grasp reflex involves C8-T1, which are spared in Erb's palsy.
The patellar reflex is a lower limb reflex (L2-L4).
The Achilles reflex is a lower limb reflex (S1-S2).
citation: The following muscles will consequently be paralyzed: the supraspinatus... biceps brachii... and the greater part of the brachialis

statement: A patient has a deep infection in the medial aspect of the mid-arm. The surgeon notes a large superficial vein diving deep through the fascia at this level. Which vein is this?
optionExplanations: The basilic vein pierces the deep fascia halfway up the arm.
The cephalic vein remains superficial until the deltopectoral triangle.
The median cubital vein is in the cubital fossa.
The axillary vein is formed at the lower border of the teres major.
The brachial veins are deep veins accompanying the artery.
citation: Halfway up the arm, it pierces the deep fascia and at the lower border of the teres major joins the venae comitantes

statement: A patient presents with weakness in finger extension but normal wrist extension after a deep penetrating injury to the lateral side of the cubital fossa. Which specific nerve structure was likely damaged?
optionExplanations: The deep branch of the radial nerve (posterior interosseous nerve) supplies the finger extensors and is found in the lateral cubital fossa.
The superficial radial nerve is purely sensory.
The median nerve supplies the flexors, not extensors.
The ulnar nerve is on the medial side of the elbow.
The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the biceps and brachialis.
citation: Deep branch of the radial nerve winds around the neck of the radius, within the supinator muscle, and enters the posterior compartment

statement: A nurse is struggling to find a vein for an IV in an elderly patient. Which maneuver is recommended by the text to distend the superficial veins with blood for easier identification?
optionExplanations: Clenching and relaxing the fist with proximal pressure distends the veins.
Elevating the arm would drain the veins, making them harder to see.
Ice causes vasoconstriction, making veins less visible.
Massaging downward might help, but the text specifically mentions the fist maneuver.
Forceful extension does not specifically distend the veins.
citation: To identify these veins easily, apply firm pressure around the upper arm and repeatedly clench and relax the fist.

statement: A patient presents with 'ape hand' deformity and loss of sensation in the lateral palm. If the lesion is at the elbow, between which two muscle heads does the affected nerve exit the cubital fossa?
optionExplanations: The median nerve exits the cubital fossa between the humeral and ulnar heads of the pronator teres.
The ulnar nerve passes between the heads of the flexor carpi ulnaris.
The biceps tendon is a content, not an exit point for the nerve.
The brachioradialis is a boundary.
The deep radial nerve passes through the supinator.
citation: The median nerve leaves the cubital fossa by passing between the two heads of the pronator teres muscle

statement: A medical student is performing a physical exam. To test the C7 nerve root specifically, where should they tap with the reflex hammer?
optionExplanations: Tapping the triceps tendon tests the C7 segment (along with C6 and C8).
The biceps tendon tests C5-C6.
The brachioradialis tendon tests C5-C6-C7.
The patellar tendon tests L2-L4.
The flexor carpi radialis is not a standard reflex test site mentioned.
citation: extension of the elbow joint by tapping the triceps tendon

statement: A physician is trying to locate the brachial pulse before measuring blood pressure. In the cubital fossa, where is the artery located relative to the biceps tendon?
optionExplanations: The brachial artery lies medial to the biceps tendon in the cubital fossa.
The radial nerve is lateral to the tendon.
The brachialis muscle is deep to the artery.
The bicipital aponeurosis is superficial to the artery.
The artery is anterior to the humerus.
citation: The brachial artery can be palpated in the arm as it lies on the brachialis and is overlapped from the lateral side by the biceps brachii

statement: Which vein connects the cephalic and basilic veins in the cubital fossa?
optionExplanations: The median cubital vein is the standard shunt between the two main superficial veins at the elbow.
The axillary vein is the proximal continuation.
The brachial veins are deep.
The profunda veins are deep.
The ulnar veins are deep.
citation: The median cubital vein, a branch of the cephalic vein in the cubital fossa, runs upward and medially and joins the basilic vein.

statement: What is the normal motor response when eliciting the biceps brachii reflex?
optionExplanations: The biceps reflex causes elbow flexion.
Extension is the triceps reflex response.
Supination is a function of the biceps but not the primary reflex response described.
Pronation is not a biceps function.
Abduction is a deltoid/supraspinatus function.
citation: flexion of the elbow joint by tapping the biceps tendon

statement: In the cubital fossa, the radial nerve is situated between which muscles?
optionExplanations: The radial nerve lies in the interval between the brachialis (medial) and brachioradialis (lateral).
The musculocutaneous nerve lies between the biceps and brachialis.
The pronator teres is on the medial side of the fossa.
The supinator is in the floor.
The triceps is in the posterior compartment.
citation: lying between the brachialis muscle on the medial side and the brachioradialis and extensor carpi radialis longus muscles on the lateral side

statement: A 12-year-old boy is brought to the ER after falling from a tree. Examination reveals a supracondylar fracture of the humerus. The physician is concerned about the structures in the cubital fossa. Which of the following is the most medially located structure in this region?
optionExplanations: The median nerve is the most medial content of the cubital fossa.
The brachial artery lies lateral to the median nerve.
The biceps tendon is lateral to the brachial artery.
The radial nerve is the most lateral structure in the fossa.
The ulnar artery is a terminal branch, not the most medial structure.
citation: The cubital fossa contains the following structures, enumerated from the medial to the lateral side: the median nerve, the bifurcation of the brachial artery into the ulnar and radial arteries, the tendon of the biceps muscle, and the radial nerve and its deep branch.

statement: During a difficult venipuncture in the cubital fossa, a junior doctor accidentally passes the needle too deep while attempting to access the median cubital vein. Which anatomical structure normally provides a protective barrier for the underlying brachial artery in this scenario?
optionExplanations: The bicipital aponeurosis separates the median cubital vein from the brachial artery.
The pronator teres forms the medial boundary, not the roof barrier.
The brachialis forms the floor of the fossa.
The supinator forms the lateral part of the floor.
While deep fascia is present, the specific protective reinforcement is the aponeurosis.
citation: the aponeurosis protects the artery from the mistaken introduction of irritating drugs

statement: Which of the following muscles forms the lateral boundary of the cubital fossa?
optionExplanations: The brachioradialis muscle defines the lateral limit of the cubital fossa.
The pronator teres forms the medial boundary.
The brachialis forms part of the floor.
The supinator forms the lateral part of the floor.
The biceps tendon is a content, not a boundary.
citation: Laterally: Brachioradialis muscle

statement: A patient in hypovolemic shock requires urgent intravenous access, but the superficial veins are not visible. Where should the clinician palpate to find the cephalic vein at the wrist?
optionExplanations: The cephalic vein is consistently found posterior to the radial styloid.
The ulnar styloid is on the medial side of the wrist.
The pisiform is a landmark for the ulnar artery/nerve.
The cephalic vein is lateral to the biceps in the arm, not wrist.
The deltopectoral triangle is for termination, not wrist access.
citation: The cephalic vein lies fairly constantly in the superficial fascia, immediately posterior to the styloid process of the radius.

statement: A 45-year-old laborer presents with neck pain and weakness in elbow flexion. The physician performs a deep tendon reflex test by tapping the biceps tendon. Which spinal cord segments are primarily being evaluated?
optionExplanations: The biceps reflex specifically tests the C5 and C6 segments.
C6 and C7 are more related to the brachioradialis or triceps.
C7 and C8 are primarily for the triceps reflex.
C8 and T1 are for intrinsic hand muscles.
C4 is not involved in the biceps reflex.
citation: Biceps brachii tendon reflex: C5 and 6

statement: A patient presents with a deep laceration in the cubital fossa. The surgeon notes that the bicipital aponeurosis has been transected. Which nerve is located immediately deep to this structure and at risk of injury?
optionExplanations: The median nerve is the content lying deep to the aponeurosis.
The radial nerve is deeper and more lateral in the fossa.
The ulnar nerve passes behind the medial epicondyle.
The musculocutaneous nerve is lateral to the biceps tendon.
The axillary nerve is in the shoulder region.
citation: The median nerve lies deep to the bicipital aponeurosis in the cubital fossa

statement: From which specific structure does the basilic vein originate?
optionExplanations: The basilic vein starts at the medial end of the dorsal venous arch.
The lateral side gives rise to the cephalic vein.
The palmar plexus drains into the arch but is not the direct origin.
The median cubital vein is a communication, not the origin.
The axillary vein is the termination of the basilic vein.
citation: The basilic vein arises from the medial side of the dorsal venous arch

statement: A patient suffers a fracture of the lateral epicondyle of the humerus. During surgical repair, the surgeon must identify the radial nerve in the cubital fossa. At what specific landmark does this nerve typically bifurcate?
optionExplanations: The radial nerve divides at the level of the lateral epicondyle.
The medial epicondyle is on the opposite side.
The deep branch winds around the neck of the radius later.
The radial tuberosity is distal to the bifurcation.
The biceps tendon is medial to the radial nerve.
citation: The radial nerve divides into superficial and deep branches at the level of the lateral epicondyle

statement: A patient with a suspected C7 nerve root compression undergoes neurological testing. The physician taps the tendon of the muscle responsible for elbow extension. Which reflex is being tested?
optionExplanations: The triceps reflex tests C6, C7, and C8 segments.
The biceps reflex tests C5 and C6.
The brachioradialis reflex tests C5, C6, and C7.
There is no standard supinator reflex for C7.
There is no standard pronator reflex for C7.
citation: Triceps tendon reflex: C6, 7, and 8

statement: A clinician needs to perform central venous catheterization. Why is the basilic vein preferred over the cephalic vein for this procedure?
optionExplanations: The basilic vein is in a direct line with the axillary vein.
Both veins contain valves.
Both are superficial veins.
The basilic vein actually increases in diameter.
The cephalic vein passes through the deltopectoral triangle, which is a disadvantage.
citation: The median cubital or basilic veins are the veins of choice for central venous catheterization

statement: Which two muscles constitute the floor of the cubital fossa?
optionExplanations: The floor is formed by the supinator (lateral) and brachialis (medial).
The biceps is a content, not the floor.
These muscles form the boundaries.
The pronator teres is a boundary.
The coracobrachialis is in the arm, not the fossa floor.
citation: Floor: Supinator muscle laterally; the brachialis muscle medially

statement: A 7-year-old girl is brought to the clinic with a cold, pulseless hand following a supracondylar humeral fracture. Which structure in the cubital fossa is most likely compromised?
optionExplanations: The brachial artery is frequently damaged in supracondylar fractures.
Median nerve injury causes motor/sensory loss, not pulselessness.
Radial nerve injury causes wrist drop, not ischemia.
Vein injury causes swelling, not loss of pulse.
Basilic vein injury does not cause a cold, pulseless hand.
citation: Damage to or pressure on the brachial artery can occur at the time of the fracture

statement: During a blood donation drive, a nurse selects the median cubital vein for collection. What is the typical anatomical course of this vein in the cubital fossa?
optionExplanations: The median cubital vein typically runs upward and medially.
It does not run downward or laterally.
It is not horizontal.
It is a superficial vein, not deep to muscles.
It is anterior in the cubital fossa.
citation: The median cubital vein, a branch of the cephalic vein in the cubital fossa, runs upward and medially and joins the basilic vein.

statement: A neurologist is assessing a patient with a suspected upper motor neuron lesion. Tapping the insertion of the brachioradialis tendon results in supination of the radioulnar joints. Which spinal segments are being tested?
optionExplanations: The brachioradialis reflex involves C5, C6, and C7.
C6, C7, and C8 are for the triceps reflex.
C4 is not part of this reflex arc.
C8 and T1 are for hand muscles.
While C5 and C6 are involved, C7 is also part of the segmental supply.
citation: Brachioradialis tendon reflex: C5, 6, and 7

statement: What structures form the roof of the cubital fossa?
optionExplanations: The roof consists of skin, fascia, and the reinforcing aponeurosis.
These form the floor.
These form the boundaries.
These are contents.
These are contents.
